topics.title: Conceptual Design and Detailed Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT616 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Conceptual Design and Detailed Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Conceptual Design – Formal Definition

**Conceptual Design** in chemical process engineering is the **front-end loading (FEL)** phase where the overall process concept is established. It involves translating a laboratory-proven chemistry into a viable industrial process by defining the basic flow scheme, selecting core unit operations, performing preliminary mass and energy balances, identifying the most hazardous substances, and embedding **inherent safety** principles (attenuate, substitute, moderate, simplify) at the earliest possible stage. This phase is governed by approximate data, heuristic rules, and short-cut calculation methods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Conceptual design answers the question — *"Can we make this chemical SAFELY and PROFITABLY at scale?"* It is also called **Basic Engineering Design** or **Project Definition Phase (FEL-1, FEL-2)**.

## 1.2 Detailed Design – Formal Definition

**Detailed Design** (also termed *Engineering Design* or *FEL-3 / Execution Phase*) is the rigorous engineering stage where every piece of equipment, pipe, instrument, and safety device is fully specified. It includes finalizing **Process Flow Diagrams (PFDs)**, generating **Piping & Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs)**, performing detailed mechanical design of pressure vessels and heat exchangers, sizing relief valves, selecting materials of construction, and integrating layers of protection per **IEC 61511 / ISA 84**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are **building a house**:

| Design Phase | House Analogy | Chemical Plant Equivalent |
|--------------|---------------|---------------------------|
| **Conceptual Design** | Architectural sketch — *how many rooms, where, basic style* | Flowsheet, reactor type, separation train, inherent safety choice |
| **Detailed Design** | Full engineering drawings, plumbing, electrical wiring | P&ID, vessel thickness, relief valve orifice, instrument loops |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Conceptual comes first:** In a chemical plant, **80% of the lifecycle safety cost is committed during the conceptual stage** (Kletz, 1984). A wrong solvent choice at FEL-1 cannot be fixed by adding more safety interlocks later — it requires expensive retrofits.

---

## 1.4 Design Phases — KTU-Standard Stage Gate Model

$$\boxed{\text{Conceptual} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Basic} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Detailed} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Construction} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Commissioning} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Operation}}$$

**Stage Gate Reviews (KTU / Industry Practice):**
1. **Gate 1 — Process Concept Review** (after conceptual design)
2. **Gate 2 — P&ID Review / HAZOP 0** (after 30% P&ID)
3. **Gate 3 — HAZOP 1 / SIL Classification** (after 60% P&ID)
4. **Gate 4 — Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)**

---

## 1.5 Why Both Phases Matter for Safety

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Students often treat "design" as a single activity. Process safety literature (CCPS, 2008) clearly separates the two because **safety decisions made in conceptual design CANNOT be compensated for by engineered safeguards added in detailed design.** This is the *Inherent Safety Hierarchy* (most effective → least effective):
>
> **Eliminate → Substitute → Attenuate → Moderate → Simplify → Add Add-on Safety → Procedural → PPE**

---

## 1.6 Core Deliverables — Quick Reference

| Deliverable | Conceptual Stage | Detailed Stage |
|-------------|------------------|----------------|
| Block Flow Diagram (BFD) | ✅ Created | — |
| Process Flow Diagram (PFD) | Preliminary | Finalized |
| P&ID | — | ✅ Created |
| Material/Energy Balance | Order-of-magnitude | Refined |
| Equipment Sizing | Heuristic-based | Rigorous (ASME) |
| Relief Device Sizing | Approximate | API 520/521 rigorous |
| HAZOP | Preliminary | Full P&ID-based |
| LOPA | Initial assessment | Final SIL determination |
| Inherent Safety Review | ✅ Maximum leverage | Limited options left |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Conceptual Design — Step-by-Step Logic

The conceptual design proceeds as a structured decision tree:

1. **Define Product & Capacity** (basis: tons/year)
2. **Select Reaction Pathway** (chemistry, conversion, selectivity)
3. **Identify Most Hazardous Chemicals** (flammability, toxicity, reactivity — Dow Fire & Explosion Index preliminary)
4. **Choose Inherent Safety Strategy** (e.g., dilute acid instead of concentrated; aqueous reaction instead of organic solvent)
5. **Construct Block Flow Diagram** — major unit operations as boxes
6. **Develop Preliminary PFD** — streams, recycles, utilities
7. **Perform Order-of-Magnitude (OOM) Mass/Energy Balance** — accuracy ±50%
8. **Preliminary Equipment Sizing** — using shortcut methods (e.g., 6-tenths rule, Douglas heuristic)
9. **Preliminary Economic Analysis** — Capital (FCI) + Operating cost (WC)
10. **Identify Long-Lead Items & Critical Safety Concerns** — flag for detailed phase

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula — Six-Tenths Rule for Conceptual Cost Scaling:**
>
> $$\text{Cost}_B = \text{Cost}_A \cdot \left(\frac{\text{Capacity}_B}{\text{Capacity}_A}\right)^{0.6}$$
>
> Used for rapid cost estimation between similar plants at conceptual stage.

## 2.2 Detailed Design — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Finalize PFD** with full stream data (T, P, composition, phase, flow rate)
2. **Generate P&ID** (every valve, instrument, line, fitting shown)
3. **Perform Detailed Mass/Energy Balance** — accuracy ±5%
4. **Mechanical Design** — pressure vessel (UG-27 ASME BPVC Section VIII), heat exchanger (TEMA), pump NPSH
5. **Instrumentation & Control** — Distributed Control System (DCS) architecture, Safety Instrumented System (SIS)
6. **Relief System Design** — API 520/521; size for fire case, blocked outlet, tube rupture
7. **Piping Design** — ASME B31.3 process piping, pipe class, flexibility analysis
8. **Materials of Construction** — corrosion charts, ASME B16.5 flanges
9. **Hazard Analysis** — HAZOP, What-If, FMEA on finalized P&ID
10. **LOPA & SIL Determination** — IEC 61511
11. **Operating Procedures & Emergency Response** — written programs

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Six-tenths rule (cost) | $C_B = C_A (Q_B/Q_A)^{0.6}$ | — |
| 2 | Vessel wall thickness (cylindrical) | $t = \dfrac{PR}{SE - 0.6P}$ | in / mm; ASME BPVC VIII UG-27 |
| 3 | Vessel head thickness (2:1 elliptical) | $t = \dfrac{PD}{2SE - 0.2P}$ | — |
| 4 | Design pressure rule | $P_{\text{design}} = \max(MAWP, P_{\text{safety relief set}})$ | — |
| 5 | Design temperature | $T_{\text{design}} = \max(T_{\text{operating}}, T_{\text{design}})$ | — |
| 6 | Corrosion allowance | $t_{\text{actual}} = t_{\text{calc}} + CA$ | mm |
| 7 | Joint efficiency $E$ | For spot-examined $= 0.70$; full RT $= 1.00$ | ASME BPVC |
| 8 | Allowable stress $S$ | From ASME II Part D, at design T | MPa / psi |
| 9 | NPSH available | $NPSH_A = (P_{\text{suction}}/\rho g) - (P_{\text{vapor}}/\rho g) + h_s - h_f$ | m |
| 10 | NPSH margin | $NPSH_A \geq NPSH_R + 1\text{ m (safety margin)}$ | m |
| 11 | API 520 relief orifice | $A = \dfrac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot \sqrt{(P_1 - P_2)/\rho}}$ | in² |
| 12 | LOPA risk reduction | $f_{\text{SIL}} = f_{\text{initiating}} \times PFD_{\text{SIF}}$ | dimensionless |
| 13 | LOPA target frequency | $f_{\text{target}} = 10^{-6}\,\text{per yr}$ (typical worker-fatal) | per year |
| 14 | Inherent Safety Index (ISI) | $ISI = I_{\text{RI}} + I_{\text{FI}} + I_{\text{TI}} + I_{\text{MI}}$ | score, lower = safer |
| 15 | Dow F&EI Penalty | $F\&EI = F_3 \times MF$ | Dow Index |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Safety:** All absolute values use `\vert` / `\mid` style; no bare pipe characters in tables.

## 2.4 Inherent Safety Concepts — KTU Favourite

**Kletz's Four Principles of Inherent Safety:**

1. **Intensification** — use less inventory of hazardous material
2. **Substitution** — replace hazardous chemical with safer one
3. **Attenuation** — use lower temperature, lower pressure, dilute solution
4. **Limitation of Effects (Moderate)** — change geometry to reduce spill area (e.g., bunds)

**Plus the modern extension:**

5. **Simplification** — fewer components, fail-safe design, easier procedures

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inherent Safety is 100×–1000× more reliable than add-on protection** because it removes the hazard rather than managing it.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in Real Plants

| Application | Why It Matters |
|-------------|----------------|
| Refinery process units | Conceptual choice of hydrocracker vs. coker affects explosion risk |
| Pharmaceutical batch reactors | Dilute chemistry prevents runaway |
| LNG plants | Inventory minimization reduces BLEVE potential |
| Chlor-alkali plants | Substitution of mercury cell by membrane cell eliminated mercury hazard |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: ASME BPVC Section VIII — Cylindrical Pressure Vessel Wall Thickness

**Problem:** A horizontal cylindrical pressure vessel has:
- Internal radius $R = 0.6$ m
- Internal design pressure $P = 2.0$ MPa
- Joint efficiency $E = 0.85$
- Allowable stress $S = 137$ MPa (at design temperature)
- Corrosion allowance $CA = 3$ mm

Find the **required wall thickness $t$**.

**Step 1 — Recall the ASME BPVC Section VIII, Div. 1, UG-27 formula for cylindrical shell under internal pressure:**

$$t = \dfrac{PR}{SE - 0.6P}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (using consistent SI units: convert everything to mm and MPa):**

$$R = 600\ \text{mm},\quad P = 2.0\ \text{MPa},\quad S = 137\ \text{MPa},\quad E = 0.85$$

$$t = \dfrac{(2.0)(600)}{(137)(0.85) - 0.6(2.0)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator:**

$$\begin{aligned}
SE &= 137 \times 0.85 = 116.45\ \text{MPa} \\
0.6P &= 0.6 \times 2.0 = 1.2\ \text{MPa} \\
SE - 0.6P &= 116.45 - 1.2 = 115.25\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator:**

$$PR = 2.0 \times 600 = 1200\ \text{MPa·mm}$$

**Step 5 — Divide:**

$$t = \dfrac{1200}{115.25} = 10.41\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 6 — Add corrosion allowance:**

$$t_{\text{nominal}} = t_{\text{calc}} + CA = 10.41 + 3.0 = 13.41\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 7 — Round up to nearest standard plate:**

$$t_{\text{used}} = 14\ \text{mm}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{t_{\text{used}} = 14\ \text{mm}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: LOPA Risk Reduction Calculation

**Problem:** A reactor runaway scenario is evaluated. Given:
- Initiating event frequency $f_{\text{initiating}} = 1 \times 10^{-2}$ per year (BPCS loop fails once per 100 years? No — typical: control valve fails 0.1/yr)
- Independent Protection Layer 1 (relief valve): $PFD_1 = 1 \times 10^{-2}$
- Independent Protection Layer 2 (high-temperature interlock, SIL 1): $PFD_2 = 1 \times 10^{-2}$
- Worker exposure (already in safe area): $P_{\text{fatal}\vert\text{event}} = 0.1$

**Step 1 — Compute Mitigated Event Frequency:**

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{mitigated}} &= f_{\text{initiating}} \times PFD_1 \times PFD_2 \\
&= 1 \times 10^{-2} \times 1 \times 10^{-2} \times 1 \times 10^{-2} \\
&= 1 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{per year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Risk (Fatalities/year):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Risk} &= f_{\text{mitigated}} \times P_{\text{fatal}\vert\text{event}} \\
&= 1 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.1 \\
&= 1 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{fatalities/year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compare to Tolerable Risk Target:**

Typical company target = $1 \times 10^{-6}$ fatalities/year (CCPS guideline).

Since $1 \times 10^{-7} < 1 \times 10^{-6}$ → **Risk is ALARP-acceptable.**

**Step 4 — Required SIF PFD if adding new SIL 2 SIF:**

If we wanted to add a SIL-2 SIF ($PFD = 10^{-3}$) instead of the SIL-1:

$$f_{\text{new}} = 1 \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-3} = 1 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{per year}$$

$$\boxed{\text{LOPA confirms SIL-1 is sufficient; SIL-2 gives extra margin}}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Conceptual vs Detailed Design Comparison

| Parameter | Conceptual Design | Detailed Design |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Accuracy of mass balance** | ±50% | ±5% |
| **Type of diagram** | Block Flow Diagram (BFD) + preliminary PFD | Finalized PFD + complete P&ID |
| **Equipment sizing** | Heuristic / 6-tenths rule | Rigorous (ASME, TEMA, API) |
| **Cost estimate** | AACE Class 5 (±50%) | AACE Class 2/3 (±15%) |
| **Safety analysis** | Inherent safety review + preliminary HAZOP | Full HAZOP, LOPA, SIL, QRA |
| **Time duration** | 2–6 months | 6–18 months |
| **Cost % of total project** | 2–5% engineering cost | 15–25% engineering cost |
| **Cost commitment** | ~70% of lifecycle cost | 25% of lifecycle cost |
| **Data required** | Lab data, literature | Vendor data, pilot plant data |
| **Hazard analysis** | Preliminary Dow F&EI / CEI | Full P&ID-level HAZOP, LOPA |
| **Relief sizing** | Order of magnitude | API 520/521 rigorous |
| **Materials of construction** | General (CS / SS) | Specific (e.g., SS316L with Cl⁻ limits) |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Six-Tenths Rule

**Problem:** A reference plant produces 50,000 tons/yr of acetic acid at a fixed capital cost of ₹80 crore. Estimate the capital cost of a similar plant producing 80,000 tons/yr.

**Given:**
$$\text{Cost}_A = ₹80\ \text{crore},\quad Q_A = 50{,}000\ \text{t/yr},\quad Q_B = 80{,}000\ \text{t/yr}$$

**Formula:**

$$C_B = C_A \left(\dfrac{Q_B}{Q_A}\right)^{0.6}$$

**Step 1 — Capacity ratio:**

$$\dfrac{Q_B}{Q_A} = \dfrac{80{,}000}{50{,}000} = 1.6$$

**Step 2 — Exponentiate:**

$$(1.6)^{0.6} = e^{0.6 \ln 1.6} = e^{0.6 \times 0.4700} = e^{0.2820} = 1.326$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$C_B = 80 \times 1.326 = ₹106.1\ \text{crore}$$

$$\boxed{C_B \approx ₹106\ \text{crore}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This estimate is **for cost only**, not including safety adders. At conceptual stage, an additional 10–15% must be reserved for **inherent safety modifications**.

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Relief Valve Sizing (Conceptual Level)

**Step 1 — Identify design scenario** (most credible: external fire per API 521 §6.4).

**Step 2 — Compute required relief capacity (vapor generation):**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dfrac{F \cdot A^{0.82}}{1000} \\
\text{where } F &= \text{environment factor},\ A = \text{wetted surface (ft}^2\text{)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to mass flow:**

$$W = \dfrac{Q \cdot M}{22.4} \cdot \dfrac{520}{T}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate orifice area:**

$$A = \dfrac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot P_1} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{TZ}{M}}$$

**Step 5 — Select standard API 520 orifice size (D, E, F, G, H, J, K, L, M, N, P, Q, R).**

This sequence appears in **KTU typical exam questions** (14-mark problems).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid — Process Safety Design Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Conceptual Design FEL1] --> B[Inherent Safety Review]
    B --> C[Block Flow Diagram BFD]
    C --> D[Preliminary PFD with Mass Energy Balance]
    D --> E[Gate 1 Stage Review]
    E --> F[Detailed Design FEL3]
    F --> G[Final PFD and P and ID]
    G --> H[HAZOP Study]
    H --> I[LOPA and SIL Assignment]
    I --> J[Mechanical Design ASME]
    J --> K[Relief Valve Sizing API 520]
    K --> L[Construction and Commissioning]
    L --> M[Operation and Maintenance]
    M --> N[Periodic HAZOP Re validation]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style F fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style H fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000
    style I fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — Conceptual vs Detailed Design — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        A1[Define Product and Capacity] --> A2[Select Reaction Chemistry]
        A2 --> A3[Identify Hazards Dow FEI]

        A4 --> A5[Build Block Flow Diagram]
        A5 --> A6[Preliminary PFD]

    end

    subgraph DETAILED[DETAILED DESIGN PHASE]
        B1[Finalize PFD] --> B2[Develop P and ID]

        B4 --> B5[LOPA and SIL Determination]
        B5 --> B6[Mechanical Design ASME BPVC]
        B6 --> B7[Relief System API 520 521]

    end

    Gate1 --> B1

```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA) Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    I[Initiating Event Frequency] --> M[Multiply]
    IPL1[IPL1 BPCS Control Loop] --> M
    M --> IPL2[IPL2 Alarm Operator Action]

    IPL3 --> IPL4[IPL4 SIF Safety Instrumented Function]
    IPL4 --> O[Mitigated Event Frequency]

    style I fill:#FF6347

```

## 4.4 Mermaid — Inherent Safety Hierarchy (Effectiveness Pyramid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Eliminate the Hazard - Most Effective] --> L2[Substitute Less Hazardous Material]
    L2 --> L3[Attenuate Temperature Pressure Concentration]
    L3 --> L4[Limit Effects Bunding Spacing]
    L4 --> L5[Simplify Plant Design]
    L5 --> L6[Add Engineered Safeguards SIS]
    L6 --> L7[Procedural Controls and PPE - Least Effective]

```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** Always justify going DOWN the pyramid only after exhausting options ABOVE.
