<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Differentiate between conceptual design and detailed design of a chemical plant.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Conceptual Design | Detailed Design |
|--------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Stage | Early / FEL-1, FEL-2 | Late / FEL-3 |
| Output | BFD + Preliminary PFD | Final PFD + P&ID |
| Accuracy of mass balance | ±50% | ±5% |
| Safety review | Inherent safety check | Full HAZOP + LOPA |
| Cost estimate class | AACE Class 5 (±50%) | AACE Class 2 (±15%) |

Conceptual design establishes **what** to build and **why**, while detailed design defines **how** to build it safely and reliably. *(3 marks)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** State Kletz's four principles of inherent safety.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Intensification** — minimize inventory of hazardous materials.
2. **Substitution** — replace hazardous substance with a less hazardous one (e.g., water-based paint instead of solvent-based).
3. **Attenuation** — operate at lower temperature, pressure, or concentration.
4. **Limitation of effects (Moderate)** — geometry/spatial design to minimize damage if release occurs.

*(Plus the modern 5th principle: **Simplification** — fail-safe, fewer components.)* *(3 marks)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 MARKS**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 1 | Conceptual Design & Detailed Design]**

> **(a)** Describe in detail the **stages of conceptual design** of a chemical process plant. Discuss the role of **inherent safety** at each stage with an example. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A cylindrical pressure vessel has internal diameter $D = 1.2$ m, internal design pressure $P = 1.5$ MPa, allowable stress $S = 120$ MPa, joint efficiency $E = 0.80$, corrosion allowance $CA = 2$ mm. Determine the **required wall thickness** as per ASME BPVC Section VIII Div. 1. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**(a) Stages of Conceptual Design (7 marks):**

**Stage 1 — Definition of Product and Capacity:** Identify target molecule, plant capacity (tons/year), purity specification, and raw material availability.

**Stage 2 — Reaction Pathway Selection:** Evaluate alternative chemistries based on conversion, selectivity, and especially **thermodynamic hazard potential** (heat of reaction, gas evolution). Choose the route with the smallest exotherm if multiple routes exist.

**Stage 3 — Hazard Identification:** Apply preliminary Dow Fire & Explosion Index, chemical reactivity matrix, and toxicity screening (LC₅₀, IDLH).

**Stage 4 — Inherent Safety Application (Kletz's principles):**
- **Substitute:** Use aqueous route instead of organic solvent (e.g., water-based paints).
- **Attenuate:** Run reaction at lower T/P where feasible.
- **Intensify:** Use microreactor inventory <1 L instead of stirred tank reactor (STR) of 5000 L.

**Stage 5 — Block Flow Diagram:** Major unit operations drawn as blocks.

**Stage 6 — Preliminary PFD:** Streams with approximate T, P, composition.

**Stage 7 — Order-of-Magnitude Mass/Energy Balance:** Accuracy ±50%.

**Stage 8 — Preliminary Equipment Sizing & Costing:** Use six-tenths rule, heuristic factors.

**Example:** In the Bhopal disaster (1984), the conceptual design stored **41 tonnes of methyl isocyanate (MIC)** — a textbook violation of intensification. Had the plant used **on-demand generation of MIC** (intensification), the disaster magnitude would have been vastly reduced.

*(7 marks — 1 mark per key point + example)*

---

**(b) Pressure Vessel Thickness Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
$$D = 1.2\ \text{m} = 1200\ \text{mm},\quad P = 1.5\ \text{MPa},\quad S = 120\ \text{MPa},\quad E = 0.80,\quad CA = 2\ \text{mm}$$

**Radius:**
$$R = \dfrac{D}{2} = 600\ \text{mm}$$

**ASME UG-27 Formula:**

$$t = \dfrac{PR}{SE - 0.6P}$$

**Substitution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
SE &= 120 \times 0.80 = 96\ \text{MPa} \\
0.6P &= 0.6 \times 1.5 = 0.9\ \text{MPa} \\
SE - 0.6P &= 96 - 0.9 = 95.1\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

$$t = \dfrac{(1.5)(600)}{95.1} = \dfrac{900}{95.1} = 9.46\ \text{mm}$$

**Add corrosion allowance:**

$$t_{\text{nominal}} = 9.46 + 2 = 11.46\ \text{mm}$$

**Round up to nearest standard plate thickness:**

$$\boxed{t_{\text{used}} = 12\ \text{mm}}$$

*(7 marks — full marks for showing formula, substitution, denominator calculation, numerator calculation, division, corrosion allowance, and final rounded value)*

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 MARKS** *(Alternative to Question A)*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 1]**

> **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **difference between Process Flow Diagram (PFD) and Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)**. List the key information present in a P&ID. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A runaway reaction scenario is analyzed by LOPA. Initiating event (loss of cooling) frequency = $1 \times 10^{-1}$/yr. Three independent protection layers: BPCS ($PFD = 10^{-1}$), Operator Alarm Response ($PFD = 10^{-1}$), and Relief Valve ($PFD = 10^{-2}$). Worker fatality probability given event = $0.2$. **Determine the mitigated risk and compare with tolerable risk of $10^{-5}$ fatalities/yr.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**(a) PFD vs P&ID (7 marks):**

| Feature | PFD | P&ID |
|---------|-----|------|
| Purpose | Shows process flow & mass balance | Shows mechanical design & control |
| Equipment | Major items only | Every valve, instrument, fitting |
| Piping | Primary process lines | All lines incl. drains, vents |
| Instrumentation | Few control loops | Every instrument, tag number |
| Used in | Conceptual / early detailed | Detailed design + operations |
| Standards | None strict | ISA 5.1, ISO 14617 |

**Key Information in a P&ID:**
1. All process equipment (vessels, pumps, exchangers)
2. All piping — line numbers, sizes, service, material
3. All valves — manual, check, control, safety relief
4. All instruments — tag numbers (e.g., TT-101, FCV-202)
5. Control loops & interlocks
6. Safety devices (PSV, Rupture Disc, SIF)
7. Operating, design, and test pressures
8. Insulation, tracing, special notes

*(7 marks — 3 for comparison, 4 for list of P&ID info)*

---

**(b) LOPA Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $f_I = 1 \times 10^{-1}$ per year
- $PFD_1$ (BPCS) $= 10^{-1}$
- $PFD_2$ (Operator) $= 10^{-1}$
- $PFD_3$ (Relief) $= 10^{-2}$
- $P_{\text{fatal}\vert\text{event}} = 0.2$

**Step 1 — Mitigated Event Frequency:**

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{mitigated}} &= f_I \times PFD_1 \times PFD_2 \times PFD_3 \\
&= 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2} \\
&= 10^{-5}\ \text{per year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Fatality Risk:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Risk} &= f_{\text{mitigated}} \times P_{\text{fatal}\vert\text{event}} \\
&= 10^{-5} \times 0.2 \\
&= 2 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{fatalities/year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison with Tolerable Risk:**

$$\text{Risk} = 2 \times 10^{-6} \lt 10^{-5}\ (\text{target})$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{\text{Risk is below tolerable limit; ALARP demonstrated.}}$$

*(7 marks — 2 marks each step + 1 mark for comparison/conclusion)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up PFD and P&ID** — PFD does NOT contain control valves, tag numbers, or every pipe. KTU examiners deduct 2 marks if you list these as PFD features.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Corrosion Allowance (CA)** in vessel thickness — students often quote $t_{\text{calc}}$ without adding CA, losing 1–2 marks.
>
> 3. **Not rounding UP to standard plate** — vessel thickness must be rounded UP to the next standard plate thickness (6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16 mm…). Never round DOWN.
>
> 4. **LOPA: forgetting consequence probability** — students compute $f_{\text{mitigated}}$ correctly but skip multiplying by $P_{\text{fatal}\vert\text{event}}$. This is a 2-mark loss.
>
> 5. **Confusing PFD with BFD** — Block Flow Diagram (BFD) shows only major process blocks; PFD shows full stream data. They are NOT the same.
>
> 6. **Writing "HAZOP is done in conceptual design"** — Wrong! Conceptual design has preliminary hazard review (DOW F&EI), while full HAZOP happens during detailed design on the P&ID.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Conceptual Design (FEL-1, FEL-2):** establishes chemistry, route, hazards, and inherent safety strategy; outputs BFD + preliminary PFD; ±50% accuracy.
- **Detailed Design (FEL-3):** finalizes PFD + P&ID, performs mechanical design (ASME BPVC), relief sizing (API 520/521), piping (B31.3), and full HAZOP/LOPA.
- **Inherent Safety Hierarchy:** Eliminate → Substitute → Attenuate → Moderate → Simplify → Add-on → Procedural → PPE (most → least effective).
- **ASME BPVC Section VIII Div. 1, UG-27 (cylindrical):**
$$t = \dfrac{PR}{SE - 0.6P} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_{\text{used}} = t_{\text{calc}} + CA \quad \text{(round UP)}$$
- **PFD ≠ P&ID:** PFD is mass/energy focused; P&ID is construction and operations focused.
- **LOPA Risk Equation:**
$$\text{Risk} = f_{\text{initiating}} \times \prod PFD_i \times P_{\text{consequence}}$$
- **Six-tenths Rule:**
$$C_B = C_A \left(\dfrac{Q_B}{Q_A}\right)^{0.6}$$
- **Stage Gate Reviews:** Gate 1 → Process Concept; Gate 2 → HAZOP 0; Gate 3 → HAZOP 1 + SIL; Gate 4 → PSSR (Pre-Startup Safety Review).
- **Bhopal (1984)** is the most cited KTU example of *failed conceptual design* (MIC inventory 41 t — no intensification, no substitution, no attenuation).
- **Typical Tolerable Risk:** $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-6}$ fatalities/yr (CCPS).
- **Final wall thickness must ALWAYS be rounded UP to the next standard plate + corrosion allowance.**
- **Always justify the choice of safety strategy using the Inherent Safety Hierarchy before resorting to add-on safeguards.**
- **HAZOP is performed on finalized P&ID during detailed design** — never on conceptual flow sheets.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT616 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flow Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flow Diagrams (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** A *Flow Diagram* is a standardized graphical engineering drawing used in the chemical process industry (CPI) to represent the sequence of unit operations, process streams, major equipment, control loops, and instrumentation of an entire chemical plant. As per the KTU OESFT616 syllabus (Module 1), flow diagrams constitute the foundational language of process safety, enabling engineers to perform **HAZOP** (Hazard and Operability Studies), **HAZID** (Hazard Identification), **What-If Analysis**, and **LOPA** (Layer of Protection Analysis).

In chemical process safety, flow diagrams are categorized into **three hierarchical levels** of detail, moving from conceptual design toward detailed engineering:

1. **Block Flow Diagram (BFD)** – Conceptual level
2. **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** – Process engineering level
3. **Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)** – Detailed engineering / Safety level

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are planning a road trip from **Kochi to Delhi**:

- **Block Flow Diagram (BFD)** is like saying: *“Kochi → Bangalore → Hyderabad → Delhi.”* You only know the major cities you pass through — the stops are "blocks."
- **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** is like a more detailed travel plan: *“Kochi (8 AM, full tank) → Bangalore (refuel + breakfast, 50 km left) → Hyderabad (lunch stop) → Delhi (reach by 9 PM).”* Now you see timing, fuel status, and key stops.
- **Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)** is the **GPS-level engineering blueprint**: every kilometer-marker, every toll booth sensor, every highway patrol camera, every alternate route is mapped. This is the level used by the **safety auditor** to prevent accidents.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Flow Diagrams matter in Process Safety:** Without a unified graphical language, a chemical engineer in India and a safety inspector in another country cannot communicate the layout of a distillation column, a relief valve, or an emergency interlock. Flow diagrams eliminate ambiguity. **ANSI/ISA-S5.1** and **ISO 14617** are the international standards governing these symbols.

## 1.3 Physical Standards & Key Metrics

| Standard | Full Form | Governing Body |
|----------|-----------|----------------|
| **ANSI/ISA-S5.1** | Instrumentation Symbols and Identification | International Society of Automation |
| **ISO 14617** | Graphical Symbols for Diagrams | International Organization for Standardization |
| **ASME Y14.1** | Decimal Inch Drawing Sheet Size | American Society of Mechanical Engineers |

> [!NOTE]
> Standard stream line width in P&IDs: **0.35 mm to 0.7 mm**. Process lines are thicker; utility/instrument lines are thinner.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Levels — Step-by-Step Logic

### **Level 1: Block Flow Diagram (BFD)**

- **What it shows:** Major process sections (e.g., *Reactor → Separator → Distillation Column*) as labeled rectangles ("blocks") connected by **process streams** (lines with arrows).
- **Information density:** Very low. Typically **5 to 10 blocks** maximum.
- **When used:** Conceptual/feasibility stage of a project.
- **Symbols used:** Rectangles for equipment, single arrows for streams.
- **Stream data shown:** Stream number, flow rate (kg/hr), temperature, pressure.
- **Safety relevance:** Used in early-stage **Inherent Safety** reviews (Kletz's principles: minimize, substitute, moderate, simplify).

### **Level 2: Process Flow Diagram (PFD)**

- **What it shows:** Every major piece of equipment (heat exchangers, pumps, columns, reactors, vessels) drawn with simplified geometry. All major process lines, utility lines, and the **major control loops** (the ones governing plant operation, not safety) are included.
- **Information density:** Moderate. Includes:
  - **Material and energy balances** on every stream
  - Equipment sizes (column diameter, heat exchanger area)
  - Major **control valves** and primary instruments
  - Stream composition tables
- **Safety relevance:** Forms the basis for **HAZOP** node identification and **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)**.

### **Level 3: Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)**

- **What it shows:** Every pipe, every fitting, every valve (including isolation, check, relief, and control valves), every instrument (local and DCS-mounted), every interlock, and every safety device.
- **Information density:** Maximum. P&IDs are sometimes called the **"engineering schematic of truth"** because everything required to construct, operate, and maintain the plant is shown.
- **Safety relevance:** **The most critical document for process safety.** Used for:
  - HAZOP & LOPA studies
  - **Management of Change (MOC)** procedures
  - **Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)**
  - Emergency isolation logic verification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The KTU 2024 Scheme frequently asks: *"Differentiate between PFD and P&ID"* (often a 7-mark sub-question). Memorize the **absence** of certain items in PFDs as much as their **presence** in P&IDs.

## 2.2 Instrumentation Identification (ISA Tagging Logic)

The **ISA Tag Numbering Convention** is a structured alphanumeric code with the form:

$$
\text{ISA Tag} = \underbrace{\text{XX}}_{\text{Function Letters}} \; \underbrace{\text{Y}}_{\text{Loop Number}} \; \underbrace{\text{Z}}_{\text{Suffix}}
$$

**Where:**
- **Function Letters** (XX) describe the *measured variable* and *function*. Examples:
  - **T** = Temperature, **P** = Pressure, **F** = Flow, **L** = Level
  - First letter = *measured variable*; Subsequent letters = *function* (e.g., I = Indicate, C = Control, A = Alarm, V = Valve, S = Switch, T = Transmitter).
- **Loop Number** (Y): Unique integer assigned to each control loop.
- **Suffix** (Z): Optional, used for multiple instruments on the same loop.

**Example:** `FT-101` → **F**low **T**ransmitter in loop **101**. `FCV-101` → **F**low **C**ontrol **V**alve in loop **101**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> Although "flow diagrams" are graphical, KTU evaluates analytical mastery of the **quantitative information content** and **safety logic loops** represented. The following reference table consolidates the high-yield relationships.

| Concept / Metric | Expression / Symbol | Standard / Boundary Condition | Typical Unit |
|------------------|--------------------|-------------------------------|--------------|
| Stream flow rate (mass) | $\dot{m}$ | Steady-state assumption | kg/hr or kmol/hr |
| Stream flow rate (volumetric) | $\dot{V} = \dot{m} / \rho$ | Incompressible fluid | m³/hr |
| Overall material balance | $\sum \dot{m}_{\text{in}} = \sum \dot{m}_{\text{out}}$ | Closed, steady-state system | kg/hr |
| Energy balance (no reaction) | $\sum \dot{m}h_{\text{in}} = \sum \dot{m}h_{\text{out}} + \dot{Q}$ | Steady-flow, $h$ = specific enthalpy | kW |
| ISA tag – Function code | First letter denotes *measured variable* | ISA-S5.1 standard | — |
| Control loop gain (basic) | $K_c = \Delta P_{\text{valve}} / \Delta x_{\text{controller}}$ | Linear region | Dimensionless |
| Relief valve set pressure | $P_{\text{set}}$ | Typically $1.05 \times P_{\text{MAWP}}$ | barg |
| Layer of Protection (LOPA) | $\text{Mitigated Frequency} = \text{Initiating Event} \times \prod \text{PFD}_i$ | Independent IPLs | per year |
| Process stream line width | 0.35 – 0.70 mm | Visual clarity standard | mm |
| P&ID drawing scale | Not to scale (NTS) | Functional, not dimensional | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not use the `|` pipe symbol inside KTU markdown tables** — it breaks the markdown parser. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in inline LaTeX. (Example: $\vert x \vert$ instead of `|x|`.)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **Bharat Petroleum Kochi Refinery** or the **Fertilizers and Chemicals Travancore Limited (FACT)** plant, every shift handover, every safety audit, and every emergency shutdown is verified using the **latest revision of the P&ID**. A wrong valve type, a missing check valve, or an omitted pressure safety valve (PSV) on a P&ID can lead to catastrophic loss of containment — the **Bhopal disaster (1984)** and **Texas City Refinery (2005)** post-incident reports both highlighted **incomplete or outdated flow documentation** as a contributing cause.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Converting a Block Diagram into a PFD

The transition from a **BFD** to a **PFD** is governed by adding equipment-level detail. The **equipment count** grows according to the following analytical relationship:

$$
N_{\text{PFD}} = N_{\text{BFD}} + N_{\text{aux}} + N_{\text{util}}
$$

**Where:**
- $N_{\text{BFD}}$ = number of process blocks in the BFD (typically 3 – 8)
- $N_{\text{aux}}$ = number of auxiliary equipment (pumps, compressors, heat exchangers, reboilers, condensers) required to make each block functional
- $N_{\text{util}}$ = number of utility connections (cooling water, steam, refrigerant, instrument air, nitrogen)

**Step-by-step logic:**

- **Step 1 — Identify each BFD block.** Suppose we have a simple alkylation plant with three blocks: **Reactor**, **Separator**, **Distillation Column**.
- **Step 2 — For each block, enumerate required auxiliaries.**
  - Reactor requires: feed pump, feed preheater, catalyst handling system, reactor effluent cooler.
  - Separator requires: level control instrumentation, dump valve.
  - Distillation Column requires: reboiler, condenser, reflux pump, reflux drum, bottom pump.
- **Step 3 — Add utility connections.**
  - Reactor preheater → low-pressure steam.
  - Reboiler → high-pressure steam or hot oil.
  - Condenser → cooling water or refrigerant.
- **Step 4 — Apply material and energy balances** to size each piece and label stream flow rates, temperatures, and pressures.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example

**Problem (KTU-Style):** A simple BFD has the following three blocks in series:

> **Block-1 (Reactor)** $\rightarrow$ **Block-2 (Absorber)** $\rightarrow$ **Block-3 (Stripper)**

The process involves reacting CO₂ with monoethanolamine (MEA) in **Block-1**, absorbing in **Block-2**, and stripping the rich solvent in **Block-3**. Determine the **minimum number of auxiliary equipment items** required to elevate this BFD to a PFD.

**Solution (exhaustive step-by-step):**

**Step 1 — Reactor (Block-1) auxiliary equipment:**
- MEA feed pump (P-101)
- CO₂ feed compressor / blower (K-101)
- Feed preheater (E-101) using low-pressure steam
- Reactor temperature indicator and controller (TIC-101)

Auxiliary count = **4**

**Step 2 — Absorber (Block-2) auxiliary equipment:**
- Lean MEA pump (P-102) to lift solvent to top of absorber
- Lean MEA cooler (E-102) using cooling water
- Level transmitter on absorber sump (LT-103)
- Flow control on rich solvent line (FCV-104)

Auxiliary count = **4**

**Step 3 — Stripper (Block-3) auxiliary equipment:**
- Rich solvent feed pump (P-103)
- Stripper reboiler (E-103) using high-pressure steam
- Stripper condenser (E-104) using cooling water
- Reflux drum (V-101)
- Reflux pump (P-104)
- Bottom product cooler (E-105)

Auxiliary count = **6**

**Step 4 — Apply the analytical equation:**

$$
N_{\text{PFD}} = N_{\text{BFD}} + N_{\text{aux}} + N_{\text{util}}
$$

$$
N_{\text{PFD}} = 3 + (4 + 4 + 6) + N_{\text{util}}
$$

$$
N_{\text{PFD}} = 3 + 14 + N_{\text{util}}
$$

$$
N_{\text{PFD}} = 17 + N_{\text{util}}
$$

**Step 5 — Add utility connections** (e.g., utility headers, isolation valves per stream):

$$
N_{\text{util}} \approx 8 \; (\text{steam, CW, instrument air, N}_2\text{, drain})
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{\text{PFD}} = 17 + 8 = 25 \; \text{equipment items}}
$$

This analytical result quantifies the **information complexity** of the PFD relative to the BFD.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: PFD vs. P&ID (The KTU High-Yield Comparison)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is the single most-tested KTU concept on this topic. Master every row.

| Feature / Attribute | **Block Flow Diagram (BFD)** | **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** | **Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Information content** | Lowest | Moderate | Maximum |
| **Number of equipment** | 3 – 10 (blocks) | 20 – 100+ | Hundreds to thousands |
| **Stream data shown** | Stream number, flow rate | Flow rate, T, P, composition | Tag number, line spec, insulation |
| **Instrumentation shown** | None | Major control loops only | Every instrument, every loop |
| **Piping details** | None | Major process lines | All pipes, fittings, valves |
| **Safety devices** | Not shown | May show major relief valves | Every PSV, RSD, interlock, ESD |
| **Equipment geometry** | Rectangular block | Simplified schematic | Detailed with nozzles |
| **Scale** | Not to scale | Not to scale | Not to scale (NTS) |
| **Used in stage** | Feasibility / Conceptual | Basic engineering | Detailed engineering / Construction / Operation |
| **Used for HAZOP?** | No (insufficient detail) | Preliminary HAZOP | **Full HAZOP, LOPA, SIL assessment** |
| **Updating during MOC** | Rarely | Occasionally | **Every change — mandatory** |

## 3.4 Analytical Derivative: Degree of Freedom for Control Loops

For a single control loop (e.g., TIC-101 on the reactor), the **degree of freedom** indicates whether the loop is properly constrained. For a closed loop:

$$
DOF = N_{\text{variables}} - N_{\text{independent equations}}
$$

**Step-by-step:**
- **Step 1 — Variables in the loop:** $T_{\text{process}}$ (PV), $T_{\text{setpoint}}$ (SP), controller output $u$, valve position $x_v$, manipulated variable $F_{\text{coolant}}$.
- **Step 2 — Independent equations:** Controller equation, valve characteristic equation, process dynamic equation.
- **Step 3 — Evaluation:**

$$
N_{\text{variables}} = 5
$$

$$
N_{\text{equations}} = 3
$$

$$
DOF = 5 - 3 = 2
$$

- **Step 4 — Closure:** A properly designed loop uses **cascade control** (e.g., TIC master controlling a TIC slave on the coolant outlet) to reduce DOF to zero, ensuring unique, stable setpoint tracking.

$$
\boxed{DOF_{\text{cascade}} = 4 - 4 = 0}
$$

This zero-DOF condition is the analytical signature of a **functionally safe** control loop on a P&ID.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchy of Flow Diagrams (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CONCEPTUAL DESIGN STAGE] --> B[Block Flow Diagram BFD]
    B --> C[Process Flow Diagram PFD]
    C --> D[Piping and Instrumentation Diagram P and ID]
    B --> B1[Mission Define major process sections]
    B --> B2[Output Stream numbers and major flow rates]
    B --> B3[Used for Feasibility and Inherent Safety Review]
    C --> C1[Mission Include all major equipment geometry]
    C --> C2[Output Material and Energy Balances per stream]
    C --> C3[Used for Preliminary HAZOP and PHA]
    D --> D1[Mission Show every pipe valve and instrument]
    D --> D2[Output Complete P and ID set with ISA tags]
    D --> D3[Used for Detailed HAZOP LOPA SIL MOC PSSR]
    style A fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#3a6ea5,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#5a8cc4,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#7aa9d9,color:#000000
    style B1 fill:#e8eef5,color:#000000
    style B2 fill:#e8eef5,color:#000000
    style B3 fill:#e8eef5,color:#000000
    style C1 fill:#dce6f0,color:#000000
    style C2 fill:#dce6f0,color:#000000
    style C3 fill:#dce6f0,color:#000000
    style D1 fill:#c8d8e8,color:#000000
    style D2 fill:#c8d8e8,color:#000000
    style D3 fill:#c8d8e8,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 ISA Tag Decoding Flow (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start[ISA Tag String e.g. FT 101 A] --> split[Parse into 3 components]
    split --> comp1[Function Letters FT]
    split --> comp2[Loop Number 101]
    split --> comp3[Suffix A optional]
    comp1 --> f1[First Letter F equals Measured Variable]
    comp1 --> f2[Second Letter T equals Function Transmitter]
    comp2 --> loop[Loop 101 is unique across entire plant]
    comp3 --> suff[Suffix A distinguishes duplicate instruments]
    f1 --> final[Meaning Flow Transmitter loop 101]
    f2 --> final
    loop --> final
    suff --> final
    style start fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style split fill:#5a8cc4,color:#ffffff
    style final fill:#7aa9d9,color:#000000
    style comp1 fill:#e8eef5,color:#000000
    style comp2 fill:#e8eef5,color:#000000
    style comp3 fill:#e8eef5,color:#000000
    style f1 fill:#dce6f0,color:#000000
    style f2 fill:#dce6f0,color:#000000
    style loop fill:#dce6f0,color:#000000
    style suff fill:#dce6f0,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 P&ID Safety Device Layer (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    process[Process Vessel e.g. Reactor R 101] --> primary[Primary Containment Vessel Wall]
    primary --> relief[Pressure Safety Valve PSV 101]
    primary --> rupture[Rupture Disc RD 101]
    primary --> instrument[Independent Pressure Transmitter PT 101 and PT 102]
    primary --> sisk[SIS Logic Solver SIL rated]
    relief --> atm[Atmosphere Safe Discharge]
    rupture --> atm
    instrument --> sisk
    sisk --> eiv[Emergency Isolation Valve EIV 101]
    eiv --> block[Block Inlet Flow within seconds]
    style process fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style primary fill:#3a6ea5,color:#ffffff
    style relief fill:#a04040,color:#ffffff
    style rupture fill:#a04040,color:#ffffff
    style instrument fill:#5a8cc4,color:#ffffff
    style sisk fill:#c08040,color:#ffffff
    style eiv fill:#c08040,color:#ffffff
    style block fill:#7aa9d9,color:#000000
    style atm fill:#7aa9d9,color:#000000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define a Process Flow Diagram (PFD). List any four pieces of information typically shown on a PFD that are NOT shown on a Block Flow Diagram (BFD).**

**Model Answer:**

A **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** is a schematic engineering drawing that depicts the major process equipment of a chemical plant, the interconnecting process streams, and the primary control loops, drawn at a level of detail suitable for basic engineering and preliminary safety studies.

Four pieces of information shown on a PFD but **not** on a BFD are:

1. **Equipment geometry** — simplified but recognizable shapes of columns, vessels, heat exchangers, pumps, and compressors.
2. **Material and energy balances** — stream flow rate, composition, temperature, and pressure for every major line.
3. **Utility streams** — steam, cooling water, refrigerant, and instrument air connections.
4. **Major control loops** — primary feedback controllers governing plant operation (e.g., temperature, pressure, level, flow controllers).

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the significance of flow diagrams in chemical process safety with reference to HAZOP studies.**

**Model Answer:**

Flow diagrams — particularly the **P&ID** — form the **primary documentary basis** for conducting a **Hazard and Operability (HAZOP)** study. Their significance is as follows:

1. **Node identification:** The P&ID is divided into *study nodes* (typically each major vessel or section of piping), and each node is examined using guide words (No, More, Less, As Well As, Part Of, Reverse, Other Than).
2. **Cause–Consequence tracing:** Deviations in process variables (temperature, pressure, flow) can be traced through the diagram to identify plausible causes and potential consequences.
3. **Safeguard verification:** Existing safeguards (relief valves, alarms, interlocks) shown on the P&ID are evaluated for adequacy; gaps lead to safety instrumented function (SIF) recommendations.
4. **Recommendations documentation:** Output of HAZOP (recommendations for new alarms, interlocks, or procedural changes) is recorded against specific P&ID line numbers and ISA tags.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Many students mention "HAZOP" but forget to state **which** diagram drives it. The examiner expects: *"HAZOP uses P&IDs as the base document; BFDs and PFDs lack sufficient detail to perform a meaningful HAZOP."* Loss of 1 mark if omitted.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between a **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** and a **Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)** with respect to **at least six technical attributes**. Why is the P&ID considered indispensable for process safety management?

**(b) [7 Marks]** The following instruments are tagged on a P&ID:

| Tag | Tag |
|-----|-----|
| TT-201 | PI-205 |
| TIC-201 | FRC-207 |
| PSV-203 | EIV-209 |

**Decode each ISA tag** as per ANSI/ISA-S5.1 convention. State the **function** of each instrument and identify which one is a **final control element**.

---

### **Model Answer for Question A:**

**Part (a) — PFD vs. P&ID (7 Marks):**

| Attribute | **PFD** | **P&ID** |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Equipment geometry | Simplified shapes shown | Detailed shapes with nozzle connections |
| 2. Process lines | Major process lines only | Every pipe, including drains, vents, bypasses |
| 3. Valves | Only major control valves shown | Every valve type — gate, globe, check, relief, butterfly, ball, control, solenoid |
| 4. Instrumentation | Only major control loops (regulating) shown | Every instrument — local, DCS, SIS, indicator, recorder, controller, alarm, switch |
| 5. Safety devices | May show only primary relief valves | Every PSV, rupture disc, emergency isolation valve, interlock, ESD pushbutton |
| 6. Stream data | Flow rate, T, P, composition | Pipe spec, line number, insulation, tracing |
| 7. Utility lines | Major utility headers | All utility connections incl. instrument air, N₂, lube oil |

**Why P&ID is indispensable for safety:**

- It is the **only document** that captures every safeguard (relief valves, SIFs, ESDs) needed for HAZOP, LOPA, SIL classification, and PSSR.
- **Management of Change (MOC):** Any plant modification must be reflected in the P&ID before implementation — failure to update P&IDs caused real-world incidents.
- It is the **legal as-built record** presented to regulatory bodies (e.g., Factory Inspectorate, PESO, OISD).
- It enables **emergency response planning** — first responders can identify isolation valves within seconds.

---

**Part (b) — ISA Tag Decoding (7 Marks):**

**Step-by-step decoding logic:**

Applying the rule **First letter = measured variable, subsequent letters = instrument/control function**:

1. **TT-201** → **T**emperature **T**ransmitter, Loop 201. *Function:* Sends measured reactor temperature signal to the DCS. *(1 mark)*
2. **TIC-201** → **T**emperature **I**ndicating **C**ontroller, Loop 201. *Function:* Compares TT-201 signal to setpoint and outputs control signal. *(1 mark)*
3. **PSV-203** → **P**ressure **S**afety **V**alve, Loop 203. *Function:* Spring-loaded relief device that opens automatically when process pressure exceeds set pressure to prevent vessel overpressure. *(1.5 marks)*
4. **PI-205** → **P**ressure **I**ndicator, Loop 205. *Function:* Local/DCS display of pressure; no control action. *(1 mark)*
5. **FRC-207** → **F**low **R**ecording **C**ontroller, Loop 207. *Function:* Records flow rate history and outputs control signal to a flow control valve. *(1 mark)*
6. **EIV-209** → **E**mergency **I**solation **V**alve, Loop 209. *Function:* Automatically closes upon ESD/SIS activation to isolate the section. *(1.5 marks)*

**Final control element:** **PSV-203** (a relief valve is itself a self-actuated final control element) and **EIV-209** (emergency isolation valve — actuated by the SIS). Of these, **PSV-203** is the traditional answer expected by KTU examiners, as it directly modulates a process variable (pressure) without external power. *(1 mark)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a clear sketch, describe the **three hierarchical levels** of flow diagrams used in the chemical process industry. For each level, state (i) one stage of project development where it is used, and (ii) one process safety application.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A P&ID shows a centrifugal pump (P-101) drawing suction from a storage tank (T-101) and discharging into a reactor (R-101). Identify and list **at least six safety-critical elements** that **must** appear on the P&ID for this configuration to be considered safe for operation. Justify the inclusion of each.

---

### **Model Answer for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Three Levels of Flow Diagrams (7 Marks):**

1. **Block Flow Diagram (BFD):** *(2 marks)*
   - **Sketch description:** Rectangular blocks connected by arrows. Each block represents a major process section.
   - **Stage used:** *Feasibility / Conceptual Design.*
   - **Safety application:** *Inherent Safety review* (Kletz principles: minimize, substitute, moderate, simplify) to evaluate major process options.

2. **Process Flow Diagram (PFD):** *(2.5 marks)*
   - **Sketch description:** Major equipment drawn with simplified geometry (e.g., distillation column shown with trays, condenser, reboiler). Stream labels show flow rate, T, P, composition.
   - **Stage used:** *Basic / Front-End Engineering Design (FEED).*
   - **Safety application:** *Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)* and *Major control loop verification.*

3. **Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID):** *(2.5 marks)*
   - **Sketch description:** Detailed schematic showing every pipe, valve (gate, globe, check, control, relief), instrument (indicator, controller, recorder, alarm, switch, transmitter), and safety device (PSV, rupture disc, ESD, interlock). Drawn **not to scale**.
   - **Stage used:** *Detailed Engineering, Construction, Commissioning, and Operation.*
   - **Safety application:** *Full HAZOP, LOPA, SIL Classification, MOC, and PSSR.*

---

**Part (b) — Safety-Critical Elements for Pump–Tank–Reactor (7 Marks):**

| # | Element (on P&ID) | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Suction-side foot valve / check valve** on P-101 discharge | Prevents **reverse flow / siphoning** when pump trips, avoiding reactor contents draining back into the tank. *(1 mark)* |
| 2 | **Strainer / Y-strainer** at pump suction | Protects pump impeller from debris; failure causes cavitation and pump damage. *(1 mark)* |
| 3 | **Pressure Safety Valve (PSV) on R-101** | Overpressure protection; opens automatically at set pressure to prevent catastrophic vessel rupture. *(1 mark)* |
| 4 | **Pressure Transmitter (PT) and Pressure Indicator (PI) on pump discharge** | Monitors for dead-head condition; high pressure trips pump to prevent seal failure. *(1 mark)* |
| 5 | **Level Indicator/Controller (LIC) on T-101** | Prevents tank dry-out (which causes pump cavitation) and tank overfill. *(1 mark)* |
| 6 | **Emergency Isolation Valve (EIV) on tank outlet line** | Allows rapid isolation during fire, leak, or toxic release — critical for **ESD-1** activation. *(1 mark)* |
| 7 | **Rupture Disc upstream of PSV on R-101** | Protects PSV from corrosive polymerizing media; mandatory for services where PSV plugging is a risk. *(1 mark)* |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Critical):** Students often *list* safety devices but *fail to justify* them. The KTU 2024 marking scheme allocates **0.5 to 1 mark per device AND 0.5 mark per justification**. A bare list scores ≤ 3 marks out of 7. Always pair each element with its **process safety consequence**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Flow diagrams have THREE hierarchical levels:** BFD → PFD → P&ID. The information density and safety relevance **increase** at every level.
- [x] **BFD** uses rectangular blocks; used in **conceptual design** and **inherent safety** review.
- [x] **PFD** shows major equipment geometry, material/energy balances, and primary control loops; used in **FEED** and **preliminary HAZOP**.
- [x] **P&ID** shows **every pipe, every valve, every instrument, every safety device**; drawn **Not To Scale (NTS)**; used for **full HAZOP, LOPA, SIL, MOC, and PSSR**.
- [x] **ISA Tag format:** First letter = *measured variable*; subsequent letters = *function* (I = Indicate, C = Control, R = Record, T = Transmit, A = Alarm, S = Switch, V = Valve).
- [x] **Final control element examples:** Control valve (FCV), solenoid valve, pressure safety valve (PSV), emergency isolation valve (EIV).
- [x] **Standards:** ANSI/ISA-S5.1 (instrumentation symbols), ISO 14617 (graphical symbols), ASME Y14.1 (drawing sheet size).
- [x] **Quantitative information content** grows from BFD to P&ID; analytical equation: $N_{\text{PFD}} = N_{\text{BFD}} + N_{\text{aux}} + N_{\text{util}}$.
- [x] **Zero-DOF condition** for a control loop ensures functionally safe operation (achievable through cascade control).
- [x] **Failure to update P&IDs during MOC** is a documented root cause of major process industry incidents.
- [x] **PSV set pressure** typically: $P_{\text{set}} = 1.05 \times P_{\text{MAWP}}$ (Maximum Allowable Working Pressure).
- [x] **KTU high-yield comparison** — "PFD vs. P&ID" — expect this as a 7-mark sub-question in **every** KTU end-semester paper.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equipment Specifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT616 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Equipment Specifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Equipment Specifications** in Chemical Process Safety refer to the comprehensive, code-compliant engineering documents that define the essential design, construction, material, and operational parameters of process equipment (such as pressure vessels, storage tanks, heat exchangers, columns, and piping systems). These specifications act as the legally binding technical "identity card" of the equipment and are mandatory inputs for mechanical design, fabrication, inspection, and regulatory compliance under codes such as **ASME Section VIII**, **IS 2825**, and **PD 5500**.

An Equipment Specification Sheet (often called a **Data Sheet** or **Vessel Datasheet**) encapsulates parameters such as design pressure, design temperature, material of construction (MOC), corrosion allowance, joint efficiency, radiographic testing level, and applicable design code.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Equipment specifications form the foundation for the design of **overpressure protection systems** (PSV sizing) and **relief device** selection. Incorrect specification directly leads to catastrophic failures such as the **Bhopal disaster (1984)** and the **Texas City refinery explosion (2005)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a **water tank for a high-rise building at 100 m elevation**. The plumber cannot just say "make a strong tank." They must specify:
- Wall thickness (because hydrostatic pressure is **~10 bar** at the base)
- Material (stainless steel vs. mild steel affects corrosion)
- Safety factor (because pressure surges may occur)
- Test pressure (to verify integrity before commissioning)

In the same way, a **chemical reactor operating at 50 bar and 300 °C** with flammable hydrocarbons cannot be designed by intuition. The **Equipment Specification Sheet** is the engineering equivalent of a *medical prescription* — precise, code-bound, and traceable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics used universally in Equipment Specifications:**
> - **Design Pressure (P_design):** in **MPa** or **bar** (1 MPa = 10 bar)
> - **Design Temperature (T_design):** in **°C**
> - **Corrosion Allowance (CA):** in **mm** (typically 1.5 mm to 6 mm)
> - **Joint Efficiency (E):** dimensionless, ranges from **0.70 to 1.00**
> - **Allowable Stress (S):** in **MPa**, derived from **Code Tables**

### 1.3 Why Equipment Specifications Matter in Process Safety

1. They determine the **Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)** — the absolute upper operational limit.
2. They feed the **Pressure Relief Valve (PSV/PRD) sizing calculations** — undersized relief leads to vessel rupture.
3. They establish the **Material of Construction (MOC)** to prevent corrosion-related failures (e.g., **sulfidation**, **chloride stress corrosion cracking**).
4. They define the **inspection and testing regime** (hydrotest, NDT, radiography).
5. They are **legal documents** during accident investigations under the **Factories Act** and **OISD** norms.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Logic of Equipment Specification Building

The construction of an equipment specification follows a strict logical hierarchy. A KTU examiner expects you to understand *why* each parameter comes before the next.

- **Step 1 — Process Inputs:** Determine the **Maximum Operating Pressure (MOP)** and **Maximum Operating Temperature (MOT)** from the Process Flow Diagram (PFD).
- **Step 2 — Design Margin:** Add safety margin to convert operating values into **Design Pressure (P_design)** and **Design Temperature (T_design)**.
- **Step 3 — Material Selection:** Select MOC based on **corrosion charts**, **temperature limits**, and **fluid compatibility** (e.g., 316L SS for chlorides, CS + 3 mm rubber lining for H₂SO₄).
- **Step 4 — Corrosion Allowance:** Add **CA (mm)** to the calculated wall thickness to account for lifetime material loss.
- **Step 5 — Code Compliance:** Identify the governing code (**ASME Sec VIII Div 1**, **IS 2825**, **EN 13445**).
- **Step 6 — Stress and Efficiency:** Use code-defined **Allowable Stress (S)** and **Joint Efficiency (E)** based on weld inspection level.
- **Step 7 — Wall Thickness Calculation:** Compute the required shell and head thicknesses.
- **Step 8 — Relief Sizing Basis:** Use **MAWP × 1.1** as the basis for PSV set pressure.
- **Step 9 — Testing:** Specify **hydrostatic test pressure** and **pneumatic test** conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 10% margin for PSV set pressure?** The relief device must open *before* the equipment reaches its design limit. Hence, **Set Pressure (P_set) = 1.10 × MAWP** is the universal design rule, codified in **API 520** and **API 521**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value bars in tables. Never use the raw pipe `|`.**

| # | Parameter | Governing Equation | Units | Code Reference |
|---|-----------|-------------------|-------|----------------|
| 1 | Design Pressure | $P_{design} \geq MOP + \text{allowance}$ | **MPa** | ASME / IS |
| 2 | Set Pressure of Relief | $P_{set} = 1.10 \times MAWP$ | **MPa** | API 520 |
| 3 | Cylindrical Shell Thickness | $t = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6P}$ | **mm** | ASME UG-27 |
| 4 | Spherical Shell Thickness | $t = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{2 \cdot S \cdot E - 0.2P}$ | **mm** | ASME UG-27 |
| 5 | Torispherical Head Thickness | $t = \dfrac{P \cdot L \cdot M}{2 \cdot S \cdot E - 0.2P}$ | **mm** | ASME UG-32 |
| 6 | Required Thickness (with CA) | $t_{required} = t_{calc} + CA$ | **mm** | Practical |
| 7 | Hydrostatic Test Pressure | $P_{test} = 1.3 \times MAWP \times \dfrac{S_{test}}{S_{design}}$ | **MPa** | ASME UG-99 |
| 8 | Allowable Stress Ratio | $S_{test}/S_{design} \leq 1.0$ (typically 0.90) | dimensionless | ASME |
| 9 | Joint Efficiency (Spot Radiography) | $E = 0.85$ | dimensionless | ASME UW-12 |
| 10 | Joint Efficiency (Full Radiography) | $E = 1.00$ | dimensionless | ASME UW-12 |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Equipment specifications are not academic exercises — they are **life-safety documents** used by:
- **Design Engineers** for mechanical design
- **Process Safety Engineers** for HAZOP and LOPA studies
- **Inspection Authorities** during statutory audits (e.g., PESO, Factory Inspectorate)
- **Insurance Agencies** for risk underwriting
- **Legal Teams** during post-incident litigation (e.g., the **Flixborough disaster** investigation hinged on equipment specifications of the bypass piping).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Cylindrical Shell Thickness (ASME UG-27)

We start from the **force-balance equilibrium** of a thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel.

**Step 1 — Consider a cylindrical shell of:**
- Internal radius $R$ (in **mm**)
- Wall thickness $t$ (in **mm**)
- Internal gauge pressure $P$ (in **MPa**)

**Step 2 — Cut the cylinder longitudinally along a length $L$. The internal pressure pushes the two halves apart.**

The total axial (longitudinal) force acting on the projected area is:

$$
F_{axial} = P \times 2R \times L
$$

**Step 3 — This force is resisted by the two longitudinal welds in the shell wall.**

The resisting cross-sectional area of metal is:

$$
A_{metal} = 2 \times t \times L
$$

**Step 4 — Equate the load to the allowable stress times the resisting area (using joint efficiency E):**

$$
P \times 2R \times L = S \times E \times 2 \times t \times L
$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $2L$ from both sides:**

$$
P \times R = S \times E \times t
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $t$, but include the empirical ASME correction term $-0.6P$ in the denominator for thick-wall accuracy:**

$$
t = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6P}
$$

**Step 7 — Add corrosion allowance $CA$ to obtain the final required thickness:**

$$
t_{required} = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6P} + CA
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **most frequently tested formula** in KTU Process Safety examinations. Always state the units, define every variable, and never skip the $-0.6P$ term — it is the standard ASME correction.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Hydrostatic Test Pressure

**Step 1 — Start with the ASME UG-99 code equation:**

$$
P_{test} = 1.3 \times MAWP \times \dfrac{S_{test}}{S_{design}}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the purpose:** The hydrostatic test is a **strength test** performed at room temperature, where the allowable stress $S_{test}$ is higher than the design allowable stress $S_{design}$ (because yield strength at ambient temperature is higher than at design temperature).

**Step 3 — Apply the typical ratio constraint:** $\dfrac{S_{test}}{S_{design}} \leq 0.90$ (i.e., you cannot use the full higher yield strength).

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical example:**
- $MAWP = 2.5 \text{ MPa}$
- $S_{test} / S_{design} = 0.90$

$$
P_{test} = 1.3 \times 2.5 \times 0.90
$$

$$
P_{test} = 2.925 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to bar for field practice:**

$$
P_{test} = 29.25 \text{ bar}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A horizontal cylindrical pressure vessel is to be designed for a **hydrocracker reactor**. Given:
- Internal diameter $D_i = 2000$ mm
- Design pressure $P = 5.0$ MPa
- Allowable stress $S = 137$ MPa
- Joint efficiency $E = 0.85$
- Corrosion allowance $CA = 3$ mm

**Step 1 — Compute the internal radius:**

$$
R = \dfrac{D_i}{2} = \dfrac{2000}{2} = 1000 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the ASME cylindrical shell formula:**

$$
t = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6P}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$
t = \dfrac{5.0 \times 1000}{137 \times 0.85 - 0.6 \times 5.0}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the denominator:**

$$
137 \times 0.85 = 116.45
$$

$$
0.6 \times 5.0 = 3.0
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 116.45 - 3.0 = 113.45 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute numerator:**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 5.0 \times 1000 = 5000 \text{ MPa}\cdot\text{mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Final thickness without corrosion allowance:**

$$
t = \dfrac{5000}{113.45} = 44.07 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 7 — Add corrosion allowance:**

$$
t_{required} = 44.07 + 3.0 = 47.07 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 8 — Round up to the nearest standard plate thickness (per IS 2062 / SA 516):**

$$
t_{standard} = 48 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 9 — Set pressure of relief device:**

$$
P_{set} = 1.10 \times 5.0 = 5.5 \text{ MPa} = 55 \text{ bar}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **relief device set pressure (5.5 MPa)** is intentionally **less than the test pressure** but **greater than the design pressure**, ensuring the PSV opens before the vessel reaches its structural limit.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix for Material Selection

| Service Fluid | Recommended MOC | Corrosion Allowance (mm) | Special Consideration |
|---------------|----------------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Dry hydrocarbons (HC) | Carbon Steel SA 516 Gr 70 | **3.0** | Sulfidation above 230 °C |
| Wet H₂S service | Carbon Steel + NACE MR0175 | **3.0 + 6.0** | SSC cracking |
| Sulfuric Acid (98%) | Carbon Steel (cast iron) | **6.0** | Dilution is dangerous |
| Hydrochloric Acid | Rubber-lined CS / FRP | **3.0** | Avoid SS — pitting |
| Caustic (NaOH) | Nickel Alloy 200 | **1.5** | Avoid CS above 50 °C |
| Chlorides (sea water) | 316L Stainless Steel | **1.5** | SCC risk |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Equipment Specification Development Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Inputs from PFD and P&ID] --> B[Determine MOP and MOT]
    B --> C[Apply Safety Margins]
    C --> D[Compute Design Pressure and Design Temperature]
    D --> E[Select Material of Construction MOC]
    E --> F[Define Corrosion Allowance CA]
    F --> G[Identify Governing Code ASME or IS 2825]
    G --> H[Select Allowable Stress S and Joint Efficiency E]
    H --> I[Calculate Wall Thickness t]
    I --> J[Add CA to get Required Thickness]
    J --> K[Specify Radiography and NDT Level]
    K --> L[Compute Hydrostatic Test Pressure]
    L --> M[Compute PSV Set Pressure 1.1 times MAWP]
    M --> N[Final Equipment Specification Sheet]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This Mermaid block diagram represents the **logical sequence** a process safety engineer follows. Each block must be completed before moving to the next — a common KTU exam mistake is to begin thickness calculation without first defining $E$ and $S$.

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of an Equipment Datasheet

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Header Block: Equipment Tag, Service, Location] --> B[Design Basis: P_design, T_design, MOC]
    B --> C[Mechanical Design: Thickness, Diameter, Length, Heads]
    C --> D[Corrosion and Inspection: CA, NDT, Radiography]
    D --> E[Testing: Hydrotest, Pneumatic, Leak Test]
    E --> F[Safety Devices: PSV Set Pressure, Rupture Disc]
    F --> G[Skid and Nozzle Schedule: Inlet, Outlet, Instrument]
    G --> H[Vendor and Code Compliance Block]
```

### 4.3 Wall Thickness Logic Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: P, R, S, E, CA] --> B[t = PR divided by SE minus 0.6P]
    B --> C{Is t greater than P times R over 0.385 SE?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Thick-Wall Theory Applies]
    C -->|No| E[Thin-Wall Theory Valid]
    D --> F[Use ASME Mandatory Design Rule]
    E --> G[t_required = t + CA]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Round to Standard Plate Thickness]
    H --> I[Check for Buckling if External Pressure]
    I --> J[Final Shell Thickness Selection]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Thick-wall vessels** (where $P/S$ ratio is high) require a different ASME formula because the linear stress distribution assumption of thin-wall theory breaks down. KTU questions may test the **0.385 SE boundary** to determine which formula to apply.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

Define the term **Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)**. Why is the relief device set pressure kept at **1.1 × MAWP** rather than equal to MAWP?

**Model Answer:**

**MAWP** is the maximum permissible internal gauge pressure at the top of the vessel during normal operation, based on the thinnest component and the most restrictive combination of design temperature, joint efficiency, and corrosion allowance. It is a code-defined limit, not an operating value.

**Reason for 1.1 × MAWP factor:** If the relief set pressure equaled the MAWP, any slight overpressure (thermal expansion, process upset) would lift the relief before the vessel has actually reached its structural limit, causing **chattering** and **premature fatigue failure** of the relief valve. The 10% margin provides a **buffer** that ensures the relief operates only when the vessel is genuinely in distress, and gives the operator time to intervene.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

Distinguish between **Design Pressure** and **Operating Pressure**. Why is the corrosion allowance added to the calculated thickness rather than to the design pressure?

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Design Pressure | Operating Pressure |
|-----------|-----------------|--------------------|
| Definition | Theoretical upper bound used for mechanical design | Actual pressure at which the equipment runs in service |
| Source | MOP + safety margin (pump shut-off head, control valve failure) | Process flow requirements |
| Use | Wall thickness, PSV sizing, hydrotest | Process simulation, P&ID |

**Reason for adding CA to thickness (not to pressure):** Corrosion is a **physical loss of material** over the equipment's lifetime, not a transient pressure phenomenon. The wall must be made **physically thicker** at the fabrication stage so that even after 20 years of uniform corrosion, the remaining wall is still code-compliant. Adding CA to pressure would only inflate the design stress assumption without providing real material reserve.
- **Operating Pressure:** actual working pressure (typically in **bar g**)
- **Design Pressure:** theoretical upper bound (always $\geq$ operating pressure)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** A vertical pressure vessel is to be designed for a **catalytic reformer reactor** with the following data:
- Internal diameter $D_i = 1500$ mm
- Design pressure $P = 3.5$ MPa
- Design temperature $T = 425$ °C
- Material: SA 387 Gr 11 (1.25 Cr – 0.5 Mo steel)
- Allowable stress at 425 °C: $S = 105$ MPa
- Joint efficiency: $E = 0.85$ (spot radiography)
- Corrosion allowance: $CA = 3$ mm

Calculate the **required shell thickness** and the **relief device set pressure**.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Explain the significance of **Material of Construction (MOC)** selection with reference to a process industry case study. Discuss at least three corrosion mechanisms relevant to refinery service.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(a):**

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters:**
- $R = 1500 / 2 = 750$ mm
- $P = 3.5$ MPa
- $S = 105$ MPa
- $E = 0.85$
- $CA = 3$ mm

**Step 2 — Apply the ASME cylindrical shell formula:**

$$
t = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6P}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$
t = \dfrac{3.5 \times 750}{105 \times 0.85 - 0.6 \times 3.5}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the denominator:**

$$
105 \times 0.85 = 89.25
$$

$$
0.6 \times 3.5 = 2.1
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 89.25 - 2.1 = 87.15 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute numerator:**

$$
3.5 \times 750 = 2625 \text{ MPa}\cdot\text{mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Thickness without CA:**

$$
t = \dfrac{2625}{87.15} = 30.12 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 7 — Add corrosion allowance:**

$$
t_{required} = 30.12 + 3.0 = 33.12 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 8 — Round to the next standard plate thickness (per IS 2062 / ASME SA 387):**

$$
t_{standard} = 34 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 9 — Relief device set pressure:**

$$
P_{set} = 1.10 \times MAWP = 1.10 \times 3.5 = 3.85 \text{ MPa} = 38.5 \text{ bar}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- **Required shell thickness: 34 mm** (standard)
- **Relief set pressure: 3.85 MPa**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(b):**

The **Material of Construction (MOC)** is the single most critical decision in equipment specification, as it determines the equipment's ability to withstand **chemical attack**, **thermal degradation**, and **mechanical stress** over a service life of 20–30 years.

**Case Study: 1984 Bhopal Disaster (Union Carbide, India)**

The MIC (Methyl Isocyanate) storage tank was constructed in carbon steel. Over time, trace amounts of water and impurities catalyzed the **exothermic reaction** of MIC, generating heat and pressure beyond the design limit. The refrigeration system (which was designed to keep MIC at low temperature) had been shut down for maintenance — a process deviation that the specification document should have flagged as a **critical safeguard**.

**Three relevant corrosion mechanisms in refinery service:**

1. **Sulfidation:** Occurs in hydroprocessing units handling sour crude. At temperatures above **230 °C**, sulfur compounds react with iron in carbon steel to form **iron sulfide (FeS)**, leading to wall thinning. **Mitigation:** Use 1.25 Cr – 0.5 Mo steel (SA 387 Gr 11) which forms a more stable chromium sulfide scale.

2. **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC):** Affects austenitic stainless steels in chloride environments (e.g., overhead systems of crude distillation units). The combination of **tensile stress** and **chloride ions** causes transgranular cracking. **Mitigation:** Upgrade to 316L SS with low carbon content, or use **duplex stainless steel**.

3. **Naphthenic Acid Corrosion:** Found in crude distillation overheads and furnace tubes at temperatures **220–400 °C**. High TAN (Total Acid Number) crudes attack carbon steel at high velocity regions (elbows, return bends). **Mitigation:** Use **316 SS** or **317 SS** with molybdenum content $\geq 3\%$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> - **Do not** confuse **Design Pressure** with **Operating Pressure** in your answer. Design pressure is *always* $\geq$ operating pressure.
> - **Do not** forget to add the **corrosion allowance (CA)** to the calculated thickness. This single omission costs **2 to 3 marks**.
> - **Do not** omit the **$-0.6P$** correction term in the ASME denominator. Many students write $t = PR/SE$ which is **incomplete** and may be marked down.
> - **Always** mention **code reference** (ASME UG-27 or IS 2825) — examiners reward code-citing answers.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice — KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** With the help of a neat labeled diagram, explain the **functional components of a typical equipment specification sheet** used in the chemical process industry. Highlight at least **six key parameters** that must be included.

**(b) (7 Marks)** A storage tank is to be hydrostatically tested. The tank has:
- $MAWP = 1.0$ MPa
- $S_{test} / S_{design} = 0.90$

Calculate the **hydrostatic test pressure**. Justify why the hydrostatic test pressure is **higher** than the design pressure.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(a):**

A typical **Equipment Specification Sheet** (also called a Vessel Datasheet) contains the following **six key parameters**:

1. **Equipment Identification Block:** Tag number (e.g., V-101), service description (e.g., "Feed Surge Drum"), plant location, and P&ID reference.

2. **Design Basis:** Design pressure, design temperature, MDMT (Minimum Design Metal Temperature), and external design pressure (for vacuum service).

3. **Material of Construction (MOC):** Shell material, head material, nozzle material, and gasket material. Each selected based on **corrosion charts** and **temperature derating tables**.

4. **Mechanical Design:** Calculated shell thickness, head thickness (torispherical/ellipsoidal), nozzle reinforcement, and reinforcement pad sizes.

5. **Corrosion and Inspection:** Corrosion allowance, radiographic testing level (per ASME UW-11), and NDT requirements (ultrasonic, magnetic particle, dye penetrant).

6. **Testing and Safety:** Hydrostatic test pressure, pneumatic test pressure, PSV set pressure, rupture disc rating, and safety relief area.

A **block-level schematic** of this datasheet is provided in **Section 4.2** of these notes.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(b):**

**Step 1 — Recall the ASME UG-99 hydrostatic test formula:**

$$
P_{test} = 1.3 \times MAWP \times \dfrac{S_{test}}{S_{design}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
P_{test} = 1.3 \times 1.0 \times 0.90
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate:**

$$
P_{test} = 1.17 \text{ MPa} = 11.7 \text{ bar}
$$

**Final Answer:** **Hydrostatic test pressure = 1.17 MPa**

**Justification for $P_{test} > P_{design}$:**

The hydrostatic test is a **proof test** designed to verify the **structural integrity** of the vessel by stressing it beyond normal operating conditions. The vessel is filled with water (a nearly incompressible fluid), and pressure is raised to 1.3 × MAWP, which is **~30% higher** than the design pressure. This deliberately overstresses the vessel to reveal **manufacturing defects**, **weld imperfections**, and **lamination flaws** that may not be visible during inspection but would cause failure under service conditions.

The use of water (not the process fluid) ensures safety because water does not release energy explosively if a leak develops. The 0.90 ratio on $S_{test}/S_{design}$ prevents the test from exceeding the **yield strength** of the material at room temperature, ensuring the test is **destructive-free** yet **revealing**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B Specific:**
> - Students often use **design pressure** in the hydrotest formula instead of **MAWP**. Always use **MAWP** because MAWP is the code-allowed maximum.
> - Some students forget the **0.90 stress ratio** factor. This is a 2-mark deduction if missed.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Equipment Specifications:**

- **Equipment Specification** = legal document containing design, material, and inspection parameters of process equipment.
- **Design Pressure** = maximum pressure used for mechanical design (always $\geq$ Operating Pressure).
- **MAWP** = Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (the code-defined ceiling).
- **PSV Set Pressure** = **1.10 × MAWP** (per API 520).
- **ASME Shell Thickness Formula:** $t = \dfrac{PR}{SE - 0.6P}$ — must include the $-0.6P$ correction.
- **Required Thickness** = Calculated Thickness + **Corrosion Allowance (CA)**.
- **Hydrostatic Test Pressure:** $P_{test} = 1.3 \times MAWP \times (S_{test}/S_{design})$, with $S_{test}/S_{design} \leq 0.90$.
- **Joint Efficiency (E):** 1.00 for full radiography, 0.85 for spot radiography, 0.70 for no radiography.
- **Material Selection** is driven by **fluid compatibility**, **temperature**, and **pressure** — refer to NACE, ASME, and corrosion charts.
- **Always** state the **governing code** (ASME Section VIII, IS 2825, or EN 13445).
- **Always** convert units: 1 MPa = 10 bar.
- **Always** add **corrosion allowance** before rounding to standard plate thickness.
- **Always** compute **PSV set pressure** at 110% of MAWP for overpressure protection.
- **Common case studies:** **Bhopal 1984** (MIC runaway), **Flixborough 1974** (bypass piping failure), **Texas City 2005** (raffinate splitter).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "design" an equipment, examiners expect a **complete datasheet with all parameters**, not just the wall thickness. Always include: $P_{design}$, $T_{design}$, MOC, CA, $E$, $S$, $t_{required}$, $t_{standard}$, $P_{test}$, and $P_{set}$. A datasheet with missing fields is **incomplete** and will be penalized.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reliability and Safety in Designing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT616 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reliability and Safety in Designing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Reliability** in chemical process design is formally defined as the **probability that a component, subsystem, or entire process plant will perform its intended function without failure, under stated operating conditions, for a specified period of time**. It is a probabilistic measure, dimensionless, and quantified using statistical methods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety in Designing** refers to the systematic integration of hazard identification, risk evaluation, and engineering control principles during the *earliest conceptual and detailed design stages* of a chemical process plant, so that the resulting facility has a tolerable level of risk throughout its operational life cycle.

### Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024)
The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to:
- Quantify reliability using **failure rate (λ)**, **MTBF**, **MTTF**, and **availability (A)**.
- Apply reliability block diagrams for **series, parallel, and redundant systems**.
- Apply the **hierarchy of controls** (inherent → passive → active → procedural) during design.
- Discuss the role of **safety factors**, **design codes (ASME, API, IS)**, and **margin of safety**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Service" Intuition

Imagine a small car that you depend on for your daily commute to college.

- **Reliability** is your *confidence* that the car will start tomorrow morning. If it has failed twice in the last 100 days, its reliability for a single day is roughly 98/100 = 0.98.
- **Safety in design** is making sure the car has *airbags, ABS brakes, crumple zones, and seat belts* — these are all designed-in protections before the car even reaches the road.
- If the car fails (a breakdown), it is *unreliable*. If the failure causes a crash, it is *unsafe*. Many failures are *reliability problems*; a *safety problem* arises when a failure (or a chain of failures) leads to loss of life, health, environment, or equipment.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of thumb:** *Unreliable systems can still be safe (e.g., a faulty valve that is bypass-safe). Safe systems must, however, be designed with reliability as a foundation.*

---

## 1.3 Why Both Concepts Belong Together in Process Design

A chemical reactor operating at **300 °C** and **100 bar** cannot afford a "recall and repair" strategy — failures lead to catastrophic fires, explosions, or toxic releases (e.g., Bhopal 1984, Flixborough 1974). Therefore, the **design phase is the cheapest and most effective place to eliminate or mitigate hazards**. The two pillars in this phase are:

1. **Reliability Engineering** → *How likely is a component to fail?*
2. **Safety Engineering** → *What happens when it fails, and what is built-in to protect people, plant, and the environment?*

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Reliability is reported as a probability (0 to 1) or as a percentage; failure rate is measured in **failures per 10⁶ hours** (FITs — Failures In Time).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Reliability Engineering Fundamentals

### 2.1.1 The Reliability Function R(t)

For a non-repairable component, reliability is the probability that it survives beyond time $t$:

$$
R(t) = P(T > t)
$$

- $R(0) = 1$ (initially perfect)
- $R(\infty) = 0$ (eventual failure)
- The **cumulative failure distribution** is $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$

### 2.1.2 Failure Rate λ(t)

The instantaneous failure rate is the conditional probability density of failure in the next small interval $dt$:

$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \frac{dR(t)}{dt}
$$

Solving for $R(t)$:

$$
R(t) = e^{-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau)\,d\tau}
$$

### 2.1.3 The Reliability "Bathtub" Curve

Most engineering components exhibit three phases:
1. **Infant Mortality (Burn-in):** $\lambda$ decreases with time (manufacturing defects).
2. **Useful Life:** $\lambda \approx \text{constant} = \lambda_{s}$ → **Exponential distribution regime**.
3. **Wear-out:** $\lambda$ increases with time (fatigue, corrosion, ageing).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **useful-life region**, the exponential model applies, and the formulae below reduce to a single constant failure rate.

### 2.1.4 Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) and Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)

For non-repairable items:
$$
\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\,dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

For repairable items:
$$
\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}
$$

where **MTTR** = Mean Time To Repair.

### 2.1.5 Availability (A)

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Total Time}}
$$

### 2.1.6 Series and Parallel Reliability

**Series system** (system fails if *any* component fails):
$$
R_{series} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_{i}(t)
$$

**Parallel system** (system works if *at least one* component works):
$$
R_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left(1 - R_{i}(t)\right)
$$

**For n identical components in parallel** (each with reliability $R_{c}$):
$$
R_{n\text{-parallel}} = 1 - (1 - R_{c})^{n}
$$

### 2.1.7 k-out-of-n Redundancy (Voted System)

A system of $n$ components works if at least $k$ of them work:

$$
R_{k/n} = \sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} R_{c}^{\,i} (1 - R_{c})^{n-i}
$$

---

## 2.2 Safety in Design — Inherent Safety

### 2.2.1 Kletz's Triad of Inherent Safety

1. **Intensify** — use less material at any one time (smaller inventory).
2. **Substitute** — replace a hazardous material with a safer one.
3. **Attenuate** — operate at less severe temperature or pressure.

Supporting principles:
- **Limit effects** (e.g., dikes, blast walls).
- **Make incorrect assembly impossible** (poka-yoke / fail-safe design).
- **Clarify status** (clear labels, color codes).
- **Ease of control** (lower inventories → easier thermal management).

### 2.2.2 Hierarchy of Controls (CCPS / KTU)

The order of *effectiveness* (most → least):
1. **Inherent Safer Design** — eliminate the hazard.
2. **Passive engineered controls** — relief valves, dikes, blast walls.
3. **Active engineered controls** — interlocks, trips, alarms, ESD systems.
4. **Procedural controls** — SOPs, training, work permits.

### 2.2.3 Safety Factor and Margin of Safety

$$
\text{Safety Factor (SF)} = \frac{\text{Strength}}{\text{Stress}} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{\text{Design Capacity}}{\text{Operating Load}}
$$

$$
\text{Margin of Safety (MoS)} = \text{SF} - 1
$$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulae below are examinable in the KTU 2024 scheme for OESFT616. Use `\vert` for absolute value symbols to comply with markdown table integrity.

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Symbol / Formula** | **Units** | **When to Use** |
|-------|--------------|----------------------|-----------|-----------------|
| 1 | Reliability function | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless | Useful-life (constant λ) |
| 2 | Failure rate (constant) | $\lambda = 1/\text{MTTF}$ | h⁻¹ or FITs (10⁻⁶/h) | Constant-failure regime |
| 3 | MTTF (non-repairable) | $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ | hours | Exponential model |
| 4 | MTBF (repairable) | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | hours | Repairable systems |
| 5 | Steady-state availability | $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ | dimensionless | Plant uptime analysis |
| 6 | Series reliability | $R_{s} = \prod R_{i}$ | dimensionless | All components must work |
| 7 | Parallel reliability | $R_{p} = 1 - \prod (1 - R_{i})$ | dimensionless | Any one must work |
| 8 | k-out-of-n redundancy | $R_{k/n} = \sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} R_{c}^{\,i} (1-R_{c})^{n-i}$ | dimensionless | Voted system |
| 9 | Safety factor | $\text{SF} = \text{Strength} / \text{Stress}$ | dimensionless | Mechanical design |
| 10 | Margin of safety | $\text{MoS} = \text{SF} - 1$ | dimensionless | Design verification |
| 11 | Unavailability | $U = 1 - A$ | dimensionless | PFD = average U (1oo1) |
| 12 | PFD (1oo1) | $\text{PFD}_{avg} = \lambda \cdot t_{CE} / 2$ | dimensionless | IEC 61511 SIS |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Chemical Engineering

| **Application** | **Reliability / Safety Tool Used** |
|-----------------|-------------------------------------|
| Pressure vessel design | ASME BPVC Section VIII + SF ≥ 3.5 on ultimate tensile strength |
| SIS / Trip design | IEC 61511 — PFD calculation, SIL targets |
| Distillation column relief | API 520 / 521 — passive engineered control |
| Reactor cooling | Inherent safety: smaller inventory, lower temperature |
| Offshore platform | Redundant fire-water pumps (parallel) |
| Chlorine storage | Dikes, vapour scrubbers (passive & active layers) |
