# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section follows the **KTU Valuation Key Pattern**: every algebraic step is shown with the physical or logical reasoning. Examiners award partial credit for showing the *transition logic*, not just the final number.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Reliability Function from Failure Rate (Exponential Model)

**Given:** A component in the constant-failure-rate region of the bathtub curve, with rate $\lambda = \text{const}$.

**Step 1 — Definition of failure rate**
The instantaneous failure rate is the conditional probability of failure in the next small interval $dt$:

$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $f(t) = -dR(t)/dt$**

$$
\lambda = \frac{-dR(t)/dt}{R(t)} \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \frac{dR}{dt}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate $dR$**

$$
\frac{dR}{R} = -\lambda \, dt
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate both sides from 0 to t, with $R(0) = 1$**

$$
\int_{1}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integrals**

$$
\ln R(t) - \ln 1 = -\lambda t
$$

Since $\ln 1 = 0$:

$$
\ln R(t) = -\lambda t
$$

**Step 6 — Exponentiate**

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** Reliability decays *exponentially* with time. Doubling the mission time reduces reliability by the square (e.g., $R(2t) = R(t)^{2}$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: MTTF for the Exponential Distribution

**Step 1 — Definition of expected (mean) failure time**

$$
\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \, f(t) \, dt
$$

**Step 2 — Use $f(t) = -dR/dt = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$**

$$
\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Integration by parts**
Let $u = t$, $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} dt$, so $du = dt$, $v = -e^{-\lambda t}$.

$$
\text{MTTF} = \left[ -t e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate each term**
At $t \to \infty$: $-t e^{-\lambda t} \to 0$ (exponential dominates).
At $t = 0$: $-0 \cdot e^{0} = 0$.
So the boundary term is **0**.

For the second integral:

$$
\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \left[ -\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 5 — Final result**

$$
\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Series-System Reliability for n Identical Components

**Step 1 — Apply the multiplication rule for independent events**

For $n$ independent components each with reliability $R_c$:

$$
R_{series} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_{c} = R_{c}^{\,n}
$$

**Step 2 — Substituting the exponential model $R_c = e^{-\lambda t}$**

$$
R_{series} = \left(e^{-\lambda t}\right)^{n} = e^{-n\lambda t}
$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent failure rate of the system**

Comparing with the standard form $R = e^{-\lambda_{eq} t}$:

$$
\boxed{\lambda_{eq,\,series} = n\lambda}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Insight for KTU exams:** Adding components in *series* always *reduces* reliability, even if each one is very good. E.g., 10 components at $R_c = 0.99$ give $R_s = 0.99^{10} = 0.904$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Parallel-System Reliability (1-out-of-2) for Identical Components

**Step 1 — System fails only if BOTH components fail**

Probability of one component failing: $Q = 1 - R_c$.
Probability of both failing (independent): $Q_1 \cdot Q_2 = (1 - R_c)^{2}$.

**Step 2 — Probability of survival**

$$
R_{parallel} = 1 - (1 - R_c)^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Expand**

$$
R_{parallel} = 1 - (1 - 2R_c + R_{c}^{2}) = 2R_c - R_{c}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical sanity check**
If $R_c = 0.9$:
- Series (2 components): $0.9 \times 0.9 = 0.810$
- Parallel (2 components): $2(0.9) - 0.81 = 0.990$

> [!NOTE]
> **Parallel redundancy dramatically improves reliability** but does *not* increase the failure *rate*; it only changes the *structure* of the system.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Worked Mini-Case

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (KTU-style, 7 marks):** A pump has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-6} /h$. Two identical pumps are installed in **parallel**, with a common mission time of 8760 hours (1 year). Calculate (a) the reliability of a single pump, (b) the parallel-system reliability, and (c) the MTTF of a single pump.

**Step (a) — Single-pump reliability**

$$
R_c = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(5\times 10^{-6})(8760)}
$$

First compute the exponent:
$$
\lambda t = 5\times 10^{-6} \times 8760 = 0.0438
$$

$$
R_c = e^{-0.0438} = 0.9572
$$

**Step (b) — Parallel (1-out-of-2) reliability**

$$
R_{1/2} = 2R_c - R_{c}^{\,2} = 2(0.9572) - (0.9572)^{2}
$$

$$
R_{1/2} = 1.9144 - 0.9162 = 0.9982
$$

**Step (c) — MTTF of single pump**

$$
\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{5\times 10^{-6}} = 200{,}000 \text{ hours} \approx 22.83 \text{ years}
$$

**Result Summary**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $R_c$ (single) | 0.9572 |
| $R_{1/2}$ (parallel) | 0.9982 |
| MTTF | 200,000 h (~22.8 years) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake:** Using $\lambda$ in %/h without converting to h⁻¹. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for unit inconsistency. Always state units explicitly.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams below follow the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguards: alphanumeric node IDs, raw text inside quoted labels, no markdown formatting inside nodes.

---

## 4.1 Reliability Block Diagram — Series System

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[INPUT] --> B1[PUMP A]
    B1 --> B2[HEAT EXCHANGER B]
    B2 --> B3[VALVE C]
    B3 --> B4[REACTOR D]
    B4 --> C[OUTPUT]
    style A fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366
    style C fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366
    style B1 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#806600
    style B2 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#806600
    style B3 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#806600
    style B4 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#806600
```

**Reading guide:** In a series configuration, the system fails if **any** of the four blocks fails. Reliability is the **product** of the individual reliabilities. Engineers use this structure when designing critical trains where every element is necessary.

---

## 4.2 Reliability Block Diagram — Parallel (1-out-of-2) Redundancy

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[INPUT] --> S1[BRANCH 1]
    A --> S2[BRANCH 2]
    S1 --> P1[PUMP 1]
    P1 --> J[JUNCTION]
    S2 --> P2[PUMP 2]
    P2 --> J
    J --> C[OUTPUT]
    style A fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366
    style C fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366
    style J fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#4b0082
    style P1 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1f6f3a
    style P2 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1f6f3a
    style S1 fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#803300
    style S2 fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#803300
```

**Reading guide:** Two parallel paths ensure that the system continues to operate as long as **at least one** pump survives. This is the standard architecture for **firewater pumps, ESD valves, and emergency power generators** in chemical plants.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hierarchy of Controls in Design

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[LEVEL 1 INHERENT SAFETY] --> L1a[INTENSIFY REDUCE INVENTORY]
    L1 --> L1b[SUBSTITUTE SAFER CHEMICAL]
    L1 --> L1c[ATTENUATE LOWER T OR P]
    L1 --> L1d[LIMIT EFFECTS DIKES]
    L2[LEVEL 2 PASSIVE ENGINEERED] --> L2a[RELIEF VALVES]
    L2 --> L2b[BLAST WALLS]
    L2 --> L2c[CONTAINMENT DIKES]
    L3[LEVEL 3 ACTIVE ENGINEERED] --> L3a[INTERLOCKS]
    L3 --> L3b[ALARMS]
    L3 --> L3c[EMERGENCY SHUTDOWN ESD]
    L4[LEVEL 4 PROCEDURAL] --> L4a[STANDARD OPERATING PROCEDURE]
    L4 --> L4b[PERMIT TO WORK]
    L4 --> L4c[TRAINING AND DRILLS]
    style L1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style L2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style L3 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style L4 fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#263238
```

**Reading guide:** The hierarchy is read **top-down** in terms of *priority* during design — always prefer **Level 1 (inherent)** because it eliminates the hazard altogether. Levels 2–4 are *additive* layers and are typically combined to form a **layer-of-protection analysis (LOPA)** defence-in-depth strategy.

---

## 4.4 Concept Map — Linking Reliability and Safety in the Design Loop

```mermaid
graph TD
    HD[HAZARD IDENTIFICATION HAZOP WHAT IF] --> RA[RISK ASSESSMENT]
    RA --> RD[RELIABILITY TARGETS LAMBDA MTBF]
    RD --> DD[DETAILED DESIGN ASME API IS]
    DD --> SI[SIS EVALUATION IEC 61511 SIL]
    SI --> VLD[VALIDATION AND VERIFICATION]
    VLD --> OP[OPERATION PHASE]
    OP --> FB[FEEDBACK INCIDENT LEARNING]
    FB --> HD
    style HD fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style RA fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style RD fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style DD fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style SI fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
    style VLD fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92
    style OP fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#263238
    style FB fill:#bcaaa4,stroke:#3e2723
```

**Reading guide:** This is the **iterative loop** the KTU 2024 module emphasises — safety is **not a one-time activity** but a closed feedback process. Incidents and near-misses (FB) feed back into hazard identification (HD) so that future designs are safer.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions follow the **KTU 2024 assessment pattern**: Part A = 3-mark short answers, Part B = 14-mark structured long answers with module-internal choice.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define the term "reliability" of a chemical process component. How is it mathematically related to the failure rate λ?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) Reliability $R(t)$ is the probability that a component performs its intended function under stated conditions for a specified period of time.
- (1 mark) For the constant failure-rate regime (exponential model), $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.
- (1 mark) The corresponding Mean Time To Failure is MTTF $= 1/\lambda$, and the failure rate is the reciprocal of MTTF.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**List any THREE principles of inherently safer design as proposed by Kletz, with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per principle):**

| Principle | Example |
|-----------|---------|
| Intensify (use less material) | Reduce solvent inventory in a reactor from 50 m³ to 5 m³ |
| Substitute (use safer chemical) | Replace benzene with cyclohexane in a nitration process |
| Attenuate (use milder conditions) | Operate a polymerization at 80 °C instead of 200 °C |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]

**(a) [7 marks]** A Safety Instrumented System (SIS) consists of **three identical pressure transmitters** connected in a **1-out-of-3 voting configuration** with a **single shut-down valve**. The failure rate of each transmitter is $\lambda_T = 2 \times 10^{-6} /h$ and the valve failure rate is $\lambda_V = 1 \times 10^{-6} /h$. The proof-test interval is $T = 8760$ h (1 year).
Calculate:
1. The system failure rate.
2. The average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) for the 1oo3 sensor group.
3. The overall PFD of the SIS loop.

**(b) [7 marks]** With the help of a labelled block diagram, describe the **hierarchy of controls** in the design of a chemical process plant. Explain why inherent safety is placed at the top.

---

### **Model Answer to Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Solution**

**Step 1 — Identify the structure.**
The sensor group is a 1-out-of-3 (1oo3) voted system. The system *fails* only when **all three transmitters fail**. The valve is in *series* with the sensor group.

**Step 2 — Compute the 1oo3 PFD for sensors.**

For a 1oo3 voted system with constant failure rate, the average unavailability on demand is given by:

$$
\text{PFD}_{1oo3} = \frac{\lambda_{T}^{\,3} \, T^{3}}{6}
$$

> *Derivation note:* For 3 identical components in series (all must fail) at constant λ over interval T, the average unavailability scales as $\lambda^{3} T^{3}/6$ (third-order Taylor expansion of $1 - e^{-3\lambda t}$ averaged over [0, T]).

Substitute the values:

$$
\text{PFD}_{1oo3} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-6})^{3} \times (8760)^{3}}{6}
$$

Compute the numerator step by step:
- $(2\times 10^{-6})^{3} = 8 \times 10^{-18}$
- $(8760)^{3} = 6.722 \times 10^{11}$ (since $8760^{2} = 7.674 \times 10^{7}$, multiply by 8760)
- Numerator: $8 \times 10^{-18} \times 6.722 \times 10^{11} = 5.378 \times 10^{-6}$

$$
\text{PFD}_{1oo3} = \frac{5.378 \times 10^{-6}}{6} = 8.96 \times 10^{-7}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the PFD of the shut-down valve (1oo1).**

For a single component tested every $T$ hours:

$$
\text{PFD}_{1oo1,\,V} = \frac{\lambda_{V} \, T}{2} = \frac{(1 \times 10^{-6}) \times 8760}{2} = 4.38 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the loop PFD (sensor group in series with valve).**

$$
\text{PFD}_{loop} = \text{PFD}_{1oo3} + \text{PFD}_{1oo1,\,V} - (\text{PFD}_{1oo3} \times \text{PFD}_{1oo1,\,V})
$$

Since $\text{PFD}_{1oo3} \ll \text{PFD}_{1oo1,\,V}$, the product term is negligible:

$$
\text{PFD}_{loop} \approx 8.96 \times 10^{-7} + 4.38 \times 10^{-3} = 4.381 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Result Table**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $\text{PFD}_{1oo3}$ (sensors) | $8.96 \times 10^{-7}$ |
| $\text{PFD}_{1oo1}$ (valve) | $4.38 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| $\text{PFD}_{loop}$ (overall) | $4.381 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| SIL interpretation | Falls in SIL 2 range ($10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$) |

#### **Part (b) — Hierarchy of Controls**

Refer to **Section 4.3 (Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hierarchy of Controls)**. The four levels, in order of *priority and effectiveness*, are:

1. **Inherent Safety** (top-most) — eliminate the hazard itself through *Intensify, Substitute, Attenuate, Limit Effects*.
2. **Passive Engineered Controls** — relief valves, dikes, blast walls (work without external energy).
3. **Active Engineered Controls** — interlocks, alarms, emergency shutdown (require power and actuation).
4. **Procedural Controls** — SOPs, training, work permits (depend on human action).

**Why inherent safety is at the top:**
- It *removes* the hazard; the others only *manage* it.
- Cannot "fail" because the hazard no longer exists in significant quantity.
- Has zero operating cost, no sensor to drift, no valve to stick.
- The **flixborough disaster (1974)** showed that adding more procedural controls to a fundamentally unsafe plant does not prevent catastrophic failure — only redesigning the plant to reduce inventory (inherent) would have.

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified] — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain, with a neat sketch, the **bathtub curve** of failure rate. In which region does the exponential reliability model $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ apply, and why?

**(b) [7 marks]** A chemical reactor cooling system has two pumps in **parallel**. Each pump has $\lambda = 8 \times 10^{-5} /h$ and MTTR = 10 h. Compute:
1. MTBF and steady-state availability of a single pump.
2. Availability of the 1-out-of-2 parallel system (assume independent failures).
3. Comment on whether this redundancy achieves a typical process plant availability target of 99.9 %.

---

### **Model Answer to Question B**

#### **Part (a) — The Bathtub Curve**

The bathtub curve plots failure rate $\lambda(t)$ versus time on the x-axis. It has three regions:

1. **Infant Mortality (Burn-in):** $\lambda$ starts high and decreases. Caused by manufacturing defects, installation errors.
2. **Useful Life (Flat region):** $\lambda \approx \text{constant}$. Random failures dominate.
3. **Wear-out:** $\lambda$ rises sharply. Caused by corrosion, fatigue, ageing.

[Diagram description for student drawing:]
```
   lambda(t)
      |\
      | \
      |  \_____
      |        \____
      |             \____
      |__________________________ time
         I         II         III
       Infant    Useful     Wear-out
```

**Where the exponential model applies:** In the **useful-life (Region II)** phase, $\lambda$ is constant, so the integration $\int \lambda \, dt = \lambda t$, and $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ becomes exact. The other regions require Weibull, lognormal, or gamma distributions.

**Justification:** Most process-plant components (pumps, valves, instruments) spend their operating life in Region II, so the exponential model is the *industry standard* for IEC 61511 PFD calculations.

#### **Part (b) — Parallel Pump Availability**

**Step 1 — MTBF of a single pump**

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{8 \times 10^{-5}} = 12{,}500 \text{ h}
$$

**Step 2 — Steady-state availability of a single pump**

$$
A_{single} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{12{,}500}{12{,}500 + 10} = \frac{12{,}500}{12{,}510} = 0.99920
$$

**Step 3 — Unavailability of a single pump**

$$
U_{single} = 1 - A_{single} = 8.0 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 4 — Availability of 1-out-of-2 parallel system**
The system is unavailable only if **both** pumps fail. For independent failures:

$$
U_{parallel} = (U_{single})^{2} = (8.0 \times 10^{-4})^{2} = 6.4 \times 10^{-7}
$$

$$
A_{parallel} = 1 - 6.4 \times 10^{-7} \approx 0.9999994 \approx 0.99999
$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with the 99.9 % target**

| Metric | Value | Remarks |
|--------|-------|---------|
| $A_{single}$ | 99.920 % | Below 99.9 % target ✗ |
| $A_{parallel}$ | 99.99994 % | Exceeds 99.9 % target ✓ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A single pump has an availability of only 99.92 %, **just below** the 99.9 % target. Adding one parallel pump raises availability to ~99.99994 %, providing **four nines of improvement** and comfortably meeting the typical chemical plant target.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mixing:** Using λ in %/h or in 1/year without conversion. KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark per occurrence.
> 2. **Series vs Parallel confusion:** A 1oo3 system is the *parallel* voting of 3 elements; students sometimes apply the *series* formula by mistake.
> 3. **Forgetting MTTR in MTBF:** MTBF = MTTF + MTTR. Using MTTF alone for repairable systems is a frequent 2-mark loss.
> 4. **Bathtub curve sketch:** The flat region must be drawn distinctly from the rising wear-out tail. Vague sketches lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Inherent safety examples:** Generic answers like "use less chemical" get 0.5 marks; specific industrial examples (e.g., "Bhopal — replace MIC with less toxic intermediate") get full credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU 2024 revision checklist for "Reliability and Safety in Designing":**

- [x] **Reliability** = probability of *successful* function over a stated time.
- [x] **Failure rate λ** = constant in useful life; $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.
- [x] **MTTF** (non-repairable) = $1/\lambda$. **MTBF** (repairable) = MTTF + MTTR.
- [x] **Availability** $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$.
- [x] **Series system:** $R_s = \prod R_i$ — adding components *reduces* reliability.
- [x] **Parallel system:** $R_p = 1 - \prod (1 - R_i)$ — redundancy *improves* reliability dramatically.
- [x] **k-out-of-n:** Binomial sum $\sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} R^{i} (1-R)^{n-i}$.
- [x] **Bathtub curve** has THREE regions: infant mortality, useful life, wear-out. Exponential model applies in the middle one.
- [x] **Inherent safety (Kletz):** Intensify, Substitute, Attenuate, Limit effects.
- [x] **Hierarchy of controls:** Inherent → Passive → Active → Procedural (most → least effective).
- [x] **Safety Factor (SF)** = Strength / Stress; **Margin of Safety** = SF − 1.
- [x] **PFD for 1oo1 SIS** = $\lambda T / 2$ (over proof-test interval $T$).
- [x] **PFD for 1oo3** ≈ $\lambda^{3} T^{3} / 6$ (third-order term).
- [x] **Loop PFD** ≈ PFD(sensor group) + PFD(final element) for series architecture.
- [x] **Design codes to remember:** ASME BPVC, API 520/521, IEC 61508/61511, IS 2825 (Indian).
- [x] **Design is iterative** — incident feedback must update hazard identification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Pearl:** *In the design phase, the cheapest unit of safety is the one that **eliminates the hazard entirely** (inherent safety). Every additional layer of protection (passive → active → procedural) costs more and is less reliable than the layer above it.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inherently Safer Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT616 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Inherently Safer Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> **Inherently Safer Design (ISD)** is a philosophy of process safety engineering that seeks to **permanently eliminate or drastically reduce the intrinsic hazards of a process** by thoughtful selection of materials, operating conditions, and equipment geometry, rather than by adding external protective layers, control systems, or procedural safeguards.

The term *inherently* is critical here. An **inherent** safety feature is one that exists **by the very nature of the process itself** — it cannot be removed, forgotten, mis-operated, or fail in service. This is in sharp contrast to **engineered safeguards** (relief valves, interlocks, alarms) and **procedural safeguards** (training, SOPs), which are *external* to the process and therefore vulnerable to failure.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a house near a flood-prone river. You have two options:

| Option | Analogy | Engineering Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Build a 10-foot tall concrete wall** around the house. | Engineered safeguard (adds protection *outside*) | Add a relief valve, an interlock, an alarm |
| **Build the house on stilts, on higher ground, away from the river.** | **Inherently Safer Design** | Choose lower inventory, lower pressure, safer chemistry |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** The wall *can fail* (it can be overtopped, can crack, can be improperly maintained). The house on stilts *cannot be flooded* by the same river — the hazard has been *designed out*. This is the essence of ISD.

### The Origin of the Concept

The concept was popularized by **Trevor Kletz** (1922–2013), a British chemical engineer and process safety pioneer, who systematically articulated it after the **1974 Flixborough disaster** (UK) and drew further lessons from the **1984 Bhopal tragedy** (India). Kletz's central thesis: *"What you don't have, can't leak."*

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Whenever a question on ISD appears, citing **Trevor Kletz** and the **Bhopal disaster** as motivating context almost always fetches a goodwill mark from examiners.

### Core Metrics & Terminology

- **Intrinsic Hazard:** The hazard possessed by a material or process *by its nature* (e.g., flammability, toxicity, reactivity).
- **Hazard vs. Risk:** **Hazard** is the *inherent property*; **Risk** = Hazard × Probability × Consequence. ISD reduces the *hazard itself*, not just the risk.
- **Lethal Concentration (LC₅₀)**, **Lower Flammable Limit (LFL)**, **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)**: standard metrics used to *quantify* the intrinsic hazard and thereby *measure* how "inherently safe" an alternative design is.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Inherently Safer Design

Kletz formalized ISD into four guiding strategies — often memorized as the **"4 Words"** or **Kletz's Inherently Safer Design Strategies**:

#### Pillar 1 — **MINIMIZE** (also called *Intensification*)
- Use the **smallest possible quantity** of hazardous material.
- Reduce inventory in storage tanks, intermediate vessels, and piping.
- Replace batch reactors with **continuous flow reactors / microreactors**.

> **Industrial Example:** Replacing a 50,000 L bulk storage tank of methyl isocyanate (MIC) — the chemical behind the Bhopal disaster — with multiple smaller, on-demand generators. Union Carbide's post-Bhopal redesign at Institute, WV reduced MIC inventory from ~120,000 lb to **under 500 lb** by producing it on-demand.

#### Pillar 2 — **SUBSTITUTE**
- Replace a hazardous material with a **less hazardous** one that performs the same function.
- Look at both *physical* and *toxicological* properties.

> **Industrial Examples:**
> - Replace **benzene** (carcinogen, BP 80 °C) with **cyclohexane** (less toxic, BP 81 °C) as a solvent.
> - Replace **chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs)** with **hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs)** to eliminate ozone-depletion hazard.
> - Use **water-based coatings** instead of **solvent-based** paints to remove flammability hazard.

#### Pillar 3 — **MODERATE** (also called *Attenuation* or *Less Hazardous Conditions*)
- Operate the process under conditions that are **less likely to result in a release, fire, or explosion**.
- Lower temperatures, lower pressures, more dilute concentrations, less reactive forms.

> **Industrial Examples:**
> - Store **acrylic acid** with a **thermal inhibitor (MEHQ)** at ambient temperature instead of at elevated temperatures to prevent runaway polymerization.
> - Use **aqueous solutions** (e.g., 50% HCl) instead of **anhydrous HCl gas** at high pressure.
> - Refrigerated storage of **ammonia** at −33 °C at atmospheric pressure instead of compressed liquid at 10 bar — a release at 1 atm vaporizes less violently.

#### Pillar 4 — **SIMPLIFY** (also called *Simplification of Plant*)
- Design equipment and processes that are **simpler**, with fewer opportunities for human error and equipment failure.
- Reduce complexity in piping, valving, and control logic.

> **Industrial Examples:**
> - Eliminate **dead legs** in piping where hazardous material could be trapped and corrode.
> - Use **welded connections** rather than **flanged/gasketed** joints to reduce leak paths.
> - Reduce the number of **vessels, pumps, and instruments** in a process train.

### 2.2 The Hierarchy of Controls (Inverted Triangle)

KTU examiners frequently test this hierarchy. The further *up* the pyramid, the more *inherent* the safety:

```
                  ▲
                 ╱ ╲
                ╱   ╲        Most Inherent (Best)
               ╱ ISD ╲
              ╱───────╲
             ╱  PASSIVE ╲       Passive (e.g., dikes, blast walls)
            ╱───────────╲
           ╱   ACTIVE     ╲     Active (e.g., interlocks, ESDs, PSVs)
          ╱─────────────────╲
         ╱   PROCEDURAL      ╲  Procedural (e.g., SOPs, training)
        ╱─────────────────────╲
       Least Inherent (Worst)
```

### 2.3 The Kletz Equation (Conceptual Risk Reduction)

While not a single closed-form equation, the conceptual reduction can be expressed as:

$$
R_{\text{process}} = f\left(H_{\text{intrinsic}}, \, P_{\text{failure}}, \, C_{\text{consequence}}\right)
$$

where:
- $R_{\text{process}}$ = Residual process risk
- $H_{\text{intrinsic}}$ = Intrinsic hazard
- $P_{\text{failure}}$ = Probability of failure of safeguards
- $C_{\text{consequence}}$ = Consequence magnitude

**ISD acts on the most fundamental variable: $H_{\text{intrinsic}}$.** Engineered safeguards act on $P_{\text{failure}}$, and procedural safeguards act on $C_{\text{consequence}}$ through mitigation.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Expression / Statement | Units / Key Value | KTU Exam Relevance |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Conceptual Risk Equation | $R = f(H, P, C)$ | Dimensionless indices | Theory (2 marks) |
| 2 | Hazard Reduction Ratio | $\eta = \dfrac{H_{\text{original}}}{H_{\text{new design}}}$ | Dimensionless (≥ 1) | Numericals |
| 3 | Vapor Cloud Mass Released (idealized punctured tank) | $m = \rho_l \cdot V_{\text{tank}} \cdot \alpha$ | kg, where $\alpha$ = fraction released | Design calculations |
| 4 | Vapor Fraction Flashing | $f_v = \dfrac{C_p (T_{\text{liq}} - T_{\text{b}})}{H_v}$ | Dimensionless | Used in "Moderate" examples |
| 5 | Probability of Fire (pool fire) | $P_{\text{ignite}} \propto \dot{m}_{\text{leak}} \cdot t_{\text{ignition}}$ | 1/s × s = dimensionless | Risk ranking |
| 6 | TLV/LC₅₀ Reduction | $\text{New Risk} = \text{Old Risk} \times \dfrac{\text{LC}_{50,\text{old}}}{\text{LC}_{50,\text{new}}}$ | Ratio | Substitution questions |
| 7 | Failure Rate of Safeguards | $\lambda_{\text{added}} = \sum_{i} \lambda_i$ | 1/year | Justifies "Minimize" pillar |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Memory Aid:** The four pillars begin with the letters **M-S-M-S** → **M**inimize, **S**ubstitute, **M**oderate, **S**implify. Examiners love to test these.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Analytical Case: Comparing Two Inherently Safer Alternatives

**Problem Statement (KTU 14-mark style):**

> A plant stores **chlorine** in two candidate designs:
> - **Design A (Conventional):** 50 tonnes of liquid Cl₂ at 6 bar (gauge) and 10 °C.
> - **Design B (ISD Option):** 5 tonnes of liquid Cl₂ at 1 bar (gauge) and −34 °C (boiling point at 1 bar).
>
> Compare the two designs with respect to inherent safety. Assume complete catastrophic rupture of the storage vessel and instantaneous release of all inventory into the open atmosphere. Use the **Vapor Mass Fraction (Flash Fraction)** concept.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

#### Step 1: Identify the Governing Physical Principle

When pressurized liquefied chlorine catastrophically ruptures, a fraction of the liquid **instantaneously flashes** to vapor (because the new equilibrium pressure is atmospheric). This flash fraction is given by:

$$
f_v = \frac{C_p \, (T_{\text{stored}} - T_{\text{b}})}{H_v}
$$

where:
- $C_p$ = specific heat capacity of liquid Cl₂ ≈ **0.93 kJ/(kg·K)**
- $H_v$ = latent heat of vaporization at atmospheric BP ≈ **287.6 kJ/kg**
- $T_{\text{stored}}$ = storage temperature (K)
- $T_{\text{b}}$ = normal boiling point ≈ −34.04 °C = **239.11 K**

#### Step 2: Calculate Flash Fraction for Design A

Convert the storage temperature of Design A to Kelvin:

$$
T_{\text{A}} = 10\,^\circ\text{C} = 283.15\,\text{K}
$$

Substitute into the flash-fraction equation:

$$
f_{v,A} = \frac{0.93 \times (283.15 - 239.11)}{287.6}
$$

Compute the numerator:

$$
0.93 \times 44.04 = 40.9572\,\text{kJ/kg}
$$

Divide by $H_v$:

$$
f_{v,A} = \frac{40.9572}{287.6} = 0.1424 \quad (\text{or } \approx 14.24\%)
$$

#### Step 3: Calculate Flash Fraction for Design B

For Design B, the storage temperature **equals** the normal boiling point:

$$
T_{\text{B}} = -34\,^\circ\text{C} = 239.15\,\text{K} \approx T_{\text{b}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
f_{v,B} = \frac{0.93 \times (239.15 - 239.11)}{287.6} \approx \frac{0.93 \times 0.04}{287.6} \approx 0.000129
$$

The flash fraction is **negligible** — essentially, **no flashing occurs** because the liquid is already at its boiling point at atmospheric pressure.

#### Step 4: Calculate Mass of Vapor Generated (Initial Burst)

For Design A:

$$
m_{\text{liquid, A}} = 50{,}000\,\text{kg}
$$

$$
m_{\text{vapor, A}} = f_{v,A} \times m_{\text{liquid, A}} = 0.1424 \times 50{,}000 = 7{,}120\,\text{kg}
$$

The remaining liquid (50,000 − 7,120 = 42,880 kg) forms a **spreading pool** that continues to evaporate.

For Design B:

$$
m_{\text{vapor, B}} = 0.000129 \times 5{,}000 = 0.645\,\text{kg}
$$

#### Step 5: Compute Pool Evaporation Contribution (Steady-State, Simplified)

Assume the released liquid pool evaporates with a mass-transfer rate driven by wind. A simple TNO-style correlation for an unconfined pool is:

$$
\dot{m} = k_p \cdot A_{\text{pool}} \cdot \dfrac{P_{\text{vap}}(T_{\text{liq}})}{RT_{\text{liq}}}
$$

For a first-order analytical comparison, use the empirically observed rule that pool evaporation contributes an **additional ~50%–80%** of the originally stored inventory (depending on wind, ground, and time window) for pressurized liquefied gases, but **less than 20%** for refrigerated storage.

Let us assume a *conservative* 60% additional pool evaporation for Design A and 20% for Design B.

Total airborne Cl₂ in a 1-hour window:

$$
m_{\text{total, A}} = 7{,}120 + (0.60 \times 50{,}000) = 7{,}120 + 30{,}000 = 37{,}120\,\text{kg}
$$

$$
m_{\text{total, B}} = 0.645 + (0.20 \times 5{,}000) = 0.645 + 1{,}000 = 1{,}000.6\,\text{kg}
$$

#### Step 6: Compute the Inherent Safety Reduction Factor

$$
\eta = \frac{m_{\text{total, A}}}{m_{\text{total, B}}} = \frac{37{,}120}{1{,}000.6} \approx 37.1
$$

**Design B is approximately 37× inherently safer** with respect to airborne toxic release in a catastrophic rupture scenario.

#### Step 7: Conclusion for KTU Exam

| Parameter | Design A (Conventional) | Design B (ISD: Refrigerated, Minimized) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Inventory | 50,000 kg | 5,000 kg |
| Storage pressure | 6 bar (g) | 1 bar (g) |
| Flash fraction | 14.24% | ≈ 0% |
| Total airborne Cl₂ | ~37,120 kg | ~1,001 kg |
| ISD reduction factor | 1.0 (baseline) | **~37× safer** |

> [!NOTE]
> **This two-pronged design — MINIMIZE (10× inventory cut) + MODERATE (refrigerated, no flashing) — is a textbook KTU answer demonstrating the *synergy* between ISD pillars. Examiners reward answers that identify multiple pillars working together.**

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping ISD Principles to Real Hazards

| ISD Pillar | Hazard Targeted | Typical Engineering Lever | Consequence Reduction Mechanism |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Minimize | All (toxic, flammable, reactive) | Smaller tanks, microreactors, just-in-time production | Smaller source term if released |
| Substitute | Toxicity, flammability, reactivity | Safer solvents, safer refrigerants, safer monomers | Lower intrinsic hazard per kg |
| Moderate | Flammability, explosion, runaway reaction | Lower T, lower P, dilution, refrigeration | Smaller release rate, no flash |
| Simplify | Human error, equipment failure | Welded joints, fewer instruments, fewer dead legs | Fewer failure paths |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concept Map: The Four Pillars of Inherently Safer Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Inherently Safer Design ISD"] --> B["MINIMIZE Intensification"]
    A --> C["SUBSTITUTE Replacement"]
    A --> D["MODERATE Attenuation"]
    A --> E["SIMPLIFY Simplification"]

    B --> B1["Smaller inventory"]
    B --> B2["Microreactors"]
    B --> B3["Just in time production"]

    C --> C1["Less toxic solvent"]
    C --> C2["Non flammable refrigerant"]
    C --> C3["Inert chemistry"]

    D --> D1["Lower temperature"]
    D --> D2["Lower pressure"]
    D --> D3["Dilute concentration"]

    E --> E1["Welded joints"]
    E --> E2["Fewer instruments"]
    E --> E3["No dead legs"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: ISD Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["Process Requirement Identified"] --> P1["Step 1 Identify All Hazardous Materials and Conditions"]
    P1 --> P2["Step 2 Apply MINIMIZE Can inventory be reduced"]
    P2 --> P3{"Hazard reduced"}
    P3 -- "Yes" --> P4["Adopt MINIMIZE design"]
    P3 -- "No" --> P5["Step 3 Apply SUBSTITUTE Can a safer material be used"]
    P5 --> P6{"Safer alternative found"}
    P6 -- "Yes" --> P7["Adopt SUBSTITUTE design"]
    P6 -- "No" --> P8["Step 4 Apply MODERATE Can T and P be reduced"]
    P8 --> P9{"Moderation feasible"}
    P9 -- "Yes" --> P10["Adopt MODERATE design"]
    P9 -- "No" --> P11["Step 5 Apply SIMPLIFY Can complexity be removed"]
    P11 --> P12{"Simplification possible"}
    P12 -- "Yes" --> P13["Adopt SIMPLIFY design"]
    P12 -- "No" --> P14["Step 6 Engineered and Procedural Safeguards as Final Layer"]
    P4 --> P15["Document ISD decision rationale"]
    P7 --> P15
    P10 --> P15
    P13 --> P15
    P14 --> P15
    P15 --> P16["Approved Inherently Safer Process Design"]
```

### 4.3 Layered Safety Control Hierarchy (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0["Process Material and Condition Choice"] --> L1["Inherent Elimination"]
    L1 --> L2["Passive Safeguards Dikes Blast Walls"]
    L2 --> L3["Active Safeguards PSV ESD Interlocks Alarms"]
    L3 --> L4["Procedural Safeguards SOPs Training Permits"]
    L0 -.->|"Strongest"| M0["Maximum Hazard Removal"]
    L4 -.->|"Weakest"| M1["Minimum Hazard Removal"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The topmost block is *intrinsic* to the process — it cannot fail because it is *not a piece of equipment*. Each successive layer is *added on top* and represents a *failure-prone* engineering control. This visual is a frequent KTU 14-mark essay anchor.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1. Define Inherently Safer Design. State any two of its key strategies.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Inherently Safer Design (ISD) is a process safety philosophy that seeks to **permanently eliminate or reduce hazards at the source** by making the right choices in materials, inventory, and operating conditions, rather than by adding external protective equipment. The key strategies (Kletz) are:

1. **Minimize** — Use the smallest possible quantity of hazardous material.
2. **Substitute** — Replace a hazardous material with a less hazardous one.

(Other acceptable pairs: *Moderate* / *Simplify*.)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Distinguish between *Hazard* and *Risk*. How does ISD act on these quantities?**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Hazard | Risk |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Definition | Inherent property of a material/process | Probability × Consequence of an incident |
| Nature | Intrinsic, does not change with control | Can be reduced by safeguards |
| ISD action | **Reduces the hazard itself** (at the source) | Engineered safeguards reduce *risk* without touching *hazard* |

ISD is superior because reducing the hazard reduces risk across *all* failure scenarios simultaneously.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain in detail the **four principles of Inherently Safer Design** as articulated by Trevor Kletz. For each principle, give **one industrial example**.
*(7 Marks)*

**(b)** With the aid of a labeled sketch, describe the **hierarchy of process safety controls** and justify why inherently safer design occupies the top of the hierarchy.
*(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The four principles of ISD, formalized by Trevor Kletz, are:

1. **Minimize (Intensification):** Reduce the quantity of hazardous material held in process and storage. *Example:* On-demand generation of methyl isocyanate (MIC) at Union Carbide's Institute, WV plant, reducing inventory from ~120,000 lb to ~500 lb after the 1984 Bhopal disaster.

2. **Substitute:** Replace a hazardous substance with one that is less hazardous. *Example:* Substituting cyclohexane for benzene as an industrial solvent — similar boiling point, vastly lower carcinogenicity.

3. **Moderate (Attenuation):** Operate at conditions (T, P, concentration) that are inherently less dangerous. *Example:* Refrigerated storage of ammonia at −33 °C and 1 atm, avoiding the explosive vapor release that would occur from a 10 bar pressurized liquid ammonia release.

4. **Simplify:** Reduce the complexity of equipment, piping, and procedures to reduce human error and failure opportunities. *Example:* Replacing flanged, gasketed joints in toxic service with welded connections to eliminate leak paths.

*(1.5 marks for each principle: 0.5 for definition, 1.0 for industrial example = 6 marks, +1 mark for overall organization.)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

The hierarchy of process safety controls, from most to least robust, is:

1. **Inherent (Elimination/Minimization)** — embedded in process design; cannot fail because it is not a "system."
2. **Passive** — operates without external power (dikes, bunds, blast walls).
3. **Active** — requires actuation or power (PSVs, ESDs, alarms, fire suppression).
4. **Procedural** — depends on human action (SOPs, training, hot-work permits).

**Justification for ISD at the top:**

- The probability of a *passive* safeguard failing is non-zero (a dike can be breached).
- *Active* safeguards depend on sensors, signals, and actuators — each with a measurable failure rate $\lambda$.
- *Procedural* safeguards depend on human vigilance, which is variable.

Only an *inherent* safeguard, which is a property of the *process itself* (e.g., "this tank contains no toxic material at all"), cannot fail in any conventional sense. Hence the KTU-preferred motto: **"If you don't have it, you can't leak it."**

*(3 marks for hierarchy listing, 2 marks for sketch description, 2 marks for justification of ISD at top.)*

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Discuss the **Bhopal disaster of 1984** in the context of inherently safer design. What ISD principles were *not* applied that could have prevented or mitigated the catastrophe?
*(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Two process designs for producing **acrylic acid** are proposed:
- **Design A:** Batch reactor with 5-tonne MMA inventory, operated at 90 °C.
- **Design B:** Continuous-flow microreactor with 50 kg inventory, operated at 60 °C with aqueous dilution.

Identify which ISD principles are demonstrated by Design B and quantify the **inventory reduction ratio**.
*(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The Bhopal disaster of 2–3 December 1984 at Union Carbide's pesticide plant released approximately **40 tonnes of methyl isocyanate (MIC)** — an extremely toxic, reactive, and flammable gas — killing over 3,800 people and affecting hundreds of thousands.

**ISD principles that were NOT applied:**

1. **Minimize:** The plant stored **~120,000 lb (~54 tonnes)** of MIC at any time, an exceptionally large inventory for a high-toxicity intermediate. A continuous on-demand generator (as later installed at Institute, WV) would have reduced inventory by **>99%**.

2. **Moderate:** MIC was stored as a **pressurized liquid at ~15 psi** and ambient temperature, meaning a release would *flash* into a vapor cloud. **Refrigerated storage at near-atmospheric pressure** would have dramatically reduced the flash fraction, yielding a small dense liquid pool rather than a vast toxic vapor cloud.

3. **Substitute:** Although MIC was the optimal intermediate for the Sevin® process, an **alternative synthetic route** (e.g., a non-MIC pathway to carbamate pesticides) could have eliminated MIC entirely.

4. **Simplify:** The plant had **multiple redundant safety systems** (scrubbers, flare towers, water curtains) that were either in maintenance, ineffective, or overwhelmed — exactly the failure mode Kletz warned about. Inherent design would have *removed* the need for these add-ons.

*(2 marks for Bhopal facts, 1.5 marks per ISD principle = 6 marks, 1 mark for concluding recommendation.)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**ISD Principles in Design B:**

| Pillar | How Design B Embodies It |
|:---|:---|
| **Minimize** | Inventory reduced from 5,000 kg to 50 kg |
| **Moderate** | Operating temperature reduced from 90 °C to 60 °C (lower runaway risk for the polymerization hazard of acrylic acid) |
| **Moderate (continued)** | Aqueous dilution removes the bulk flammability hazard of neat acrylic acid vapor |

**Inventory Reduction Ratio:**

$$
\eta_{\text{inventory}} = \frac{m_{\text{A}}}{m_{\text{B}}} = \frac{5{,}000\,\text{kg}}{50\,\text{kg}} = 100
$$

**Conclusion:** Design B is **100× inherently safer** with respect to inventory, and additionally benefits from thermal moderation and dilution. This is a multi-pillar ISD design.

*(3 marks for identifying pillars, 2 marks for quantification, 2 marks for synthesis.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "Inherent Safety" with "Add-on Safety":** Many students write at length about *relief valves* and *interlocks* when asked about ISD. This is the **opposite** of inherent safety. Always begin with the *process choice itself*.
> 2. **Listing principles without examples:** KTU marking schemes award **at least 50% of marks** for industrial or numerical examples. A bare list of "Minimize, Substitute, Moderate, Simplify" without context scores poorly.
> 3. **Forgetting the hierarchy:** When asked to "compare" or "rank," always explicitly invoke the **Inherent → Passive → Active → Procedural** pyramid.
> 4. **Ignoring economics:** When asked *limitations* of ISD, do not forget to mention **cost of retrofit, plant redesign, and trade-offs** (e.g., higher inventory may improve process economics).
> 5. **Not citing Kletz or Bhopal:** Both are syllabus-essential historical anchors; missing them signals superficial preparation.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Inherently Safer Design = Eliminate hazard at the source**, not control it externally.
- ✅ The **four pillars (Kletz):** **M**inimize, **S**ubstitute, **M**oderate, **S**implify.
- ✅ **Trevor Kletz** is the father of ISD; **Bhopal (1984)** is the canonical case study.
- ✅ **Hazard vs. Risk:** ISD reduces *hazard*; engineered safeguards reduce *risk*.
- ✅ **Hierarchy of Controls (top to bottom):** Inherent → Passive → Active → Procedural.
- ✅ **Flash Fraction Formula:** $f_v = \dfrac{C_p (T_{\text{stored}} - T_{\text{b}})}{H_v}$ — use it in numericals.
- ✅ **Inventory Reduction Ratio:** $\eta = \dfrac{m_{\text{original}}}{m_{\text{new}}}$.
- ✅ ISD design **must be applied at the conceptual design stage**; retrofitting is expensive and limited.
- ✅ **"What you don't have, can't leak"** — Kletz's foundational maxim; often quoted in KTU answer keys.
- ✅ Limitations: cost, plant integration, performance trade-offs, and sometimes *inherent safety is not always achievable* (e.g., when the chemistry intrinsically requires the hazard).
- ✅ Common substitutes: benzene → cyclohexane; CFCs → HFCs; solvent-based → water-based paints.
- ✅ Refrigerated storage at atmospheric pressure is the classic **MODERATE** example (LPG, ammonia, chlorine).
- ✅ Microreactors and continuous-flow chemistry are the modern **MINIMIZE** approach.
- ✅ **Welded joints, no dead legs, fewer instruments** are classic **SIMPLIFY** interventions.

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Inherently Safer Design**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme, OESFT616 — Chemical Process Safety.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Engineered Safety. Process and Plant Hazards: Hazards of Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT616 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Engineered Safety. Process and Plant Hazards: Hazards of Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Engineered Safety — Formal Definition

**Engineered Safety** refers to the systematic application of scientific and engineering principles, combined with inherently safer design (ISD) philosophies, to eliminate, reduce, or control process hazards in chemical plants. It is one of the four pillars of the **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** hierarchy, the others being **Inherent Safety**, **Procedural/Passive Safety**, and **Active/Instrumented Safety**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Engineered Safety is *not* just about adding safeguards. It encompasses the deliberate design of equipment, control systems, and plant layouts such that the process is intrinsically tolerant to human error, equipment failure, and external disturbances.

According to **CCPS (Center for Chemical Process Safety)** guidelines, engineered safety features are the second line of defense after inherently safer design. These include pressure relief devices, emergency shutdown systems (ESD), interlocks, containment systems, fire protection, and explosion suppression.

### 1.2 Process and Plant Hazards — Definition

A **Process Hazard** is any condition in a chemical manufacturing operation that has the potential to cause loss of life, injury, environmental damage, or property destruction. **Plant Hazards** are the physical manifestations or equipment-related conditions (e.g., a leaking flange, an over-pressurized vessel, a hot surface) that can trigger a process hazard event.

> [!NOTE]
> **Process Hazard vs. Plant Hazard:** A *process hazard* is the source of danger (e.g., the presence of a flammable gas at high pressure). A *plant hazard* is the equipment or condition that could release or intensify the danger (e.g., a corroded pressure vessel). The KTU 2024 curriculum emphasizes that the two are inseparable in risk assessment.

### 1.3 Hazards of Pressure — Core Definition

**Hazardous Pressure** is any pressure condition (internal, external, or transient) that can cause the mechanical failure of a process vessel, pipe, or component, leading to release of energy, toxic/flammable materials, projectiles, or catastrophic rupture.

Pressure is classified as one of the **"Big Four" catastrophic hazards** in chemical process safety, alongside **temperature, flammability, and toxicity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Definition (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **Standard Atmospheric Pressure (1 atm)** = **101.325 kPa** = **1.01325 bar** = **14.696 psia**
> - **High-Pressure Hazard Threshold:** Generally, **P > 0.1 MPa (gauge)** in a confined vessel is considered a significant overpressure hazard.
> - **Vacuum Hazard Threshold:** **P < 0.5 atm absolute** can collapse thin-walled atmospheric storage tanks.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pressure Cooker Principle"

Imagine a **domestic pressure cooker** on a kitchen stove. As heat is applied, the water inside boils and produces steam. The steam pressure inside rises. The cooker is equipped with:
1. A **mechanical weight (relief valve)** on the vent that lifts and releases steam when pressure exceeds a safe limit.
2. A **gasket (seal)** to prevent leakage.
3. A **safety interlock** (the lid cannot open while pressure is high).

This is exactly how engineered safety works in a chemical plant — the **pressure vessel is the cooker**, the **relief valve is the weighted vent**, the **gaskets are the seals**, and the **interlock system** prevents catastrophic opening under pressure. The only difference is scale, energy content, and the consequences of failure.

### 1.5 Classification of Pressure Hazards (KTU Board-Exam Frequency)

| Category | Description | Typical Consequence |
|----------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Overpressure** | Internal pressure exceeds design limit | Vessel rupture, BLEVE, projectile |
| **Underpressure/Vacuum** | External pressure exceeds internal | Tank implosion, buckling |
| **Cyclic Pressure** | Repeated pressure fluctuations | Fatigue failure, crack propagation |
| **Thermal Pressure** | Pressure rise due to temperature increase | BLEVEs, runaway reactions |
| **Hydraulic Pressure** | Liquid-filled systems under external compression | Water hammer, hose whip |
| **Surge Pressure** | Rapid pressure transients (e.g., water hammer) | Pipe rupture, pump damage |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Pressure is a Critical Hazard in Chemical Plants

Pressure hazards are fundamentally different from other hazards because **pressurized systems store enormous mechanical (strain) energy**. The energy content of a pressurized gas follows the relation:

$$
E_{stored} = \frac{P_1 V}{\gamma - 1} \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{P_0}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} \right]
$$

Where:
- $E_{stored}$ = total mechanical energy stored in the gas (Joules)
- $P_1$ = internal absolute pressure (Pa)
- $P_0$ = atmospheric pressure (Pa)
- $V$ = gas volume (m³)
- $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats ($C_p / C_v$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** A 10 m³ vessel at 10 atm gauge storing methane ($\gamma \approx 1.31$) contains roughly **48 MJ** of energy — equivalent to **~11.5 kg of TNT**. This is why pressure relief is non-negotiable in KTU examinations and in real plant design.

### 2.2 Failure Modes of Pressure Vessels

Pressure vessels fail in one of four primary modes, each tied directly to KTU syllabus outcomes:

1. **Brittle Fracture** — Occurs at low temperatures or under high strain rates; no visible deformation before failure. Governed by fracture mechanics and the **Charpy V-Notch (CVN) energy criterion**.
2. **Ductile Rupture** — The vessel bulges and tears. Governed by **ultimate tensile strength (UTS)**.
3. **Creep Rupture** — Slow deformation at elevated temperatures over long periods (>0.4 $T_m$, where $T_m$ is melting point in Kelvin).
4. **Fatigue Failure** — Crack initiation and growth due to cyclic pressure fluctuations.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **frequently asked in KTU ESE** and align with the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code, Section VIII (Div. 1).

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Ideal Gas Law** | $PV = nRT$ | $P$ [Pa], $V$ [m³], $n$ [mol], $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T$ [K] | Pressure-temperature-volume relationships |
| 2 | **Combined Gas Law** | $\dfrac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}$ | Subscripts 1, 2 denote initial/final states | Constant mass processes |
| 3 | **Hoop Stress (Cylindrical Vessel)** | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$ | $P$ [Pa], $D$ [m], $t$ [m] | Tangential stress (largest in cylinder) |
| 4 | **Longitudinal Stress (Cylinder)** | $\sigma_L = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{4 \cdot t}$ | Same as above | Axial stress in cylindrical shell |
| 5 | **Spherical Vessel Stress** | $\sigma_s = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{4 \cdot t}$ | Same | Most efficient geometry (2× stronger per material) |
| 6 | **Required Thickness (Cylinder)** | $t = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 P}$ | $S$ = allowable stress, $E$ = joint efficiency | ASME Code design |
| 7 | **Hydrostatic Test Pressure** | $P_{test} = 1.3 \cdot P_{design} \cdot \dfrac{S_{test}}{S_{design}}$ | $S$ = allowable stress at respective temps | Validation test |
| 8 | **Set Pressure for Relief** | $P_{set} \leq 1.1 \cdot MAWP$ | MAWP = Max Allowable Working Pressure | API 520 guideline |
| 9 | **API 520 Required Relief Area (Fire Case)** | $A = \dfrac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{T \cdot Z}}}$ | $A$ [m²], $W$ [kg/s], $K_d$ = discharge coeff, $C$ = gas constant, $M$ = MW, $Z$ = compressibility | Sizing PSV for fire exposure |
| 10 | **Compressibility Factor (Pseudo-Redlich-Kwong)** | $Z = 1 + B' P - \dfrac{A'}{T^{1.5}}$ | $A'$, $B'$ are gas-specific constants | High-pressure gas calculations |
| 11 | **Strain Energy in Compressed Gas** | $E = \dfrac{P_1 V}{\gamma - 1} \left[ 1 - \left( \dfrac{P_0}{P_1} \right)^{\tfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} \right]$ | $E$ [J], $P$ [Pa], $V$ [m³] | Blast damage estimation |
| 12 | **Thin-Wall Criterion** | $\dfrac{D}{t} \geq 20$ or $t \leq \dfrac{D}{20}$ | Dimensionless | Validity of membrane stress formulas |

### 2.4 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Step 1 (Hoop stress = 2 × longitudinal stress):** Imagine slicing a cylinder lengthwise in half. The internal pressure tries to "push apart" the two halves along the diameter, creating **hoop (circumferential) stress**. Slicing it perpendicular to its axis creates only **half the load** (one face) over the same thickness — hence the factor of 2.
- **Step 2 (Sphere = ½ the stress of a cylinder):** A sphere has the same resistance in all directions, distributing stress uniformly. This is why **bulletproof fuel tanks and pressure spheres** are preferred for very high pressures.
- **Step 3 (Why relief valve set pressure ≤ 1.1 × MAWP):** The "10% accumulation" rule ensures that even with a blocked outlet, the pressure never exceeds 110% of the design limit, providing a **safety margin** against equipment failure.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Pressure Hazard Analysis |
|--------|------------------------------------------|
| **Oil & Gas Refineries** | Hydrocracker reactors operate at 100–200 bar; require multiple PSVs and rupture disks. |
| **LNG Plants** | Cryogenic storage at $-162^{\circ}$C, 1 atm, susceptible to vacuum collapse during pump-out. |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Autoclaves at 2–3 bar (steam sterilization) with code-stamped relief valves. |
| **Ammonia Synthesis** | Haber-Bosch reactors at 150–300 bar — historical cause of the **Oppau (1921)** and **Texas City (1947)** disasters. |
| **Power Plants** | Boiler drums at 100–180 bar with continuous online pressure monitoring. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Hoop Stress in a Thin-Walled Cylindrical Pressure Vessel

This is a **mandatory derivation** for KTU 2024 Scheme — it appears in nearly every Module 1 ESE paper.

**Given:** A thin-walled cylindrical vessel of internal diameter $D$, wall thickness $t$, internal gauge pressure $P$, and length $L$.

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram Setup**

Consider a longitudinal section of the cylinder (a "half-cylinder" cut). The internal pressure pushes outward on the projected area:

$$
A_{projected} = D \cdot L
$$

**Step 2 — Force Balance in the Hoop Direction**

The total force from internal pressure acting on the half-cylinder in the radial direction is balanced by the tensile force in the two longitudinal walls (each with cross-section $t \cdot L$):

$$
P \cdot (D \cdot L) = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot (t \cdot L)
$$

**Step 3 — Simplification**

Cancel $L$ from both sides:

$$
P \cdot D = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Hoop Stress**

$$
\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **governing design stress** in a cylindrical vessel because it is **twice as large** as the longitudinal stress. Any KTU numerical will, by default, ask for hoop stress unless longitudinal stress is explicitly mentioned.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Longitudinal Stress in a Thin-Walled Cylindrical Vessel

**Step 1 — Free Body Setup (Transverse Cut)**

Cut the cylinder perpendicular to its axis. The end cap has area:

$$
A_{end} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

**Step 2 — Force Balance**

The pressure force on the end cap is balanced by the tensile force in the circular wall cross-section (area = $\pi D t$):

$$
P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \sigma_L \cdot (\pi D \cdot t)
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify and Solve**

$$
\sigma_L = \frac{P \cdot D}{4 \cdot t}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_h = 2 \cdot \sigma_L}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Stress in a Spherical Pressure Vessel

**Step 1 — Projected Area**

A spherical cap of diameter $D$ has a projected circular area of $\pi D^2 / 4$.

**Step 2 — Force Balance**

Pressure force on the cap is balanced by tensile force in the spherical wall (cut along a great circle, area = $\pi D t$):

$$
P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \sigma_s \cdot (\pi D \cdot t)
$$

**Step 3 — Solve**

$$
\sigma_s = \frac{P \cdot D}{4 \cdot t}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison:** $\sigma_s = \sigma_L = \dfrac{1}{2} \sigma_h$. Spheres are **twice as strong** as cylinders of the same diameter, thickness, and pressure — the reason LPG and CNG tanks are often spherical.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Required Wall Thickness (ASME Code Method)

Starting from the hoop stress equation and equating to allowable stress $S$:

$$
\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 t} = S
$$

Solving for thickness:

$$
t = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 S}
$$

**ASME Modification** — incorporating **joint efficiency** $E$ and **corrosion allowance** $c$:

$$
t = \frac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 P} + c
$$

Where:
- $R$ = inner radius (m)
- $S$ = allowable stress at design temperature (Pa)
- $E$ = joint efficiency (0.7 for butt-welded, 1.0 for seamless)
- $c$ = corrosion allowance (m), typically **1.5–3 mm**

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — KTU Frequently Asked Numerical Examples

| # | Problem Type | Given | Find | Governing Equation |
|---|--------------|-------|------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Cylindrical vessel stress** | $P$ = 2 MPa, $D$ = 1.5 m, $t$ = 10 mm | $\sigma_h$, $\sigma_L$ | $\sigma_h = PD/(2t)$, $\sigma_L = PD/(4t)$ |
| 2 | **Spherical vessel thickness** | $P$ = 5 MPa, $D$ = 2 m, $S$ = 140 MPa, $E$ = 1.0 | $t$ | $t = PR/(SE - 0.6P)$ |
| 3 | **Pressure vessel energy** | $P_1$ = 30 atm, $V$ = 5 m³, $P_0$ = 1 atm, $\gamma$ = 1.4 | $E_{stored}$ | $E = P_1 V (\gamma-1)^{-1} [1 - (P_0/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}]$ |
| 4 | **Relief valve set pressure** | MAWP = 10 bar, accumulation = 10% | $P_{set}$ | $P_{set} = 1.1 \times$ MAWP |
| 5 | **Gas law application** | $V_1$ = 2 m³, $P_1$ = 5 atm, $T_1$ = 300 K, $T_2$ = 450 K (constant V) | $P_2$ | $P_1/T_1 = P_2/T_2$ |

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (Sample KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A cylindrical pressure vessel has an internal diameter of **1.2 m**, wall thickness of **15 mm**, and operates at a gauge pressure of **2.5 MPa**. Calculate:
(a) The hoop stress.
(b) The longitudinal stress.
(c) The minimum wall thickness required if the allowable stress is **140 MPa** and the joint efficiency is **0.85**. Assume negligible corrosion.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Hoop Stress:**

$$
\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t} = \frac{(2.5 \times 10^6 \, \text{Pa}) \cdot (1.2 \, \text{m})}{2 \cdot (0.015 \, \text{m})}
$$

$$
\sigma_h = \frac{3.0 \times 10^6}{0.030} = 1.0 \times 10^8 \, \text{Pa} = 100 \, \text{MPa}
$$

**Part (b) — Longitudinal Stress:**

$$
\sigma_L = \frac{\sigma_h}{2} = \frac{100}{2} = 50 \, \text{MPa}
$$

**Part (c) — Minimum Wall Thickness:**

$$
t = \frac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 P} = \frac{(2.5 \times 10^6) \cdot (0.6)}{(140 \times 10^6)(0.85) - 0.6 (2.5 \times 10^6)}
$$

$$
t = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6}{119 \times 10^6 - 1.5 \times 10^6} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6}{117.5 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
t = 0.01277 \, \text{m} \approx 12.8 \, \text{mm}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert **mm to m** when substituting thickness. Always check unit consistency: 1 mm = $10^{-3}$ m. Marks are frequently lost here.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Engineered Safety Hierarchy for Pressure Hazards

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **Layered Defense-in-Depth Architecture** used in chemical process safety for pressure-related hazards. Each layer represents an independent protection layer (IPL) per **IEC 61511** standards.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["INHERENT SAFETY LAYER 1: Eliminate hazard by design - Reduce inventories - Lower operating pressure - Substitute materials"]
    L2["ENGINEERED PASSIVE LAYER 2: Containment - Pressure vessel shell - Dikes and bunds - Blast walls"]
    L3["ENGINEERED ACTIVE LAYER 3: Control and Interlocks - Pressure transmitters - Alarms at 90 percent MAWP - Emergency Shutdown ESD valves - High-High Trips at 95 percent MAWP"]
    L4["ENGINEERED ACTIVE LAYER 4: Pressure Relief Devices - Pressure Safety Valve PSV - Rupture Disk - Thermal Relief Valve - Vent to flare or scrubber"]
    L5["PROCEDURAL LAYER 5: Operating Procedures - Permits to work - Hot work permits - Inspection schedules - Hydrostatic test every 4 to 8 years"]
    L6["EMERGENCY RESPONSE LAYER 6: Mitigation - Firewater deluge - Foam systems - Emergency evacuation - Plant emergency squad"]
    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> L6
    HAZ["HAZARD SOURCE: Pressurized Flammable or Toxic Fluid at P greater than MAWP"] --> L1
    INC["INCIDENT OUTCOME: Vessel rupture, BLEVE, toxic release, fire, explosion"] -.-> L6
    L6 -.-> INC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** If any one layer fails, the next layer should be capable of preventing or mitigating the consequence. This is the essence of **defense-in-depth** in KTU Module 1.

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Pressure Relief System (Block-Level Flow)

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **functional interaction** between a process vessel and its engineered overpressure protection subsystem. This is a fallback representation suitable for a complex P&ID-style topic.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Process Vessel with Volume V and Design Pressure Pdesign"] --> B["Pressure Transmitter PT-101 measures Pinternal"]
    B --> C{"Controller in DCS compares P to Setpoint"}
    C -- Normal range --> D["Process continues - No action required"]
    C -- P exceeds 90 percent MAWP --> E["Operator Alarm in DCS"]
    E -- P exceeds 95 percent MAWP --> F["High-High Trip - ESD Valve closes inlet"]
    F -- P exceeds 100 percent MAWP --> G["Pressure Safety Valve PSV opens automatically"]
    G -- Discharge --> H["Rupture Disk as secondary barrier"]
    H --> I["Vent Header routed to Flare Stack"]
    I --> J["Flare Tip burns flammable gases"]
    I -- Toxic case --> K["Scrubber neutralizes toxic vapors"]
    G -- Catastrophic failure --> L["Mechanical Rupture Disk bursts at 110 percent MAWP"]
```

### 4.3 Schematic of Pressure Vessel Stress Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Pressurized Cylinder Internal Pressure P"] --> B["Wall Thickness t resists the load"]
    B --> C1["Hoop Stress sigma_h equals P times D over 2 times t - Maximum stress - Direction: circumferential"]
    B --> C2["Longitudinal Stress sigma_L equals P times D over 4 times t - Half the hoop stress - Direction: axial"]
    B --> C3["Radial Stress sigma_r negligible in thin walls - equals P at inner wall and zero at outer wall"]
    C1 --> D["Failure Risk Zone 1: Rupture along longitudinal axis of cylinder"]
    C2 --> E["Failure Risk Zone 2: End-cap blow-off at circumferential seam"]
    C3 --> F["Safe in Thin-Wall Regime where D over t exceeds 20"]
```

### 4.4 Decision Flowchart: Pressure Relief Sizing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Identify Overpressure Scenario"] --> Q1{"Is the cause external fire?"}
    Q1 -- YES --> F1["Apply API 521 Fire Case Formula - W equals C times A times hc times sqrt of M times P"]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{"Is the cause blocked outlet?"}
    Q2 -- YES --> F2["Apply API 520 Blocked Outlet Case - Relieve at least 1.1 times MAWP within 3 minutes"]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{"Is the cause runaway reaction?"}
    Q3 -- YES --> F3["Use Reaction Relief Sizing - Adiabatic calorimetry data required"]
    Q3 -- NO --> F4["Apply General Overpressure Case - Sum of all credible inflows"]
    F1 --> OUT["Determine Required Relief Area A in m squared"]
    F2 --> OUT
    F3 --> OUT
    F4 --> OUT
    OUT --> SEL["Select PSV Orifice Size from API 520 Tables"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define Engineered Safety. How does it differ from Inherent Safety?**

**Model Answer:**

Engineered Safety is the application of engineered control systems, protective devices, and design features to manage process hazards. It includes **active systems (PSVs, ESDs, alarms)**, **passive systems (containment, dikes)**, and **procedural controls (operating procedures)**.

**Difference from Inherent Safety:**

| Aspect | Inherent Safety | Engineered Safety |
|--------|----------------|--------------------|
| Philosophy | Eliminate the hazard | Control the hazard |
| Cost | High initial, low lifecycle | Lower initial, ongoing maintenance |
| Example | Reduce inventory of toxic material | Install scrubber on vent |
| Reliability | Permanent, cannot fail | Requires testing, can fail |

Inherent safety removes the hazard at the design stage (e.g., using a safer solvent), whereas engineered safety adds protective layers (e.g., a relief valve) without removing the hazard. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**List any six consequences of overpressure in a chemical process vessel.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Vessel rupture** — Catastrophic release of contents.
2. **BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion)** — If a pressurized liquefied gas vessel fails.
3. **Projectile hazards** — Vessel fragments travel hundreds of meters.
4. **Toxic gas cloud dispersion** — Acute toxicity to workers and public.
5. **Flammable vapor cloud explosion (VCE)** — Secondary fires and explosions.
6. **Loss of containment** — Environmental damage, soil/water contamination.

**(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

**Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 — Module 1 Choice]**

**(a)** Derive the expressions for **hoop stress** and **longitudinal stress** in a thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical pressure vessel has an internal diameter of **0.8 m**, wall thickness of **12 mm**, and operates at an internal gauge pressure of **3 MPa**.
(i) Calculate the hoop and longitudinal stresses. **(3 marks)**
(ii) Determine the required wall thickness if the allowable stress is **130 MPa** and the joint efficiency is **0.9**. **(4 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

*Hoop Stress Derivation:*

Consider a thin-walled cylinder of internal diameter $D$, thickness $t$, length $L$, and internal pressure $P$. Cut the cylinder along a longitudinal plane and consider one half.

The force due to internal pressure acting on the projected area is:

$$
F_{pressure} = P \cdot D \cdot L
$$

This is balanced by the tensile force in the two wall sections, each of cross-sectional area $t \cdot L$:

$$
F_{wall} = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L
$$

Equating:

$$
P \cdot D \cdot L = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L
$$

Cancelling $L$:

$$
\boxed{\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}}
$$

*Longitudinal Stress Derivation:*

Cut the cylinder perpendicular to its axis. The pressure force on the end cap of area $\pi D^2 / 4$ is:

$$
F_{pressure} = P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

The tensile force in the wall (cut along a circle, area $\pi D t$) is:

$$
F_{wall} = \sigma_L \cdot \pi D \cdot t
$$

Equating:

$$
P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \sigma_L \cdot \pi D \cdot t
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\boxed{\sigma_L = \frac{P \cdot D}{4 \cdot t}}
$$

Hence, $\sigma_h = 2 \sigma_L$. **(7 marks — full derivation with free body diagram in KTU answer sheet)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $D = 0.8$ m, $t = 12$ mm $= 0.012$ m, $P = 3 \times 10^6$ Pa (gauge).

**(i) Hoop Stress:**

$$
\sigma_h = \frac{(3 \times 10^6) \cdot (0.8)}{2 \cdot (0.012)} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^6}{0.024} = 1.0 \times 10^8 \, \text{Pa} = 100 \, \text{MPa}
$$

**Longitudinal Stress:**

$$
\sigma_L = \frac{\sigma_h}{2} = 50 \, \text{MPa}
$$

**(3 marks)**

**(ii) Required Wall Thickness:**

$$
t = \frac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 P} = \frac{(3 \times 10^6)(0.4)}{(130 \times 10^6)(0.9) - 0.6 (3 \times 10^6)}
$$

$$
t = \frac{1.2 \times 10^6}{117 \times 10^6 - 1.8 \times 10^6} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^6}{115.2 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
t = 1.042 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{m} \approx 10.42 \, \text{mm}
$$

**(4 marks)**

---

**Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 1 Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **types of pressure hazards** encountered in chemical process industries. Discuss the principle and working of a **Pressure Safety Valve (PSV)** with a neat sketch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reactor operates at a pressure of **8 bar gauge** and contains **3 m³ of nitrogen gas** at **25°C**. The vessel fails catastrophically and releases its contents to the atmosphere (1 bar absolute). Assuming nitrogen behaves as an ideal gas with $\gamma = 1.4$:
(i) Calculate the initial mass of nitrogen. **(3 marks)**
(ii) Calculate the **strain energy released** during the rupture. **(4 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Types of Pressure Hazards:**

The major types are:

1. **Overpressure** — Internal pressure exceeds MAWP due to blocked outlets, fire exposure, runaway reactions, or pump malfunction.
2. **Underpressure / Vacuum** — Loss of internal pressure (e.g., during pump-out) can collapse atmospheric storage tanks.
3. **Cyclic Pressure Fluctuations** — Cause metal fatigue and crack propagation.
4. **Thermal Pressure** — Liquid trapped in a confined space expands when heated, generating extremely high pressures (e.g., "liquid slug" in pipelines).
5. **Surge / Water Hammer** — Rapid valve closure produces pressure spikes.

**Pressure Safety Valve (PSV) — Working Principle:**

A PSV is a **spring-loaded, automatic pressure-relieving device** that opens when the inlet pressure exceeds the set pressure. Key components:

- **Inlet nozzle** — Connected to the vessel.
- **Disc** — Seals against the nozzle.
- **Spring** — Exerts downward force on the disc, opposing internal pressure.
- **Adjustment screw** — Sets the spring force (= set pressure).
- **Disc holder & guide** — Aligns the disc.
- **Bonnet & lift lever** — For manual testing.

**Operating Sequence:**

1. Normal: $P < P_{set}$. Spring force $>$ pressure force on disc. Disc remains seated.
2. Set Point: $P = P_{set}$. Disc begins to lift.
3. Blowdown: After opening, pressure must drop below $P_{set} - \Delta P_{blowdown}$ before the disc reseats (prevents chattering).
4. Overpressure: Up to 10% accumulation allowed (per API 520).

**(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $P_1 = 8$ bar gauge $= 9$ bar absolute $= 9 \times 10^5$ Pa, $V = 3$ m³, $T = 25°C = 298$ K, $P_0 = 1$ bar abs $= 1 \times 10^5$ Pa, $\gamma = 1.4$.

**(i) Initial Mass of Nitrogen:**

Using $PV = nRT$ and $M_{N_2} = 28$ g/mol:

$$
n = \frac{P_1 V}{R T} = \frac{(9 \times 10^5)(3)}{(8.314)(298)} = \frac{2.7 \times 10^6}{2477.6} = 1089.6 \, \text{mol}
$$

$$
m = n \cdot M = 1089.6 \times 0.028 = 30.51 \, \text{kg}
$$

**(3 marks)**

**(ii) Strain Energy Released:**

$$
E = \frac{P_1 V}{\gamma - 1} \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{P_0}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} \right]
$$

$$
\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \frac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857
$$

$$
\left( \frac{P_0}{P_1} \right)^{0.2857} = \left( \frac{1}{9} \right)^{0.2857} = 0.6122
$$

$$
E = \frac{(9 \times 10^5)(3)}{0.4} \left[ 1 - 0.6122 \right] = (6.75 \times 10^6)(0.3878)
$$

$$
E \approx 2.62 \times 10^6 \, \text{J} = 2.62 \, \text{MJ}
$$

**Interpretation:** This is equivalent to approximately **0.625 kg of TNT** released in a few milliseconds — a major explosion hazard.

**(4 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mistake #1:** Using **bar** instead of **Pa** in the ideal gas law. Always convert: **1 bar = $10^5$ Pa**.
> 2. **Unit Mistake #2:** Forgetting to convert **gauge pressure to absolute** in gas law problems. KTU 2024 papers frequently use gauge pressure to test this.
> 3. **Formula Confusion:** Mixing up hoop stress ($PD/2t$) and longitudinal stress ($PD/4t$). Hoop is always **twice** longitudinal.
> 4. **Derivation Marks:** Students lose 2–3 marks by not drawing a **free body diagram** before writing the force balance. Always include a sketch in derivations.
> 5. **PSV Sketch:** Drawing the PSV upside-down or omitting the **spring** symbol — the KTU valuation key explicitly requires correct labeling of all 5–6 major parts.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Hazards of Pressure**

- **Engineered Safety** is a layered approach: **Inherent → Passive → Active (Controls) → Active (Relief) → Procedural → Emergency**.
- **The "Big Four" Hazards** in chemical plants: **Pressure, Temperature, Flammability, Toxicity**.
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** **1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar = 14.696 psia** — memorize this.
- **Hoop stress** $\sigma_h = \dfrac{PD}{2t}$ — the **largest** stress in a cylindrical vessel — **twice** the longitudinal.
- **Longitudinal stress** $\sigma_L = \dfrac{PD}{4t}$ — half of hoop stress.
- **Spherical vessels** are **twice as strong** as cylindrical vessels (same $D$, $t$, $P$).
- **Thin-wall validity:** $\dfrac{D}{t} \geq 20$. If less than 20, use **thick-wall (Lamé) equations** (not in KTU syllabus).
- **MAWP** = Maximum Allowable Working Pressure. The PSV set pressure $\leq$ **1.1 × MAWP** (10% accumulation rule per API 520).
- **Strain energy stored in compressed gas:** $E = \dfrac{P_1 V}{\gamma - 1} \left[ 1 - \left( \dfrac{P_0}{P_1} \right)^{\tfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} \right]$ — proportional to $P_1$ and $V$.
- **Five types of pressure hazards:** Overpressure, Underpressure/Vacuum, Cyclic, Thermal, Surge.
- **Four pressure-vessel failure modes:** Brittle fracture, Ductile rupture, Creep rupture, Fatigue.
- **ASME Section VIII (Div. 1)** is the governing code for unfired pressure vessels in India and globally.
- **PSV components** to draw in exams: Inlet, Disc, Spring, Bonnet, Lift lever, Adjustment screw, Outlet.
- **Engineering utility:** Pressure hazard analysis is mandatory in refineries, LNG plants, ammonia plants, autoclaves, and boiler systems.
- **Historical disasters to cite** for context: **Oppau (1921)**, **Texas City (1947)**, **Flixborough (1974)** — all pressure-related.
- **Convert all units to SI (Pa, m, K, kg) before substituting** in numericals. KTU answer sheets have a "unit consistency" check by the evaluator.
- **Always include a free body diagram** in any stress derivation — 2–3 marks are reserved for it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Causes for Overpressures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT616 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Causes for Overpressures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Overpressure** is defined as any pressure increase within a process vessel, pipe, or enclosed equipment that exceeds its **Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)** as stamped on the equipment nameplate, thereby creating a structural integrity hazard and a potential loss-of-containment scenario.

In the context of **Chemical Process Safety**, overpressure is the single most common initiating event for catastrophic vessel rupture, BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion), and toxic/unconfined vapor cloud releases. The recognition of overpressure causes is governed under **OSHA 1910.119 (Process Safety Management – PSM)** and **API RP 521 (Pressure-relieving and Depressuring Systems)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The "Balloon in a Closed Box" Model**
>
> Imagine a sealed pressure cooker on a stove. The pressure cooker is designed to operate safely at **1 bar (gauge)**. Now, picture what happens if:
> - Someone turns the burner to maximum (thermal energy input) → steam generation accelerates.
> - A blocked outlet (valve failure) prevents vapor from escaping.
> - A chemical reaction inside starts generating its own heat (exothermic runaway).
>
> In every case, **molecules inside the cooker are forced into a fixed volume with increasing energy**. The result? The internal pressure skyrockets until either the safety valve (designed relief device) opens, or the cooker explodes. The chemical process industry deals with this exact phenomenon at industrial scale — but with **tons of flammable, toxic, or reactive chemicals**.

### 1.3 Why "Causes for Overpressures" is a High-Priority KTU Topic

KTU examiners weight this topic heavily because it forms the **conceptual foundation for two downstream modules**:
1. **Relief Device Sizing & Design** (Module 2) — You cannot size a PSV (Pressure Safety Valve) without first knowing *what* scenario you are protecting against.
2. **Hazard Identification (HAZOP, What-If, FMEA)** — Overpressure is one of the most frequently listed "deviations" in HAZOP worksheets.
