### 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics Used

- **MAWP** — Maximum Allowable Working Pressure, in **bar (g)** or **psig**.
- **Set Pressure** — Pressure at which the relief device begins to open, typically **MAWP + 5% accumulation** (per ASME Section VIII).
- **Overpressure Accumulation** — Allowed transient pressure above MAWP: **10% for fire cases, 16% for non-fire cases** (per ASME / API 521).
- **Time to Maximum Pressure** — Critical for runaway reaction analysis, expressed in **seconds (s)** or **minutes (min)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Systematic Classification of Overpressure Causes

KTU 2024 expects students to identify and classify causes into **operational, mechanical, process, and external** categories. Below is the structured, board-exam-ready breakdown.

#### A. Operational / Process Causes

- **Thermal Expansion of Trapped Liquid** — Liquid trapped between two block valves expands when heated. Since liquids are nearly **incompressible** (Bulk Modulus ≈ 2.2 GPa for water), even a small temperature rise produces a massive pressure spike.
- **Outlet Valve Closure with Continued Pump-In** — Pump continues to deliver fluid into a blocked system. This is the classic "**deadhead pump**" failure.
- **Overfilling of a Vessel** — Filling beyond the design liquid level compresses the vapor space, raising pressure.
- **Loss of Cooling / Heat Removal** — Exothermic reactions continue without heat extraction → runaway.

#### B. Mechanical / Equipment Failure Causes

- **Failure of Pressure Control Loop** — Control valve (PCV) fails closed or air supply to the controller is lost.
- **Failure of Instruments** — Pressure transmitter failure giving false low reading.
- **Check Valve Leak or Failure** — Reverse flow from a high-pressure source into a low-pressure header.
- **Blocked Inlet/Outlet** — Mechanical obstruction, fouling, or polymerization plugging.

#### C. Chemical Reaction Causes

- **Runaway Exothermic Reaction** — Heat generation rate exceeds heat removal rate; temperature spirals → pressure via vapor pressure rise and gas generation.
- **Decomposition Reaction** — Unstable chemicals (e.g., organic peroxides, nitro compounds) decompose exothermically, often producing non-condensable gases.
- **Polymerization in Storage** — Reactive monomers (styrene, butadiene) polymerize if uninhibited, releasing heat.
- **Side Reaction with Contaminant** — Water ingress, oxygen ingress, or wrong-catalyst addition triggers unexpected exotherms.

#### D. External / Environmental Causes

- **External Fire Exposure** — The #1 design scenario per **API RP 521**. Fire heats vessel walls, vaporizes liquid contents, and rapidly raises internal pressure. Sizing is governed by:

$$Q = 21{,}000 \cdot F \cdot A^{0.82}$$

where $Q$ is the heat input in **W**, $F$ is the environment factor (**1.0** for total bare surface, **0.5** for insulated, etc.), and $A$ is the wetted surface area in **m²**.

- **Ambient Temperature Rise** — Sun exposure on uninsulated storage tanks (e.g., LPG bullets in tropical climates like Kerala).
- **Vapor Cloud Return / Implosion-Explosion Transition** — A vapor cloud outside equipment burns back, heating internal gases.

#### E. Utility Failure Causes

- **Loss of Instrument Air** — All pneumatically actuated control valves fail to their default (fail-closed) position.
- **Loss of Electrical Power** — Pumps, agitators, and instruments go down.
- **Loss of Cooling Water** — Heat exchangers lose effectiveness.
- **Loss of Steam** — Reboilers and trace-heating systems fail.

#### F. Operator Error

- **Manual Valve Misalignment** — Wrong valve sequence during startup/shutdown.
- **Charging Wrong Quantity or Wrong Material** — Especially in batch reactors.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|----------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{\beta \cdot \Delta T}{V}$ | $\beta$ = Bulk Modulus (Pa), $\Delta T$ = Temp rise (K), $V$ = Volume (m³) | Thermal expansion of trapped liquid |
| 2 | $Q = 21{,}000 \cdot F \cdot A^{0.82}$ | $Q$ in W, $A$ in m², $F$ dimensionless | External fire heat input (API 521) |
| 3 | $\tau_{ad} = \dfrac{\Delta T_{ad} \cdot C_p}{\Delta H \cdot r_a}$ | $\tau_{ad}$ = adiabatic time-to-runaway (s), $r_a$ = reaction rate | Runaway reaction screening |
| 4 | $P_{set} = MAWP \times (1 + x)$ | $x$ = 0.05 (set), 0.10 (fire), 0.16 (non-fire) | Relief device set pressure design |
| 5 | $W = \dfrac{A_v \cdot C_d \cdot P_1}{\sqrt{Z \cdot T_1 / M}}$ | $W$ = mass flow (kg/s), $C_d$ = discharge coefficient | PSV orifice sizing |
| 6 | $SARH = \dfrac{\Delta T_{ad, \text{max}}}{MTSR}$ | SARH = Self-Accelerating Decomposition Temp ratio | Reaction hazard screening |
| 7 | $Vapor Pressure (Antoine): \log_{10}(P) = A - \dfrac{B}{C+T}$ | $P$ in mmHg, $T$ in °C | Pressure rise via vapor pressure |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used by process safety engineers during **HAZOP**, **PSV sizing**, **relief load determination**, and **LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis)** studies. Mastery is essential for KTU's chemical engineering design courses and real-world plant safety audits.

### 2.3 Decision Logic for Identifying the "Worst-Credible" Case

In a real KTU problem or industrial HAZOP, you must choose the **single largest credible overpressure scenario** to size the relief device. The priority order is:

1. **External fire** (if vessel contains flammable above flash point).
2. **Runaway reaction** (if exotherm possible).
3. **Thermal expansion of trapped liquid** (always credible if block valves exist).
4. **Loss of utilities** (power, instrument air, cooling water).
5. **Operator error** (often combined with utility failure).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation #1 — Thermal Expansion Pressure of a Trapped Liquid

> **Problem:** A horizontal cylindrical process pipe of internal volume **0.085 m³** is filled with cyclohexane (Bulk Modulus $\beta = 1.1 \times 10^9$ Pa) and trapped between two closed block valves. The pipe is initially at **30 °C**. Due to a process upset, the pipe is exposed to the sun and the liquid temperature rises to **50 °C**. Determine the internal pressure rise, assuming the pipe walls are rigid (i.e., volume is fixed).

**Step 1 — Identify the governing physical law.**

For a liquid in a rigid, fixed-volume container, an isochoric (constant-volume) heating causes pressure to rise according to the isothermal bulk modulus relation. The pressure increase is given by:

$$\Delta P = \frac{\beta \cdot \Delta T}{V} \cdot \Delta V_{equivalent}$$

However, a more rigorous treatment uses the **volumetric thermal expansion coefficient** $\alpha_v$ along with the isothermal compressibility $\kappa_T$:

$$\Delta P = \frac{\alpha_v \cdot \Delta T}{\kappa_T}$$

Since $\kappa_T = 1/\beta$, the expression simplifies to:

$$\Delta P = \beta \cdot \alpha_v \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 2 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin difference.**

The temperature rise is the difference, so units of Celsius or Kelvin give the same magnitude:

$$\Delta T = 50 - 30 = 20 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the bulk modulus of cyclohexane.**

Given $\beta = 1.1 \times 10^9$ Pa, the pressure rise is calculated as:

$$\Delta P = (1.1 \times 10^{9} \text{ Pa}) \cdot \alpha_v \cdot (20 \text{ K})$$

For cyclohexane, $\alpha_v \approx 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ K⁻¹, so:

$$\Delta P = (1.1 \times 10^{9}) \cdot (1.2 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 20$$

$$\Delta P = (1.1 \times 10^{9}) \cdot (2.4 \times 10^{-2})$$

$$\Delta P = 2.64 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to bar for engineering readability.**

$$\Delta P = \frac{2.64 \times 10^{7}}{1.0 \times 10^{5}} = 264 \text{ bar}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** A modest 20 °C temperature rise in a rigid, liquid-filled pipe generates **~264 bar** of overpressure — far exceeding the yield strength of standard carbon steel piping (typically rated at 16–40 bar). This is why **thermal relief valves (TRVs)** are mandatory on all liquid lines with block valves.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation #2 — External Fire Heat Input (API RP 521)

> **Problem:** A vertical cylindrical storage tank contains a flammable solvent (e.g., acetone). The tank is uninsulated, with wetted surface area **A = 40 m²** exposed to a pool fire. Compute the heat input rate into the vessel contents.

**Step 1 — Recall the API RP 521 empirical correlation.**

The standard fire heat flux equation is:

$$Q = 21{,}000 \cdot F \cdot A^{0.82}$$

where $Q$ is the heat input in watts, $A$ is in m², and $F$ is a dimensionless factor.

**Step 2 — Determine the environment factor F.**

For a **bare, uninsulated** vessel, **F = 1.0** (per API 521 Table 1). For insulation that is rated for fire exposure, F can be as low as 0.15. We will use F = 1.0 for the worst case.

**Step 3 — Compute the wetted area term raised to the 0.82 power.**

$$A^{0.82} = (40)^{0.82}$$

Using logarithms:

$$\ln(40) = 3.689$$

$$0.82 \times \ln(40) = 0.82 \times 3.689 = 3.025$$

$$A^{0.82} = e^{3.025} = 18.60 \text{ m}^{1.64}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the API equation.**

$$Q = 21{,}000 \times 1.0 \times 18.60$$

$$Q = 390{,}600 \text{ W} \approx 390.6 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

The vessel receives nearly **391 kW** of fire heat input. If the relief device must reject this energy through latent heat of vaporization of acetone ($H_{vap} \approx 496$ kJ/kg), the required mass vent rate is:

$$\dot{m}_{vent} = \frac{Q}{H_{vap}} = \frac{390{,}600}{496{,}000} = 0.787 \text{ kg/s}$$

This is the **relief load** that the PSV must discharge to prevent vessel rupture.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation #3 — Runaway Reaction Time-to-Explosion (Simplified Semenov Model)

> **Problem:** A 2 m³ batch reactor contains a reactive mixture. The adiabatic temperature rise is $\Delta T_{ad} = 180$ °C, the heat capacity is $C_p = 3.5$ kJ/(kg·K), the heat of reaction is $\Delta H = -250$ kJ/kg, and the initial reaction rate constant gives a first-order rate parameter such that the characteristic reaction rate coefficient at the maximum temperature is $r_a = 0.05$ s⁻¹. Estimate the **adiabatic time to maximum rate (TMR_ad)**.

**Step 1 — Use the simplified adiabatic runaway criterion.**

The time to runaway is approximated as:

$$\tau_{ad} \approx \frac{\Delta T_{ad} \cdot C_p}{\Delta H \cdot r_a}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values with consistent units.**

We need $\Delta H$ in kJ/kg and $C_p$ in kJ/(kg·K), with $\Delta T_{ad}$ in K and $r_a$ in s⁻¹:

$$\tau_{ad} = \frac{(180 \text{ K}) \cdot (3.5 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)})}{(250 \text{ kJ/kg}) \cdot (0.05 \text{ s}^{-1})}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator computation:**

$$180 \times 3.5 = 630 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Denominator computation:**

$$250 \times 0.05 = 12.5 \text{ kJ/(kg·s)}$$

**Step 5 — Final division:**

$$\tau_{ad} = \frac{630}{12.5} = 50.4 \text{ s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Decision:** If $\tau_{ad} < 24$ hours, the system is considered **high-hazard**. With a TMR of ~50 seconds, this reaction is in the **explosively hazardous** category. Immediate design actions include: (1) emergency relief venting sized for runaway, (2) inhibitor addition systems, (3) quench systems, and (4) remote operator barriers.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Causes vs. Severity vs. Likelihood

| Cause | Typical Magnitude of $\Delta P$ | Likelihood in Plant | Detection Time | Relief Strategy |
|-------|--------------------------------|---------------------|----------------|-----------------|
| Thermal expansion (trapped liquid) | **100–500 bar** (extreme) | **High** (very common) | Hours (slow temp rise) | Small TRV on each block-valve section |
| External fire | **2–10 bar** above MAWP | Medium | Minutes | Large PSV sized per API 521 |
| Runaway reaction | **10–50 bar** (catastrophic) | Low (but high consequence) | Seconds to minutes | Emergency relief + inhibitor + quench |
| Pump deadhead | **= Pump shutoff head** (often 5–15 bar above MAWP) | Medium | Seconds | Pressure-controlled bypass loop |
| Outlets blocked | Depends on upstream source | Low | Minutes | Redundant outlet paths |
| Loss of utilities | Variable | Medium | Minutes | SIS (Safety Instrumented System) interlock |
| Operator error | Variable | **High** | Variable | Procedural controls + alarm management |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Hierarchical Classification of Overpressure Causes

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[OVERPRESSURE CAUSES] --> B[Operational Process]
    A --> C[Mechanical Equipment]
    A --> D[Chemical Reaction]
    A --> E[External Environmental]
    A --> F[Utility Failure]
    A --> G[Human Operator Error]

    B --> B1[Thermal Expansion of Trapped Liquid]
    B --> B2[Outlet Valve Closure with Pump Running]
    B --> B3[Vessel Overfilling]
    B --> B4[Loss of Cooling to Exotherm]

    C --> C1[Pressure Control Loop Failure]
    C --> C2[Check Valve Reverse Flow]
    C --> C3[Instrument Transmitter Failure]
    C --> C4[Plugged Line or Strainer]

    D --> D1[Runaway Exothermic Reaction]
    D --> D2[Decomposition of Unstable Chemical]
    D --> D3[Uninhibited Polymerization]
    D --> D4[Contaminant Induced Side Reaction]

    E --> E1[External Pool or Jet Fire]
    E --> E2[Ambient Sun Heating]
    E --> E3[Vapor Cloud Flashback]

    F --> F1[Loss of Instrument Air]
    F --> F2[Loss of Electrical Power]
    F --> F3[Loss of Cooling Water]
    F --> F4[Loss of Steam Heating]

    G --> G1[Manual Valve Misalignment]
    G --> G2[Wrong Material Charge]
    G --> G3[Startup Shutdown Mistake]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture: Overpressure Protection Layer

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN[Process Feed] --> PUMP[Pump or Compressor]
    PUMP --> VESSEL[Process Vessel or Column]
    VESSEL --> OUTLET[Outlet Block Valve]
    VESSEL --> VENT[Relief Device PSV PRV Rupture Disc]
    VENT --> SAFE[Safe Discharge to Flare or Quench]

    CTRL[Pressure Controller] -.->|Control Signal| VESSEL
    SIS[Safety Interlock System] -.->|Emergency Shutdown| PUMP
    TRV[Thermal Relief Valve] -.->|Local Protection| OUTLET

    HMI[Operator HMI Alarm] -.-> CTRL
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Flow for Identifying the Worst Credible Overpressure Scenario

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Start HAZOP Node Analysis] --> Q1{Vessel Contains Flammable Liquid Above Flash Point}
    Q1 -->|Yes| FIRE[Consider External Fire Case First]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Reaction Exothermic in Vessel}
    Q2 -->|Yes| RUN[Consider Runaway Reaction Case]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Trapped Liquid Between Two Block Valves}
    Q3 -->|Yes| THERMO[Consider Thermal Expansion Case]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Pump or Compressor In Service}
    Q4 -->|Yes| DEAD[Consider Deadhead or Blocked Outlet Case]
    Q4 -->|No| Q5{Utility Loss Credible}
    Q5 -->|Yes| UTIL[Consider Loss of Utilities Case]
    Q5 -->|No| OP[Consider Operator Error Scenarios]

    FIRE --> SIZE[Compute Required Relief Area]
    RUN --> SIZE
    THERMO --> SIZE
    DEAD --> SIZE
    UTIL --> SIZE
    OP --> SIZE
    SIZE --> END[Select Largest Relief Load as Design Basis]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1, Module 1]**
> **Q1.** Define overpressure. List any **four** major causes of overpressure in a chemical process plant.

**Model Answer:**
Overpressure is a condition in which the internal pressure of a process vessel exceeds its **Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)**, posing a risk of mechanical failure. Four major causes are:
1. External fire exposure heating the vessel.
2. Runaway exothermic chemical reaction inside the vessel.
3. Thermal expansion of a liquid trapped between closed block valves.
4. Pump deadheading or outlet valve closure with continued pumping.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2, Module 1]**
> **Q2.** Why is a **thermal relief valve (TRV)** mandatory on liquid pipelines fitted with two block valves?

**Model Answer:**
When liquid is trapped between two closed block valves, even a small temperature rise (e.g., 20–30 °C from ambient sun exposure) causes a **massive pressure increase** because liquids are nearly incompressible. For water, a 10 °C rise can generate over **100 bar** of pressure, exceeding the pipe rating. A TRV automatically opens at a set pressure to vent the expanding liquid, preventing pipe rupture.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern as per KTU 2024 ESE)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks, Module 1]**

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *(Choose A or B)*

**(a)** Classify the various causes of overpressure in chemical process equipment. Discuss in detail the external fire scenario as a design basis for relief device sizing, citing the relevant code equation. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A pipeline of 0.5 m³ internal volume is completely filled with benzene (Bulk Modulus $\beta = 1.05 \times 10^9$ Pa, volumetric expansion coefficient $\alpha_v = 1.24 \times 10^{-3}$ K⁻¹) and isolated between two block valves. If the temperature rises from 25 °C to 60 °C, determine: (i) the pressure rise in bar, and (ii) the minimum set pressure a TRV must have, assuming MAWP = 10 bar. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** With the help of a neat flowchart, describe the HAZOP-based decision logic used to identify the **worst-credible overpressure scenario** for relief valve sizing. Explain why thermal expansion of trapped liquid is always a mandatory check. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A batch reactor has an adiabatic temperature rise $\Delta T_{ad} = 200$ °C, $C_p = 3.2$ kJ/(kg·K), $\Delta H = -280$ kJ/kg, and reaction rate coefficient at maximum temperature $r_a = 0.04$ s⁻¹. (i) Calculate the adiabatic time to maximum rate $\tau_{ad}$. (ii) Comment on the runaway hazard level. (iii) List **two** engineering safeguards to mitigate the runaway. **[7 Marks]**

---

### 5.3 Model Solutions

#### Solution to Question A:

**(a) Classification and External Fire Sizing:**

The causes of overpressure are classified into:
- **Operational** (thermal expansion, overfilling, blocked outlet, loss of cooling).
- **Mechanical** (PCV failure, check valve failure, instrument failure).
- **Chemical** (runaway reaction, decomposition, polymerization, contamination).
- **External** (fire, sun, vapor cloud flashback).
- **Utility** (loss of air, power, cooling water, steam).
- **Human** (valve misalignment, wrong charge).

**External Fire Sizing (API RP 521 / API 2000):**
The empirical heat input equation is:

$$Q = 21{,}000 \cdot F \cdot A^{0.82}$$

This $Q$ (in watts) represents the rate at which fire energy enters the vessel contents. The relief device must be sized to vent the resulting vapor at a rate equal to $Q / H_{vap}$ to prevent the internal pressure from exceeding **MAWP × 1.10** (10% fire accumulation per ASME).

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $V = 0.5$ m³ (volume is informational; we use bulk modulus method), $\beta = 1.05 \times 10^9$ Pa, $\alpha_v = 1.24 \times 10^{-3}$ K⁻¹, $\Delta T = 60 - 25 = 35$ K, MAWP = 10 bar.

**(i) Pressure rise:**

$$\Delta P = \beta \cdot \alpha_v \cdot \Delta T$$

$$\Delta P = (1.05 \times 10^9) \cdot (1.24 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (35)$$

$$\Delta P = (1.05 \times 10^9) \cdot (4.34 \times 10^{-2})$$

$$\Delta P = 4.557 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$$

Converting to bar:

$$\Delta P = \frac{4.557 \times 10^7}{1.0 \times 10^5} = 455.7 \text{ bar}$$

**(ii) Minimum TRV set pressure:**
The TRV should be set at or just above MAWP, typically **MAWP × 1.05** per ASME accumulation allowance:

$$P_{set, \min} = 10 \text{ bar} \times 1.05 = 10.5 \text{ bar}$$

(Note: The $\Delta P$ calculation shows the *enormous* pressure generated if NO TRV is present — hence the TRV is essential.)

---

#### Solution to Question B:

**(a) HAZOP Decision Logic:**
See the Mermaid flowchart in Section 4.3. The standard hierarchy for selecting the worst-credible case is:
1. Fire case (if flammable above flash point).
2. Runaway reaction case (if exotherm possible).
3. Thermal expansion of trapped liquid (always mandatory — it is a *ubiquitous* scenario wherever block valves exist).
4. Blocked outlet / pump deadhead.
5. Utility failure.
6. Operator error.

Thermal expansion is **always** checked because it is independent of chemistry — any liquid in a constrained volume will expand when heated, regardless of whether the process is reactive or inert. The only mitigation is a TRV, and sizing it correctly requires the calculation we performed in Section 3.1.

**(b) Runaway Reaction Calculation:**

**(i) Adiabatic time to maximum rate:**

$$\tau_{ad} = \frac{\Delta T_{ad} \cdot C_p}{|\Delta H| \cdot r_a}$$

$$\tau_{ad} = \frac{(200) \cdot (3.2)}{(280) \cdot (0.04)}$$

$$\tau_{ad} = \frac{640}{11.2} = 57.14 \text{ seconds}$$

**(ii) Hazard Classification:**
Since $\tau_{ad} \approx 57$ s **< 24 hours**, the reaction is classified as **HIGHLY HAZARDOUS** per the **Stoessel Fauske criterion**. A runaway could escalate to explosion within one minute — far faster than human intervention can respond.

**(iii) Two Engineering Safeguards:**
1. **Emergency relief venting** sized for runaway mass-flow rate using the **DIERS (Design Institute for Emergency Relief Systems)** methodology (two-phase flow venting).
2. **Inhibitor injection system** with automatic triggering on a high-temperature interlock (e.g., inject a polymerization inhibitor if T > 80 °C).
3. *(Optional third)* **Quench system** — dump emergency cooling solvent or use bottom-drain rapid cooling to absorb exotherm.

---

### 5.4 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in $\Delta P$ calculation** — Students frequently write the answer in Pa without converting to bar. Always report pressure in **bar(g)** for KTU board exams.
> 2. **Wrong MAWP accumulation factor** — Fire case = 10%, Non-fire case = 16%, Set pressure = 5%. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Not justifying the choice of scenario** — When asked "which case sizes the relief device?", students must explicitly state **which scenario gives the largest required relief area** and justify it with numbers.
> 4. **Missing the HAZOP linkage** — KTU 2024 scheme rewards answers that connect overpressure causes to **HAZOP deviation keywords** ("more pressure", "less flow", "no flow"). Use HAZOP terminology to score full marks.
> 5. **Ignoring utility failures** — A common omission. If instrument air fails, ALL pneumatic valves go to fail-closed. This can deadhead pumps. Always list it.

---

### 5.5 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Overpressure** = pressure exceeding **MAWP**; caused by **operational, mechanical, chemical, external, utility, or human** factors.
- **Bulk Modulus × Volumetric Expansion × Temperature Rise** governs trapped-liquid overpressure — small $\Delta T$ gives huge $\Delta P$.
- **API RP 521 fire equation** $Q = 21{,}000 \cdot F \cdot A^{0.82}$ is the **standard** design basis for fire relief sizing.
- **Runaway reactions** with $\tau_{ad} < 24$ h are highly hazardous; use **DIERS methodology** for two-phase vent sizing.
- **Accumulation allowances** (ASME): Set pressure = MAWP + 5%; Fire = MAWP + 10%; Non-fire = MAWP + 16%.
- **TRVs** are mandatory on every block-valve-isolated liquid section — no exceptions.
- **Worst-credible scenario** is selected by computing relief loads for ALL credible cases and picking the **largest** one.
- **HAZOP keywords** for overpressure: *"more pressure," "no flow," "less flow," "reverse flow," "more temperature."*
- **Loss of utilities** is often the **most under-rated** yet **most frequent** initiator in real plant incident databases.
- **Operator error** is statistically the most common root cause — HAZOP and LOPA must explicitly address it.
- **Codes to know**: OSHA 1910.119 (PSM), API RP 521 (relief), ASME Section VIII (vessel), DIERS (two-phase vent), IEC 61511 (SIS).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Deviations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT616 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flow Deviations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Chemical Process Safety (CPS)**, a **Flow Deviation** is defined as any abnormal variation—either intentional or accidental—in the volumetric or mass flow rate of a process stream (liquid, gas, slurry, or multiphase) outside the designer's specified safe operating limits. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, flow deviations form one of the cardinal process *upset conditions* that act as root causes for loss of containment, runaway reactions, thermal shocks, and equipment mechanical failure.

Mathematically, a flow deviation is quantified as:

$$
\Delta F = F_{actual} - F_{design}
$$

where $\Delta F$ is the deviation magnitude, $F_{actual}$ is the measured instantaneous flow, and $F_{design}$ is the engineered design flow. A process hazard is triggered whenever:

$$
\vert \Delta F \vert \; > \; F_{design} \pm \epsilon
$$

Here, $\epsilon$ is the **tolerable engineering tolerance** (typically **±5 % to ±10 %** for critical services).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Flow deviations are studied under *Process Upsets and Hazards* and are directly linked to HAZOP (Hazard and Operability) guide words such as **NO/NONE, LESS, MORE,** and **REVERSE**.

## 1.2 The Four Canonical Flow Deviations

| # | Deviation Type | Guide Word (HAZOP) | Physical Meaning |
|---|----------------|--------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **No Flow** | NO / NONE | Total cessation of material transfer |
| 2 | **Low Flow** | LESS | Flow rate below design minimum |
| 3 | **High Flow** | MORE | Flow rate above design maximum |
| 4 | **Reverse Flow** | REVERSE | Backward movement against intended direction |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Garden Hose" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine a public water supply system feeding your home.
> - **No Flow** = A municipal pump failure (you open the tap but nothing comes out → your boiler could overheat).
> - **Low Flow** = A partially clogged pipe at the municipal end (weak trickle → poor cooling in your geyser).
> - **High Flow** = A burst in the main supply line (sudden gush → your pipes could burst).
> - **Reverse Flow** = A sudden negative pressure event (contaminated water from a sewer line is siphoned back into your clean line → contamination hazard).
>
> A chemical plant behaves identically, except the "water" is often a reactive monomer, a cryogenic liquefied gas, or a high-pressure hydrocarbon—and the "house" is a multi-million-dollar reactor.

## 1.4 Why Flow Deviations are Catastrophic in CPI

- **Reactor runaway:** Loss of cooling water flow = exotherm goes unchecked.
- **Pump cavitation:** Low NPSH available at suction.
- **Overpressure:** High flow into a blocked outlet (dead-head condition).
- **Cross-contamination:** Reverse flow between incompatible chemicals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme considers flow deviations as a **Level-1 Independent Protection Layer (IPL)** in the LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis) framework. A single undetected high-flow deviation can defeat multiple downstream safeguards.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Flow Deviations — Detailed Analysis

### 2.1.1 NO FLOW (Total Loss of Flow)
- **Root Causes:** Pump failure, power blackout, motor trip, broken shaft, valve closure (manual or automatic), line rupture with complete loss of suction head, plugged strainer, vapor lock in liquid lines, gas breakthrough in vertical risers, two-phase flow slugging.
- **Engineering Consequence:** Loss of feed to reactor → temperature excursion; loss of coolant → thermal runaway; loss of reflux → column flooding from below.
- **Detection:** Rotameter float drop, low-flow switch, paddle-wheel flow switch, differential pressure (dP) cell reading zero, pump motor ammeter showing underload.

### 2.1.2 LOW FLOW (Reduced Flow)
- **Root Causes:** Partial pump degradation (worn impeller), partially closed control valve, fouled heat exchanger (increased dP), undersized spare pump, viscosity increase (cold ambient), air entrainment in suction line.
- **Engineering Consequence:** Inadequate heat removal, residence time increase (side reactions), catalyst deactivation, decreased production.
- **Detection:** Sustained low reading on flow transmitter; rise in downstream temperature; decrease in pump discharge pressure.

### 2.1.3 HIGH FLOW (Excess Flow)
- **Root Causes:** Control valve failure (stuck open), overspeed of centrifugal pump, hydraulic surge (water hammer), upstream pressure surge, incorrect manual valve setting, pump dead-heading into low-resistance bypass.
- **Engineering Consequence:** Erosion of piping elbows, mechanical overload on equipment, hydraulic shock, exceedance of equipment design pressure, entrainment of liquids in vapor lines.
- **Detection:** High-high flow alarm, exceeding the design flow per ASME B31.3 velocity limits (typically **≤ 3 m/s for liquids, ≤ 60 m/s for vapors**).

### 2.1.4 REVERSE FLOW (Backflow)
- **Root Causes:** Pump shutdown while downstream pressure is high, check-valve failure, siphon effect, thermal contraction creating vacuum, sudden depressurization upstream, common header between high- and low-pressure systems.
- **Engineering Consequence:** Contamination, over-pressurization of low-pressure vessels, hydraulic shock, flame flashback in fired heaters, chemical incompatibility reactions.
- **Detection:** Reverse rotation indicator on pump, check-valve leak detection, flow transmitter showing negative reading, sudden pressure inversion across equipment.

## 2.2 Governing Physics of Flow in a Process Plant

The flow behavior of any process stream is governed by four interlinked principles:

1. **Conservation of Mass (Continuity Equation)**
2. **Conservation of Momentum (Bernoulli / Navier-Stokes)**
3. **Energy Balance (First Law)**
4. **State Equations (Ideal Gas, Steam Tables, EOS)**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are examinable in **Part A (3 marks)** as direct one-step substitutions and in **Part B (14 marks)** as multi-step derivations.

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units |
|---|---------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Volumetric Flow Rate | $Q = A \cdot v$ | $Q$: m³/s, $A$: m², $v$: m/s |
| 2 | Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v = \rho \cdot Q$ | $\dot{m}$: kg/s, $\rho$: kg/m³ |
| 3 | Continuity Equation (incompressible) | $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ | Steady-state, $\rho =$ const |
| 4 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \dfrac{v D}{\nu}$ | Dimensionless; $\mu$: Pa·s, $\nu$: m²/s |
| 5 | Flow Regime Boundary | $Re < 2300$ → Laminar, $Re > 4000$ → Turbulent | Transition zone: $2300 \le Re \le 4000$ |
| 6 | Hagen–Poiseuille (Laminar dP) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^4}$ | $L$: m, $D$: m, $Q$: m³/s |
| 7 | Darcy–Weisbach (Turbulent dP) | $\Delta P = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | $f$: Darcy friction factor |
| 8 | Bernoulli (ideal, incompressible) | $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2$ | Total head conserved |
| 9 | Mechanical Energy Balance | $\dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho} + \dfrac{\Delta v^2}{2} + g \Delta z + h_f = W_s$ | $h_f$: friction loss, $W_s$: pump work |
| 10 | Orifice Plate Flow | $Q = C_d A_o \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | $C_d$: discharge coefficient $\approx 0.6$ to $0.65$ |
| 11 | Venturi Meter | $Q = C_v A_t \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho \, (1-\beta^4)}}$ | $\beta = D_o/D_t$ |
| 12 | NPSH Available | $NPSH_A = P_{suction}/\rho g - P_{vapor}/\rho g \pm \Delta z$ | Margin must exceed $NPSH_R$ |
| 13 | Deviation Index | $DI = \dfrac{\vert F_{actual} - F_{design} \vert}{F_{design}} \times 100 \; \%$ | Alarm trigger: $DI > 5$ to $10 \; \%$ |
| 14 | Water Hammer (Joukowsky) | $\Delta P = \rho \cdot a \cdot \Delta v$ | $a$: pressure wave speed (m/s) |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **HAZOP teams** use these formulas to determine the *deviation magnitude* that would cause a node to enter a hazardous state.
- **Process engineers** select orifice plates and venturis using Eqs. (10) and (11) for accurate flow measurement.
- **Reliability engineers** use the **Deviation Index (DI)** in Eq. (13) to set alarm trip points in the Distributed Control System (DCS).
- **Mechanical engineers** use the water-hammer equation (14) to size surge vessels and check pipe stress during sudden valve closure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU 14-mark problems, the examiner expects you to (a) state the governing equation, (b) state assumptions, (c) substitute numerical values with units, and (d) interpret the result in a one-line engineering sentence. Skipping the assumption statement is the most common cause of 1–2 mark deductions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Continuity Equation (Incompressible Flow)

The continuity equation is the mathematical expression of mass conservation for a flowing fluid. We derive it for a steady, one-dimensional, incompressible flow through a pipe of varying cross-section.

### Step 1 — Control Volume Definition
Consider a fluid element entering section 1 (cross-section $A_1$, velocity $v_1$) and leaving section 2 (cross-section $A_2$, velocity $v_2$) over a time interval $dt$.

### Step 2 — Mass Entering
The mass entering at section 1 in time $dt$ is:

$$
dm_1 = \rho_1 \cdot A_1 \cdot v_1 \cdot dt
$$

### Step 3 — Mass Leaving
The mass leaving at section 2 in time $dt$ is:

$$
dm_2 = \rho_2 \cdot A_2 \cdot v_2 \cdot dt
$$

### Step 4 — Mass Conservation
For steady-state, no mass accumulates inside the control volume, so $dm_1 = dm_2$:

$$
\rho_1 A_1 v_1 = \rho_2 A_2 v_2
$$

### Step 5 — Incompressibility Assumption
If the fluid is incompressible, $\rho_1 = \rho_2 = \rho$ (constant), which simplifies to:

$$
\boxed{\;A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2\;}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** When the pipe cross-section decreases, velocity *must* increase. This explains why a choked flow or partially closed valve causes a **HIGH FLOW** deviation in the narrow section (high velocity) and a **LOW FLOW** deviation downstream if backpressure limits the throughput.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Orifice Plate Flow Equation

The orifice plate is the most common flow-measuring device in CPI and is directly related to detecting flow deviations.

### Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation
Between the upstream tap (point 1) and the vena contracta (point 2), assuming no friction loss and incompressible flow:

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho} + \frac{v_1^2}{2} = \frac{P_2}{\rho} + \frac{v_2^2}{2}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Continuity
For an incompressible fluid passing through the orifice area $A_o$ and the pipe area $A_p$:

$$
A_p v_1 = A_o v_2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; v_1 = \frac{A_o}{A_p} v_2
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute and Solve for v₂
Substitute $v_1$ into Bernoulli:

$$
\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho} = \frac{v_2^2 - v_1^2}{2} = \frac{v_2^2}{2}\left(1 - \frac{A_o^2}{A_p^2}\right)
$$

Solving for $v_2$:

$$
v_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \left(1 - \left(\dfrac{A_o}{A_p}\right)^2\right)}}
$$

### Step 4 — Introduce Discharge Coefficient
Real orifices lose energy to viscous effects and turbulence. We introduce the dimensionless discharge coefficient $C_d$ (typically **0.60 to 0.65** for standard concentric orifice):

$$
v_2 = C_d \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \left(1 - \beta^4\right)}}
$$

where $\beta = D_o/D_p$ is the diameter ratio.

### Step 5 — Final Volumetric Flow Rate

$$
\boxed{\;Q = C_d \, A_o \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho \left(1 - \beta^4\right)}}\;}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The flow is proportional to the square root of the measured differential pressure $\Delta P$. A *50 %* drop in $\Delta P$ corresponds to only a *30 %* drop in flow—**hence orifice plates are poor for low-flow deviation detection without square-root extraction in the DCS**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A process stream of density **920 kg/m³** and viscosity **0.005 Pa·s** flows through a DN100 schedule-40 pipe (internal diameter **102.3 mm**) at a volumetric flow rate of **50 m³/h**. Calculate the Reynolds number and identify the flow regime. If the operator reports that the actual flow has dropped to **30 m³/h**, determine the new Reynolds number and the percentage flow deviation.

### Solution — Part (a): Nominal Reynolds Number

**Step 1:** Convert units to SI.

$$
Q = \frac{50}{3600} = 0.01389 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}, \quad D = 0.1023 \; \text{m}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute cross-sectional area.

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.1023)^2}{4} = 8.218 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3:** Compute velocity.

$$
v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.01389}{8.218 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.690 \; \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into Reynolds number.

$$
Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{(920)(1.690)(0.1023)}{0.005} = 31{,}820
$$

**Step 5:** Interpret the result.

$$
Re = 31{,}820 \; > \; 4000 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \textbf{Turbulent flow}
$$

### Solution — Part (b): Deviation at Reduced Flow

**Step 1:** New velocity.

$$
v_{new} = \frac{30/3600}{8.218 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.014 \; \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2:** New Reynolds number.

$$
Re_{new} = \frac{(920)(1.014)(0.1023)}{0.005} = 19{,}090
$$

**Step 3:** Deviation Index.

$$
DI = \frac{\vert 30 - 50 \vert}{50} \times 100 \; \% = 40 \; \% \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \textbf{LOW FLOW deviation, well beyond alarm threshold}
$$

**Step 4:** Engineering conclusion.

$$
\text{Although still turbulent, the } 40 \; \% \text{ flow drop is a confirmed low-flow deviation requiring operator intervention.}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Causes × Consequences × Safeguards

| Deviation | Primary Cause | Worst Consequence | Engineered Safeguard (IPL) |
|-----------|---------------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| **No Flow** | Pump trip, line break | Reactor runaway | Low-low flow interlock → emergency shutdown (ESD) |
| **Low Flow** | Fouled exchanger | Loss of cooling | Flow alarm + redundant flow transmitter |
| **High Flow** | Valve stuck open | Pipe rupture, erosion | High-high flow alarm + slam-shut valve |
| **Reverse Flow** | Check-valve failure | Cross-contamination, flashback | Double block & bleed, reverse-rotation sensor |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Flow Deviations

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Flow Deviations in CPI] --> B[No Flow]
    A --> C[Low Flow]
    A --> D[High Flow]
    A --> E[Reverse Flow]

    B --> B1[Pump failure]
    B --> B2[Line rupture]
    B --> B3[Plugged strainer]

    C --> C1[Fouled heat exchanger]
    C --> C2[Control valve throttling]
    C --> C3[Increased viscosity]

    D --> D1[Valve stuck open]
    D --> D2[Hydraulic surge]
    D --> D3[Overspeed pump]

    E --> E1[Check valve failure]
    E --> E2[Siphon effect]
    E --> E3[Thermal contraction]

    B1 --> X1[Reactor runaway]
    C1 --> X2[Loss of cooling]
    D1 --> X3[Pipe erosion]
    E1 --> X4[Cross contamination]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Detection and Control Loop

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Process Pipe] --> P2[Orifice Plate]
    P2 --> P3[Differential Pressure Transmitter]
    P3 --> P4[Signal Conditioner with Square Root Extractor]
    P4 --> P5[DCS Flow Controller]
    P5 --> P6[Alarm Comparator]
    P6 --> P7[High Alarm]
    P6 --> P8[Low Alarm]
    P6 --> P9[High High Trip]
    P6 --> P10[Low Low Trip]
    P9 --> P11[Shutdown Valve SOV]
    P10 --> P11
    P11 --> P12[Safe State Isolated Equipment]
    P5 --> P13[Control Valve Final Element]
    P13 --> P1
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Flow Deviation Decision Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[Start HAZOP Node] --> S1[Measure F_actual]
    S1 --> S2{F_actual = 0?}
    S2 -->|Yes| S3[NO FLOW deviation]
    S2 -->|No| S4{F_actual less than F_min?}
    S4 -->|Yes| S5[LOW FLOW deviation]
    S4 -->|No| S6{F_actual greater than F_max?}
    S6 -->|Yes| S7[HIGH FLOW deviation]
    S6 -->|No| S8{Direction opposite?}
    S8 -->|Yes| S9[REVERSE FLOW deviation]
    S8 -->|No| S10[Normal Operation]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define flow deviation. List the four standard HAZOP guide words used to describe flow deviations in a chemical process plant.**

**Model Answer:**
A *flow deviation* is any deviation of the actual volumetric or mass flow rate of a process stream from its designed safe operating value, expressed as $\Delta F = F_{actual} - F_{design}$. It is one of the key process upsets considered in HAZOP studies.

The four standard HAZOP guide words for flow deviations are:

1. **NO FLOW** — total cessation of flow.
2. **LESS (LOW) FLOW** — flow below design minimum.
3. **MORE (HIGH) FLOW** — flow above design maximum.
4. **REVERSE FLOW** — flow opposite to intended direction.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner's note: Writing all four guide words is mandatory; missing one costs 1 mark.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain the meaning of NPSH and state its relevance in detecting low-flow deviation in a centrifugal pump.**

**Model Answer:**
*NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head)* is the total head available at the pump suction port above the vapor pressure of the liquid, expressed in meters of liquid column. It is given by:

$$
NPSH_A = \frac{P_{suction}}{\rho g} - \frac{P_{vapor}}{\rho g} \pm \Delta z
$$

**Relevance to low-flow detection:** When the suction-side flow drops, the static pressure at the pump suction falls (Bernoulli effect). If $NPSH_A$ falls below the manufacturer's required $NPSH_R$, the liquid vaporizes locally, forming vapor bubbles. These collapse in the high-pressure region of the impeller—a phenomenon called **cavitation**. Cavitation manifests as vibration, noise, and loss of pump head, and is therefore an *indirect but reliable indicator* of low-flow deviation at the pump suction.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE 2024 Scheme pattern: **Answer ONE full question from the choice. Each sub-part carries 7 marks. Total = 14 marks.**

---

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the four types of flow deviations encountered in a chemical process plant. For each type, give **one realistic cause** and **one engineered safeguard**.

**(b)** A pipeline of internal diameter **150 mm** carries an organic liquid of density **880 kg/m³** and viscosity **0.004 Pa·s** at a flow rate of **90 m³/h**. Compute the Reynolds number and state the flow regime. If the flow is observed to drop to **60 m³/h**, calculate the percentage deviation in flow rate.

---

#### Solution to (a)

The four flow deviations, with sketch representation (described verbally since drawing is permitted on answer sheet):

- **No Flow:** Pipe section shown with a *closed* isolation valve; cause: pump trip; safeguard: low-low flow interlock coupled to ESD.
- **Low Flow:** Pipe section with a *partially closed* control valve; cause: fouled heat exchanger increasing backpressure; safeguard: low-flow alarm at 90 % of design.
- **High Flow:** Pipe section with a *fully open* stuck control valve; cause: hydraulic surge on pump start; safeguard: high-high flow trip and slam-shut valve.
- **Reverse Flow:** Pipe section with a *failed check valve*; cause: sudden pump shutdown while downstream is pressurized; safeguard: double block-and-bleed isolation.

Each cause + safeguard pair = 1.5 marks × 4 = 6 marks, plus 1 mark for the sketch = **7 marks**.

---

#### Solution to (b)

**Step 1:** Convert units.

$$
Q = \frac{90}{3600} = 0.025 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}, \quad D = 0.150 \; \text{m}
$$

**Step 2:** Cross-sectional area.

$$
A = \frac{\pi (0.150)^2}{4} = 1.767 \times 10^{-2} \; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3:** Velocity at design.

$$
v_1 = \frac{0.025}{1.767 \times 10^{-2}} = 1.415 \; \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 4:** Reynolds number.

$$
Re_1 = \frac{(880)(1.415)(0.150)}{0.004} = 46{,}700
$$

**Step 5:** Flow regime.

$$
Re_1 = 46{,}700 \; > \; 4000 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \textbf{Turbulent flow}
$$

**Step 6:** New velocity at 60 m³/h.

$$
Q_{new} = \frac{60}{3600} = 0.01667 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
v_2 = \frac{0.01667}{1.767 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.943 \; \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 7:** Percentage deviation.

$$
DI = \frac{\vert 60 - 90 \vert}{90} \times 100 \; \% = 33.3 \; \% \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \textbf{Low-flow deviation}
$$

**Conclusion:** The flow has deviated by 33.3 %, far exceeding typical ±5–10 % tolerance, warranting an alarm and corrective action. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the volumetric flow equation for an orifice meter starting from Bernoulli's equation and the continuity equation. Clearly state all assumptions.

**(b)** An orifice meter installed in a **75 mm** (bore) pipe has an orifice diameter of **40 mm**. The differential pressure across the orifice is measured as **25 kPa** for an oil of density **860 kg/m³** and viscosity **0.003 Pa·s**. The discharge coefficient is **0.62**. Calculate (i) the volumetric flow rate in **m³/h**, and (ii) the Reynolds number at the pipe. State the flow regime.

---

#### Solution to (a)

Refer to **Section 3.2** of these notes for the complete derivation. Key points for the answer sheet (each key point = 1 mark approximately):

1. State Bernoulli's equation between upstream and vena contracta.
2. State continuity equation $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$.
3. Eliminate $v_1$ in favour of $v_2$.
4. Solve for $v_2 = \sqrt{2\Delta P \,/\, [\rho (1 - \beta^4)]}$.
5. Introduce discharge coefficient $C_d$ to account for real losses.
6. Write final $Q = C_d A_o \sqrt{2\Delta P \,/\, [\rho (1 - \beta^4)]}$.

**Assumptions to explicitly state:**
- Steady, incompressible flow.
- No friction loss between taps.
- Uniform velocity profile at both sections.
- $\beta$ ratio lies between 0.2 and 0.7 (standard range).

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to (b)

**Step 1:** Given data in SI units.

$$
D_p = 0.075 \; \text{m}, \quad D_o = 0.040 \; \text{m}, \quad \Delta P = 25{,}000 \; \text{Pa}
$$

$$
\rho = 860 \; \text{kg/m}^3, \quad \mu = 0.003 \; \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}, \quad C_d = 0.62
$$

**Step 2:** Compute diameter ratio.

$$
\beta = \frac{D_o}{D_p} = \frac{0.040}{0.075} = 0.5333
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $(1 - \beta^4)$.

$$
\beta^4 = (0.5333)^4 = 0.0809
$$

$$
1 - \beta^4 = 0.9191
$$

**Step 4:** Compute orifice area.

$$
A_o = \frac{\pi (0.040)^2}{4} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 5:** Compute volumetric flow rate.

$$
Q = (0.62)(1.2566 \times 10^{-3}) \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 25{,}000}{860 \times 0.9191}}
$$

$$
Q = 7.791 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{63.27} = 7.791 \times 10^{-4} \times 7.954
$$

$$
Q = 6.197 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 6:** Convert to m³/h.

$$
Q = 6.197 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = 22.31 \; \text{m}^3/\text{h}
$$

**Step 7:** Compute velocity and Reynolds number.

$$
A_p = \frac{\pi (0.075)^2}{4} = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^2
$$

$$
v = \frac{Q}{A_p} = \frac{6.197 \times 10^{-3}}{4.418 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.403 \; \text{m/s}
$$

$$
Re = \frac{\rho v D_p}{\mu} = \frac{(860)(1.403)(0.075)}{0.003} = 30{,}170
$$

**Step 8:** Flow regime.

$$
Re = 30{,}170 \; > \; 4000 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \textbf{Turbulent flow}
$$

**(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions:** Flow rates in m³/h must be converted to m³/s before substituting into SI formulas. A typical 1–2 mark deduction.
> 2. **Mixing diameters:** Using the *pipe* diameter in the orifice area formula or the *orifice* diameter in the Reynolds number formula. Read the question twice.
> 3. **Skipping the assumption statement:** Every derivation must list its assumptions (steady flow, incompressible, no friction, etc.). Missing this loses 1 mark consistently.
> 4. **No engineering interpretation:** The final line of every numerical problem *must* interpret the result ("this is a high-flow deviation requiring alarm action"). The KTU answer key reserves 0.5–1 mark for this.
> 5. **Confusing $C_d$ with $C_v$:** $C_d$ is the *discharge* coefficient for an orifice plate ($\approx$ 0.6–0.65); $C_v$ is the *valve flow* coefficient. Do not interchange.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Flow deviation = any variation of $F_{actual}$ from $F_{design}$ outside the tolerance band.
- **Four types:** No flow, Low flow, High flow, Reverse flow — directly mapped to HAZOP guide words *NO, LESS, MORE, REVERSE*.
- **Key formula (continuity):** $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ for incompressible fluids.
- **Key formula (Reynolds):** $Re = \rho v D / \mu$; laminar if $Re < 2300$, turbulent if $Re > 4000$.
- **Key formula (orifice):** $Q = C_d A_o \sqrt{2 \Delta P / [\rho (1-\beta^4)]}$.
- **Deviation Index:** $DI = \vert F_{actual} - F_{design} \vert / F_{design} \times 100 \; \%$; typical alarm threshold 5–10 %.
- **NPSH check** is essential for any low-flow scenario at a pump suction.
- **Detection devices:** Orifice plate + dP cell, venturi, rotameter, magnetic flow meter, Coriolis meter, vortex shedding meter, ultrasonic flow meter.
- **IPLs (Independent Protection Layers):** Alarms, interlocks, check valves, slam-shut valves, ESD systems, relief valves.
- **Always convert units to SI** before substituting into formulas.
- **Always state the engineering interpretation** of the numerical answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effects of Leakages/Releases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT616 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Effects of Leakages/Releases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Chemical Process Safety (KTU 2024 Scheme), a **release** is defined as the unintended or uncontrolled escape of a hazardous material (flammable, toxic, or reactive) from its containment system (pipeline, vessel, tank, reactor) into the surrounding environment. A **leakage** is a specific, generally low-rate form of release that occurs through small openings, cracks, gaskets, valves, or corrosion-induced pinholes. The **effects of leakages/releases** refer to the downstream physical, chemical, thermal, and toxicological consequences that propagate from the source point into the workplace, community, and ecosystem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Direct Recall Type):**
> A *release* is the loss of containment of a hazardous material. Its *effect* depends on the material's hazardous properties (flammable / toxic / reactive), the state of release (gas, liquid, two-phase), the release mechanism (continuous, instantaneous, transient), and the prevailing environmental conditions (wind speed, stability class, temperature, humidity).

### 1.2 Classification of Releases (KTU Board Exam Favourite)

Releases are classified along three independent axes:

| Axis | Categories |
| :--- | :--- |
| **By Rate / Duration** | Continuous (steady-state), Instantaneous (puff), Transient (time-varying) |
| **By Phase** | Gas, Liquid, Two-Phase (flashing, aerosol), Solid (dust) |
| **By Mechanism** | Pipeline rupture, Vessel rupture, Gasket leak, Pump seal failure, Relief valve discharge |

> [!NOTE]
> The **"type of release"** dictates which dispersion or consequence model is applied. KTU examiners often test this mapping.

### 1.3 The Four Principal "Effects" — The Big Picture

When a hazardous chemical escapes containment, exactly one (or several) of the following effects propagate in the atmosphere:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four "EF" Effects (Core KTU Concept)**
> 1. **E**nvironmental **D**ispersion — vapor cloud spreads, dilutes, and contaminates air.
> 2. **F**ire — pool fire, jet fire, flash fire, fireball.
> 3. **E**xplosion — vapor cloud explosion (VCE), BLEVE, confined explosion, dust explosion.
> 4. **T**oxic **E**xposure — lethal / irreversible / reversible harm to humans via inhalation or skin contact.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine a pressurized balloon (the vessel) filled with hot, smelly perfume (the hazardous chemical). When the balloon pops:

- The **perfume vapor** instantly spreads through the room — this is **dispersion**.
- If the room contains a candle, the vapor may catch fire — this is a **fire effect** (flash/back fire).
- If the vapor accumulates in a corner and a spark ignites it all at once, the room "thumps" — this is an **explosion effect** (VCE).
- If the perfume is a poison (chlorine, ammonia), even without fire, the people in the room may suffer lung damage — this is the **toxic effect**.

The **intensity and reach** of each effect depend on *how much* was released, *how fast* it was released, the *weather* outside, and the *terrain* around the plant.

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants (Board Exam Standard)

- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \; \text{kPa}$
- **Standard temperature:** $T_{std} = 298.15 \; \text{K}$ (or $25^\circ\text{C}$)
- **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- **Lower Flammability Limit (LFL)** and **Upper Flammability Limit (UFL)** — material-specific, expressed in **vol %**.
- **Immediately Dangerous to Life and Health (IDLH)** — toxicological threshold.
- **Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) / Threshold Limit Value (TLV)** — chronic exposure limits.
- **AEGL (Acute Exposure Guideline Level):** AEGL-1, AEGL-2, AEGL-3 (for 10 / 30 / 60 min exposures).
- **Probit variable** $Y$ — a dimensionless damage correlate used in probit equations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of "What Happens After a Release"

When a containment is breached, the following sequence of physical events unfolds. Each step is a *board-exam-highlight* concept.

**Step 1 — Source Characterization (How much, how fast?)**
The engineer first identifies the **release rate** $\dot{m}$ (kg/s) and the **release phase** (sub-cooled liquid, saturated liquid, two-phase, gas, vapor). The rate depends on the **driving pressure differential** $(\Delta P)$, the **discharge coefficient** $C_d$, and the **hole area** $A$.

**Step 2 — Flash / Rainout / Pool Formation (Phase Change)**
A pressurized liquid release **flashes** to vapor. The **flash fraction** $f_v$ is the mass fraction of liquid that instantly vaporizes due to depressurization. The remaining liquid either:
- **Rains out** as aerosol droplets that fall to the ground.
- Forms a **liquid pool** that spreads and evaporates.

**Step 3 — Atmospheric Dispersion (Vapor Cloud Travels)**
The vapor cloud is advected by the **mean wind speed** $u$ (m/s) and dispersed (diluted) by atmospheric turbulence. Concentration at any point $(x, y, z)$ is given by the **Gaussian Plume Model** (steady-state, continuous release) or the **Gaussian Puff Model** (instantaneous release).

**Step 4 — Outcome Selection (Fire / Explosion / Toxic)**
If the cloud concentration is between **LFL and UFL**, an ignition source causes a **flash fire** (non-damaging pressure) or a **VCE** (damaging overpressure). If the cloud contains a toxic material, harm occurs via **inhalation dose**.

**Step 5 — Damage Estimation (How bad is it?)**
Quantified by **probit equations** that translate overpressure, thermal radiation, or toxic dose into **percentage of population affected** (injury, fatality).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **directly testable** in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination). Memorize units and boundary conditions.

#### (A) Release Rate Formulas

| # | Formula | Description / Conditions |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\dot{m}_{gas} = C_d \, A \, P \sqrt{\dfrac{2M}{RT} \cdot \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}\left[\left(\dfrac{P_{atm}}{P}\right)^{2/\gamma} - \left(\dfrac{P_{atm}}{P}\right)^{(\gamma+1)/\gamma}\right]}$ | Compressible gas flow (choked / unchoked), $M$ = molar mass, $\gamma$ = $C_p/C_v$ |
| 2 | $\dot{m}_{liq} = C_d \, A \, \rho_l \sqrt{2 \, \Delta P / \rho_l}$ | Sub-cooled liquid (incompressible Bernoulli) |
| 3 | $\dot{m}_{2\phi} = C_d \, A \, \sqrt{2 \rho_v (P - P_{atm})}$ | Two-phase choked flashing flow |
| 4 | $f_v = C_p (T - T_b) / \Delta H_{vap}$ | Flash fraction (mass vaporized) |

#### (B) Gaussian Plume Dispersion (Continuous Release)

| # | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 5 | $\chi(x,y,z) = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{2\pi \, u \, \sigma_y \, \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \left[\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]$ | Ground-level centerline ($y=0$, $z=0$): $\chi = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\pi \, u \, \sigma_y \, \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$ |
| 6 | $\sigma_y = a \, x^b$ ; $\sigma_z = c \, x^d$ | Pasquill–Gifford dispersion coefficients (function of stability class A–F) |
| 7 | $H = h_s + \Delta h$ | Effective release height = stack height + plume rise |

> [!NOTE]
> In **markdown tables**, $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$ are written without vertical pipes. Always use $\vert \cdot \vert$ style if needed elsewhere.

#### (C) Gaussian Puff (Instantaneous Release)

| # | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 8 | $\chi(x,y,z,t) = \dfrac{Q^*}{(2\pi)^{3/2} \sigma_x \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-ut)^2}{2\sigma_x^2} - \dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2} - \dfrac{z^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$ | $Q^*$ = total mass released (kg) |

#### (D) Pool Evaporation

| # | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 9 | $\dot{m}_{evap} = \dfrac{M \, K \, A_{pool} \, P^{sat}(T)}{RT}$ | $K$ = mass-transfer coefficient, $P^{sat}$ = vapour pressure |
| 10 | $A_{pool} = \pi \, r_p^2$ ; $r_p$ grows as $r_p(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, \dot{m}_{release} \, t}{\pi \, \rho_l}}$ | Pool radius from continuous release |

#### (E) Fire Effects — Thermal Radiation

| # | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 11 | $q_r = \dfrac{\eta \, \dot{m} \, \Delta H_c}{4 \pi r^2}$ | Point-source radiation (fireball), $\eta$ = radiative fraction (typically 0.2–0.4) |
| 12 | $t_{fireball} = 0.45 \, M^{1/3}$ ; $D_{fireball} = 5.8 \, M^{1/3}$ | Fireball duration & diameter, $M$ in kg |
| 13 | $q_{pool}'' = \dfrac{\eta \, \dot{m}'' \Delta H_c}{1 + 4 (r/d) \tan\theta}$ | Pool fire radiation at distance $r$ |

#### (F) Explosion Effects — Overpressure

| # | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 14 | $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta_{eff} \, M \, \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}}$ | TNT equivalent mass, $E_{TNT} = 4680 \;\text{kJ/kg}$ |
| 15 | $Z = \dfrac{r}{M_{TNT}^{1/3}}$ | Scaled distance |

#### (G) Probit Equations for Damage

| # | Formula | Damage Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 16 | $Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln P_s$ (kPa) | Lethal overpressure |
| 17 | $Y = -39.83 + 3.0186 \ln(q^{4/3} t)$ ; $q$ in W/m², $t$ in s | Thermal lethality |
| 18 | $Y = a + b \ln(C^n t)$ | Toxic dose lethality (e.g., chlorine, ammonia) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion from Probit to % affected:** $P_{eff} = 50 \left[1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$. The value 5 on the probit scale corresponds to **50% fatality**.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These effects are quantitatively used in:
- **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** of petroleum refineries, LNG terminals, and ammonia plants.
- **Land-use planning** (setback distances for off-site receptors).
- **Emergency response planning** (evacuation radii, shelter-in-place).
- **Design of pressure relief systems** (API 520/521, API 2000).
- **Insurance loss-prevention engineering** (e.g., IOA, AIR Worldwide).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Compressible Gas Release Rate (Equation 1)

This is a KTU board-exam favourite (often asked for 7 marks).

**Assumptions:**
- Steady-state, isentropic expansion.
- Ideal gas behaviour.
- Discharge through an orifice of area $A$ with coefficient $C_d$.

**Step 1:** Mass flow through an orifice for compressible flow (from compressible fluid mechanics):

$$
\dot{m}_{gas} = C_d \, A \, P \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma \, M}{R T} \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{(\gamma + 1)/(\gamma - 1)}}
$$

This is the **choked flow** expression. Sonic conditions are reached at the throat when $P / P_{atm} \geq \left(\dfrac{\gamma+1}{2}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$. For air ($\gamma = 1.4$), this critical ratio is **1.893**.

**Step 2:** For $P / P_{atm} < 1.893$, flow is **subsonic** and the expression is:

$$
\dot{m}_{gas} = C_d \, A \, P \sqrt{\dfrac{2 M}{R T} \cdot \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}\left[\left(\dfrac{P_{atm}}{P}\right)^{2/\gamma} - \left(\dfrac{P_{atm}}{P}\right)^{(\gamma+1)/\gamma}\right]}
$$

**Step 3:** Reduce to **incompressible limit** for $P \approx P_{atm}$: the bracket tends to 1, and we recover:

$$
\dot{m}_{gas} \approx C_d \, A \, P_{atm} \sqrt{\dfrac{2 M \gamma}{R T (\gamma - 1)}} \approx C_d \, A \, \rho \, \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the critical pressure ratio matters:** A KTU favourite follow-up question is *"Why do relief valves need to be sized for choked flow?"* The answer: at high $P$, the flow becomes choked, and $\dot{m}$ no longer increases with $\Delta P$ — the valve reaches its maximum capacity.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Flash Fraction $f_v$ (Equation 4)

A sub-cooled liquid at temperature $T$ and pressure $P$ is released to atmospheric pressure $P_{atm}$.

**Step 1:** When pressure drops, the liquid temperature drops from $T$ to the atmospheric boiling point $T_b$.

**Step 2:** Sensible heat released: $Q_{sense} = m \, C_p (T - T_b)$.

**Step 3:** This sensible heat is consumed as latent heat to vaporize a fraction $f_v$ of the mass:

$$
f_v \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_{vap} = m \, C_p (T - T_b)
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $f_v$:

$$
f_v = \dfrac{C_p (T - T_b)}{\Delta H_{vap}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity:** $0 \le f_v \le 1$. If the RHS exceeds 1, the liquid has insufficient sensible heat to flash; an external heat flux is required (e.g., from a hot ground or pipe wall — this drives **BLEVE** in LPG tanks).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Gaussian Plume (Equation 5) — Centerline Concentration

The Gaussian Plume equation is **not derived from first principles in KTU** — instead, students are expected to know its **assumptions and key simplifications**.

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state, continuous release.
2. Constant mean wind speed $u$ in the $x$-direction.
3. Concentration is **Gaussian** in the crosswind ($y$) and vertical ($z$) directions.
4. Ground is a perfect reflector (no absorption).
5. No chemical reaction in the plume.

**Key simplification — Centerline, ground-level concentration:**

At $y = 0$ and $z = 0$ (sampling point at the ground, on the wind line directly downwind):

$$
\chi(x, 0, 0) = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\pi \, u \, \sigma_y \, \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)
$$

**Step-by-step interpretation:**
- The denominator $\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z$ represents the **effective volume per unit time** into which the cloud is spreading.
- The exponential $\exp(-H^2 / 2\sigma_z^2)$ is the **ground-reflection correction**; the larger the release height $H$, the lower the ground concentration at the source.
- As $x \to \infty$, $\sigma_y, \sigma_z \to \infty$, so $\chi \to 0$ (dilution to harmless levels).

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Pool Fire Thermal Radiation (Equation 13)

**Step 1:** Heat release rate from a pool of area $A_p$, with mass burning rate $\dot{m}''$ (kg/m²·s) and heat of combustion $\Delta H_c$:

$$
\dot{Q} = \dot{m}'' \, A_p \, \Delta H_c
$$

**Step 2:** Only a fraction $\eta$ of this heat leaves as radiation (the rest goes into heating the surroundings by convection). For most hydrocarbons, $\eta \approx 0.15 - 0.30$.

**Step 3:** For a circular pool of diameter $d$, view factor analysis yields:

$$
q_r = \dfrac{\eta \, \dot{m}'' \, \Delta H_c}{1 + 4 (r/d)\tan\theta}
$$

Here, $r$ is the distance from the pool edge, and $\theta$ is the flame tilt angle (function of wind speed). This is the standard **Shokri–Beyler / Moorhouse correlation**.

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — TNT Equivalent (Equation 14)

**Step 1:** Total chemical energy released by the explosion:

$$
E_{chem} = M \, \Delta H_c
$$

**Step 2:** Only a fraction $\eta_{eff}$ (typically 1–10% for VCE) contributes to the blast wave (the rest is lost as radiation, fragmentation, etc.):

$$
E_{blast} = \eta_{eff} \, M \, \Delta H_c
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to an equivalent mass of TNT:

$$
W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta_{eff} \, M \, \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}}
$$

with $E_{TNT} = 4680 \; \text{kJ/kg}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The use of $\eta_{eff}$ is the *biggest* source of error in VCE modelling. KTU examiners love asking: "Why is $\eta_{eff}$ usually 1–5% for hydrocarbon VCEs, while 100% for a high explosive?"

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (Board Style)

> **Problem:** A 1 cm² hole develops in a pipe containing methane at 2 MPa (gauge) and 25 °C. Estimate the mass release rate. Take $\gamma = 1.32$, $M = 0.016$ kg/mol, $C_d = 0.85$, $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa.

**Step 1:** Absolute pressure: $P = 2{,}000 + 101.325 = 2{,}101.325$ kPa.

**Step 2:** Pressure ratio: $P / P_{atm} = 2101.325 / 101.325 \approx 20.74$. Critical ratio for $\gamma = 1.32$:

$$
\left(\dfrac{\gamma+1}{2}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} = (1.16)^{4.125} \approx 1.85
$$

Since $20.74 \gg 1.85$, flow is **choked** at the orifice.

**Step 3:** Use the choked-flow equation:

$$
\dot{m} = C_d \, A \, P \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma M}{RT}\left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{(\gamma+1)/(\gamma-1)}}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the bracket: $\left(\dfrac{2}{2.32}\right)^{2.32/0.32} = (0.862)^{7.25} \approx 0.371$.

**Step 5:** Compute the inside: $\dfrac{\gamma M}{R T} = \dfrac{1.32 \times 0.016}{8.314 \times 298.15} = 8.51 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{mol/J}$ (using SI base units).

**Step 6:** $\dfrac{\gamma M}{R T} \times 0.371 = 3.16 \times 10^{-6}$.

**Step 7:** $\sqrt{3.16 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.78 \times 10^{-3}$.

**Step 8:** $A = 1 \times 10^{-4}$ m², $P = 2.10 \times 10^{6}$ Pa.

**Step 9:** $\dot{m} = 0.85 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.10 \times 10^{6} \times 1.78 \times 10^{-3}$

$$
\dot{m} = 0.85 \times 2.10 \times 1.78 \times 10^{-4+6-3} = 3.18 \times 10^{-1} \;\text{kg/s}
$$

**Result:** $\dot{m} \approx 0.32 \; \text{kg/s}$ of methane is released. This is a *medium* release rate that, if ignited, could form a dangerous vapour cloud of roughly 4 m³/s of air–methane mixture.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Causal Chain of a Release Effect

This diagram maps the **functional architecture** of how a release propagates into different hazards, starting from the source and ending in damage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SOURCE OF RELEASE] --> B[Phase Determination]
    B --> B1[Gas Jet]
    B --> B2[Liquid Pool]
    B --> B3[Two Phase Flashing]
    B --> B4[Dust Cloud]

    B1 --> C[Atmospheric Dispersion]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C

    C --> D[Ignition Source Present]
    C --> E[Toxic Exposure Pathway]

    D --> F1[Flash Fire]
    D --> F2[Pool Fire]
    D --> F3[Jet Fire]
    D --> F4[Fireball and BLEVE]
    D --> F5[Vapor Cloud Explosion]

    F1 --> G1[Thermal Radiation Damage]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1
    F4 --> G1
    F5 --> G2[Overpressure Damage]
    F5 --> G3[Missile Projection]

    E --> G4[Inhalation Toxicity]
    E --> G5[Skin and Eye Damage]

    G1 --> H[Final Damage and Casualties]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    G5 --> H
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Decision Logic for Selecting the Right Model

KTU examiners often ask: "Given a release scenario, which model do you use?" The decision tree below captures the official KTU teaching convention.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is the release type?}
    Q1 -->|Continuous| QC[Use Gaussian Plume Model]
    Q1 -->|Instantaneous| QI[Use Gaussian Puff Model]
    Q1 -->|Pool Spreading| QP[Use Pool Evaporation Model]

    QC --> R1{What is the effect?}
    QI --> R1
    QP --> R1

    R1 -->|Flammable, ignited| R1A[Fire Model: Pool Jet Flash]
    R1 -->|Flammable, delayed ign.| R1B[Vapor Cloud Explosion TNT]
    R1 -->|Toxic material| R1C[Probit Equation on Inhalation Dose]
    R1 -->|LPG under fire| R1D[BLEVE and Fireball Model]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Pasquill Atmospheric Stability Classes

The dispersion coefficient $\sigma_y, \sigma_z$ depends on the **Pasquill stability class**, which is determined by wind speed and incoming solar radiation. The functional architecture below shows how this is derived in a typical KTU problem.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wind Speed u m/s] --> B{Determine Stability}
    C[Daytime Solar Insolation] --> B
    D[Nighttime Cloud Cover] --> B

    B --> S1[Class A: Very Unstable]
    B --> S2[Class B: Unstable]
    B --> S3[Class C: Slightly Unstable]
    B --> S4[Class D: Neutral]
    B --> S5[Class E: Slightly Stable]
    B --> S6[Class F: Stable]

    S1 --> P[Pasquill Gifford coefficients a b c d for sigma y and sigma z]
    S2 --> P
    S3 --> P
    S4 --> P
    S5 --> P
    S6 --> P

    P --> X[Concentration field chi x y z]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Class D (neutral)** is the *default* assumed in most KTU numerical problems unless stated otherwise. The corresponding coefficients are $\sigma_y = 0.08 x^{0.894}$ and $\sigma_z = 0.06 x^{0.894}$ (with $x$ in metres, $\sigma$ in metres).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "vapor cloud explosion (VCE)" and list the three conditions necessary for its occurrence.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE) is the **combustion of a cloud of flammable vapour in an open (or partially confined) space, producing significant overpressure**. The three conditions are:
1. **Release** of a quantity of flammable material (typically > 1 tonne) in a short time.
2. **Delayed ignition** such that a cloud forms and disperses before combustion begins.
3. **Partial confinement** (or congestion from process equipment) that accelerates flame and generates blast overpressure.

*(Award 1 mark each for the three conditions, plus 0.5 marks for the definition, with the remaining 0.5 for the connecting sentence.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between a "flash fire" and a "pool fire" in terms of fuel source, duration, and damage mechanism.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Flash fire:** Fuel source = *vapour cloud*; Duration = *a few seconds*; Damage = *thermal radiation* to objects within the cloud (no significant overpressure).
- **Pool fire:** Fuel source = *liquid pool on ground*; Duration = *sustained (minutes to hours)*; Damage = *continuous thermal radiation* to surroundings and possible escalation to BLEVE if adjacent vessel fails.
*(1 mark for each axis, with 0.5 for the contrast statement.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE regulation: Each module question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. The student answers **either** OR.

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**
**(a)** Derive the steady-state mass release rate for a gas discharging through an orifice from a high-pressure reservoir. Clearly state the assumptions and the condition for choked flow. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A pressurized vessel contains propane at 1.2 MPa (gauge) and 30 °C. A 5 cm diameter hole ruptures in the side. Estimate the mass release rate. Take $C_d = 0.85$, $\gamma = 1.13$, $M = 0.044$ kg/mol. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**
- **Assumptions** (2 marks): steady, isentropic, ideal gas, $C_d$ constant, horizontal orifice.
- **Derivation** (3 marks): From the compressible Bernoulli equation, show the bracket $\left[\left(P_{atm}/P\right)^{2/\gamma} - \left(P_{atm}/P\right)^{(\gamma+1)/\gamma}\right]$ and the final form of the equation.
- **Choked flow condition** (2 marks): $P/P_{atm} \ge \left(\frac{\gamma+1}{2}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$; for propane ($\gamma = 1.13$) the critical ratio $\approx 1.7$. For $P/P_{atm} = (1200+101.3)/101.3 \approx 12.85 \gg 1.7$, flow is **choked**.

**Model Solution (b):**
- Absolute pressure: $P = 1.30 \times 10^6$ Pa; Pressure ratio: $\approx 12.85$; Choked.
- Choked flow equation: $\dot{m} = C_d A P \sqrt{\frac{\gamma M}{R T}\left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{(\gamma+1)/(\gamma-1)}}$
- Compute the bracket: $\left(\frac{2}{2.13}\right)^{2.13/0.13} = (0.939)^{16.4} \approx 0.361$.
- $\frac{\gamma M}{R T} = \frac{1.13 \times 0.044}{8.314 \times 303.15} = 1.97 \times 10^{-5}$.
- Inside: $1.97 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.361 = 7.11 \times 10^{-6}$; sqrt: $2.67 \times 10^{-3}$.
- $A = \pi/4 \times (0.05)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3}$ m².
- $\dot{m} = 0.85 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.30 \times 10^{6} \times 2.67 \times 10^{-3}$
- **$\dot{m} \approx 5.79 \; \text{kg/s}$** *(5.8 kg/s propane).* This is a *large* release — equivalent to 20.8 tonnes/hour.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Students frequently forget the **gauge-to-absolute pressure conversion**. Always add 101.325 kPa. Also, never use gauge pressure directly in gas release equations — this is the **#1 marking trap** in KTU Process Safety problems. Marks lost: 2–3.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the *Pasquill–Gifford Gaussian Plume Model* for atmospheric dispersion of a continuous release. Define all terms and write the governing equation. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A chlorine plant releases 0.5 kg/s of Cl₂ at 25 m effective height in neutral stability (Class D), wind speed 4 m/s. Compute the ground-level centerline concentration at 1 km downwind. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**
- **Block diagram** (2 marks): Source → advection by wind → Gaussian spread in $y$ and $z$ → ground reflection.
- **Governing equation** (3 marks):

$$
\chi(x,y,z) = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \left[\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]
$$

- **Term definitions** (2 marks): $\chi$ = concentration (kg/m³), $u$ = wind speed (m/s), $\sigma_y$ / $\sigma_z$ = crosswind / vertical dispersion coefficients (functions of $x$ and stability class), $H$ = effective release height (m).

**Model Solution (b):**
- At $x = 1000$ m, Class D (neutral):
- $\sigma_y = 0.08 \times 1000^{0.894} = 0.08 \times 533.3 \approx 42.66 \; \text{m}$
- $\sigma_z = 0.06 \times 1000^{0.894} \approx 32.00 \; \text{m}$
- Ground level, $y = 0$:

$$
\chi(1000, 0, 0) = \dfrac{0.5}{\pi \times 4 \times 42.66 \times 32.00} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{25^2}{2 \times 32^2}\right)
$$

- Denominator: $4 \times 42.66 \times 32 = 5460.5$; with $\pi$: $\pi \times 4 \times 42.66 \times 32 \approx 17,158$.
- Exponential: $\exp(-625 / 2048) = \exp(-0.305) \approx 0.737$.
- $\chi \approx \dfrac{0.5 \times 0.737}{17{,}158} \approx 2.15 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{kg/m}^3 = 21.5 \; \text{mg/m}^3$.
- **Converting to ppm:** $C_{ppm} = \dfrac{21.5 \times 24.45}{70.9} \approx 7.4 \; \text{ppm}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Many students **forget to multiply by the exponential term** for non-zero release height $H$. Without the term $\exp(-H^2/2\sigma_z^2)$, the answer will be ~36% higher, which at 1 km is significant. **Marks lost: 2.** Also, ppm conversion requires dividing by the molar mass of Cl₂ (70.9 g/mol) — not 71 — and using the correct molar volume of 24.45 L/mol at 25 °C.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Release** = loss of containment. **Leakage** = small / continuous form of release. The four effects are **dispersion, fire, explosion, toxicity**.
- A release is classified by **rate (continuous/instantaneous)**, **phase (gas/liquid/two-phase)**, and **mechanism (rupture, gasket, etc.)**.
- **Choked flow** occurs when $P/P_{atm} > \left(\frac{\gamma+1}{2}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$. At this point, $\dot{m}$ becomes independent of downstream pressure.
- **Flash fraction** $f_v = C_p (T - T_b)/\Delta H_{vap}$ — applies only for sub-cooled liquid releases to atmospheric pressure.
- The **Gaussian Plume** applies to *continuous* releases; the **Gaussian Puff** applies to *instantaneous* releases.
- **Dispersion coefficients** $\sigma_y, \sigma_z$ are functions of downwind distance and Pasquill stability class A–F. **Class D = neutral = default.**
- **Thermal radiation damage** is calculated using fire models (point source, pool, jet). Fireball duration and diameter scale as $M^{1/3}$.
- **Explosion overpressure** is quantified by the **TNT-equivalent** method, with $\eta_{eff} \approx 1-10\%$ for hydrocarbon VCEs and 100% for high explosives.
- **Probit equations** convert physical quantities (overpressure, thermal dose, toxic dose) to a probability of damage via a normal-distribution function.
- **Default values** KTU loves: $C_d = 0.85$–$0.95$, $\eta = 0.2$ for radiative fraction, $E_{TNT} = 4680$ kJ/kg, $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa.
- **Always convert** gauge pressure to absolute before using in flow equations.
- **AEGLs (1, 2, 3)** are the modern standard for emergency response planning; old IDLH values are increasingly used only for occupational safety.
- The word **"release"** in KTU papers almost always implies the *initial event*; the word **"effect"** implies the *consequence* — be careful in definitions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hazards of Temperature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT616 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hazards of Temperature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Temperature Hazards in Chemical Processes?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Temperature Hazards** in chemical process safety refer to any process deviation—either elevation (high temperature) or depression (low temperature)—that compromises the inherent safety of a chemical operation. These deviations can initiate secondary hazardous events such as **runaway reactions**, **thermal decomposition**, **vapor cloud formation**, **vessel over-pressurization**, **loss of containment**, or **auto-ignition** of flammable mixtures.
