### 2.4 Real-World Utility

In the petrochemical industry (e.g., **BPCL Kochi Refinery**, **Cochin Shipyard**), every operator handling benzene, toluene, sulfuric acid, or hydrogen fluoride must consult the SDS **before** commencing work. The SDS forms the **legal backbone** of:
- **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)**
- **Hazard and Operability Studies (HAZOP)**
- **Job Safety Analysis (JSA)**
- **OSHA / Factory Act compliance audits**
- **Transportation of Dangerous Goods (TDG) per UN Recommendations**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derived Calculation — Lethal Concentration (LC₅₀) from Vapor Concentration

**Problem Context:** A chemical engineer must convert a reported **LC₅₀** value from **ppm (parts per million by volume)** to **mg/m³** for an industrial hygiene report.

**Given:**
- Chemical: Toluene (C₇H₈)
- Reported LC₅₀ (rat, 4-hour inhalation): **8000 ppm**
- Molecular weight of toluene: $M = 92.14$ g/mol
- Molar volume at NTP: $V_m = 24.45$ L/mol (at **25 °C, 1 atm**)

**Conversion Formula:**

$$C_{(mg/m^3)} = C_{(ppm)} \times \dfrac{M}{V_m}$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Recall the ideal gas relationship at NTP:} \\
& PV = nRT \implies V_m = \dfrac{RT}{P} \\
& V_m = \dfrac{(0.0821 \text{ L·atm/mol·K})(298 \text{ K})}{1 \text{ atm}} \\
& V_m = 24.47 \text{ L/mol} \quad (\text{accepted value: } 24.45 \text{ L/mol})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Convert ppm to mole fraction:} \\
& 8000 \text{ ppm} = 8000 \times 10^{-6} = 8.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol toluene / mol air}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{In } 1 \text{ m}^3 = 1000 \text{ L of air at NTP:} \\
& n_{air} = \dfrac{1000 \text{ L}}{24.45 \text{ L/mol}} = 40.90 \text{ mol of air}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Moles of toluene in 1 m}^3: \\
& n_{toluene} = 40.90 \times 8.0 \times 10^{-3} = 0.3272 \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Mass of toluene in 1 m}^3: \\
& m = n \times M = 0.3272 \text{ mol} \times 92.14 \text{ g/mol} \\
& m = 30.15 \text{ g} = 30150 \text{ mg}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Apply direct conversion formula:} \\
& C_{(mg/m^3)} = 8000 \times \dfrac{92.14}{24.45} \\
& C_{(mg/m^3)} = 8000 \times 3.769 \\
& C_{(mg/m^3)} = 30150 \text{ mg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $LC_{50} = 30{,}150 \text{ mg/m}^3$ (rat, 4-hour inhalation) for toluene.

### 3.2 Derived Calculation — Flammability Range Determination

**Problem Context:** A process safety engineer at a chemical plant needs to determine if the vapor-air mixture in a storage tank falls within the flammable range.

**Given:**
- Acetone vapor in air: $C_{acetone} = 4.0\% \text{ by volume}$
- For acetone: $LEL = 2.5\%$, $UEL = 12.8\%$

**Step-by-Step:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Check if } C_{acetone} \text{ lies within } [LEL, UEL]: \\
& LEL = 2.5\% \leq C_{acetone} = 4.0\% \leq UEL = 12.8\% \\
& \text{Result: TRUE — the mixture IS flammable.}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Calculate flammability range:} \\
& \Delta F = UEL - LEL = 12.8\% - 2.5\% = 10.3\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Calculate the position within the range:} \\
& \text{Position} = \dfrac{C_{acetone} - LEL}{UEL - LEL} \times 100\% \\
& \text{Position} = \dfrac{4.0 - 2.5}{10.3} \times 100\% = 14.56\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The acetone-air mixture is in the **lower 14.56%** of the flammable range. Any ignition source (spark, hot surface above **−20 °C** flash point) will cause combustion.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference for Common Industrial Chemicals

| Chemical | CAS No. | LD₅₀ (oral, rat) | LC₅₀ (inhalation) | Flash Point | GHS Pictograms |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Acetone | 67-64-1 | 5800 mg/kg | 50100 mg/m³ | −20 °C | Flame, Exclamation |
| Benzene | 71-43-2 | 930 mg/kg | 13700 ppm | −11 °C | Flame, Health Hazard, Exclamation |
| Sulfuric Acid | 7664-93-9 | 2140 mg/kg | 320 mg/m³ | None (non-flammable) | Corrosion |
| Toluene | 108-88-3 | 636 mg/kg | 8000 ppm | 4 °C | Flame, Exclamation |
| Methanol | 67-56-1 | 5628 mg/kg | 64000 ppm | 11 °C | Flame, Exclamation, Health Hazard |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SDS Section Hierarchy Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Chemical Substance / Mixture]) --> S1[Section 1: Identification]
    Start --> S2[Section 2: Hazard Identification]
    Start --> S3[Section 3: Composition]
    Start --> S4[Section 4: First Aid Measures]
    Start --> S5[Section 5: Fire Fighting Measures]
    Start --> S6[Section 6: Accidental Release]
    Start --> S7[Section 7: Handling and Storage]
    Start --> S8[Section 8: Exposure Controls and PPE]
    Start --> S9[Section 9: Physical Chemical Properties]
    Start --> S10[Section 10: Stability and Reactivity]
    Start --> S11[Section 11: Toxicological Information]
    Start --> S12[Section 12: Ecological Information]
    Start --> S13[Section 13: Disposal Considerations]
    Start --> S14[Section 14: Transport Information]
    Start --> S15[Section 15: Regulatory Information]
    Start --> S16[Section 16: Other Information]
    
    S1 --> ClusterA[Cluster A: Hazard ID - Sections 1-4]
    S2 --> ClusterA
    S3 --> ClusterA
    S4 --> ClusterA
    
    S5 --> ClusterB[Cluster B: Emergency Response - Sections 5-8]
    S6 --> ClusterB
    S7 --> ClusterB
    S8 --> ClusterB
    
    S9 --> ClusterC[Cluster C: Properties - Sections 9-11]
    S10 --> ClusterC
    S11 --> ClusterC
    
    S12 --> ClusterD[Cluster D: Regulatory - Sections 12-16]
    S13 --> ClusterD
    S14 --> ClusterD
    S15 --> ClusterD
    S16 --> ClusterD
    
    ClusterA --> Emergency[Emergency Response Team Uses Cluster A and B]
    ClusterB --> Emergency
    ClusterC --> Engineer[Process Engineer Uses Cluster C]
    ClusterD --> Regulatory[Regulatory Compliance Uses Cluster D]
```

### 4.2 GHS Pictogram Reference Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GHS[(GHS Pictograms - Red Diamond Border)] --> P1[Exploding Bomb - Explosives]
    GHS --> P2[Flame - Flammable]
    GHS --> P3[Flame Over Circle - Oxidizers]
    GHS --> P4[Gas Cylinder - Compressed Gas]
    GHS --> P5[Corrosion - Skin Eye Damage]
    GHS --> P6[Skull and Crossbones - Acute Toxicity]
    GHS --> P7[Exclamation Mark - Irritant]
    GHS --> P8[Health Hazard - Carcinogen]
    GHS --> P9[Environment - Aquatic Toxicity]
    GHS --> P10[Biohazard - Infectious]
    
    P2 --> Example[Example: Acetone uses Flame and Exclamation]
    P5 --> Example2[Example: H2SO4 uses Corrosion]
    P6 --> Example3[Example: Benzene uses Skull and Health Hazard]
```

### 4.3 SDS Workflow in a Chemical Plant — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Supplier([Chemical Supplier]) --> Manufacture[Manufactures Chemical]
    Manufacture --> DraftSDS[Drafts SDS per GHS Standards]
    DraftSDS --> Verify[Quality Check Regulatory Compliance]
    Verify --> Dispatch[SDS Dispatched with Shipment]
    Dispatch --> Receive[Plant Receives Chemical with SDS]
    Receive --> Store[Store in Designated Area]
    Store --> Train[Train Workers on SDS Content]
    Train --> Use[Chemical Used in Process]
    Use --> Emergency{Emergency Occurs?}
    Emergency -->|Yes| Respond[Responder Reads Section 4 5 6]
    Emergency -->|No| Audit[Periodic Audit and SDS Review]
    Respond --> IncidentReport[Incident Report and SDS Update]
    Audit --> Review[Every 3 Years Review SDS]
    Review --> UpdateSDS[Update if New Hazard Data Available]
    UpdateSDS --> Notify[Notify All Stakeholders]
    Notify --> Train
    IncidentReport --> Train
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — SDS Information Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SDS[(Master SDS Document)] --> A[Section 1: Identification]
    SDS --> B[Section 2: Hazard ID]
    SDS --> C[Section 3: Composition]
    SDS --> D[Section 4: First Aid]
    SDS --> E[Section 5: Fire Fighting]
    SDS --> F[Section 6: Spill]
    SDS --> G[Section 7: Handling]
    SDS --> H[Section 8: PPE]
    SDS --> I[Section 9: Properties]
    SDS --> J[Section 10: Stability]
    SDS --> K[Section 11: Toxicology]
    SDS --> L[Section 12: Ecology]
    SDS --> M[Section 13: Disposal]
    SDS --> N[Section 14: Transport]
    SDS --> O[Section 15: Regulations]
    SDS --> P[Section 16: Other Info]
    
    A --> User1[Procurement Officer]
    B --> User2[Safety Officer]
    C --> User3[Chemist]
    D --> User4[Medical Team]
    E --> User5[Fire Brigade]
    F --> User6[HAZMAT Team]
    G --> User7[Plant Operator]
    H --> User7
    I --> User8[Process Engineer]
    J --> User8
    K --> User9[Toxicologist]
    L --> User10[Environmental Officer]
    M --> User11[Waste Management]
    N --> User12[Logistics]
    O --> User13[Compliance Officer]
    P --> User14[All Users]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Q: Define Safety Data Sheet (SDS). List any four major sections of an SDS as per GHS standards.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Safety Data Sheet (SDS) is a **standardized 16-section document** prepared by chemical manufacturers and suppliers that provides comprehensive information about a chemical's hazards, composition, protective measures, first-aid procedures, and emergency response guidelines, conforming to the **Globally Harmonized System (GHS)** of classification and labelling.

**Four Major Sections:**
1. **Section 1: Identification** — product name, supplier details, emergency contacts
2. **Section 4: First-Aid Measures** — actions to take upon exposure
3. **Section 9: Physical and Chemical Properties** — flash point, boiling point, density
4. **Section 14: Transport Information** — UN number, hazard class for shipping

*(Marks: 1 for definition + 0.5 each for 4 sections = 3 marks)*

---

#### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Q: Distinguish between LD₅₀ and LC₅₀ with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | LD₅₀ | LC₅₀ |
|---|---|---|
| Full form | Lethal Dose, 50% | Lethal Concentration, 50% |
| Definition | Single dose that kills 50% of test animals | Concentration in air that kills 50% of test animals |
| Route | Oral, dermal | Inhalation |
| Unit | mg/kg body weight | ppm or mg/m³ |
| Example (Acetone) | 5800 mg/kg (oral, rat) | 50100 mg/m³ (inhalation, rat) |

*(Marks: 1.5 for each distinction = 3 marks)*

---

### **Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **structure and content of the 16 sections of a Safety Data Sheet (SDS)** as per the Globally Harmonized System (GHS). Highlight the purpose of **Sections 4, 5, and 6** in emergency response. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A chemical plant stores **acetone** in a bulk tank. Using the following data, calculate:
- **(i)** Vapor density of acetone
- **(ii)** Whether the vapor concentration of **3.5% by volume** in air is flammable
- **(iii)** Position of this concentration within the flammability range

Given: $LEL_{acetone} = 2.5\%$, $UEL_{acetone} = 12.8\%$, Molecular weight of acetone = **58.08 g/mol**, Molecular weight of air = **28.97 g/mol**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A (a):**

The SDS is organized into **16 standardized sections** divided into **four functional clusters**:

**Cluster A — Hazard Identification (Sections 1–4):**
- Section 1 contains the product identifier, supplier name, address, and emergency phone number. **(0.5 mark)**
- Section 2 contains GHS classification, signal word (**DANGER** or **WARNING**), hazard statements (H-codes), precautionary statements (P-codes), and pictograms (red-bordered diamonds). **(0.5 mark)**
- Section 3 lists the chemical composition, **CAS numbers**, and concentration ranges of each ingredient. **(0.5 mark)**
- Section 4 describes first-aid measures for inhalation, skin contact, eye contact, and ingestion, including symptoms. **(0.5 mark)**

**Cluster B — Fire-Fighting and Accidental Release (Sections 5–8):**
- Section 5 specifies suitable extinguishing media (water spray, foam, CO₂), specific combustion hazards, and protective equipment for firefighters. **(0.5 mark)**
- Section 6 outlines personal precautions, environmental precautions, and containment/cleanup methods for spills. **(0.5 mark)**
- Section 7 describes safe handling practices and storage conditions (temperature, incompatibilities). **(0.5 mark)**
- Section 8 lists exposure limits (TWA, STEL, TLV, PEL) and required PPE (respirators, gloves, eye protection). **(0.5 mark)**

**Cluster C — Physical/Chemical Properties (Sections 9–11):**
- Section 9: appearance, odor, pH, flash point, vapor pressure, density, solubility. **(0.5 mark)**
- Section 10: chemical stability, incompatible materials, hazardous decomposition products. **(0.5 mark)**
- Section 11: toxicological data — LD₅₀, LC₅₀, carcinogenicity, mutagenicity. **(0.5 mark)**

**Cluster D — Regulatory and Other Information (Sections 12–16):**
- Section 12: ecological toxicity and environmental fate. **(0.25 mark)**
- Section 13: disposal considerations per local regulations. **(0.25 mark)**
- Section 14: UN number, transport hazard class, packing group (I, II, III). **(0.25 mark)**
- Section 15: regulatory information (OSHA, REACH, TSCA, Indian Factories Act). **(0.25 mark)**
- Section 16: date of preparation, revision history, glossary. **(0.25 mark)**

**Purpose of Sections 4, 5, and 6 in Emergency Response:**

- **Section 4 (First-Aid Measures):** Used by **medical teams and first responders** to provide immediate treatment to exposed workers — saves time by listing exact symptoms, antidotes, and actions for inhalation, ingestion, skin, and eye contact. **(0.5 mark)**

- **Section 5 (Fire-Fighting Measures):** Used by **firefighters** to select the correct extinguishing agent, understand combustion by-products (e.g., toxic hydrogen cyanide from burning acrylonitrile), and deploy proper protective gear. **(0.5 mark)**

- **Section 6 (Accidental Release Measures):** Used by **HAZMAT teams and plant operators** to safely contain spills, prevent environmental contamination (soil, water bodies), and clean up using absorbents or neutralization agents. **(0.5 mark)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A (b):**

**Part (i): Vapor Density Calculation**

The vapor density is the ratio of molecular weight of the gas to that of air:

$$V_D = \dfrac{M_{acetone}}{M_{air}}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_D &= \dfrac{58.08 \text{ g/mol}}{28.97 \text{ g/mol}} \\
V_D &= 2.005
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Acetone vapor is approximately **2 times heavier than air**. Consequently, acetone vapors will accumulate in **low-lying areas, pits, basements, and trenches**, posing an asphyxiation and explosion hazard. Proper ventilation must be ensured. **(1.5 marks)**

**Part (ii): Flammability Check**

Given vapor concentration $C = 3.5\%$ by volume.

The condition for flammability is:

$$LEL \leq C \leq UEL$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2.5\% \leq 3.5\% \leq 12.8\% \\
& \text{TRUE}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The acetone vapor concentration of **3.5%** lies within the flammability range. **The mixture is flammable and will ignite in presence of any ignition source** (spark, hot surface above flash point of −20 °C). **(2.5 marks)**

**Part (iii): Position Within Flammability Range**

First, calculate the flammability range:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta F &= UEL - LEL \\
\Delta F &= 12.8\% - 2.5\% = 10.3\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Position within the range:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Position} &= \dfrac{C - LEL}{UEL - LEL} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{3.5 - 2.5}{10.3} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{1.0}{10.3} \times 100\% \\
&= 9.71\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The mixture is positioned at the **lower 9.71%** of the flammability range, which is a **lean mixture** (closer to LEL). Although ignition is possible, it will burn less violently than a stoichiometric mixture. However, this is still **highly dangerous** because the lower flammability limit is dangerously close. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Question B [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the **GHS classification system** for chemical hazards. Describe in detail the **9 GHS pictograms** with their hazard meanings. Discuss the difference between **Hazard Statements (H-codes)** and **Precautionary Statements (P-codes)** with two examples each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An industrial hygienist reports a toluene vapor concentration of **12000 ppm** in a confined workspace. Using SDS data, determine whether this is **immediately dangerous to life or health (IDLH)**.

Given: $IDLH_{toluene} = 500 \text{ ppm}$, $TLV-TWA = 50 \text{ ppm}$, $TLV-STEL = 150 \text{ ppm}$, Molecular weight of toluene = **92.14 g/mol**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B (a):**

**GHS Classification System:**

The **Globally Harmonized System of Classification and Labelling of Chemicals (GHS)** is a United Nations-adopted international framework (first published 2003, currently Rev. 9 in 2021) that standardizes the **classification criteria, hazard communication elements (labels and SDS), and hazard pictograms** for chemicals across all UN member countries. It addresses **physical hazards, health hazards, and environmental hazards**. **(1 mark)**

**The 9 GHS Pictograms:**

| Pictogram | Symbol | Hazard Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Exploding Bomb** | Bursting explosion | Explosives, self-reactives, organic peroxides |
| 2. **Flame** | Fire | Flammable liquids, solids, gases; self-heating substances |
| 3. **Flame Over Circle** | Fire over oxygen symbol | Oxidizing gases, liquids, and solids |
| 4. **Gas Cylinder** | Gas bottle | Compressed, liquefied, or dissolved gases under pressure |
| 5. **Corrosion** | Test tubes damaging surfaces | Corrosive to metals; causes severe skin burns and eye damage |
| 6. **Skull and Crossbones** | Skull | Acute toxicity (fatal or toxic) |
| 7. **Exclamation Mark** | Exclamation | Irritant, mild toxicity, narcotic effects |
| 8. **Health Hazard** | Human silhouette with starburst | Carcinogen, mutagen, reproductive toxin, respiratory sensitizer |
| 9. **Environment** | Dead fish and tree | Aquatic toxicity, hazardous to aquatic environment |

*(2 marks for describing all 9 pictograms)*

**Difference Between Hazard Statements (H-codes) and Precautionary Statements (P-codes):**

| Feature | Hazard Statements (H-codes) | Precautionary Statements (P-codes) |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Describe the **nature of the hazard** | Describe the **measures to prevent or minimize adverse effects** |
| Nature | **Mandatory** and standardized | **Recommended** and standardized |
| Format | "H" followed by 3-digit number | "P" followed by 3-digit number |
| **Example 1** | **H225**: Highly flammable liquid and vapour | **P210**: Keep away from heat, hot surfaces, sparks, open flames, and other ignition sources. No smoking |
| **Example 2** | **H302**: Harmful if swallowed | **P264**: Wash hands thoroughly after handling |

*(4 marks for comparison and examples)*

---

### **Model Solution for Question B (b):**

**Step 1: Compare with IDLH**

The IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health) value is the airborne concentration above which an unprotected worker would be exposed to **irreversible health effects or loss of escape capability**.

Given: $C_{toluene} = 12000 \text{ ppm}$, $IDLH = 500 \text{ ppm}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ratio} &= \dfrac{C}{IDLH} \\
&= \dfrac{12000}{500} \\
&= 24
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The toluene concentration is **24 times the IDLH value**. This is **EXTREMELY DANGEROUS** and **immediately life-threatening**. The workspace must be **immediately evacuated**, and rescue personnel must wear **self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA)**. **(3 marks)**

**Step 2: Compare with TLV-TWA**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TWA exceedance ratio} &= \dfrac{12000}{50} = 240
\end{aligned}
$$

The concentration is **240 times the TLV-TWA**, indicating that prolonged exposure would cause **central nervous system depression, narcosis, liver damage, and death**. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3: Compare with TLV-STEL**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{STEL exceedance ratio} &= \dfrac{12000}{150} = 80
\end{aligned}
$$

The concentration is **80 times the TLV-STEL**, far exceeding the 15-minute short-term exposure limit. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4: Convert ppm to mg/m³ (for industrial hygiene report)**

Using the conversion formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{(mg/m^3)} &= C_{(ppm)} \times \dfrac{M}{24.45} \\
&= 12000 \times \dfrac{92.14}{24.45} \\
&= 12000 \times 3.769 \\
&= 45230 \text{ mg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Recommendation (2 marks):**
- **Immediate evacuation** of all personnel from the confined space
- **SCBA-equipped rescue team** to enter the area
- **Ventilation and air monitoring** before re-entry
- **Root cause investigation** of vapor release
- **Medical examination** for any exposed personnel
- **Update the Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)** and revise engineering controls

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing MSDS with SDS:** Both terms refer to the same document, but KTU 2024 Scheme prefers **SDS (Safety Data Sheet)**. Using only "MSDS" may cost 0.5 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the 16-Section Order:** When listing sections, **strict sequential order matters**. Jumbling Section 4 with Section 8 will lose marks.
> 3. **Units Confusion:** ppm vs. mg/m³ — always specify which unit is being used.
> 4. **Not converting molecular weight of air:** Use **M_air = 28.97 g/mol** explicitly.
> 5. **Skipping interpretation:** Calculation alone without explaining the engineering meaning (e.g., "vapor is 2× heavier than air, so it accumulates in low areas") loses at least 1–2 marks.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- [x] An **SDS (formerly MSDS)** is a **16-section standardized document** mandated under **GHS** for chemical hazard communication.
- [x] The **16 sections** are grouped into **4 clusters**: Hazard ID (1–4), Emergency Response (5–8), Properties (9–11), Regulatory (12–16).
- [x] **Signal Words:** "DANGER" (severe) or "WARNING" (less severe).
- [x] **CAS Number** uniquely identifies a chemical (e.g., Benzene = 71-43-2).
- [x] **LD₅₀** = dose killing 50% (mg/kg); **LC₅₀** = concentration killing 50% (ppm or mg/m³).
- [x] **TLV-TWA** is averaged over **8 hours**; **TLV-STEL** over **15 minutes**.
- [x] **IDLH** is the most critical exposure limit — exceedance is **immediately life-threatening**.
- [x] **9 GHS pictograms** are red-bordered diamonds representing specific hazard classes.
- [x] **H-codes (Hazard Statements)** describe the nature of hazard; **P-codes (Precautionary Statements)** describe prevention measures.
- [x] **Flash Point** is the lowest temperature at which a liquid gives off vapor that can ignite.
- [x] **LEL** (Lower Explosive Limit) and **UEL** (Upper Explosive Limit) define the flammable range.
- [x] **Vapor Density** > 1 means vapor is heavier than air → accumulates in low-lying areas.
- [x] **Conversion formula:** $C_{(mg/m^3)} = C_{(ppm)} \times \dfrac{M}{24.45}$
- [x] SDS must be **readily accessible** to all employees at the workplace at all times (OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1200(g)(8)).
- [x] SDS must be **reviewed every 3 years** or whenever new hazard information becomes available.
- [x] In India, **Factory Act 1948 (Section 41B)** mandates SDS for hazardous chemicals in factories.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimating Work Exposure to Toxic Vapours
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT616 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Estimating Work Exposure to Toxic Vapours

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Work Exposure to Toxic Vapours** is the quantitative estimate of the airborne concentration of a hazardous chemical substance that a worker inhales (or is subjected to) over a specified duration of time during a routine operational, maintenance, or accidental scenario in an industrial environment.

In KTU 2024 Scheme Chemical Process Safety terminology, **exposure** is a function of two physical variables:

$$ \text{Exposure} = f(\text{Concentration}, \text{Time}) $$

This is mathematically expressed by **Haber's Law** (1918):

$$ C \cdot t = k $$

where $C$ is the airborne concentration of the toxic vapour, $t$ is the duration of exposure, and $k$ is a constant that represents the toxicological threshold (response) for that substance.

**Work Exposure Estimation** is the engineering practice of predicting the value of $C$ (and therefore $C \cdot t$) that a worker would experience, given physical parameters such as:

- Liquid spill volume / pool area
- Vapour pressure of the toxic substance
- Temperature of the workplace
- Ventilation rate (volumetric air flow)
- Room / work zone geometry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module focuses on the *engineering estimation* of exposure — not on the medical/toxicological response. Students must master the ability to compute airborne concentrations from first principles using mass-balance and mass-transfer concepts.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are cooking in a small closed kitchen with a pan of hot oil that has just splashed. Within minutes, the room fills with visible haze, and your eyes begin to water.

**Why does this happen?**
- The oil heats up → vapour pressure rises.
- Molecules leave the oil surface → evaporate into air.
- In a closed room with poor ventilation, the concentration ($C$) builds up.
- Your nose, lungs, and eyes respond after a *time* ($t$).

Now, if you open the kitchen window and turn on the exhaust fan:
- Fresh air dilutes the vapour.
- Concentration drops, so even though $t$ is the same, $C \cdot t$ drops.
- You feel less irritation.

**Worker exposure estimation is essentially "doing this calculation before it happens"** — predicting $C$ in ppm (parts per million) or mg/m³ for a worker standing in a chemical plant near a solvent tank, pump, or flange leak.

---

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU High-Frequency Vocabulary)

| Metric | Full Form | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **TLV-TWA** | Threshold Limit Value – Time Weighted Average | 8-hr average exposure allowed |
| **TLV-STEL** | Short Term Exposure Limit | 15-min average, max 4×/day |
| **TLV-C** | Ceiling Limit | Instantaneous upper limit |
| **PEL** | Permissible Exposure Limit (OSHA) | Legal 8-hr TWA |
| **REL** | Recommended Exposure Limit (NIOSH) | NIOSH recommended TWA |
| **IDLH** | Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health | Max concentration for 30 min escape |
| **LC₅₀** | Lethal Concentration, 50% | Concentration killing 50% test animals |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units to memorize (board-favourite):**
> - $1 \text{ ppm} = \frac{\text{Volume of vapour}}{10^6 \text{ volumes of air}}$ (dimensionless, but expressed as a ratio)
> - Conversion: $C_{\text{mg/m}^3} = C_{\text{ppm}} \times \frac{MW}{24.45}$ at **25 °C, 1 atm**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Step Logic of Exposure Estimation

The KTU 2024 Scheme board examiner expects students to demonstrate a logical two-step reasoning when solving any vapour-exposure problem:

**STEP 1 — Source-Rate Calculation:**
Determine *how fast* the toxic substance enters the air (mass-generation rate, $G$ in kg/s or mol/s).

**STEP 2 — Concentration Calculation:**
Use the well-mixed compartment (box-model) assumption to convert $G$ and the ventilation flow rate $Q$ into a steady-state airborne concentration $C$.

$$ C_{\text{air}} = \frac{G}{Q} $$

where:
- $G$ = mass generation rate (mg/s)
- $Q$ = ventilation flow rate (m³/s)
- $C_{\text{air}}$ = concentration (mg/m³)

---

### 2.2 The Generation Rate $G$ — Where Does the Vapour Come From?

There are three common physical scenarios a KTU question may present:

#### (a) Evaporation from a Liquid Pool (spill scenario)

For a volatile liquid spilled on the floor or inside an open vessel:

$$ G = \frac{M \cdot k_c \cdot A}{R \cdot T} \cdot (P^{\text{sat}} - P_{\text{ambient}}) $$

This is the **mass-transfer limited evaporation** equation, where:
- $M$ = molecular weight (kg/kmol)
- $k_c$ = mass transfer coefficient (m/s), typically $k_c \approx 0.01$ to $0.05$ m/s for still indoor air
- $A$ = exposed liquid surface area (m²)
- $R$ = universal gas constant, **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $P^{\text{sat}}$ = saturation vapour pressure (Pa)
- $P_{\text{ambient}}$ = partial pressure of the substance already in air (Pa)

#### (b) Vapor Pressure from Antoine Equation

If the examiner provides temperature in °C, use the **Antoine Equation** to find $P^{\text{sat}}$:

$$ \log_{10}(P^{\text{sat}}) = A - \frac{B}{C + T(^{\circ}\text{C})} $$

where $P^{\text{sat}}$ is in mmHg and constants $A, B, C$ are substance-specific.

#### (c) Volumetric Ventilation (dilution approach)

For a sealed leak into a ventilated room of volume $V$:

$$ G = Q_{\text{vent}} \cdot C_{\text{steady}} $$

So the concentration is:

$$ C_{\text{steady}} = \frac{G}{Q_{\text{vent}}} $$

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **This table is the single most important revision sheet for Module 1.**

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C \cdot t = k$ | Haber's Law (toxic response) | ppm·min |
| 2 | $C_{\text{mg/m}^3} = C_{\text{ppm}} \times \frac{MW}{24.45}$ | ppm ↔ mg/m³ conversion at 25 °C | mg/m³ |
| 3 | $G = \dfrac{M \cdot k_c \cdot A}{R \cdot T} \cdot (P^{\text{sat}} - P_{\text{a}})$ | Pool evaporation rate | kg/s |
| 4 | $\log_{10}(P^{\text{sat}}) = A - \dfrac{B}{C + T}$ | Antoine equation for vapour pressure | mmHg |
| 5 | $C_{\text{air}} = \dfrac{G}{Q_{\text{vent}}}$ | Steady-state concentration in ventilated room | mg/m³ |
| 6 | $C(t) = C_{\text{ss}} \cdot (1 - e^{-t \cdot Q/V})$ | Transient (build-up) concentration | mg/m³ |
| 7 | $\text{IDLH}_{\text{ppm}} = \dfrac{IDLH_{\text{mg/m}^3} \times 24.45}{MW}$ | Convert IDLH to ppm form | ppm |
| 8 | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | Ventilation time constant | s |
| 9 | $\% LEL = \dfrac{C_{\text{ppm}}}{LFL_{\text{ppm}}} \times 100$ | Distance to flammability limit | % |
| 10 | $Q_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{G \times 10^6}{TLV \times MW}$ | Required dilution airflow | m³/s |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common sign convention:** In Antoine equation, $T$ is in **°C**, but in the mass-transfer equation $T$ must be in **Kelvin**. Always convert: $T(K) = T(^{\circ}C) + 273.15$.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This estimation framework is used by:
- **Process Safety Engineers** to design ventilation systems in chemical plants.
- **Industrial Hygienists** to decide whether workers need respirators.
- **EPA / OSHA regulators** to set legal workplace limits.
- **Disaster Investigators** (e.g., Bhopal 1984) to reconstruct airborne concentrations after MIC release.

The same math that calculates a coffee-shop aroma concentration can predict the airborne toxic load during a styrene leak from a fiberglass plant.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation of Transient Concentration in a Well-Mixed Room

Consider a room of volume $V$ (m³) into which a toxic vapour is released at a constant rate $G$ (mg/s), and air is exhausted at volumetric flow rate $Q$ (m³/s). Initially the room is clean.

**Mass balance on the room at time $t$:**

$$ \frac{d(VC)}{dt} = G - Q \cdot C(t) $$

Since $V$ is constant:

$$ V \cdot \frac{dC}{dt} = G - Q \cdot C $$

Rearrange into standard first-order linear ODE form:

$$ \frac{dC}{dt} + \frac{Q}{V} \cdot C = \frac{G}{V} $$

**Solve by integrating factor** $I = e^{\int (Q/V) \, dt} = e^{(Q/V)t}$:

$$ \frac{d}{dt}\left[ C \cdot e^{(Q/V)t} \right] = \frac{G}{V} \cdot e^{(Q/V)t} $$

Integrate from $0$ to $t$ with initial condition $C(0) = 0$:

$$ C(t) \cdot e^{(Q/V)t} - 0 = \frac{G}{V} \cdot \int_{0}^{t} e^{(Q/V)\tau} \, d\tau $$

$$ C(t) \cdot e^{(Q/V)t} = \frac{G}{V} \cdot \frac{V}{Q} \left[ e^{(Q/V)t} - 1 \right] $$

$$ C(t) = \frac{G}{Q} \left[ 1 - e^{-(Q/V)t} \right] $$

This is the **canonical transient concentration equation**.

$$ \boxed{\, C(t) = C_{\text{ss}} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right), \quad \tau = \frac{V}{Q} \,} $$

**Physical interpretation:**
- At $t \to \infty$, $C \to C_{\text{ss}} = G/Q$ (steady-state).
- $\tau = V/Q$ is the **ventilation time constant** — time to reach $\approx 63.2\%$ of $C_{\text{ss}}$.
- After $5\tau$, concentration is $\approx 99\%$ of steady-state.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A worker is exposed to a leak of **acetone** from a flange in a closed storage room.
> - Room volume: $V = 100 \text{ m}^3$
> - Ventilation: $Q = 0.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
> - Leak rate: $G = 50 \text{ mg/s}$
> - TLV-TWA of acetone: **750 ppm**
> - Molecular weight of acetone: $M = 58.08 \text{ g/mol}$
>
> **Find:** (a) Steady-state concentration in mg/m³ and ppm.
> (b) Time required to reach $99\%$ of steady-state.
> (c) Compare with TLV.

#### Part (a): Steady-state concentration

$$ C_{\text{ss}} = \frac{G}{Q} = \frac{50 \text{ mg/s}}{0.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}} = 100 \text{ mg/m}^3 $$

Convert to ppm at 25 °C:

$$ C_{\text{ppm}} = C_{\text{mg/m}^3} \times \frac{24.45}{MW} = 100 \times \frac{24.45}{58.08} $$

$$ C_{\text{ppm}} = 100 \times 0.4210 = 42.1 \text{ ppm} $$

#### Part (b): Time to reach 99% of $C_{\text{ss}}$

We need $1 - e^{-t/\tau} = 0.99$, so $e^{-t/\tau} = 0.01$.

$$ t = -\tau \cdot \ln(0.01) = \tau \cdot \ln(100) \approx 4.605 \cdot \tau $$

Compute $\tau$:

$$ \tau = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{100 \text{ m}^3}{0.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}} = 200 \text{ s} $$

$$ t \approx 4.605 \times 200 = 921 \text{ s} \approx 15.35 \text{ min} $$

#### Part (c): Compare with TLV

$$ \frac{C_{\text{ppm}}}{TLV} = \frac{42.1}{750} = 0.056 \approx 5.6\% $$

**Conclusion:** Concentration is only $5.6\%$ of TLV → worker is safe under steady-state. However, if the leak rate were $10\times$ higher ($G = 500 \text{ mg/s}$), concentration would jump to $421 \text{ ppm}$ → still below TLV, but if TLV were $100 \text{ ppm}$ (e.g., benzene), it would be a hazard.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: How to Choose the Right Equation

| Scenario Given in Question | Equation to Use | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Spilled liquid with known area, $T$, $P^{\text{sat}}$ | Pool evaporation (Eq. 3) | Mass-transfer limited source |
| Given leak rate $G$ directly in mg/s | Use $C = G/Q$ | Source rate is known |
| Asked "time to reach 99%" | $t = 4.605 \cdot \tau$ | Standard engineering rule |
| Need ppm from mg/m³ | Multiply by $24.45/MW$ | Standard NIOSH conversion |
| Asked for required ventilation | $Q_{\text{req}} = G \times 10^6 / (TLV \times MW)$ | Inversion of $C = G/Q$ |
| Given Antoine constants only | Find $P^{\text{sat}}$ first, then plug into Eq. 3 | Two-step calculation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
