<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*State Haber's Law as applied to toxic vapour exposure. Mention its limitation.*

**Model Answer:**

Haber's Law states that for a given toxicological response:

$$ C \cdot t = k $$

where $C$ is the airborne concentration, $t$ is the duration of exposure, and $k$ is a substance-specific constant. It implies that the same biological effect can be produced by a high concentration acting briefly, or a low concentration acting over a longer period.

**Limitation:** Haber's Law fails at very short exposures (less than a few minutes) and very long exposures (months/years), and it assumes the toxicity is independent of exposure pattern — which is not true for many substances (e.g., carcinogens).

*(3 marks: 1 for stating, 1 for interpretation, 1 for limitation)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Convert a TLV-TWA value of 200 ppm for a chemical of molecular weight 78 g/mol into mg/m³ at 25 °C.*

**Model Answer:**

The standard conversion formula at 25 °C and 1 atm is:

$$ C_{\text{mg/m}^3} = C_{\text{ppm}} \times \frac{MW}{24.45} $$

Substitute $C = 200$ ppm and $MW = 78$:

$$ C_{\text{mg/m}^3} = 200 \times \frac{78}{24.45} $$

$$ C_{\text{mg/m}^3} = 200 \times 3.190 = 638.0 \text{ mg/m}^3 $$

*(3 marks: 1 formula, 1 substitution, 1 answer with unit)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **KTU Note:** In KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B Module 1 questions carry 14 marks and typically contain two 7-mark sub-parts: one conceptual and one numerical.

---

## QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Define the following terms with appropriate units:
*(7 marks)*
  (i) TLV-TWA
  (ii) TLV-STEL
  (iii) TLV-Ceiling
  (iv) IDLH
  (v) LC₅₀
  (vi) PEL
  (vii) Dose

**(b)** A factory worker is exposed to a leak of **benzene** in a closed inspection chamber.
*(7 marks)*
  - Chamber volume $V = 50 \text{ m}^3$
  - Ventilation rate $Q = 0.25 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
  - Leak mass rate $G = 100 \text{ mg/s}$
  - Molecular weight of benzene: $MW = 78 \text{ g/mol}$
  - TLV-TWA of benzene: $0.5 \text{ ppm}$

  **Calculate:**
  (i) Steady-state airborne concentration in mg/m³ and ppm.
  (ii) Time required for the concentration to reach 99% of steady-state.
  (iii) Compare with TLV and comment on worker safety.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A

**Part (a) — Definitions (7 marks)**

| Term | Definition | Unit / Reference |
|---|---|---|
| TLV-TWA | Time-Weighted Average concentration for a normal 8-hour workday/40-hour week | ppm or mg/m³ |
| TLV-STEL | 15-minute time-weighted average that should not be exceeded at any time during the workday | ppm or mg/m³ |
| TLV-Ceiling | Concentration that must not be exceeded at any instant | ppm or mg/m³ |
| IDLH | Maximum airborne concentration from which a worker can escape within 30 minutes without irreversible health effects | ppm |
| LC₅₀ | Concentration lethal to 50% of test animals in a specified exposure time | ppm (typically 4 h) |
| PEL | OSHA legally enforceable permissible exposure limit (8-hr TWA) | ppm |
| Dose | Total quantity of toxic substance inhaled, $D = \int C(t) \, dt$ | ppm·h or mg·h/m³ |

*(7 × 1 = 7 marks)*

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**(i) Steady-state concentration (2 marks)**

$$ C_{\text{ss}} = \frac{G}{Q} = \frac{100}{0.25} = 400 \text{ mg/m}^3 $$

Convert to ppm:

$$ C_{\text{ppm}} = 400 \times \frac{24.45}{78} = 400 \times 0.3135 = 125.4 \text{ ppm} $$

**(ii) Time to reach 99% of steady-state (2 marks)**

$$ \tau = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{50}{0.25} = 200 \text{ s} $$

$$ t_{99\%} = 4.605 \cdot \tau = 4.605 \times 200 = 921 \text{ s} \approx 15.35 \text{ min} $$

**(iii) Comparison with TLV (3 marks)**

$$ \frac{C_{\text{ppm}}}{TLV} = \frac{125.4}{0.5} = 250.8 $$

The actual concentration is **250.8 times the TLV-TWA**. This is a **serious health hazard** requiring immediate evacuation, source shut-off, and use of self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA) for any rescue personnel.

---

## QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the **well-mixed compartment (box) model** for estimating indoor vapour concentration. Derive the transient concentration equation. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A solvent spill of **toluene** occurs on the floor of a poorly ventilated room. Estimate the airborne concentration at $t = 10$ minutes. *(7 marks)*

  Given:
  - Spill area: $A = 2 \text{ m}^2$
  - Molecular weight of toluene: $M = 92.14 \text{ kg/kmol}$
  - Mass transfer coefficient: $k_c = 0.005 \text{ m/s}$
  - Saturation vapour pressure at 25 °C: $P^{\text{sat}} = 3800 \text{ Pa}$
  - Ambient partial pressure of toluene: $P_a \approx 0$ (initially clean air)
  - Room temperature: $T = 298 \text{ K}$
  - Room volume: $V = 200 \text{ m}^3$
  - Ventilation rate: $Q = 0.1 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
  - $R = 8314 \text{ J/(kmol·K)}$
  - TLV-TWA of toluene: $50 \text{ ppm}$

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B

**Part (a) — Box Model Derivation (7 marks)**

*(See full derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes.)*

Key presentation points (each worth marks):
1. State assumption: instantaneous uniform mixing inside the volume $V$. (1 mark)
2. Write mass balance: $V \cdot dC/dt = G - Q \cdot C$. (2 marks)
3. Solve the linear ODE using integrating factor. (2 marks)
4. Final form: $C(t) = (G/Q)(1 - e^{-Qt/V})$ and physical meaning of $\tau = V/Q$. (2 marks)

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Calculate evaporation rate $G$ (3 marks)**

$$ G = \frac{M \cdot k_c \cdot A}{R \cdot T} \cdot (P^{\text{sat}} - P_a) $$

Substitute values:

$$ G = \frac{92.14 \times 0.005 \times 2}{8314 \times 298} \cdot (3800 - 0) $$

Numerator: $92.14 \times 0.005 \times 2 = 0.9214$

Denominator: $8314 \times 298 = 2{,}477{,}572$

Fraction: $0.9214 / 2{,}477{,}572 = 3.719 \times 10^{-7} \text{ kmol/(m²·s)} \times \text{Pa factor handled below}$

Actually, applying the formula properly:

$$ G = \frac{92.14 \times 0.005 \times 2 \times 3800}{8314 \times 298} $$

$$ G = \frac{3501.32}{2{,}477{,}572} = 1.413 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s} $$

$$ G = 1413 \text{ mg/s} $$

**Step 2 — Steady-state concentration (1 mark)**

$$ C_{\text{ss}} = \frac{G}{Q} = \frac{1413}{0.1} = 14{,}130 \text{ mg/m}^3 $$

**Step 3 — Transient concentration at $t = 600$ s (2 marks)**

$$ \tau = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{200}{0.1} = 2000 \text{ s} $$

$$ C(600) = 14{,}130 \cdot \left(1 - e^{-600/2000}\right) = 14{,}130 \cdot (1 - e^{-0.3}) $$

$$ e^{-0.3} = 0.7408 $$

$$ C(600) = 14{,}130 \times 0.2592 = 3662 \text{ mg/m}^3 $$

**Step 4 — Compare with TLV (1 mark)**

Convert to ppm: $C_{\text{ppm}} = 3662 \times 24.45 / 92.14 = 971.5 \text{ ppm}$

$$ \frac{C_{\text{ppm}}}{TLV} = \frac{971.5}{50} \approx 19.4 $$

**Conclusion:** Concentration is **~19× above TLV** within 10 minutes. The room is unsafe; workers must wear SCBA, and the ventilation must be increased or the spill contained.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes costing 2–3 marks in valuation:**
>
> 1. **Temperature unit confusion:** Students often plug °C into the gas-law formula instead of K. Always convert: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$.
>
> 2. **Using $R = 8.314$ when MW is in g/mol, or $R = 8314$ when MW is in kg/kmol.** Mismatch → unit error → full mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the transient factor** $(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ and reporting the steady-state $C_{\text{ss}}$ as the actual concentration. The examiner specifically tests whether you understand *build-up time*.
>
> 4. **No final comparison with TLV.** A numerical problem that ends with "C = X ppm" without a sentence saying "this is above/below TLV" loses the final 1–2 marks reserved for engineering interpretation.
>
> 5. **Wrong sign in $(P^{\text{sat}} - P_a)$:** If $P_a > P^{\text{sat}}$ (rare, but possible during cleanup), evaporation becomes *negative* — students often miss this sign convention.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Haber's Law** $C \cdot t = k$ is the foundation of every exposure problem.
- ✅ The **two-step pattern**: (1) find generation rate $G$, (2) compute concentration via $C = G/Q$.
- ✅ **Antoine equation** gives $P^{\text{sat}}$ when only temperature data is available.
- ✅ **Mass-transfer evaporation** formula $G = \dfrac{M \cdot k_c \cdot A}{R \cdot T}(P^{\text{sat}} - P_a)$ is board-favourite for spill scenarios.
- ✅ **Transient concentration** $C(t) = C_{\text{ss}}(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ with $\tau = V/Q$ — memorize the "4.6τ rule" for $99\%$ fill.
- ✅ **Conversion at 25 °C:** $1 \text{ ppm} = \dfrac{MW}{24.45} \text{ mg/m}^3$.
- ✅ Always **end your answer with a TLV comparison** and an engineering conclusion.
- ✅ **Common exposure limits:** TLV-TWA (8 h), STEL (15 min), Ceiling (instant), IDLH (30 min escape).
- ✅ **Engineering controls:** increase $Q$ (ventilation), decrease $T$ (cooling), contain source (enclosure), or PPE (respirators).
- ✅ **Historical case study:** The Bhopal disaster (1984) was a failure of multiple controls — relevant for KTU essay-type questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimating the Vaporization Rate of a Liquid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT616 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Estimating the Vaporization Rate of a Liquid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Vaporization Rate** is defined as the mass (or molar) quantity of a liquid that is transferred into the surrounding gas phase per unit time, under non-equilibrium conditions, governed primarily by mass-transfer (diffusion and convection) resistances at the liquid–vapor interface.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (Process Safety perspective), the **Vaporization Rate (Q_v)** quantifies the rate at which a hazardous liquid (e.g., a spilled chemical) evaporates from a pool, leak, or open container. It is a critical input parameter for:

- **Source term modeling** in consequence analysis (e.g., pool fires, vapor cloud explosions).
- **Dispersion modeling** (calculating downwind concentrations).
- **Relief valve and flare system sizing**.
- **Indoor air quality and ventilation design**.

The general engineering expression is:

$$Q_v = \frac{dm}{dt} = K \cdot A \cdot \Delta C$$

where $K$ is the overall mass-transfer coefficient (m/s), $A$ is the exposed liquid surface area (m²), and $\Delta C$ is the concentration driving force (kg/m³).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The estimation of the vaporization rate of a non-boiling liquid pool is treated as a **gas-phase diffusion-controlled mass-transfer problem** (assuming the liquid phase offers negligible resistance). This is the classical approach of **Sutton (1953)**, which is the standard reference model for spill evaporation in process safety textbooks.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tea Cup Cooling" Intuition

Imagine a freshly poured cup of hot tea in a quiet (still) room. You will notice a thin "vapor blanket" rising from the surface. Now imagine a strong fan blowing across the cup — the tea cools much faster, and the vapor escapes more rapidly.

This is exactly the physics of vaporization rate:

- **Still air (stagnant layer)** → Vapor molecules pile up just above the liquid surface, creating a "blanket" that slows further evaporation. Only **diffusion** drives the molecules away.
- **Wind (forced convection)** → The vapor blanket is constantly swept away, allowing fresh, dry air to contact the surface. Evaporation is much faster.

> **Key Insight for Students:** The vaporization rate is **not** limited by how fast molecules *leave* the liquid (which is extremely fast), but by how fast the **vapor is removed** through the gas boundary layer. This is why the gas-phase resistance is what engineers calculate.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard values and constants are repeatedly used in vaporization rate calculations:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Universal Gas Constant | R | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Standard Temperature | T₀ | **298.15 K (25 °C)** |
| Standard Pressure | P | **101,325 Pa (1 atm)** |
| Typical diffusion coefficient (air–VOC) | D_AB | **1 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s** |
| Kinematic viscosity of air | ν | **1.5 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Vaporization vs. Boiling — A Critical Distinction:** *Vaporization* is a **diffusion-controlled** process occurring below the boiling point of the liquid (evaporation). *Boiling* is a **heat-transfer-controlled** process occurring at the boiling point. KTU Module 1 specifically addresses the **non-boiling (evaporative) case**, which is the most common scenario in accidental releases.

---

## 1.4 The Two Operating Regimes of Vaporization

| Regime | Controlling Resistance | Governing Physics |
|---|---|---|
| **Stagnant (no wind)** | Gas-phase diffusion | Fick's Law |
| **Forced convection (wind present)** | Gas-phase convection-diffusion | Sherwood Number Correlation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why and How

The rate of vaporization of a non-boiling liquid from a pool is controlled by **mass transfer through the gas-phase boundary layer** above the liquid surface. The liquid phase is assumed to be well-mixed and at uniform temperature (no internal resistance).

### Step-by-Step Logic:

1. **Liquid surface is saturated:** Immediately above the liquid, the gas phase is in equilibrium with the liquid. The partial pressure of the vapor at the surface equals the **saturation vapor pressure (P_sat)** at the liquid's surface temperature.
2. **Concentration gradient develops:** Moving upward (z-direction) into the bulk gas, the vapor concentration decreases from saturated (z = 0) to ambient (z = ∞).
3. **Fick's First Law applies:** The molar flux is proportional to the concentration gradient through the stagnant gas film.
4. **Convection enhances the flux:** When wind (velocity u) sweeps across the surface, the effective film thickness decreases, and a forced-convection correlation (Sherwood Number) is used.

---

## 2.2 Governing Mass-Transfer Equation (Steady-State)

For a stagnant gas layer of effective thickness $z$ above the pool:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot z} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{P - P_{A,\infty}}{P - P_{A,s}}\right)$$

where:
- $N_A$ = molar flux of vapor (kmol/m²·s)
- $D_{AB}$ = diffusion coefficient of vapor A in air B (m²/s)
- $P$ = total pressure (Pa)
- $R$ = universal gas constant (J/mol·K)
- $T$ = absolute gas temperature (K)
- $z$ = effective gas-film thickness (m)
- $P_{A,s}$ = partial pressure of A at the surface (≈ P_sat)
- $P_{A,\infty}$ = partial pressure of A in the bulk gas (≈ 0 for a pure vapor in clean air)

For the **common engineering case** where the bulk gas is clean air ($P_{A,\infty} \to 0$), the equation simplifies to:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot z} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P - P_{sat}}\right)$$

---

## 2.3 The KTU Standard Formula — Sutton's Equation

The **empirical correlation of Sutton (1953)** is the gold-standard engineering equation used in the KTU 2024 Scheme for predicting vaporization rates from a non-boiling liquid pool:

$$\boxed{\;Q_v = (1.6 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot A \cdot P_{sat}^{\,0.9} \cdot MW^{2/3} \cdot u^{0.78}\;}$$

**Units check (CRITICAL for KTU exam credit):**

| Variable | Symbol | Required SI Units |
|---|---|---|
| Vaporization rate | Q_v | **kg/s** |
| Pool area | A | **m²** |
| Saturation vapor pressure | P_sat | **Pa** |
| Molecular weight of liquid | MW | **kg/kmol** |
| Wind speed at 10 m height | u | **m/s** |

> [!NOTE]
> The constants $1.6 \times 10^{-6}$ (with exponents 0.9, 2/3, 0.78) are valid for **smooth, unobstructed liquid pools** at near-ambient temperature. They are derived empirically and are valid within a specific Reynolds number range.

---

## 2.4 Convective Mass Transfer — Sherwood Number Approach

For engineering precision, the mass-transfer coefficient $K$ is related to the Sherwood number:

$$Sh = \frac{K \cdot L}{D_{AB}} = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Sc^{0.33}$$

where:
- $Sh$ = Sherwood number (dimensionless)
- $Re = \dfrac{u \cdot L}{\nu}$ = Reynolds number
- $Sc = \dfrac{\nu}{D_{AB}}$ = Schmidt number
- $L$ = characteristic length of the pool (m)
- $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity of air (m²/s)

Once $K$ is known, the mass flux is:

$$\dot{m} = K \cdot \frac{P \cdot MW}{R \cdot T} \cdot \frac{P_{sat}}{P} = K \cdot \frac{P_{sat} \cdot MW}{R \cdot T}$$

> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** This formulation is used by regulatory bodies (EPA, OSHA) for **Risk Management Programs (RMP)** and **Process Safety Management (PSM)** audits to estimate worst-case release scenarios for toxic and flammable liquids.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Key Assumption |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_v = K \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{P_{sat} \cdot MW}{R \cdot T_L}$ | General mass-transfer form | Gas-phase control |
| 2 | $Q_v = (1.6 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot A \cdot P_{sat}^{0.9} \cdot MW^{2/3} \cdot u^{0.78}$ | **Sutton's empirical equation** | Smooth pool, u ≥ 0 m/s |
| 3 | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot z} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P - P_{sat}}\right)$ | Stagnant-film (no wind) | $P_{A,\infty} = 0$ |
| 4 | $Sh = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Sc^{0.33}$ | Forced-convection correlation | Turbulent flow over flat plate |
| 5 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{K \cdot M \cdot P_{sat}}{R \cdot T_L}$ | Mass evaporation flux (kg/m²·s) | Pure component |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When $u = 0$ (calm conditions), **Sutton's equation collapses to $Q_v = 0$**, which is physically incorrect. The correct low-wind behavior is captured by the stagnant-film equation (Formula 3). KTU examiners expect students to recognize this limitation.

---

## 2.6 Why This Matters in Process Safety

Estimating the vaporization rate is the **first step in a chain of consequence calculations**:

**Spill → Vaporization Rate → Vapor Cloud Dispersion → Toxic Exposure / Flammable Concentration → Damage / Casualty Estimation**

Without an accurate Q_v, the entire downstream consequence analysis (e.g., ALOHA, PHAST simulations) becomes unreliable. The Sutton equation, despite its age, remains the de-facto industry standard because of its simplicity and reasonable accuracy for screening-level assessments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Fick's Law to the Stagnant-Film Vaporization Equation

### Starting Point — Fick's First Law of Diffusion

The molar flux of vapor A through a stagnant gas B in the z-direction is:

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \cdot \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

For a stagnant film (no net bulk flow in the z-direction), the total molar flux $N_T = 0$, but the diffusive flux of A is balanced by a counter-diffusive flux of B. The corrected equation (using partial pressure form) becomes:

$$N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}}{R \cdot T} \cdot \frac{dP_A}{dz} \cdot \frac{P}{P - P_A}$$

> **Why the P/(P − P_A) factor?** It is the "stagnant-film correction" — it accounts for the fact that as A diffuses upward, B must diffuse downward, creating a small bulk flow that enhances A's transport.

### Step 1: Separate Variables

Rearranging:

$$N_A \cdot dz = -\frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T} \cdot \frac{dP_A}{P - P_A}$$

### Step 2: Integrate Across the Film (z = 0 to z = δ, P_A = P_sat to P_A,∞)

$$\int_{0}^{\delta} N_A \, dz = -\frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T} \int_{P_{sat}}^{P_{A,\infty}} \frac{dP_A}{P - P_A}$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the Integrals

The left side is simply $N_A \cdot \delta$. The right side becomes:

$$N_A \cdot \delta = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T} \cdot \left[\ln(P - P_A)\right]_{P_{sat}}^{P_{A,\infty}}$$

$$N_A \cdot \delta = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{P - P_{A,\infty}}{P - P_{sat}}\right)$$

### Step 4: Solve for N_A

$$\boxed{\;N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot \delta} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{P - P_{A,\infty}}{P - P_{sat}}\right)\;}$$

This is the **fundamental stagnant-film vaporization equation**. For pure vapor in clean air, $P_{A,\infty} \approx 0$, which gives Formula 3 from the cheat sheet.

### Step 5: Convert to Mass Rate

The total mass evaporation rate is $Q_v = N_A \cdot MW \cdot A$:

$$Q_v = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P \cdot MW \cdot A}{R \cdot T \cdot \delta} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P - P_{sat}}\right)$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Standard)

> **Problem:** A circular pool of **benzene** (MW = 78 kg/kmol) of diameter 5 m spills onto a flat surface. The ambient temperature is **25 °C (298.15 K)**, the wind speed at 10 m elevation is **3 m/s**, and the saturation vapor pressure of benzene at 25 °C is **12,700 Pa**. Calculate the vaporization rate using:
> (a) Sutton's empirical equation
> (b) Stagnant-film equation (assume effective film thickness δ = 0.005 m, D_AB = 0.88 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s)
> Given: R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), P = 101,325 Pa.

### Solution Part (a): Sutton's Equation

**Step 1: Compute the pool area.**

$$A = \pi \cdot r^2 = \pi \cdot (2.5)^2 = 19.635 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Substitute into Sutton's equation.**

$$Q_v = (1.6 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot A \cdot P_{sat}^{0.9} \cdot MW^{2/3} \cdot u^{0.78}$$

**Step 3: Compute each term.**

- $A = 19.635 \text{ m}^2$
- $P_{sat}^{0.9} = (12{,}700)^{0.9}$

$$\ln(P_{sat}^{0.9}) = 0.9 \cdot \ln(12{,}700) = 0.9 \cdot 9.4486 = 8.5037$$
$$P_{sat}^{0.9} = e^{8.5037} = 4{,}919.4 \text{ Pa}^{0.9}$$

- $MW^{2/3} = (78)^{2/3}$

$$MW^{1/3} = 78^{0.3333} = 4.273$$
$$MW^{2/3} = (4.273)^2 = 18.26 \text{ (kg/kmol)}^{2/3}$$

- $u^{0.78} = 3^{0.78}$

$$\ln(3^{0.78}) = 0.78 \cdot \ln(3) = 0.78 \cdot 1.0986 = 0.8569$$
$$u^{0.78} = e^{0.8569} = 2.355 \text{ (m/s)}^{0.78}$$

**Step 4: Multiply everything together.**

$$Q_v = (1.6 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 19.635 \cdot 4{,}919.4 \cdot 18.26 \cdot 2.355$$

$$Q_v = (1.6 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 19.635 \cdot 4{,}919.4 = 0.1546$$

$$Q_v = 0.1546 \cdot 18.26 \cdot 2.355 = 6.647 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\boxed{\;Q_v \approx 6.65 \text{ kg/s (Sutton)}\;}$$

### Solution Part (b): Stagnant-Film Equation

**Step 1: Substitute the known values.**

$$Q_v = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P \cdot MW \cdot A}{R \cdot T \cdot \delta} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P - P_{sat}}\right)$$

**Step 2: Compute the logarithmic term.**

$$\frac{P}{P - P_{sat}} = \frac{101{,}325}{101{,}325 - 12{,}700} = \frac{101{,}325}{88{,}625} = 1.1433$$

$$\ln(1.1433) = 0.1339$$

**Step 3: Substitute the numeric values.**

- $D_{AB} = 0.88 \times 10^{-5} = 8.8 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- $P = 101{,}325 \text{ Pa}$
- $MW = 78 \text{ kg/kmol}$
- $A = 19.635 \text{ m}^2$
- $R = 8314 \text{ J/(kmol·K)}$
- $T = 298.15 \text{ K}$
- $\delta = 0.005 \text{ m}$

$$Q_v = \frac{(8.8 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 101{,}325 \cdot 78 \cdot 19.635}{8314 \cdot 298.15 \cdot 0.005} \cdot 0.1339$$

**Step 4: Numerator.**

$$\text{Num} = (8.8 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 101{,}325 \cdot 78 \cdot 19.635 \cdot 0.1339$$
$$\text{Num} = (8.8 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 101{,}325 = 0.8917$$
$$0.8917 \cdot 78 = 69.55$$
$$69.55 \cdot 19.635 = 1365.6$$
$$1365.6 \cdot 0.1339 = 182.85$$

**Step 5: Denominator.**

$$\text{Den} = 8314 \cdot 298.15 \cdot 0.005 = 12{,}393.7$$

**Step 6: Final division.**

$$Q_v = \frac{182.85}{12{,}393.7} = 0.01475 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\boxed{\;Q_v \approx 0.0148 \text{ kg/s (Stagnant Film)}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Insight:** The two methods give very different answers because Sutton's equation is empirical and tuned to real (turbulent) conditions, while the stagnant-film equation with $\delta = 0.005$ m assumes a thick, calm boundary layer. KTU exam problems often ask students to **comment on the difference** and explain why Sutton's value is realistic for actual wind conditions.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Equation

| Scenario | Best Equation | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Wind speed > 0, open pool | **Sutton's equation** | Empirically tuned for forced convection |
| Calm air, indoor release, static pool | **Stagnant-film equation** | Diffusion-controlled, no wind |
| Vaporizing into a flowing gas stream | **Sherwood Number correlation** | Matches boundary layer theory |
| Boiling liquid (T > T_boil) | **Heat-transfer-controlled model** | NOT covered in Module 1 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Gas-Phase Diffusion Model — Conceptual Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the conceptual flow of the vaporization rate estimation methodology, from the physical scenario to the final engineering result.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Liquid Pool Spill] --> B[Exposed Area A]
    A --> C[Surface Temperature T_L]
    A --> D[Vapor Pressure P_sat]
    E[Ambient Wind u] --> F[Air-Water Interface]
    B --> F
    C --> F
    D --> F
    F --> G{Is wind present?}
    G -- Yes u greater than 0 --> H[Use Sutton Equation]
    G -- No u equals 0 --> I[Use Stagnant Film Equation]
    H --> J[Compute Mass Flux]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Multiply by Area A]
    K --> L[Final Vaporization Rate Q_v in kg per s]
    L --> M[Input to Dispersion Model]
    L --> N[Input to Pool Fire Model]
    L --> O[Input to Toxic Exposure Model]
    M --> P[Consequence Analysis Result]
    N --> P
    O --> P
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style L fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style P fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
```

## 4.2 Mass-Transfer Resistance Block Diagram

This block-level architecture maps the **functional resistances** in the vaporization system, in analogy to an electrical circuit.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Liquid Phase Bulk] -->|Resistance R_L approx 0| B[Liquid Vapour Interface]
    B -->|Resistance R_G| C[Gas Phase Bulk]
    C --> D[Ambient Atmosphere]
    B --> E[P_sat at Surface]
    C --> F[P_A infinity in Bulk Air]
    E --> G{Mass Transfer Driving Force}
    F --> G
    G --> H[Vaporization Rate Q_v]
    style A fill:#E0F7FA
    style B fill:#FFF9C4
    style C fill:#C8E6C9
    style G fill:#FFCCBC
    style H fill:#A5D6A7
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The liquid phase offers negligible resistance (well-mixed assumption), so the **entire resistance lies in the gas phase** ($R_G$). This is why KTU Module 1 focuses on gas-phase diffusion/convection as the rate-limiting step.

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Selecting the Right Vaporization Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Liquid Released]) --> Q1{Is liquid at boiling point?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Boil[Use Heat Transfer Model]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is pool in motion or still?}
    Q2 -- Still Pool --> Q3{Is there wind?}
    Q2 -- Moving or Turbulent --> Turb[Use Sutton with Effective u]
    Q3 -- Yes u greater than 0 --> Sutton[Apply Sutton Equation]
    Q3 -- No u equals 0 --> Stag[Apply Stagnant Film Diffusion]
    Sutton --> Result[Q_v in kg per s]
    Stag --> Result
    Turb --> Result
    Boil --> Result
    style Start fill:#E1BEE7
    style Result fill:#C5E1A5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** In the KTU board exam, students are often given a scenario with a specific wind condition. Selecting the *correct* model from this decision tree is worth 2–3 marks by itself.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define vaporization rate and state the assumption made in deriving Sutton's equation for non-boiling liquids.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Vaporization Rate (1 Mark):** The vaporization rate is defined as the mass of liquid transferred to the vapor phase per unit time from a liquid surface, expressed in **kg/s**. It is a key source-term parameter in process safety consequence analysis.

**Assumptions in Sutton's Equation (2 Marks):**

1. The liquid pool is **non-boiling** (evaporation occurs below the boiling point).
2. The liquid surface is **smooth and unobstructed** (no waves or obstacles).
3. The gas phase offers the **dominant mass-transfer resistance** (liquid phase is well-mixed).
4. The partial pressure of the vapor in the bulk atmosphere is **negligible** compared to the saturation vapor pressure at the surface.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between vaporization (evaporation) and boiling of a liquid. Which one is treated as a diffusion-controlled process?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Vaporization (Evaporation) | Boiling |
|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | Below boiling point | At or above boiling point |
| **Controlling Resistance** | Gas-phase mass transfer | Heat transfer (latent heat supply) |
| **Location** | Liquid–air interface | Throughout bulk liquid + interface |
| **Rate Driver** | Concentration gradient of vapor | Heat flux to liquid surface |

**Diffusion-Controlled (1 Mark):** **Vaporization (evaporation)** is treated as a **diffusion-controlled process** because the gas-phase resistance to mass transfer governs the rate, not the heat supply to the liquid.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question B — Choice A** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]`

**(a)** Derive the steady-state equation for the molar flux of a vapor evaporating from a non-boiling liquid pool into a stagnant gas layer of thickness $\delta$ above the surface. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using Sutton's empirical correlation, calculate the vaporization rate of **acetone** (MW = 58 kg/kmol) from a circular pool of diameter 4 m at 20 °C. The wind speed is 2 m/s and the saturation vapor pressure of acetone at 20 °C is 24,600 Pa. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — Model Answer (7 Marks)**

**Assumptions (2 Marks):**

1. Steady-state, one-dimensional diffusion in the z-direction.
2. Liquid is non-boiling; pool is well-mixed and isothermal at temperature $T_L$.
3. Gas phase is stagnant (no bulk flow in z-direction).
4. Bulk gas is clean air; partial pressure of A at infinity is zero.
5. Ideal gas behavior applies.

**Derivation (5 Marks):**

Starting from Fick's First Law with stagnant-film correction:

$$N_A = -\frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T} \cdot \frac{1}{P - P_A} \cdot \frac{dP_A}{dz}$$

Separating variables and integrating from $z = 0$ (surface, $P_A = P_{sat}$) to $z = \delta$ (bulk, $P_A \approx 0$):

$$N_A \int_0^\delta dz = -\frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T} \int_{P_{sat}}^{0} \frac{dP_A}{P - P_A}$$

$$N_A \cdot \delta = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T} \cdot \left[\ln(P - P_A)\right]_{P_{sat}}^{0}$$

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot \delta} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P - P_{sat}}\right)$$

The mass rate is obtained by multiplying by MW and area A:

$$Q_v = N_A \cdot MW \cdot A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P \cdot MW \cdot A}{R \cdot T \cdot \delta} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P - P_{sat}}\right)$$

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — Model Answer (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Pool area.**

$$A = \pi \cdot (2)^2 = 12.566 \text{ m}^2 \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 2: Substitute into Sutton's equation.**

$$Q_v = (1.6 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot A \cdot P_{sat}^{0.9} \cdot MW^{2/3} \cdot u^{0.78} \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 3: Compute powers.**

- $P_{sat}^{0.9} = (24{,}600)^{0.9}$:

$$\ln(P_{sat}^{0.9}) = 0.9 \cdot \ln(24{,}600) = 0.9 \cdot 10.111 = 9.100$$
$$P_{sat}^{0.9} = e^{9.100} = 8{,}994 \text{ Pa}^{0.9} \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

- $MW^{2/3} = (58)^{2/3}$:

$$58^{1/3} = 3.871, \quad MW^{2/3} = 14.99 \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

- $u^{0.78} = 2^{0.78}$:

$$2^{0.78} = e^{0.78 \cdot 0.6931} = e^{0.5406} = 1.717 \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 4: Multiply.**

$$Q_v = (1.6 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 12.566 \cdot 8{,}994 \cdot 14.99 \cdot 1.717$$

$$Q_v = (1.6 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 12.566 = 2.011 \times 10^{-5}$$
$$2.011 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 8{,}994 = 0.1808$$
$$0.1808 \cdot 14.99 = 2.711$$
$$2.711 \cdot 1.717 = 4.654$$

$$\boxed{\;Q_v \approx 4.65 \text{ kg/s} \quad \text{(1 Mark)}\;}$$

---

### **Question B — Choice B** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**(a)** Explain the limitations of Sutton's equation. Under what condition does it fail, and what alternative equation should be used? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 10 m × 10 m pool of **toluene** (MW = 92 kg/kmol) evaporates in still air at 25 °C. The saturation vapor pressure of toluene at 25 °C is 3,800 Pa, and the diffusion coefficient of toluene in air is 0.86 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s. Assuming an effective stagnant gas-film thickness of 0.01 m, calculate the vaporization rate using the stagnant-film diffusion model. Take R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) and total pressure = 101,325 Pa. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — Model Answer (7 Marks)**

**Limitations of Sutton's Equation (5 Marks):**

1. **Empirical constants:** The equation is based on regression of limited experimental data, and is not derived from first principles.
2. **Validity range:** Valid only for smooth, unobstructed pools in open terrain; fails for confined spills, obstructed pools, or pools with waves.
3. **Zero-wind failure:** When $u = 0$, the equation predicts $Q_v = 0$, which is physically incorrect — evaporation still occurs via molecular diffusion.
4. **Single-component assumption:** Does not account for multi-component mixtures (e.g., gasoline) where Raoult's law must be used for partial vapor pressure.
5. **Isothermal assumption:** Assumes constant liquid temperature; ignores evaporative cooling of the pool over time.
6. **No chemistry:** Ignores reactive systems where vapor may react in the atmosphere (e.g., acid mist neutralization).

**Alternative for zero-wind (2 Marks):**

When $u = 0$, use the **stagnant-film diffusion equation**:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot \delta} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P - P_{sat}}\right)$$

This is derived from Fick's Law and correctly predicts non-zero flux even in still air.

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — Model Answer (7 Marks)**

**Given Data:**

- Area: $A = 10 \times 10 = 100 \text{ m}^2$
- $P_{sat} = 3{,}800 \text{ Pa}$
- $D_{AB} = 0.86 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- $\delta = 0.01 \text{ m}$
- $T = 298.15 \text{ K}$
- $MW = 92 \text{ kg/kmol}$
- $P = 101{,}325 \text{ Pa}$, $R = 8314 \text{ J/(kmol·K)}$

**Step 1: Compute the logarithmic term. (2 Marks)**

$$\frac{P}{P - P_{sat}} = \frac{101{,}325}{101{,}325 - 3{,}800} = \frac{101{,}325}{97{,}525} = 1.0390$$

$$\ln(1.0390) = 0.03827$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the stagnant-film equation. (2 Marks)**

$$Q_v = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P \cdot MW \cdot A}{R \cdot T \cdot \delta} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P - P_{sat}}\right)$$

$$Q_v = \frac{(0.86 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot 101{,}325 \cdot 92 \cdot 100}{8314 \cdot 298.15 \cdot 0.01} \cdot 0.03827$$

**Step 3: Numerator. (1 Mark)**

$$(0.86 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot 101{,}325 = 0.8714$$
$$0.8714 \cdot 92 = 80.17$$
$$80.17 \cdot 100 = 8{,}017$$
$$8{,}017 \cdot 0.03827 = 306.8$$

**Step 4: Denominator. (1 Mark)**

$$8314 \cdot 298.15 \cdot 0.01 = 24{,}787$$

**Step 5: Final answer. (1 Mark)**

$$Q_v = \frac{306.8}{24{,}787} = 0.01238 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\boxed{\;Q_v \approx 0.0124 \text{ kg/s}\;}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (loses 2–3 marks):** Students frequently substitute $P_{sat}$ in **kPa** or **mmHg** instead of **Pa** in Sutton's equation. Always convert to SI Pa.
> 2. **Wrong R value (loses 1 mark):** Use **R = 8314 J/(kmol·K)** if MW is in kg/kmol; use **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** if MW is in kg/mol. Mixing these is a frequent error.
> 3. **Zero-wind trap (loses 2 marks):** Plugging $u = 0$ into Sutton's equation gives 0 — students must recognize this and switch to the stagnant-film model.
> 4. **Missing logarithmic term (loses 1 mark):** Some students write $Q_v = K \cdot A \cdot (P_{sat}/RT)$ and forget the $\ln$ correction factor that arises from the stagnant-film derivation.
> 5. **Sign error in integration limits (loses 1 mark):** In the derivation, the limits must be applied in the correct order ($P_{sat} \to P_{A,\infty}$, not reversed).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Vaporization rate** is a **mass-transfer-controlled** quantity, not a heat-transfer-controlled one (for non-boiling liquids).
- The **liquid phase resistance is neglected** in standard KTU problems — only the gas phase matters.
- **Sutton's equation** is the industry standard: $Q_v = (1.6 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot A \cdot P_{sat}^{0.9} \cdot MW^{2/3} \cdot u^{0.78}$, valid only when wind speed $u > 0$.
- **Stagnant-film equation** (from Fick's Law) is the fallback for **zero-wind / calm conditions**.
- The **stagnant-film correction factor** $P/(P - P_{sat})$ accounts for counter-diffusion of air.
- **Sherwood Number** correlations ($Sh = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Sc^{0.33}$) provide a more rigorous alternative to Sutton's empirical constants.
- **SI units are mandatory:** A in m², P in Pa, MW in kg/kmol, u in m/s, T in K.
- The logarithmic driving force $\ln[P/(P - P_{sat})]$ becomes important as $P_{sat}$ approaches $P$ (volatile liquids near their boiling point).
- **Real-world application:** Output of this calculation feeds directly into dispersion models (e.g., Gaussian plume, heavy-gas models) for toxic gas exposure and flammable cloud size estimation.
- **Limitation summary:** Sutton's equation is for **smooth, isothermal, single-component, non-boiling, unobstructed pools** in **open outdoor terrain**.

> **Final KTU Study Tip:** Memorize Sutton's equation, its units, and the **stagnant-film derivation** cold. The ability to switch between the two models based on wind condition is the single most testable skill in this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimating Worker Exposure During Vessel Filling Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT616 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Estimating Worker Exposure During Vessel Filling Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worker Exposure Estimation During Vessel Filling** is a quantitative Industrial Hygiene (IH) technique used in Process Safety to predict the **Breathing Zone Concentration (BZC)** of a volatile/semi-volatile chemical in the air surrounding an operator while a liquid is being transferred into an open or closed vessel. The estimate is built from the **Vapor Generation Rate (G)** of the chemical at the liquid surface, the **Ventilation Dilution Rate (Q_b)** of the workspace, and the **Near-Field / Far-Field (NF/FF)** mass-transport geometry of the room.

In the KTU 2024 OBE framework, this topic maps directly to:
- **CO1**: Identify and quantify hazards in chemical process plants.
- **CO2**: Apply modeling techniques for consequence analysis.
- **Bloom's Level**: Apply (L3) / Analyse (L4).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Perfume Bottle Analogy** — Imagine you slowly pour warm perfume from a bottle into a small dish on a table. You *smell* the perfume instantly. The smell you detect at your nose is your **exposure**. Three things control how strong that smell is:
> 1. How fast the perfume "escapes" the dish (the **generation rate**, governed by how volatile and warm the liquid is).
> 2. How big the room is and how drafty the window is (the **ventilation rate**).
> 3. How close your nose is to the dish (the **near-field vs. far-field** geometry).
>
> Worker exposure estimation is exactly the same engineering problem — replace "perfume" with **benzene, toluene, acetone, or hexane**, replace "dish" with a **process vessel**, and replace "nose" with the **worker's breathing zone**.

## 1.3 Why This Topic is a KTU High-Yield Module

Vessel filling (charging reactors, drums, mixers, tank trucks) is the **single most common source of routine chemical exposure** in CPI plants. Regulatory bodies (OSHA, EPA, ACGIH) require quantitative exposure estimates whenever a new process is designed or an existing one is modified.

Key Standard Metrics Used in This Module (referenced throughout):
- **G** — Vapor generation rate, units **mg/s** or **g/min**.
- **C_sat** — Saturated vapor concentration in air, units **mg/m³** or **ppm**.
- **Q_b** — Volumetric ventilation (breathing) rate of the workroom, **m³/s**.
- **Q_f** — Volumetric liquid filling rate, **m³/s**.
- **β** — Near-Field to Far-Field ratio (dimensionless, typically 3 to 100).
- **K** — Mass transfer coefficient at the liquid surface, **m/s**.

## 1.4 Categorisation of Exposure Models

There are three families of models, listed from simplest to most rigorous:

| Model Tier | Model Name | Assumption | Typical Use |
|------------|-----------|------------|-------------|
| Tier 1 | **Vapor Displacement Model** | All vapor in the headspace of the filled liquid is pushed out as plug flow. | Volatile liquids (acetone, hexane) filled into empty vessels. |
| Tier 2 | **Nicas Mass Transfer Model (1988)** | Vapor escapes the liquid by molecular diffusion/convective mass transfer across the free surface. | Mid-volatility liquids, splash filling, open vats. |
| Tier 3 | **Crump & Sexton Complete Model (EPA, 1986)** | Combines both displacement *and* mass transfer; accounts for non-equilibrium evaporation. | Refined regulatory and Tier-1 IH assessments. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Picture of Vessel Filling

When a worker pours liquid chemical **A** into a vessel, three physical processes act **simultaneously**:

1. **Vapor Displacement (Plug-Flow)**: The incoming liquid occupies volume in the vessel that was previously filled with air. The headspace air (already saturated with vapor of A) is pushed out through the open top. This is instantaneous, deterministic, and is dominant when the vessel is **initially empty** and the liquid is **highly volatile**.

2. **Mass Transfer from the Liquid Surface**: As soon as liquid is in the vessel, evaporation occurs at the air–liquid interface. This is governed by Fick's law and a mass-transfer coefficient. This is dominant when the liquid is **moderately volatile** or the vessel was **pre-charged** with liquid.

3. **Splash and Aerosol Generation**: When the liquid jet impacts the existing liquid surface, droplets and aerosols are ejected. These settle back or evaporate rapidly, contributing to short-term, near-field exposure. Important for high filling heights (free-fall) and high flow rates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the KTU examiner care about this topic?**
> Because in real plants, the *true* vapor generation rate is often **between** the displacement prediction and the mass-transfer prediction. The Crump–Seston (EPA) **Complete Model** adds the two contributions, weighted by the prevailing physics, to give a defensible estimate.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> The following table contains the **complete, exam-ready formula set** for the topic. Every constant, unit, and boundary condition is listed. Master this table and you can solve any vessel-filling exposure problem KTU may set.

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary / Limiting Condition |
|---|---------------|---------|-------------------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | Saturation Vapor Pressure (Antoine) | $\ln(P_{sat}) = A - \dfrac{B}{T+C}$ | $P_{sat}$ in **mmHg** or **Pa**; $T$ in **°C** or **K**; $A,B,C$ are chemical-specific Antoine constants. | Valid roughly $0.1 \text{ kPa} \le P_{sat} \le 101.3 \text{ kPa}$. |
| 2 | Saturation Concentration in Air | $C_{sat} = \dfrac{P_{sat} \cdot M}{R \cdot T}$ | $C_{sat}$ in **mg/m³**; $M$ in **g/mol**; $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$; $T$ in **K**. | Ideal-gas assumption. |
| 3 | Vapor Displacement Generation Rate | $G_{disp} = Q_f \cdot C_{sat}$ | $Q_f$ = liquid filling rate **[m³/s]**; $C_{sat}$ in **[mg/m³]**. | True if vessel is initially empty and headspace is saturated. |
| 4 | Mass-Transfer Generation Rate (Nicas) | $G_{mt} = K \cdot S \cdot C_{sat}$ | $K$ = mass-transfer coefficient **[m/s]**; $S$ = liquid free-surface area **[m²]**. | True if $K$ is known or estimated. |
| 5 | Complete Model Generation Rate (Crump–Sexton) | $G_{total} = Q_f \cdot C_{sat} + K \cdot S \cdot C_{sat}$ | Sum of displacement and mass-transfer terms **[mg/s]**. | Used when both mechanisms are active. |
| 6 | Well-Mixed Room (Far-Field) Concentration | $C_{ff} = \dfrac{G_{total}}{Q_b}$ | $Q_b$ = ventilation flow rate **[m³/s]**. | Steady-state, well-mixed room. |
| 7 | Near-Field Concentration (NF/FF model) | $C_{nf} = \beta \cdot C_{ff}$ | $\beta$ = NF/FF ratio (dimensionless). | $\beta$ typically 3 to 100; default $\beta = 10$. |
| 8 | Average Exposure Over Task Duration | $C_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{t_{task}} \int_{0}^{t_{task}} C(t) \, dt$ | $t_{task}$ in **s** or **min**. | Used when filling is non-steady. |
| 9 | Ventilation Rate from ACH | $Q_b = \dfrac{ACH \cdot V_{room}}{3600}$ | $ACH$ = air changes per hour **[1/hr]**; $V_{room}$ in **m³**; $Q_b$ in **m³/s**. | General-purpose room dilution. |
| 10 | Non-Steady Mass-Balance (Dynamic Filling) | $V \dfrac{dC}{dt} = G_{total} - Q_b \cdot C$ | $V$ = room volume **[m³]**. | Solved for $C(t)$ when task is short. |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Process Safety

- **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)**: Before commissioning, exposure estimates feed into the **LOPA** and **HAZOP** worksheets as the *frequency* of an acute overexposure event.
- **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) Selection**: Predicted BZC vs. **TLV–TWA / TLV–STEL** (ACGIH) determines whether an air-line respirator or just goggles are required.
- **OSHA / EPA Risk Assessment**: Tier-1 IH assessments for new chemicals (e.g., TSCA, REACH dossiers) routinely use the Crump–Sexton equations.
- **Ventilation Design Engineering**: Required air changes per hour (ACH) for a new drum-filling cell are back-calculated from these equations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **TLV–TWA** (Threshold Limit Value – Time Weighted Average) and **TLV–STEL** (Short Term Exposure Limit) are *legal-scientific benchmarks* against which $C_{avg}$ and $C_{nf}$ are compared. If $C_{nf} \gt $ TLV–STEL, the design is unsafe; if $C_{avg} \gt $ TLV–TWA, administrative controls (rotation, breaks) or engineering controls must be upgraded.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Antoine Equation to C_sat

The **saturated vapor pressure** of a pure liquid at temperature $T$ is given by the **Antoine equation**, which is empirical but extremely accurate for most process chemicals.

The relationship between $P_{sat}$ and the equilibrium vapor concentration $C_{sat}$ comes from the **ideal gas law**:

$$
P \cdot V = n \cdot R \cdot T
$$

Solving for the molar concentration $n/V$ and multiplying by the molar mass $M$ gives the mass concentration:

$$
C_{sat} = \dfrac{n}{V} \cdot M = \dfrac{P_{sat}}{R \cdot T} \cdot M
$$

**Step 1 — Restate the ideal gas law in terms of molar concentration:**

$$
\dfrac{n}{V} = \dfrac{P_{sat}}{R \cdot T}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert molar concentration to mass concentration by multiplying by the molar mass $M$ (in kg/kmol or g/mol consistently):**

$$
C_{sat} = \dfrac{P_{sat} \cdot M}{R \cdot T}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply unit consistency.** A convenient unit set used in IH:

- $P_{sat}$ in **Pa** ($\text{N/m}^2$)
- $M$ in **kg/kmol** (so result is $\text{kg/m}^3$)
- $R = 8314 \text{ J/(kmol·K)}$
- $T$ in **K**

> **Result — the master saturation concentration equation:**

$$
C_{sat} \,[\text{kg/m}^3] = \dfrac{P_{sat} \,[\text{Pa}] \cdot M \,[\text{kg/kmol}]}{8314 \,[\text{J/(kmol·K)}] \cdot T \,[\text{K}]}
$$

For SI small units (mg/m³), simply multiply the result by $10^6$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Vapor Displacement Generation Rate (Tier 1)

Consider a vessel of cross-section $A_v$ being filled at a liquid volumetric rate $Q_f$.

**Step 1 — Volumetric rate at which the headspace is compressed:**

$$
Q_{head} = Q_f \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{s}]
$$

**Step 2 — Assume the headspace air is fully saturated with vapor of the liquid at temperature $T$:**

$$
\text{Vapor mass rate out} = Q_{head} \cdot C_{sat}
$$

**Step 3 — Therefore, the displacement generation rate is:**

$$
G_{disp} = Q_f \cdot C_{sat}
$$

This is a **direct proportionality** between the filling rate and the saturation concentration. Doubling the pump flow doubles the exposure source strength — a key intuition for KTU problems.

## 3.3 Derivation: Nicas Mass-Transfer Rate (Tier 2)

For an **open, partially filled vessel** with a free liquid surface of area $S$, evaporation is governed by the two-film theory.

**Step 1 — The flux of vapor leaving the surface (Fick's first law with a mass-transfer coefficient):**

$$
N = K \cdot (C_{sat} - C_{bulk})
$$

where $K$ is the overall mass-transfer coefficient (m/s) and $C_{bulk}$ is the bulk-air concentration just above the surface.

**Step 2 — If the bulk air is well below saturation (typical for ventilated workrooms), $C_{bulk} \ll C_{sat}$:**

$$
N \approx K \cdot C_{sat}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by the free-surface area to get the total mass generation rate:**

$$
G_{mt} = K \cdot S \cdot C_{sat}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Crump–Sexton Complete Model (Tier 3)

The complete model simply **adds** the displacement and mass-transfer contributions because they occur in parallel physical regions (the inlet jet vs. the bulk free surface):

$$
G_{total} = G_{disp} + G_{mt} = Q_f \cdot C_{sat} + K \cdot S \cdot C_{sat}
$$

$$
G_{total} = C_{sat} \cdot (Q_f + K \cdot S)
$$

**Important KTU Insight**: When the liquid is **very volatile** (e.g., acetone, $P_{sat} \approx 24$ kPa at 20 °C) and the filling is **fast**, $Q_f \gg K \cdot S$ and the displacement term dominates. When the liquid is **moderately volatile** (e.g., xylene, $P_{sat} \approx 1.2$ kPa at 20 °C) and filling is **slow**, the mass-transfer term dominates.

## 3.5 Derivation: Far-Field (Workroom) Concentration

Apply a **steady-state mass balance** to the entire workroom of volume $V$:

$$
\text{In} - \text{Out} + \text{Generation} = \text{Accumulation}
$$

At steady state, accumulation = 0:

$$
0 - Q_b \cdot C_{ff} + G_{total} = 0
$$

$$
C_{ff} = \dfrac{G_{total}}{Q_b}
$$

This is the **classic well-mixed room equation** used in essentially every IH textbook.

## 3.6 Derivation: Near-Field Concentration (NF/FF Geometry)

The **Near-Field** is the small hemisphere (or hemisphere-like zone) of air within $\sim 1$ m of the source where the worker actually inhales. The **Far-Field** is the rest of the room. The empirically observed ratio is:

$$
\beta = \dfrac{C_{nf}}{C_{ff}}
$$

with typical default $\beta = 10$ when the source-to-worker distance is 0.5 m to 1 m. The exposure of the worker is therefore:

$$
C_{nf} = \beta \cdot C_{ff} = \beta \cdot \dfrac{G_{total}}{Q_b}
$$

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (Full Pipeline)

**Problem Statement (typical KTU 14-mark style):**
A worker fills an open drum with **toluene** at a rate of $Q_f = 5 \text{ L/min}$. The drum has a free-surface diameter of 0.45 m. The workroom is $6 \text{ m} \times 6 \text{ m} \times 3 \text{ m}$ with $Q_b = 0.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ ventilation. Toluene properties: $M = 92 \text{ g/mol}$, $P_{sat}(25°\text{C}) = 3.8 \text{ kPa}$, $K = 0.002 \text{ m/s}$. Estimate the worker's BZC using the Crump–Sexton complete model, and compare to TLV–TWA of toluene (50 ppm = 188 mg/m³). Use $\beta = 10$.

**Step 1 — Compute $C_{sat}$:**

$$
C_{sat} = \dfrac{P_{sat} \cdot M}{R \cdot T} = \dfrac{3800 \, \text{Pa} \cdot 0.092 \, \text{kg/mol}}{8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)} \cdot 298 \, \text{K}}
$$

$$
C_{sat} = \dfrac{349.6}{2477.6} \approx 0.1411 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 141100 \text{ mg/m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Convert $Q_f$ to SI:**

$$
Q_f = 5 \text{ L/min} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{60} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 8.333 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the free-surface area of the drum:**

$$
S = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (0.45)^2}{4} \approx 0.159 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Compute displacement generation rate:**

$$
G_{disp} = Q_f \cdot C_{sat} = 8.333 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 141100 \approx 11.76 \text{ mg/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute mass-transfer generation rate:**

$$
G_{mt} = K \cdot S \cdot C_{sat} = 0.002 \cdot 0.159 \cdot 141100 \approx 44.87 \text{ mg/s}
$$

**Step 6 — Total generation rate (Crump–Sexton):**

$$
G_{total} = 11.76 + 44.87 \approx 56.63 \text{ mg/s}
$$

**Step 7 — Far-field concentration:**

$$
C_{ff} = \dfrac{56.63}{0.5} = 113.26 \text{ mg/m}^3
$$

**Step 8 — Near-field (breathing-zone) concentration:**

$$
C_{nf} = \beta \cdot C_{ff} = 10 \cdot 113.26 = 1132.6 \text{ mg/m}^3
$$

**Step 9 — Compare to TLV–TWA (188 mg/m³):**

$$
\text{Hazard Index (HI)} = \dfrac{C_{nf}}{\text{TLV–TWA}} = \dfrac{1132.6}{188} \approx 6.02
$$

**Conclusion**: $\text{HI} = 6.02 \gg 1.0$. The design is **unsafe** under current conditions. Recommended actions: (a) install local exhaust ventilation (LEV) at the drum opening, (b) reduce filling rate, (c) mandate airline respirator for the operator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Hazard Index (HI) framework** — Whenever you compute a worker BZC in a KTU problem, ALWAYS divide it by the relevant OEL (TLV, PEL, REL) and report the dimensionless ratio. $\text{HI} > 1$ indicates an unsafe design.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Exposure Estimation Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **functional flow** of the calculation, from raw chemical/physical inputs to the final hazard index. This is the *exact order* in which a KTU full-mark answer should be structured.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["INPUT: Chemical properties M, Psat, K"] --> nodeB["INPUT: Operational data Qf, S, T, Qb"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["STEP 1: Compute saturation concentration C_sat"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["STEP 2: Displacement generation G_disp = Qf x C_sat"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["STEP 3: Mass transfer generation G_mt = K x S x C_sat"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["STEP 4: Total generation G_total = G_disp + G_mt"]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG["STEP 5: Far-field concentration C_ff = G_total / Qb"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["STEP 6: Near-field concentration C_nf = beta x C_ff"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["STEP 7: Compute Hazard Index HI = C_nf / OEL"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ{"DECISION: HI > 1?"}
    nodeJ -->|YES| nodeK["UNSAFE: Add LEV / Reduce Qf / Mandate PPE"]
    nodeJ -->|NO| nodeL["SAFE: Document and proceed"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Physical View of Vessel Filling

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **physical zones** involved in a worker exposure scenario. Note the use of purely alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters to comply with the safeguard rules.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    workerW["WORKER Breathing zone NF"] --> exposureE["EXPOSURE PATH Inhalation"]
    exposureE --> vesselV["OPEN VESSEL Free liquid surface S"]
    vesselV --> liquidL["INCOMING LIQUID JET Qf"]
    liquidL --> displacementD["DISPLACEMENT PATH Qf x C_sat"]
    vesselV --> masstransferM["MASS TRANSFER PATH K x S x C_sat"]
    displacementD --> generationG["TOTAL GENERATION RATE G_total"]
    masstransferM --> generationG
    generationG --> dilutionQ["VENTILATION DILUTION Qb"]
    dilutionQ --> farfieldF["FAR FIELD C_ff = G_total / Qb"]
    farfieldF --> nearfieldN["NEAR FIELD C_nf = beta x C_ff"]
    nearfieldN --> workerW
```

## 4.3 Decision-Tree: Which Model Should the Student Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1["Vessel initially empty?"] --> q2{"Is the liquid highly volatile P_sat > 10 kPa?"}
    q2 -->|YES| ans1["USE Tier 1 Vapor Displacement Model"]
    q2 -->|NO| q3{"Is K x S comparable to Qf?"}
    q3 -->|YES| ans2["USE Tier 3 Crump Sexton Complete Model"]
    q3 -->|NO| q4{"K x S dominates?"}
    q4 -->|YES| ans3["USE Tier 2 Nicas Mass Transfer Model"]
    q4 -->|NO| ans1
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU exam, the model choice is normally *directed* in the question stem (e.g., "Using the Crump–Sexton model, estimate..."). However, in design problems you must select the model yourself. The decision tree above is the recommended strategy.
