It is relevant because it can defeat conventional vent sizing calculations designed for isolated vessels, and must be specifically accounted for in multi-compartment process equipment such as baghouse filters, cyclones, and ductwork. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2 Choice Question Set]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3 (a). A propane-air mixture exhibits $(dP/dt)_{max} = 120$ bar/s when tested in a 1 m³ closed vessel. Estimate $(dP/dt)_{max}$ in (i) a 27 m³ storage tank and (ii) a 125 m³ bulk container. Comment on the engineering implication. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V_1 = 1$ m³, $(dP/dt)_{max,1} = 120$ bar/s
- Find $(dP/dt)_{max,2}$ for $V_2 = 27$ m³ and $V_2 = 125$ m³

**Step 1 — Apply the Cubic Law:**
$$\left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max,2} = \left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max,1} \cdot \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate for $V_2 = 27$ m³:**
$$\left(\frac{1}{27}\right)^{1/3} = \frac{1}{3} = 0.333$$
$$\left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max,2} = 120 \cdot 0.333 = 40 \text{ bar/s}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate for $V_2 = 125$ m³:**
$$\left(\frac{1}{125}\right)^{1/3} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.200$$
$$\left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max,2} = 120 \cdot 0.200 = 24 \text{ bar/s}$$

**Engineering Implication:** Although larger vessels have a *lower* peak rate of pressure rise per unit time, the *total* pressure rise is sustained for a longer duration and the absolute pressure reached ($P_{max}$) is approximately constant. Therefore, large vessels still require robust explosion protection. The milder rate is offset by the larger vent area required (proportional to $V^{2/3}$). (7 marks)

---

**Q3 (b). With a neat diagram, explain the pressure–time history of a gas explosion in a closed vessel. Identify the location of $P_{max}$ and $(dP/dt)_{max}$ on the curve. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A closed-vessel gas explosion has a characteristic S-shaped pressure–time curve divided into four phases:

**Phase 1 — Induction Period:** From ignition to flame kernel formation, pressure remains essentially at $P_{atm}$ (negligible duration).

**Phase 2 — Propagation Phase:** Spherical flame front expands from the ignition point. The pressure rises with increasing slope (rate of pressure rise accelerates as flame radius grows). The **inflection point** of the curve is where $(dP/dt)_{max}$ is recorded.

**Phase 3 — Peak Pressure Phase:** The flame front reaches the vessel wall; the entire volume is filled with hot combustion products. Pressure reaches its absolute maximum $P_{max}$ (typically 7–9 bar gauge for hydrocarbons in air at atmospheric initial conditions).

**Phase 4 — Plateau/Cooling Phase:** If the vessel is truly adiabatic, pressure remains near $P_{max}$ (plateau). In practice, wall cooling causes a slow pressure decay.

**Curve Sketch (ASCII representation):**

```
P (bar)
  |
Pmax|........_______
  |       /
  |      /
  |     /  <-- (dP/dt)_max = slope here
  |    /
  |   /
  |  /
  | /
  |/______________________ t (s)
   t_ign   inflection
```

**Identification:**
- $P_{max}$ is the **peak (apex)** of the curve.
- $(dP/dt)_{max}$ is the **maximum slope**, occurring at the inflection point of the rising portion (typically when the flame radius is about 0.4–0.5 of the vessel radius for spherical propagation).

(7 marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4 (a). Derive the Cubic Law (Hopkinson's Law) for confined gas explosions. State the assumptions clearly. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Closed spherical vessel of radius $R$ and volume $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3$.
2. Ignition at the geometric center.
3. Spherical flame propagation at constant laminar burning velocity $S_u$.
4. Combustion is essentially isobaric at the flame front (valid for low-Mach-number deflagrations).
5. Combustion is adiabatic.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Flame radius at time $t$:
$$r_f(t) = S_u \cdot t$$

**Step 2:** Volume of burned gas (sphere of radius $r_f$):
$$V_b = \frac{4}{3}\pi r_f^3 = \frac{4}{3}\pi S_u^3 t^3$$

**Step 3:** Burn fraction:
$$x = \frac{V_b}{V} = \frac{S_u^3 t^3}{R^3}$$

**Step 4:** Mass conservation — total moles fixed at $n_0 = n_b + n_u$, where subscript $b$ = burned, $u$ = unburned.

**Step 5:** From ideal gas law applied to the unburned gas (which gets compressed and heated as the flame advances), the pressure of the unburned gas depends on its temperature rise and compression. Differentiating the pressure relation with respect to time:
$$\frac{dP}{dt} = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial r_f}\right) \cdot \frac{dr_f}{dt} = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial r_f}\right) \cdot S_u$$

**Step 6:** Substituting the spherical geometry and the ideal gas law for the unburned gas region, after algebraic manipulation one finds that the dominant term in the early-to-mid combustion phase scales as:
$$\frac{dP}{dt} \propto \frac{S_u^3 \cdot (\text{thermo factor})}{R^3}$$

**Step 7:** Since $R \propto V^{1/3}$, this becomes:
$$\left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right) \cdot V = \text{const} \cdot V^{2/3}$$

A more rigorous treatment (accounting for the $r_f$ dependence near the vessel wall) yields the **cube-root** form:
$$\boxed{\left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3} = K_G = \text{const for given mixture}}$$

(7 marks)

---

**Q4 (b). A reactor contains 500 kg of cyclohexane ($\Delta H_c = 43.9$ MJ/kg). If 8% of the inventory is released, mixes with air, and the resulting vapor cloud explodes inside a partially confined shed with an explosion yield factor $\eta = 0.04$, calculate the TNT equivalent mass. Use $E_{TNT} = 4680$ kJ/kg. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Released mass:**
$$m_{\text{released}} = 0.08 \cdot 500 = 40 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Energy released (gross, ideal):**
$$E_{\text{gross}} = m_{\text{released}} \cdot \Delta H_c = 40 \cdot 43{,}900 = 1{,}756{,}000 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Effective explosion energy (with yield factor):**
$$E_{\text{exp}} = \eta \cdot E_{\text{gross}} = 0.04 \cdot 1{,}756{,}000 = 70{,}240 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 4 — TNT equivalent mass:**
$$m_{TNT} = \frac{E_{\text{exp}}}{E_{TNT}} = \frac{70{,}240}{4680} = 15.0 \text{ kg of TNT}$$

**Conclusion:** The 40 kg cyclohexane release is equivalent in blast damage to **15.0 kg of TNT**. The yield factor $\eta = 0.04$ is a critical parameter that accounts for incomplete combustion, mechanical inefficiencies, and energy radiated as heat rather than blast. (7 marks)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing $V^{1/3}$ with $V$** in the Cubic Law. The exponent is **1/3**, NOT 1. Many students write $(dP/dt) \cdot V = K_G$ — this is **wrong** and costs 2–3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting to use absolute pressure or gauge pressure consistently.** $P_{max}$ is typically expressed as **gauge** (above atmospheric), but the ideal gas law calculations use **absolute** pressure.
> 3. **Confusing $K_G$ (gases) with $K_{St}$ (dusts).** They are analogous but use different test standards. $K_{St}$ dust classes: St-1 (0–200), St-2 (200–300), St-3 (>300) bar·m/s.
> 4. **TNT equivalency with $\eta$ omitted.** If the problem states "5% of fuel participates," this is the yield factor — students often forget to multiply by it.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert units** (e.g., kJ to MJ, bar to kPa, m³ to L for the 20-L Siwek apparatus).
> 6. **In vent-area problems, students often omit the $V^{2/3}$ dependence**, leading to massively undersized vents.

---

### 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **The 15 High-Yield Points Every KTU Student Must Remember:**

- [x] A **confined explosion** occurs when combustion is constrained by vessel walls, causing pressure buildup.
- [x] Two key parameters describe severity: **$P_{max}$** (peak pressure) and **$(dP/dt)_{max}$** (peak rate of pressure rise).
- [x] **$P_{max}$ is roughly independent of vessel size** (for a given fuel-air mixture).
- [x] **$(dP/dt)_{max}$ scales as $V^{-1/3}$** — the Cubic Law.
- [x] The Cubic Law constant is called the **deflagration index $K_G$** (gases) or **$K_{St}$** (dusts).
- [x] $K_G$ is measured in a standardized **1 m³ sphere** (ASTM E1226); for dusts in a **20 L Siwek sphere**.
- [x] **Turbulence dramatically amplifies** both $P_{max}$ and $(dP/dt)_{max}$.
- [x] **Obstructions** (baffles, pipe bends) increase flame stretch and turbulence → higher severity.
- [x] **Pressure piling** in multi-compartment equipment can cause 2–5× higher $P_{max}$ than isolated vessels.
- [x] **Deflagration** is subsonic flame propagation; **detonation** is supersonic shock-driven.
- [x] **TNT equivalency** uses $E_{TNT} = 4680$ kJ/kg and a yield factor $\eta$ (typically 0.02–0.10).
- [x] **Vent area** scales roughly as $V^{2/3}$ (NFPA 68 form): $A_v = C \cdot V^{2/3} \cdot (dP/dt)_{max}^{0.5} / P_{red}^{0.5}$.
- [x] **Inherent design** (inventory reduction, substitution) is the highest level of explosion protection.
- [x] **Laminar burning velocity** $S_u$ governs flame speed; hydrogen has the highest $S_u$ among common fuels.
- [x] The **Hopkinson number / Cubic Law** is the single most-tested equation in KTU 2024 confined explosion problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blast Damage Resulting from Overpressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT616 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Blast Damage Resulting from Overpressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

**Blast Damage Resulting from Overpressure** refers to the destructive consequences (on people, structures, and equipment) generated by the abrupt and rapid release of energy that produces a pressure wave (shock wave) propagating through the surrounding atmosphere. The key parameter governing damage is the **peak side-on (incident) overpressure**, $P_{so}$, which is the pressure above ambient in the wave front measured at a station perpendicular to the direction of propagation.

$$P_{so} = P_{peak} - P_{atm}$$

The associated parameter is the **reflected overpressure**, $P_{r}$, which acts when the shock wave strikes a surface oriented perpendicular to its travel:

$$P_{r} = 2 P_{so} + \frac{6 P_{so}^2}{P_{so} + 7 P_{atm}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The blast damage topic typically sits inside the broader module of "Fire and Explosion Hazard Analysis." Examiners expect students to know the difference between **deflagration** (subsonic, $P_{so}$ < 1 bar) and **detonation** (sonic/supersonic, $P_{so}$ > 1 bar), as the pressure-damage correlation differs for each.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine dropping a pebble (TNT equivalent mass, $W_{TNT}$) into a perfectly still pond (the surrounding air). The circular ripple that travels outward represents the **blast wave**. The **height of the wave** at the shoreline corresponds to the **peak overpressure**. A small pebble (small explosion) produces a thin ripple — only delicate items at the shore break. A boulder (large explosion) produces a tall, violent wave — even strong buildings collapse. The **cube-root scaling law** is the engineering principle that tells us how far from the shoreline a wave of a given height will travel; doubling the explosive mass does not double the destruction distance — it only increases it by roughly $2^{1/3} \approx 1.26$ times.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant Used in Blast Analysis:**
> - Heat of combustion of TNT: $\Delta H_{TNT} = 4686 \; \text{kJ/kg} = 4.686 \; \text{MJ/kg}$
> - Standard sea-level atmospheric pressure: $P_{atm} = 101.325 \; \text{kPa} = 1.01325 \; \text{bar}$
> - Standard ambient temperature: $T_0 = 288.15 \; \text{K}$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physics of an Explosion — Step-by-Step Logic

A chemical explosion (deflagration or detonation of a vapor cloud, condensed-phase high explosive, or pressure vessel burst) converts chemical energy into mechanical work in essentially zero time. The sequence of damage-generating phenomena is:

1. **Energy release:** Rapid oxidation or sudden phase change liberates energy $E$ at the source.
2. **Primary shock front formation:** The expanding gas creates a discontinuity — the **blast wave** — moving at speed $U > c_0$ (speed of sound in air, $\approx 343$ m/s).
3. **Air ingestion & expansion:** Surrounding air is pushed outward, raising local density and pressure at the wave front.
4. **Propagation & decay:** As the wave moves outward over distance $R$, the wave front radius grows; by conservation of energy, the peak overpressure **decays monotonically** with $R$.
5. **Damage interaction:** When the wave meets a target (person, wall, vessel), the energy is partially reflected, partially absorbed — producing structural damage and bodily injury.

### 2.2 Why Use TNT Equivalence?

Chemical process incidents seldom involve pure TNT. Engineers need a **common yardstick** to compare a release of hydrocarbon vapor to a standardized explosive. The **TNT Equivalence Method** (used in API RP 753, AIChE/CCPS guidelines, and KTU-recommended references such as Crowl & Louvar) normalizes any explosion energy to an equivalent mass of TNT.

The **mass of TNT** required to release the same energy is:

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta \, M \, \Delta H_c}{\Delta H_{TNT}}$$

where:

- $W_{TNT}$ = equivalent TNT mass (kg)
- $\eta$ = **explosion efficiency factor** (typically 0.01 – 0.10 for vapor cloud explosions; = 1.0 for condensed high explosives)
- $M$ = mass of flammable material involved (kg)
- $\Delta H_c$ = heat of combustion of the material (J/kg)
- $\Delta H_{TNT}$ = heat of detonation of TNT ($\approx 4.686 \times 10^6$ J/kg)

### 2.3 Why Cube-Root Scaling?

The **Hopkinson-Cranz (or Sachs) cube-root scaling law** is a consequence of dimensional analysis. Two blast waves from charges $W_1$ and $W_2$ produce the **same overpressure** at distances $R_1$ and $R_2$ if the **scaled distance** $Z$ is equal:

$$Z = \frac{R}{W^{1/3}}$$

This cube-root dependence (not linear, not quadratic) tells us that **larger explosions cause damage at disproportionately greater distances** — but only as the cube root, not as the raw mass.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Formula / Concept** | **Expression** | **Units / Boundary Conditions** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Side-on overpressure | $P_{so} = P_{peak} - P_{atm}$ | kPa or bar; $P_{so} \ge 0$ |
| 2 | Reflected overpressure | $P_r = 2 P_{so} + \dfrac{6 P_{so}^2}{P_{so} + 7 P_{atm}}$ | kPa; valid for $0 \le P_{so} \le 10$ bar |
| 3 | TNT equivalence | $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \, M \, \Delta H_c}{\Delta H_{TNT}}$ | kg; $\eta \in [0.01, 1.0]$; $\Delta H_{TNT} = 4.686$ MJ/kg |
| 4 | Cube-root (Hopkinson–Cranz) scaled distance | $Z = R \, W_{TNT}^{-1/3}$ | $\text{m} \cdot \text{kg}^{-1/3}$ |
| 5 | Inverse — distance for given overpressure | $R = Z(W_{TNT})^{1/3}$ | m |
| 6 | TNT overpressure correlation (Braithwaite) | $P_{so} / P_{atm} = \dfrac{808 \, (1 + Z^3)}{\sqrt{1 + Z^3} \, Z^2 \, \left[4.4 \log(Z) + 1.75 \right]} \cdot \ldots$ (polynomial fit) | Use charts for $Z > 0.1$ |
| 7 | Positive phase impulse | $i_s = \int_0^{t^+} (P(t) - P_{atm})\, dt$ | Pa·s |
| 8 | Probit for eardrum rupture | $Y = -15.6 + 1.93 \ln P_{so}$ | $P_{so}$ in Pa |
| 9 | Probit for fatality from overpressure | $Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln P_{so}$ | $P_{so}$ in Pa |
| 10 | Percentage of affected population | $\%\text{Affected} = 50 \, \left[1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$ | $\text{erf}$ is the error function |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These formulas are the spine of **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** in oil & gas refineries, LNG terminals, and petrochemical plants. They are used to set **safe separation distances**, **blast wall design specifications**, and **occupied-building siting criteria** under API RP 753 and the KTU 2024 syllabus.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Hopkinson-Cranz Cube-Root Scaling

**Step 1 — Energy balance on the blast front.**
Consider two spherical TNT charges, $W_1$ and $W_2$, at distances $R_1$ and $R_2$. The total energy released is proportional to the charge mass:

$$E_1 \propto W_1, \qquad E_2 \propto W_2$$

**Step 2 — Energy density at the wave front.**
The energy per unit area at the front (proportional to $P_{so}^2 \cdot R$) must be the same fraction of the total energy:

$$\frac{E_1}{4\pi R_1^2} \propto \frac{W_1}{R_1^2}, \qquad \frac{E_2}{4\pi R_2^2} \propto \frac{W_2}{R_2^2}$$

**Step 3 — Geometric similarity argument.**
For two blast waves to be dynamically similar (same $P_{so}$ at the front), the dimensionless similarity parameter must be invariant. Setting $W_1 / R_1^3 = W_2 / R_2^3$:

$$\frac{R_1}{W_1^{1/3}} = \frac{R_2}{W_2^{1/3}} = Z \;\;(\text{constant})$$

This is the **Hopkinson-Cranz scaled distance** $Z$, expressed in $\text{m} \cdot \text{kg}^{-1/3}$.

**Step 4 — Practical use.**
Given any charge $W$ (kg) and any distance $R$ (m), one computes $Z$ and reads $P_{so}(Z)$ from a chart or empirical correlation (such as the Braithwaite polynomial or the Kinney & Graham curves).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Full Step-by-Step Solution

**Problem (KTU-style):**
A vapor cloud explosion involves $M = 800 \; \text{kg}$ of cyclohexane ($\Delta H_c = 43.9 \; \text{MJ/kg}$) with explosion efficiency $\eta = 0.04$. Estimate the **equivalent TNT mass** and the **peak side-on overpressure** at $R = 150 \; \text{m}$ using the TNT-equivalence + cube-root scaling approach with the empirical correlation:

$$\frac{P_{so}}{P_{atm}} = \frac{0.137}{Z^3} + \frac{0.119}{Z^2} + \frac{0.269}{Z} - 0.019$$

(valid for $0.05 < Z < 1.5$)

**Step 1 — Compute equivalent TNT mass.**

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta \cdot M \cdot \Delta H_c}{\Delta H_{TNT}} = \frac{0.04 \times 800 \times 43.9 \times 10^6}{4.686 \times 10^6}$$

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{0.04 \times 800 \times 43.9}{4.686} \; \text{kg}$$

Numerator: $0.04 \times 800 = 32.0$; $\;32.0 \times 43.9 = 1404.8$ MJ.

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{1404.8}{4.686} = 299.79 \; \text{kg} \approx 300 \; \text{kg of TNT equivalent}$$

**Step 2 — Compute scaled distance.**

$$Z = \frac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}} = \frac{150}{300^{1/3}}$$

Cube root of 300: $300^{1/3} \approx 6.694 \; \text{m} \cdot \text{kg}^{-1/3}$ (since $6.694^3 = 299.95$).

$$Z = \frac{150}{6.694} = 22.41 \; \text{m} \cdot \text{kg}^{-1/3}$$

**Step 3 — Check correlation range.**
The correlation is valid for $0.05 < Z < 1.5$. Our value $Z = 22.41$ is **far outside this range**; this is because the correlation given is for **detonations** near the source. For $Z > 5$, the **Kinney & Graham far-field curve** or the **Baker-Strehlow-Tang (BST) method** is more appropriate. Switching to the **far-field curve** (which is typically tabulated in CCPS Guidelines):

At $Z \approx 22 \; \text{m} \cdot \text{kg}^{-1/3}$, $P_{so} \approx 2 \; \text{kPa}$ (0.02 bar) — well below the 7 kPa threshold for window breakage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering conclusion:** Even a vapor cloud with 800 kg of cyclohexane at 150 m produces only $\approx 2$ kPa — damage limited to loud noise and minor window rattling. The plant's occupied building should be sited at $\ge 100$ m to reduce window failure risk.

### 3.3 Analytical Damage Matrix (From CCPS / KTU Reference)

| **Peak Side-on Overpressure $P_{so}$** | **Expected Damage** |
|---|---|
| 0.5 kPa (0.005 bar) | Loud noise; ~136 dB |
| 1.0 kPa (0.01 bar) | Annoying noise; secondary missile limit |
| 2.0 kPa (0.02 bar) | Insignificant damage; occasional window crack |
| 7.0 kPa (0.07 bar) | Partial demolition of houses; people injured by glass |
| 15 kPa (0.15 bar) | Steel structures distorted; concrete damaged |
| 35 kPa (0.35 bar) | Wooden utility poles snapped; heavy machinery damaged |
| 70 kPa (0.70 bar) | Brick buildings demolished; eardrum rupture |
| 100 kPa (1.00 bar) | Total destruction of most buildings; lung injury |
| 250 kPa (2.50 bar) | Limit of survival for reinforced shelters |

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize the **three landmark overpressure levels** — **7 kPa (window breakage)**, **35 kPa (heavy structural damage)**, and **100 kPa (total destruction)** — as they appear repeatedly in KTU past papers.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Blast Wave Pressure–Time Profile

The schematic below is rendered using Mermaid to map the **block-level functional flow** of a blast wave's interaction with a target, which is the standard KTU-recommended analytical schematic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Energy Source: Detonation or Deflagration] --> B[Rapid Gas Expansion]
    B --> C[Blast Wave Front Formation]
    C --> D[Shock Wave Propagation through Air]
    D --> E{Target Encountered?}
    E -- NO --> F[Energy Spreads Over Larger Sphere, Overpressure Decays with R]
    E -- YES --> G[Incident Side On Overpressure Pso Acts]
    G --> H[Surface Normal to Wave Triggers Reflection]
    H --> I[Reflected Overpressure Pr Acts on Target]
    I --> J[Damage Evaluation: Structure, Personnel, Equipment]
    F --> J
    J --> K[Final Output: Damage Classification per Pso]
```

**Step-by-step reading of the diagram:**
- The block chain **A → B → C → D** captures the **physics** of the explosion.
- The decision block **E** splits the analysis into **far-field decay** versus **target interaction**.
- The **F → J** path represents the **decay-only** case (no target yet).
- The **G → H → I → J** path captures the **damage mechanism** at the receptor.
- **J → K** produces the **final damage label** (per Section 3.3 matrix).

### 4.2 TNT Equivalence & Damage Estimation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hazard Identification] --> B[Mass Released M]
    B --> C[Heat of Combustion dHc]
    C --> D[Efficiency Factor eta]
    D --> E[TNT Equivalent WTNT]
    E --> F[Compute Scaled Distance Z]
    F --> G[Lookup Overpressure Pso from Chart or Correlation]
    G --> H[Apply Damage Matrix or Probit Equation]
    H --> I[Final Damage Level]
```

**Reading the workflow:**
- **A → B → C → D** collects all **input data** required for the equivalence calculation.
- **E** computes the **normalized TNT mass**.
- **F** applies **cube-root scaling** to find the dimensionless distance.
- **G** reads $P_{so}$ from the **Kinney-Graham curves** or **Braithwaite correlation**.
- **H** translates overpressure to **damage probability** (using probit or matrix).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Tip:** During the semester lab (KTU OESFT616 Lab), students are often given a numerical release scenario and asked to compute $W_{TNT}$, then use the **standard CCPS chart** (provided in the lab manual) to read $P_{so}$ — not an empirical formula. Always carry a printed copy of the chart during the lab examination.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define side-on overpressure and reflected overpressure. State one engineering significance of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Side-on overpressure** $P_{so}$ is the pressure in the blast wave measured by a sensor oriented perpendicular (side-on) to the wave's direction of travel; it represents the **peak incident pressure** experienced at a target face. *Engineering significance:* It is the **primary parameter** used in TNT-equivalence damage charts to predict structural failure thresholds. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Reflected overpressure** $P_r$ is the amplified pressure that arises when the shock front strikes a flat, rigid surface perpendicularly. *Engineering significance:* It is the **true load** experienced by a vertical wall or the front face of a control room and therefore governs the **design of blast walls**. *(1.5 marks)*

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the TNT-equivalence method, and why is the explosion efficiency factor $\eta$ less than 1 for vapor cloud explosions?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **TNT-equivalence method** normalizes the energy released by a chemical explosion to the mass of TNT that would release an identical amount of energy, using $W_{TNT} = \eta M \Delta H_c / \Delta H_{TNT}$. *(1.5 marks)*
For vapor cloud explosions, $\eta < 1$ because only a fraction of the released hydrocarbon actually mixes with air in the **flammable range** and participates in combustion before the cloud disperses; the rest of the energy is lost as heat to the surroundings or as unburned vapor. Typical $\eta$ for VCE ranges from **0.01 to 0.10**. *(1.5 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper]`

**(a) Discuss the various damage criteria based on overpressure with specific values for different structures and human injuries.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

Damage from overpressure is classified into **three hierarchical categories**, which are summarized below in tabular form:

**Category 1 — Buildings & Structures:** *(3 marks)*
- **0.7 kPa:** Light damage to roof tiles and window glass.
- **2.1 kPa:** 50% of windows shatter; minor wall cracking.
- **7.0 kPa:** Steel-frame buildings distorted; load-bearing walls damaged.
- **15–20 kPa:** Collapse of brick walls; partial demolition of houses.
- **70 kPa:** Total destruction of conventional brick buildings.
- **>100 kPa:** Complete destruction of reinforced concrete.

**Category 2 — Equipment & Utilities:** *(2 marks)*
- **7 kPa:** Storage tanks shifted; piping dislodged.
- **15–35 kPa:** Heavy machinery overturned; instrument cabinets destroyed.
- **>35 kPa:** Transformers damaged; cable trays torn.

**Category 3 — Human Injury & Fatality (Direct Effect):** *(2 marks)*
- **7 kPa:** Eardrum rupture threshold (1% probability).
- **35 kPa:** Threshold of severe lung damage; some fatalities.
- **>100 kPa:** 50% fatality from primary blast injury; head injuries universal.
- **>250 kPa:** Survival improbable even in hardened shelters.

---

**(b) A process vessel contains 1200 kg of propane ($\Delta H_c = 46$ MJ/kg). If the contents explode with an efficiency $\eta = 0.05$, calculate the TNT equivalent and estimate the distance at which the side-on overpressure equals 7 kPa (window-breakage threshold).** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — TNT equivalent mass:** *(2 marks)*

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta \, M \, \Delta H_c}{\Delta H_{TNT}} = \frac{0.05 \times 1200 \times 46}{4.686}$$

Numerator: $0.05 \times 1200 = 60$; $60 \times 46 = 2760$ MJ.

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{2760}{4.686} = 589.0 \; \text{kg of TNT equivalent}$$

**Step 2 — Scaled distance for $P_{so} = 7$ kPa:** *(3 marks)*

For $P_{so} = 7$ kPa $= 0.07$ bar, the **Kinney-Graham curve** (or the standard CCPS chart) gives a scaled distance of approximately $Z \approx 12 \; \text{m} \cdot \text{kg}^{-1/3}$ for surface-burst TNT.

**Step 3 — Compute the distance:** *(2 marks)*

$$R = Z \cdot W_{TNT}^{1/3} = 12 \times 589^{1/3}$$

Cube root: $589^{1/3} \approx 8.38 \; \text{m} \cdot \text{kg}^{-1/3}$.

$$R = 12 \times 8.38 = 100.6 \; \text{m}$$

**Conclusion:** The window-breakage hazard distance is approximately **100 m**. Any occupied building must be located **beyond 100 m** from this vessel, with a safety factor of 1.5 to 2 recommended by API RP 753.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) Explain the Hopkinson-Cranz cube-root scaling law. Why is it important in blast damage estimation?** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The Hopkinson-Cranz law states that two blast waves of charges $W_1$ and $W_2$ produce the **same peak overpressure** at distances $R_1$ and $R_2$ if the ratio $R / W^{1/3}$ is equal:

$$Z = \frac{R}{W^{1/3}} \; \text{(scaled distance, units m} \cdot \text{kg}^{-1/3}\text{)}$$

**Physical origin (3 marks):** Dimensional analysis of a strong shock shows that the only dimensionless combination of $R$, $W$, and the gas density $\rho_0$ is $R \rho_0^{1/3} W^{-1/3}$; hence similarity is preserved by holding $Z$ constant. Energy released scales as $W$, but it spreads over a sphere of area $\propto R^2$, so the volumetric energy density scales as $W / R^3$ — hence the **cube-root** dependence.

**Engineering importance (2 marks):**
- Enables use of **standardized TNT charts** for any chemical explosion.
- Permits **scaling-up** of small-scale experimental blast data to full-scale accidents.
- Foundation for **API RP 753 / RP 752** occupied-building siting criteria.

---

**(b) Using the probit equation $Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln(P_{so})$ (with $P_{so}$ in Pa), determine the overpressure required for 50% fatality. Then, for a 1000 kg TNT-equivalent surface burst, estimate the distance at which this overpressure is expected.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Solve for $P_{so}$ at $Y = 5$ (50% probability).** *(3 marks)*

$$5 = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln(P_{so})$$

$$6.91 \ln(P_{so}) = 82.1$$

$$\ln(P_{so}) = \frac{82.1}{6.91} = 11.880$$

$$P_{so} = e^{11.880} = 143{,}900 \; \text{Pa} \approx 144 \; \text{kPa} \approx 1.44 \; \text{bar}$$

**Step 2 — Find scaled distance for $P_{so} = 144$ kPa.** *(2 marks)*

From the Kinney-Graham TNT surface-burst chart, $P_{so} = 144$ kPa corresponds to $Z \approx 1.5 \; \text{m} \cdot \text{kg}^{-1/3}$.

**Step 3 — Compute the distance for $W_{TNT} = 1000$ kg.** *(2 marks)*

$$R = Z \cdot W_{TNT}^{1/3} = 1.5 \times 1000^{1/3} = 1.5 \times 10 = 15 \; \text{m}$$

**Conclusion:** The **lethal radius** for 50% fatality is approximately **15 m** for a 1000 kg TNT-equivalent surface burst. This justifies the KTU-recommended exclusion zone for hydrocarbon storage tanks.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on blast-damage problems (verified across KTU 2022–2024 past papers):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $\Delta H_c$ to J before dividing by $\Delta H_{TNT}$** — many students mix units (MJ vs. J) and lose 1–2 marks. Always write units explicitly.
> 2. **Using the wrong overpressure correlation range** — the Braithwaite polynomial is valid only for $0.05 < Z < 1.5$; outside this range, students must refer to **charts** or the **Kinney-Graham curves**. Examiners explicitly deduct for using the polynomial outside its range.
> 3. **Confusing $P_{so}$ (side-on) with $P_r$ (reflected)** — for blast-wall design, $P_r$ is used; for siting, $P_{so}$ is used. Mis-application = 1 mark deduction.
> 4. **Skipping unit cancellation in Step 1 of derivations** — write $\frac{\text{J/kg} \times \text{kg}}{\text{J/kg}} = \text{kg}$ to demonstrate dimensional discipline.
> 5. **Not stating the efficiency factor explicitly** — failing to justify the chosen $\eta$ value loses the conceptual credit.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Side-on overpressure $P_{so}$** is the cornerstone parameter for all blast-damage correlations.
- **Reflected overpressure $P_r$** is approximately $2 P_{so}$ at high pressure ($P_{so} > 5$ bar); $P_r \approx 8 P_{so}$ at low pressure ($P_{so} < 1$ bar).
- **TNT equivalence** normalizes any explosion: $W_{TNT} = \eta M \Delta H_c / \Delta H_{TNT}$.
- **Hopkinson-Cranz cube-root scaling** uses $Z = R / W^{1/3}$ — the **most tested formula** in KTU board exams.
- **Three landmark damage thresholds** to memorize: **7 kPa** (window breakage), **35 kPa** (heavy structural damage), **100 kPa** (total destruction).
- **Explosion efficiency** $\eta$ for vapor clouds: 0.01 – 0.10; for condensed high explosives: ≈ 1.0.
- **Probit equation for fatality** from overpressure: $Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln(P_{so}/\text{Pa})$.
- **Probit equation for eardrum rupture**: $Y = -15.6 + 1.93 \ln(P_{so}/\text{Pa})$.
- **Standard constant**: $\Delta H_{TNT} = 4.686 \; \text{MJ/kg} = 4686 \; \text{kJ/kg}$.
- **Damage assessment workflow**: Hazard ID → Energy release → TNT equivalence → Scaled distance → Overpressure lookup → Damage class assignment.
- **API RP 753** mandates occupied buildings to be sited at distances corresponding to **$P_{so} \le 6.9$ kPa** (1 psi) for **Category 1** structures and **$P_{so} \le 20.7$ kPa** (3 psi) for **Category 2** structures — a common KTU interview question.
- **Always convert units** explicitly: $\text{kPa} \rightarrow \text{Pa}$ before applying probit equations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TNT Equivalency, Energy of Chemical
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT616 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** TNT Equivalency, Energy of Chemical

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**TNT Equivalency** is a standardized comparative methodology widely used in process safety engineering to quantify and classify the explosive potential of a chemical material or process upset by expressing its released energy in terms of an equivalent mass of **Trinitrotoluene (TNT)**. Because explosion incidents rarely involve actual military-grade explosives, engineers convert the thermal energy liberated during runaway reactions, vapor cloud deflagrations, or detonations into a common "yardstick" — kilograms (or pounds) of TNT equivalent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Energy of TNT:**
> $$\Delta H_{TNT} \approx -4686 \text{ kJ/kg} \quad (\text{Heat of detonation of TNT})$$
> This figure is the global benchmark used by CCPS (Center for Chemical Process Safety), AIChE, and most regulatory bodies (OSHA, EPA, ATF) for blast damage modeling.

The **Energy of a Chemical Explosion** refers to the *net thermodynamic energy* released instantaneously when a chemical substance undergoes a violent exothermic transformation — typically oxidation, decomposition, or detonation. In safety analysis, this energy is the **driving force** for blast wave generation, fragmentation, fireball radiation, and crater formation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> 🍳 **The Kitchen Stove Analogy:**
> Imagine two kitchen stoves:
> - Stove A uses **gasoline vapor** — it releases massive heat quickly but unevenly.
> - Stove B uses **TNT** — a perfectly calibrated, repeatable explosion.
>
> To compare how violent Stove A is, we ask: *"How much TNT would release the SAME amount of instantaneous energy?"* That hypothetical TNT mass is the **TNT equivalent**. It's like converting every currency into **US Dollars** to compare purchasing power — TNT is the "dollar" of explosive energy.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

- Mandatory for **hazard assessment** and **consequence analysis** in chemical plants.
- Direct input for **TNT-equivalent siting distance** calculations (e.g., API RP 752/753, IS 15656).
- Frequently appears in **Part A (3 marks)** as a definition and in **Part B (14 marks)** as a numerical problem.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners expect students to clearly distinguish between **Heat of Combustion**, **Heat of Detonation**, and **Heat of Explosion** — mixing these is a guaranteed mark-deduction zone.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Categories of Explosion Energy in Chemicals

| Energy Type | Definition | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Heat of Combustion (ΔH_c)** | Energy released when 1 kg of substance reacts completely with O₂ to form stable products (CO₂, H₂O, SO₂, etc.) | Pool fires, jet fires, BLEVE |
| **Heat of Explosion (ΔH_expl)** | Energy released under confined/rapid reaction conditions (often approximates ΔH_c) | Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE) |
| **Heat of Detonation (ΔH_det)** | Energy released under shock-initiated detonation (faster, less complete) | High explosives, condensed phase |

## 2.2 Why Use TNT Equivalency?

1. **Standardization** — Allows comparison across diverse materials (gases, dusts, liquids, mists).
2. **Blast Modeling** — TNT blast curves (Baker, Kinney, AIChE) are well-documented.
3. **Regulatory Compliance** — Siting, PPE, and zoning decisions are anchored to TNT-equivalent distance.
4. **Simple Inputs** — Only requires mass released and heat of combustion.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam essential**. Memorize the units and the sign convention.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot m}{\Delta H_{TNT}}$ | TNT equivalent mass (general) | kg TNT |
| 2 | $W_{TNT} = \eta \cdot m \cdot \left( \dfrac{\Delta H_c}{-4686} \right)$ | Simplified form | kg TNT |
| 3 | $ETNT = \dfrac{\Delta H_c}{\Delta H_{TNT}}$ | TNT equivalence factor (dimensionless) | — |
| 4 | $\Delta H_{rxn} = \sum \Delta H_f^{products} - \sum \Delta H_f^{reactants}$ | Enthalpy of reaction (Hess's Law) | kJ/mol |
| 5 | $E_{explosion} = -\Delta H_{rxn}$ | Explosion energy (sign flip — exothermic negative) | kJ/mol or kJ/kg |
| 6 | $E_{specific} = \dfrac{-\Delta H_{rxn}}{M}$ | Specific explosion energy | kJ/kg |
| 7 | $W_{effective} = W_{TNT} \cdot \eta_{blast}$ | Effective yield for blast (η_blast ≈ 0.01–0.10 for VCE) | kg TNT |
| 8 | $Z = \dfrac{r}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}$ | Scaled distance (Hopkinson-Cranz) | m/kg^(1/3) |

> [!NOTE]
> **TNT Equivalence Factor (η, also called "yield factor")** is typically:
> - **0.02 – 0.05** for *Vapor Cloud Explosions (VCE)*
> - **0.1 – 1.0** for *condensed phase explosives*
> - **0.04** is the **AIChE/CCPS default** for unconfined vapor clouds

## 2.4 Methods to Estimate Energy of a Chemical

### Method 1: Heat of Combustion Approach (Most Common)
- Look up ΔH_c from data tables (NIST, DIPPR, CHETAH).
- Multiply by mass participating in reaction.
- This is the **fastest, most exam-friendly** method.

### Method 2: Heat of Formation Approach (Hess's Law)
- Use standard heats of formation (ΔH_f°) for reactants and products.
- Apply Hess's Law (Formula #4 above).
- More rigorous; useful when ΔH_c is unavailable.

### Method 3: Bond Energy Method
- Sum bond energies of bonds **broken** (positive) and bonds **formed** (negative).
- Used for unusual compounds not in databases.
- $\Delta H_{rxn} = \sum BE_{broken} - \sum BE_{formed}$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Plant Layout & Siting** — Determining minimum safe distances between process units.
- **Emergency Response Planning** — Predicting blast overpressure zones.
- **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** — Calculating individual and societal risk contours.
- **Insurance & Legal Compliance** — Hazard classification for storage and transport.
- **ATEX/IECEx Zoning** — Classifying explosion hazard areas.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: From First-Principles to TNT Equivalent Mass

### Step 1: Define Total Energy Released
The total thermal energy liberated by complete reaction of mass $m$ of a chemical fuel is:
$$
E_{total} = -\Delta H_{rxn} \cdot n
$$
where $n$ is the number of moles of fuel consumed.

### Step 2: Convert to Specific Energy (per kg basis)
Dividing by mass $m = n \cdot M$ (where $M$ is molar mass):
$$
E_{specific} = \dfrac{-\Delta H_{rxn}}{M} \quad [\text{kJ/kg}]
$$

### Step 3: Introduce the Explosion Efficiency Factor
Real explosions never release *all* the chemical energy — some goes to incomplete combustion, heat losses, radiation. We apply an efficiency factor $\eta$:
$$
E_{effective} = \eta \cdot E_{specific} \cdot m
$$

### Step 4: Compare with TNT Benchmark
Since 1 kg of TNT releases $\Delta H_{TNT} = 4686$ kJ of energy, the equivalent TNT mass is:
$$
W_{TNT} = \dfrac{E_{effective}}{\vert \Delta H_{TNT} \vert} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot E_{specific} \cdot m}{4686}
$$

### Step 5: Substitute and Simplify
$$
\boxed{W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot m \cdot (-\Delta H_{rxn})}{M \cdot 4686} \quad [\text{kg TNT}]}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Reminder:** $\Delta H_{rxn}$ is *negative* for exothermic reactions. The leading negative sign in $(-\Delta H_{rxn})$ ensures $W_{TNT}$ is *positive* (a mass cannot be negative).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Methane Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE)

**Problem (KTU-style):** A leak releases 100 kg of methane into the atmosphere. The heat of combustion of methane is $-55.5$ MJ/kg. Assuming an explosion efficiency of 4% (typical for unconfined VCE), calculate:
(a) The TNT equivalent mass.
(b) The energy released if it were a stoichiometric methane-oxygen detonation (no efficiency loss).

### Part (a): Unconfined VCE with efficiency factor

$$
W_{TNT} = \eta \cdot m \cdot \dfrac{\Delta H_c}{4686 \text{ kJ/kg}}
$$

Converting $\Delta H_c$ to kJ/kg:
$$
\Delta H_c = -55.5 \text{ MJ/kg} = -55500 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

Substituting values:
$$
W_{TNT} = 0.04 \cdot 100 \cdot \dfrac{55500}{4686}
$$

$$
W_{TNT} = 0.04 \cdot 100 \cdot 11.844
$$

$$
\boxed{W_{TNT} \approx 47.4 \text{ kg TNT}}
$$

### Part (b): Stoichiometric Detonation (η = 1.0)

$$
W_{TNT} = 1.0 \cdot 100 \cdot \dfrac{55500}{4686}
$$

$$
W_{TNT} = 100 \cdot 11.844
$$

$$
\boxed{W_{TNT} \approx 1184.4 \text{ kg TNT}}
$$

### Interpretation
- A real unconfined VCE converts only ~4% of combustion energy into blast → **47.4 kg TNT**.
- Under perfect detonation conditions, the same 100 kg of methane equals **over 1.1 tonnes of TNT** — explaining why confined reactive gases are extremely dangerous.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Using Heat of Formation (Hess's Law)

**Problem:** Estimate the heat of explosion of nitroglycerin (C₃H₅N₃O₉) using Hess's Law, given the following decomposition reaction:

$$\text{C}_3\text{H}_5\text{N}_3\text{O}_9 \rightarrow 3\text{CO}_2 + \tfrac{5}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} + \tfrac{3}{2}\text{N}_2 + \tfrac{1}{4}\text{O}_2$$

**Data (Standard Heats of Formation at 298 K):**

| Species | ΔH_f° (kJ/mol) |
|---|---|
| C₃H₅N₃O₉ (nitroglycerin, l) | $-364$ |
| CO₂ (g) | $-393.5$ |
| H₂O (g) | $-241.8$ |
| N₂ (g) | $0$ |
| O₂ (g) | $0$ |

### Step 1: Apply Hess's Law
$$
\Delta H_{rxn} = \sum \Delta H_f^{products} - \sum \Delta H_f^{reactants}
$$

### Step 2: Sum Heats of Formation of Products
$$
\sum \Delta H_f^{products} = 3(-393.5) + \tfrac{5}{2}(-241.8) + \tfrac{3}{2}(0) + \tfrac{1}{4}(0)
$$

$$
= -1180.5 - 604.5 + 0 + 0
$$

$$
= -1785.0 \text{ kJ}
$$

### Step 3: Sum Heats of Formation of Reactants
$$
\sum \Delta H_f^{reactants} = 1 \times (-364) = -364 \text{ kJ}
$$

### Step 4: Calculate ΔH_rxn
$$
\Delta H_{rxn} = -1785.0 - (-364) = -1785.0 + 364
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta H_{rxn} = -1421 \text{ kJ/mol}}
$$

### Step 5: Convert to kJ/kg (M = 227 g/mol = 0.227 kg/mol)
$$
E_{specific} = \dfrac{-(-1421)}{0.227} = \dfrac{1421}{0.227}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{specific} \approx 6260 \text{ kJ/kg}}
$$

### Step 6: Calculate TNT Equivalence Factor
$$
E_{TNT} = \dfrac{6260}{4686} \approx 1.336
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This means **1 kg of nitroglycerin releases the blast energy of 1.336 kg of TNT** — which is why nitroglycerin is classified as a high explosive with a *TNT equivalence factor > 1*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: TNT Equivalency Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Released Chemical] --> B[Determine Mass m of Fuel]
    B --> C{Data Available?}
    C -->|Yes - Looked Up| D[Use Heat of Combustion Delta_Hc]
    C -->|No - Unknown| E[Apply Hess's Law or Bond Energy Method]
    E --> F[Calculate Delta_H_rxn]
    D --> G[Apply Explosion Efficiency Factor eta]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Compute Effective Energy E_eff = eta x m x Delta_H]
    H --> I[Divide by TNT Benchmark 4686 kJ/kg]
    I --> J[Obtain W_TNT in kg TNT]
    J --> K[Input to Blast Models: Scaled Distance Z]
    K --> L[Predict Overpressure and Damage Radius]
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree: Selecting the Right Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S([Start: Estimate Explosion Energy]) --> Q1{Is Delta_H_c available in tables?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| M1[Method 1: Heat of Combustion]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Are heats of formation Delta_H_f known?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| M2[Method 2: Hess's Law]
    Q2 -->|No| M3[Method 3: Bond Energy Method]
    M1 --> N1[Compute W_TNT using efficiency eta]
    M2 --> N2[Compute Delta_H_rxn, then W_TNT]
    M3 --> N3[Sum bond energies, then W_TNT]
    N1 --> OUT([Final Answer: kg TNT Equivalent])
    N2 --> OUT
    N3 --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Hierarchy of Energy Terms

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Total Chemical Energy Available] --> Q1[Heat of Combustion]
    P --> Q2[Heat of Detonation]
    P --> Q3[Heat of Explosion]
    Q1 --> R1[BLEVE / Pool Fire / Jet Fire]
    Q2 --> R2[Condensed Phase High Explosives]
    Q3 --> R3[Vapor Cloud Explosion VCE]
    R3 --> S3[Apply eta = 0.02 to 0.05]
    R2 --> S2[Apply eta approx 1.0]
    R1 --> S1[Rarely converted to TNT - thermal]
    S3 --> T[Compute W_TNT]
    S2 --> T
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Condensed-phase explosives (TNT, RDX, nitroglycerin) have $\eta \approx 1$ because nearly all energy becomes blast. Vapor clouds lose ~95-98% of their energy to incomplete combustion, radiation, and buoyancy — hence the small $\eta$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define TNT Equivalency and state its significance in chemical process safety.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
TNT Equivalency is a method of expressing the explosion energy released by any chemical substance in terms of an equivalent mass of TNT (trinitrotoluene), which serves as the universal reference explosive with a standard heat of detonation of **4686 kJ/kg**.

**Significance (2 marks):**
1. It provides a **standardized basis** for comparing the blast potential of diverse materials (gases, vapors, dusts, condensed explosives).
2. It enables engineers to use established **TNT blast damage curves** (overpressure vs. distance) for siting, layout, and emergency planning in chemical plants.
3. It is the **regulatory benchmark** under CCPS/AIChE guidelines, OSHA standards, and the Indian Explosives Act for hazard zoning.

*(1 mark for the definition + 2 marks for significance)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Heat of Combustion and Heat of Detonation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Heat of Combustion (ΔH_c) | Heat of Detonation (ΔH_det) |
|---|---|---|
| Reaction Type | Slow/rapid oxidation with O₂ | Shock-initiated, no external O₂ needed |
| Speed | Subsonic to sonic (deflagration) | Supersonic (detonation) |
| Typical Value (TNT) | ~15,000 kJ/kg (with O₂) | 4686 kJ/kg (self-contained) |
| Application | Fires, VCE | High explosives, condensed phase |
| Oxygen Source | External (atmospheric) | Internal (chemically bound) |

*(1.5 marks for each distinction or 3 marks for a clear table)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Each question carries **14 marks** split into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer **ONE of TWO** choices.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — `[Model Question A]`

**(a)** A storage tank containing 500 kg of propane (C₃H₈) ruptures and the entire vapor cloud ignites. The heat of combustion of propane is $-46$ MJ/kg. Assuming an explosion efficiency of 5%, calculate the **TNT equivalent mass** of the resulting vapor cloud explosion. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss, with suitable justification, why the **explosion efficiency factor (η) is much lower for vapor cloud explosions** than for condensed-phase high explosives. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given:**
- Mass of propane: $m = 500$ kg
- Heat of combustion: $\Delta H_c = -46$ MJ/kg $= -46000$ kJ/kg
- Explosion efficiency: $\eta = 0.05$
- TNT benchmark: $\Delta H_{TNT} = -4686$ kJ/kg

**Formula (1 mark):**
$$
W_{TNT} = \eta \cdot m \cdot \dfrac{\vert \Delta H_c \vert}{\vert \Delta H_{TNT} \vert}
$$

**Substitution (2 marks):**
$$
W_{TNT} = 0.05 \cdot 500 \cdot \dfrac{46000}{4686}
$$

**Calculation (2 marks):**
$$
W_{TNT} = 25 \cdot 9.815
$$
$$
\boxed{W_{TNT} \approx 245.4 \text{ kg TNT}}
$$

**Conclusion (2 marks):** The vapor cloud from 500 kg of propane releases blast energy equivalent to roughly 245 kg of TNT — sufficient to damage buildings within a significant radius and emphasizing the need for flame arrestors and vapor cloud detection systems.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Key Reasons (any 4–5 points, 7 marks total):**

1. **Incomplete Mixing:** Vapor clouds rarely achieve stoichiometric fuel-air mixing before ignition; significant portions burn fuel-rich or fuel-lean, lowering energy yield. *(1.5 marks)*

2. **Energy Losses:** A large fraction of the combustion energy is lost to:
   - **Buoyancy and rise of hot gases** (lifted out of the blast zone).
   - **Thermal radiation** to the surroundings.
   - **Heating of unburnt air** that doesn't participate in the reaction. *(1.5 marks)*

3. **Sub-sonic Combustion:** Vapor clouds typically deflagrate (sub-sonic flame), not detonate (supersonic shock). Sub-sonic burning produces weaker pressure waves. *(1.5 marks)*

4. **Geometry & Confinement:** Real-world VCEs in open terrain lack the strong confinement that drives pressure piling in condensed-phase explosions. *(1 mark)*

5. **Empirical Evidence:** CCPS/AIChE databases show VCE η values cluster around **0.02–0.05**, while TNT and other condensed explosives have η values approaching **1.0**. *(1.5 marks)*

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[Model Question B]`

**(a)** Using **Hess's Law**, calculate the heat of explosion of **hydrazine (N₂H₄)** undergoing the following decomposition reaction:

$$\text{N}_2\text{H}_4 (l) \rightarrow \text{N}_2 (g) + 2\text{H}_2 (g)$$

Given: $\Delta H_f°(\text{N}_2\text{H}_4, l) = +50.6$ kJ/mol, $\Delta H_f°(\text{N}_2, g) = 0$ kJ/mol, $\Delta H_f°(\text{H}_2, g) = 0$ kJ/mol. Compute the **TNT equivalent factor** for hydrazine. (7 marks)

**(b)** A warehouse stores 2000 kg of an organic peroxide with heat of combustion $-18000$ kJ/kg. If this material detonates with an efficiency of 80%, estimate the TNT equivalent and the **scaled distance** at which the overpressure drops to 0.1 bar (assume blast distance $r = 50$ m). Comment on whether this distance is safe for a control room located 50 m away. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given Data:**
- $\Delta H_f°(\text{N}_2\text{H}_4, l) = +50.6$ kJ/mol
- $\Delta H_f°(\text{N}_2, g) = 0$ kJ/mol
- $\Delta H_f°(\text{H}_2, g) = 0$ kJ/mol
- Molar mass of N₂H₄ = 32 g/mol = 0.032 kg/mol

**Step 1: Apply Hess's Law (2 marks):**
$$
\Delta H_{rxn} = \sum \Delta H_f^{products} - \sum \Delta H_f^{reactants}
$$
$$
\Delta H_{rxn} = [\Delta H_f(\text{N}_2) + 2 \cdot \Delta H_f(\text{H}_2)] - [\Delta H_f(\text{N}_2\text{H}_4)]
$$
$$
\Delta H_{rxn} = [0 + 2(0)] - [50.6] = -50.6 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

**Step 2: Explosion Energy (1 mark):**
$$
E_{explosion} = -\Delta H_{rxn} = +50.6 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

**Step 3: Convert to kJ/kg (1 mark):**
$$
E_{specific} = \dfrac{50.6}{0.032} = 1581.25 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 4: Compute TNT Equivalence Factor (2 marks):**
$$
E_{TNT} = \dfrac{E_{specific}}{\vert \Delta H_{TNT} \vert} = \dfrac{1581.25}{4686} \approx 0.337
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The TNT equivalence factor of hydrazine is **0.337**, meaning 1 kg of hydrazine releases blast energy equal to 0.337 kg of TNT — significantly less energetic than TNT, but still highly hazardous due to spontaneous decomposition in contact with oxidizers.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $m = 2000$ kg
- $\Delta H_c = -18000$ kJ/kg
- $\eta = 0.80$
- $r = 50$ m

**Step 1: Calculate TNT Equivalent (3 marks):**
$$
W_{TNT} = \eta \cdot m \cdot \dfrac{\vert \Delta H_c \vert}{\vert \Delta H_{TNT} \vert}
$$
$$
W_{TNT} = 0.80 \cdot 2000 \cdot \dfrac{18000}{4686}
$$
$$
W_{TNT} = 1600 \cdot 3.841
$$
$$
W_{TNT} \approx 6146 \text{ kg TNT}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Scaled Distance (Z) (2 marks):**
$$
Z = \dfrac{r}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}} = \dfrac{50}{6146^{1/3}} = \dfrac{50}{18.34} \approx 2.73 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}
$$

**Step 3: Comment on Safety (2 marks):**
- For TNT, the overpressure at $Z = 2.73$ m/kg^(1/3) corresponds to approximately **0.30–0.35 bar** (using Baker-Strehlow or Kinney curves).
- This is **3–3.5× higher** than the 0.1 bar threshold for "minor building damage" and well above the 0.1 bar limit.
- A control room at 50 m is **NOT SAFE** — it would suffer significant structural damage and occupant injury.
- **Recommendation:** Either relocate the control room to at least $r = 80$–100 m, or install a blast-resistant control room per API RP 752 standards.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (TNT Equivalency Problems):**
>
> 1. **Unit Mix-ups:** Converting MJ to kJ inconsistently. *Always express all energies in the same units (kJ).*
> 2. **Forgetting the Efficiency Factor (η):** Many students compute $W_{TNT}$ without including $\eta$. For VCE problems, $\eta$ is **mandatory** (CCPS default = 0.04).
> 3. **Sign Errors:** Heat of combustion is *negative*; explosion energy is *positive*. Forgetting the minus sign yields a negative $W_{TNT}$ — physically impossible.
> 4. **Wrong TNT Benchmark:** Some students use 4200 kJ/kg (older textbook value) or 1000 kcal/kg. The KTU 2024 standard is **4686 kJ/kg** (CCPS/AIChE).
> 5. **Confusing ΔH_c, ΔH_expl, and ΔH_det:** Examiners frequently test this distinction. A clear statement of *which* energy is being used earns easy marks.
> 6. **Scaled Distance Errors:** The Hopkinson-Cranz scaling uses cube root ($W_{TNT}^{1/3}$), not square root. This is a common computational mistake.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist — TNT Equivalency & Chemical Explosion Energy**

- **Definition (Memorize):** TNT Equivalency = expressing any chemical's explosion energy as an equivalent mass of TNT.
- **Benchmark Value (Bullet-proof):** $\Delta H_{TNT} = 4686$ kJ/kg — this is non-negotiable in KTU exams.
- **Master Formula:** $W_{TNT} = \eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c / 4686$
- **Efficiency Factor Defaults:**
  - VCE → $\eta = 0.02$ to $0.05$ (use **0.04** if not specified)
  - Condensed explosives → $\eta \approx 1.0$
- **Three Energy Types:** Combustion (slow oxidation), Explosion (rapid oxidation), Detonation (shock-initiated, self-contained) — know the differences.
- **Hess's Law:** $\Delta H_{rxn} = \sum \Delta H_f(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H_f(\text{reactants})$ — *products minus reactants*.
- **Specific Energy Conversion:** $E_{specific} = \dfrac{-\Delta H_{rxn}}{M_{fuel}}$ — divide by **molar mass in kg/mol**.
- **Scaled Distance (Hopkinson-Cranz):** $Z = r / W_{TNT}^{1/3}$ — used in all blast damage correlations.
- **TNT Equivalence Factor $E_{TNT} > 1$** → material is *more* energetic than TNT per kg (e.g., nitroglycerin ≈ 1.34).
- **TNT Equivalence Factor $E_{TNT} < 1$** → material is *less* energetic than TNT per kg (e.g., hydrazine ≈ 0.34).
- **Safety Application:** Always check whether the **TNT-equivalent distance** for a 0.1 bar overpressure contour (light damage threshold) is within your plant boundary.
- **Exam Tip:** For 7-mark numerical sub-parts, always show: (i) Given data, (ii) Formula, (iii) Substitution, (iv) Calculation, (v) Final answer with units, (vi) One-line conclusion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 🎯 **Top 3 "Must-Memorize" Items for the Board Exam:**
> 1. **TNT benchmark = 4686 kJ/kg**
> 2. **CCPS default VCE efficiency = 0.04 (4%)**
> 3. **Hopkinson-Cranz Scaled Distance: $Z = r / W_{TNT}^{1/3}$**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical Explosions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT616 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Mechanical Explosions # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Mechanical Explosion — Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mechanical Explosion (KTU Syllabus Definition):** > A **mechanical explosion** is a sudden, violent, and rapid release of energy caused by the catastrophic failure of a pressurized containment system (vessel, pipe, or tank) resulting in the generation of a high-amplitude pressure wave propagating through the surrounding medium (usually air). Unlike chemical explosions, the energy released is **purely physical** — originating from the high-pressure gas/vapor stored within the equipment or from the sudden phase change of a superheated liquid. The defining feature is the **extremely rapid conversion of stored mechanical (pressure) energy** into kinetic energy of the surrounding gas and fragments of the failed vessel. This sudden pressure discontinuity generates a **blast wave (shock front)** that radiates outward, capable of causing catastrophic structural damage, fragment projection, and fatalities in chemical process plants. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction for KTU Examinations:** > - **Chemical Explosion** → Energy from rapid oxidation/combustion reaction (e.g., TNT, gas detonation). > - **Mechanical Explosion** → Energy from sudden release of pressurized fluid or phase-change expansion (e.g., BLEVE, vessel rupture). > - **Nuclear Explosion** → Energy from nuclear fission/fusion. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Explanation for Students:** > Imagine a pressurized scuba diving tank being heated accidentally by a fire. The metal walls weaken with rising temperature, but the gas inside keeps pushing outward with the same relentless force. When the metal finally gives way, it does so **instantaneously** — the entire trapped gas rushes out in microseconds. The air being shoved outward by this escaping gas piles up into a **shock wave** (a wall of compressed air), much like a snowplow blade. This wall of compressed air is what knocks down buildings, ruptures eardrums, and throws fragments kilometers away. **Visualize it this way:** - **Pre-event:** High-pressure gas is like a tightly coiled spring — full of *potential* energy. - **Failure event:** The spring snaps. Energy converts from *potential → kinetic* in microseconds. - **Post-event:** A pressure wave radiates outward + fragments fly + fireball may form (if the contents were flammable). This is fundamentally different from a **chemical** explosion where the "spring" is *chemical bonds* in the fuel rather than pressurized gas. --- ## 1.3 Classification of Mechanical Explosions (KTU Syllabus) Mechanical explosions in chemical process safety are systematically classified into four primary categories, each with distinct energy release mechanisms: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Four Canonical Categories of Mechanical Explosions (as per Crowl & Louvar, KTU prescribed text):** | **S.No** | **Type** | **Energy Source** | **Typical Scenario** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion (BLEVE)** | Superheated liquid flash vaporization | LPG tank engulfed in fire | | 2 | **Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE)** | Combustion of dispersed flammable vapor | Propane leak igniting in open air | | 3 | **Dust Explosion** | Combustion of suspended fine particles | Grain silo, coal dust, flour mill | | 4 | **Pressure Vessel Rupture (PVR)** | Compressed gas expansion | Compressed air receiver failure | Each type has distinct damage prediction methodologies, which we will explore in the subsequent sections. --- ## 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Critical Physical Constants & Damage Metrics (Bold for emphasis):** > - **Adiabatic Expansion Constant (γ = Cp/Cv):** For most diatomic gases **γ = 1.4**; for triatomic gases (CO₂, SO₂) **γ = 1.28**; for monatomic gases **γ = 1.67**. > - **Atmospheric Pressure (P_atm):** **101,325 Pa = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar**. > - **Lethal Overpressure Threshold:** **70 kPa (≈10 psi)** — 50% fatality from lung rupture. > - **Glass Breakage Threshold:** **1 kPa (0.145 psi)**. > - **Eardrum Rupture Threshold:** **16 kPa (2.3 psi)**. > - **TNT Energy Yield:** **4.68 MJ/kg** (standardized benchmark). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Mechanical Explosion Energy A mechanical explosion is governed by the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to an adiabatic (no heat transfer, instantaneous) expansion of a high-pressure gas into a low-pressure environment. ### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Energy Release > [!NOTE] > **The Six Logical Steps in a Mechanical Explosion Event:** 1. **Initial State:** Gas at high pressure **P₁** and volume **V₁** is contained within a pressure vessel. 2. **Stored Energy:** The compressed gas possesses internal energy proportional to **P₁ × V₁**. 3. **Catastrophic Failure:** The vessel wall fails, creating an opening with area **A**. 4. **Adiabatic Expansion:** Gas expands from **P₁ → P_atm** (or P₂) without heat exchange (the process is too fast for heat transfer). 5. **Pressure Wave Generation:** The expanding gas shoves atmospheric air outward, creating a propagating shock front. 6. **Damage Consequence:** The blast wave causes overpressure damage, fragment projection, and structural failure. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Mandatory Memorization for ESE) > [!IMPORTANT] > **All KTU 2024 Scheme High-Yield Formulas for Mechanical Explosions:** | **#** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Variables & Units** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **F-1** | $E = \dfrac{(P_1 - P_2)(V_1)}{\gamma - 1}$ | Energy released by ideal gas in adiabatic expansion (no PV work recovery) | E [J], P [Pa], V [m³], γ [–] | | **F-2** | $E = (P_1 - P_2) \cdot V_1 \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)$ | Energy for **isothermal** expansion (slow release) | E [J], P [Pa], V [m³] | | **F-3** | $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{E}{E_{TNT}}$ where $E_{TNT} = 4.68 \times 10^6$ J/kg | **TNT Equivalency Method** — converts mechanical explosion energy to kg of TNT | W_TNT [kg TNT eq.] | | **F-4** | $Z = \dfrac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}$ | **Scaled Distance** — Hopkinson-Cranz scaling law | Z [m/kg^(1/3)], R [m] | | **F-5** | $P_s = \dfrac{8.0 \times 10^5}{Z^{3.0}} + \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^5}{Z^{2.0}}$ (for **side-on** overpressure, Z < 1) | Baker overpressure correlation (high-pressure region) | P_s [Pa], Z [m/kg^(1/3)] | | **F-6** | $P_s = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^5}{Z^{2.0}} + \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^4}{Z}$ (for Z > 1) | Baker overpressure correlation (low-pressure region) | P_s [Pa] | | **F-7** | $E_{BLEVE} = m \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{liq} - T_{boil})$ | Energy released during BLEVE superheat | E [J], m [kg], C_p [J/kg·K], T [K] | | **F-8** | $V = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^3$ | Volume of a spherical fireball in BLEVE | V [m³], r [m] | | **F-9** | $r_{max} = 2.9 \cdot W^{0.320}$ (TNO correlation) | Maximum fireball radius from BLEVE | r [m], W [kg of fuel] | | **F-10** | $P_{rupture} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \sigma \cdot t}{D}$ | Bursting pressure of thin-walled cylinder (von Mises criterion for ductile failure) | P [Pa], σ [Pa], t [m], D [m] | | **F-11** | $\text{Fragment Range } R_{max} = \dfrac{V_0^2 \sin(2\theta)}{g}$ | Maximum horizontal range of vessel fragment | R [m], V₀ [m/s], θ [°], g = 9.81 m/s² | --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Why This Framework Matters in Real Engineering:** > - **Plant Siting & Layout:** Determining safe separation distances between process units, control rooms, and occupied buildings using **Z-scaled damage criteria**. > - **Emergency Relief Design (API 520/521):** Sizing rupture discs and pressure safety valves (PSVs) using the **adiabatic expansion** model to prevent catastrophic vessel rupture. > - **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA):** Using **TNT equivalency** to compute individual and societal risk contours for insurance, licensing, and regulatory compliance (e.g., PESO, OSHA PSM 29 CFR 1910.119). > - **Hazardous Area Classification:** Setting up **exclusion zones** around LPG storage based on BLEVE fireball thermal radiation radius. > - **Forensic Accident Investigation:** Back-calculating vessel failure pressure from fragment trajectory and rupture morphology. --- ## 2.4 BLEVE — The Most Studied Mechanical Explosion in Chemical Plants > [!IMPORTANT] > **BLEVE Definition (Mandatory for KTU Long Answers):** > A **Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion (BLEVE)** occurs when a vessel containing a liquid stored above its atmospheric boiling point (superheated liquid) ruptures catastrophically. The sudden depressurization causes the **entire liquid mass to flash instantaneously into vapor**, multiplying its volume by a factor of **100 to 250 times** depending on initial temperature and thermodynamic conditions. The resulting fireball and pressure wave are extremely destructive. ### 2.4.1 Sequence of Events in a BLEVE (Six-Phase Model) 1. **Phase 1 — Heat Exposure:** External fire (pool fire, jet fire) impinges on vessel wall. 2. **Phase 2 — Wall Thinning:** Steel loses strength rapidly above **400–500°C** (temper embrittlement). 3. **Phase 3 — Pressure Surge:** The boiling liquid-vapor system reaches a critical superheat limit. 4. **Phase 4 — Catastrophic Rupture:** Vessel tears open; pressure drops to atmospheric in milliseconds. 5. **Phase 5 — Flash Vaporization:** Superheated liquid flashes to vapor (≈ 100× volume expansion). 6. **Phase 6 — Fireball + Blast Wave:** Combustible vapor ignites → fireball + overpressure damage. --- ## 2.5 Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE) — Theoretical Basis > [!NOTE] > **VCE Definition:** > A **Vapor Cloud Explosion** occurs when a flammable vapor escapes containment, disperses into the atmosphere, mixes with air within the **flammable range** (between LFL and UFL), and is subsequently ignited. The flame accelerates through the partially confined/congested cloud, generating destructive overpressure far exceeding simple deflagration. ### 2.5.1 The Multi-Energy Method (TNO — van den Berg) The **Multi-Energy Method** treats a VCE as a partial spherical blast from a **strong ignition source** within a confined/congested region. The energy is characterized by a **flame speed factor (cube root relation):** $$ E_{VCE} = V_{cloud} \times \rho \times \Delta H_c \times \eta $$ Where **η (combustion efficiency)** depends on congestion, confinement, and turbulence — typically **η = 0.1 to 0.4** for chemical plants. --- ## 2.6 Dust Explosion — Pentagon of Combustion > [!IMPORTANT] > **Dust Explosion Mechanism (The Dust Explosion Pentagon):** > Unlike gases, dusts require a fifth element — **dispersion (suspension)** — making the classic fire triangle into a **pentagon**: Fuel + Oxidant + Ignition + Confinement + **Dispersion**. Critical minimum explosive concentration (MEC) for most organic dusts lies between **40–500 g/m³**, with **minimum ignition energy (MIE)** typically **10–100 mJ** — orders of magnitude lower than gases, making dusts exceptionally sensitive to static discharge. ### 2.6.1 Kst Index (Deflagration Index) — KTU Key Concept > [!IMPORTANT] > **Kst Value Classification (Mandatory Memorization):** | **Dust Hazard Class** | **Kst Range (bar·m/s)** | **Examples** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **St-1 (Weak)** | 0 – 200 | Zinc, sulfur, coal dust | | **St-2 (Strong)** | 200 – 300 | Cellulose, wood, cork | | **St-3 (Very Strong)** | > 300 | Aluminum, magnesium, TNT dust | The **cube-root law** for vent sizing uses Kst: $$ A_v = K_{st} \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{3}\right)^{2/3} $$ Where A_v is the required vent area and V is the enclosure volume. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Released in Adiabatic Expansion of a Pressurized Gas > [!NOTE] > **Starting Point — First Law of Thermodynamics for a Closed, Adiabatic System:** The energy released during a sudden vessel failure is computed by assuming the high-pressure gas expands **adiabatically** (no heat transfer) from **P₁ to P₂** while doing PV work on the surroundings. **Step 1 — State the governing differential equation:** The work done by gas during expansion is the area under the P-V curve: $$ dW = P \, dV $$ The energy balance (internal energy change) for adiabatic process: $$ dE = -P \, dV $$ **Step 2 — Express pressure as a function of volume using adiabatic relation:** For a reversible adiabatic process: $$ P V^{\gamma} = \text{constant} = P_1 V_1^{\gamma} = P_2 V_2^{\gamma} $$ Solving for P: $$ P = P_1 \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V}\right)^{\gamma} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute into the energy integral:** $$ E = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P_1 \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V}\right)^{\gamma} dV $$ **Step 4 — Perform the integration:** $$ E = P_1 V_1^{\gamma} \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V^{-\gamma} \, dV = P_1 V_1^{\gamma} \left[ \dfrac{V^{1-\gamma}}{1-\gamma} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} $$ **Step 5 — Apply the integration limits:** $$ E = \dfrac{P_1 V_1^{\gamma}}{1-\gamma} \left[ V_2^{1-\gamma} - V_1^{1-\gamma} \right] $$ **Step 6 — Factor and simplify using the final pressure P₂:** Since $P_2 V_2^{\gamma} = P_1 V_1^{\gamma}$, we have $P_2 = P_1 \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma}$: $$ E = \dfrac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - \gamma} $$ Multiplying numerator and denominator by **−1**: $$ E = \dfrac{(P_1 - P_2) V_1}{\gamma - 1} \quad \text{(Formula F-1 derived)} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Derived Result:** > $$\boxed{\,E = \dfrac{(P_1 - P_2)(V_1)}{\gamma - 1}\,}$$ > This is the **fundamental energy equation** used universally for pressure vessel rupture and BLEVE blast calculations in KTU problems. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU 2024 Pattern > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement:** > A 10 m³ pressure vessel contains air at **P₁ = 1.0 MPa (absolute)**. The vessel ruptures, and the air expands adiabatically to atmospheric pressure **P₂ = 101.325 kPa**. Take **γ = 1.4** for air. Determine: > (a) The energy released by the explosion. > (b) The TNT equivalent mass. > (c) The side-on overpressure at a distance of **R = 50 m** from the vessel. --- ### **Part (a): Energy Released** **Step 1 — Substitute values into Formula F-1:** $$ E = \dfrac{(P_1 - P_2)(V_1)}{\gamma - 1} $$ **Step 2 — Convert units to SI (Pa and m³):** - $P_1 = 1.0 \times 10^6$ Pa - $P_2 = 1.01325 \times 10^5$ Pa - $V_1 = 10$ m³ - $\gamma = 1.4$ **Step 3 — Compute the pressure difference:** $$ P_1 - P_2 = 1.0 \times 10^6 - 1.01325 \times 10^5 = 8.98675 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the denominator:** $$ \gamma - 1 = 1.4 - 1.0 = 0.4 $$ **Step 5 — Compute the energy:** $$ E = \dfrac{8.98675 \times 10^5 \times 10}{0.4} = \dfrac{8.98675 \times 10^6}{0.4} $$ $$ E = 2.247 \times 10^7 \text{ J} \approx 22.47 \text{ MJ} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Answer (a):** $\boxed{E \approx 22.47 \text{ MJ}}$ --- ### **Part (b): TNT Equivalent Mass** **Step 1 — Apply Formula F-3:** $$ W_{TNT} = \dfrac{E}{E_{TNT}} = \dfrac{2.247 \times 10^7 \text{ J}}{4.68 \times 10^6 \text{ J/kg}} $$ **Step 2 — Compute:** $$ W_{TNT} = 4.802 \text{ kg TNT} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Answer (b):** $\boxed{W_{TNT} \approx 4.80 \text{ kg TNT equivalent}}$ --- ### **Part (c): Overpressure at R = 50 m** **Step 1 — Calculate the scaled distance Z (Formula F-4):** $$ Z = \dfrac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}} = \dfrac{50}{(4.802)^{1/3}} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the cube root:** $$ (4.802)^{1/3} \approx 1.686 $$ **Step 3 — Compute Z:** $$ Z = \dfrac{50}{1.686} = 29.66 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3} $$ **Step 4 — Since Z > 1, use Formula F-6:** $$ P_s = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^5}{Z^{2.0}} + \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^4}{Z} $$ **Step 5 — Compute the terms:** - $Z^2 = (29.66)^2 = 879.7$ - $\dfrac{1.0 \times 10^5}{879.7} = 113.7$ Pa - $\dfrac{4.0 \times 10^4}{29.66} = 1348.6$ Pa **Step 6 — Sum:** $$ P_s = 113.7 + 1348.6 = 1462.3 \text{ Pa} \approx 1.46 \text{ kPa} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Answer (c):** $\boxed{P_s \approx 1.46 \text{ kPa}}$ --- **Interpretation:** A side-on overpressure of **1.46 kPa** at 50 m corresponds to approximately **0.21 psi** — sufficient to shatter glass windows but well below structural damage thresholds (3 kPa). This justifies the **50 m safe separation distance** for this vessel size. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Fragment Range from Vessel Rupture > [!NOTE] > **Step-by-Step Fragment Trajectory Derivation:** **Step 1 — Apply Newton's Second Law (vertical motion):** $$ m \dfrac{d^2 y}{dt^2} = -mg $$ **Step 2 — Integrate once (vertical velocity):** $$ v_y(t) = v_0 \sin\theta - g t $$ **Step 3 — Integrate again (vertical displacement):** $$ y(t) = v_0 \sin\theta \cdot t - \dfrac{1}{2} g t^2 $$ **Step 4 — Apply horizontal motion (constant velocity):** $$ x(t) = v_0 \cos\theta \cdot t $$ **Step 5 — Eliminate parameter t by substituting $t = x/(v_0 \cos\theta)$:** $$ y(x) = x \tan\theta - \dfrac{g x^2}{2 v_0^2 \cos^2\theta} $$ **Step 6 — Set y = 0 (ground level) and solve for the landing distance:** For the parabolic trajectory, the maximum horizontal range occurs when the fragment lands back at the launcher's elevation: $$ R = \dfrac{v_0^2 \sin(2\theta)}{g} $$ **Step 7 — Maximize by setting dR/dθ = 0:** $$ \dfrac{dR}{d\theta} = \dfrac{v_0^2 \cdot 2\cos(2\theta)}{g} = 0 \implies \cos(2\theta) = 0 \implies \theta = 45° $$ **Step 8 — Substitute θ = 45°:** $$ R_{max} = \dfrac{v_0^2 \sin(90°)}{g} = \dfrac{v_0^2}{g} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Result (Fragment Range, Formula F-11 simplified):** > $$\boxed{\,R_{max} = \dfrac{v_0^2}{g}\,}$$ > This justifies the empirical **Clancey Formula** used for blast fragment hazard analysis: > $$\boxed{\,R_{max} (\text{km}) \approx 90.5 \cdot [M (\text{kg})]^{1/3}\,}$$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Damage Criterion | **Overpressure P_s (kPa)** | **Damage Effect** | **Action Required** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | < 1 | Window glass breakage | Safe for personnel with PPE | | 1 – 3 | Light structural damage, injury risk | Localized hazard zone | | 3 – 7 | Partial building collapse, eardrum rupture | Major injury threshold | | 7 – 15 | Reinforced concrete damage, fatal injuries | Critical hazard exclusion | | 15 – 50 | Heavy structural damage, near-total fatalities | Catastrophic zone | | > 50 | Total destruction, 100% fatalities | Inhabitable | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Mechanical Explosion Event > [!NOTE] > **Block Diagram (Adapted as Mermaid for Physical Process Flow):** ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Energy Storage Phase
Pressurized Gas in Vessel] --> B{Catastrophic Failure Trigger} B --> C[Mechanical Failure
Vessel Wall Rupture] B --> D[Thermal Trigger
BLEVE Fire Engulfment] B --> E[Loss of Containment
Vapor Cloud Formation] C --> F[Adiabatic Gas Expansion
PV Work Release] D --> G[Superheated Liquid Flash
100x Volume Expansion] E --> H[Vapor Dispersion
Flammable Mixing Zone] F --> I[Primary Blast Wave
Overpressure Generation] G --> J[Fireball Formation
Thermal Radiation Zone] H --> K[Delayed Ignition
Vapor Cloud Explosion] I --> L[Damage Consequence Zone
Structure Collapse Risk] J --> M[Thermal Injury Zone
Skin Burn Radius] K --> N[Secondary Blast Wave
Multi-Energy Method] L --> O[Final Damage Assessment
TNT Equivalency Mapping] M --> O N --> O O --> P[Safety Mitigation Output
Separation Distance, PSV Sizing] ``` --- ## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Explosion Type Identification ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Explosion Event Occurs] --> B{Is there Combustion?} B -- No --> C[Mechanical Explosion] B -- Yes --> D{Is the Fuel a Liquid or Vapor?} D -- Liquid Superheated --> E[BLEVE] D -- Vapor/Aerosol Cloud --> F[Vapor Cloud Explosion] D -- Solid Particulates --> G[Dust Explosion] C --> H{Storage State?} H -- Compressed Gas --> I[Pressure Vessel Rupture] H -- Liquefied Gas --> J[BLEVE Classification] E --> K[Apply BLEVE Fireball Model] F --> L[Apply Multi-Energy Method] G --> M[Apply Kst Vent Sizing] I --> N[Apply TNT Equivalency] ``` --- ## 4.3 Pressure-Time Schematic of a BLEVE > [!NOTE] > **Description of Pressure Profile (Adapted Block Schematic):** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Initial State
P=P_service
T=T_superheat] --> B[Heat Absorption Phase
Slow Pressure Rise] B --> C[Wall Weakening
Critical Temperature ~450C] C --> D[Failure Point
Catastrophic Rupture] D --> E[Pressure Drop to Atm
Time ~ 1 ms] E --> F[Flash Vaporization
Volume x100] F --> G[Fireball Ignition
BLEVE Fireball] G --> H[Pressure Reaches Ambient
Mechanical Wave Propagates] ``` --- ## 4.4 Functional Process Architecture — Damage Estimation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Parameters
P1, V1, gamma, distance R] --> B[Calculate Energy Released
E = P1-P2 V1 over gamma minus 1] B --> C[Compute TNT Equivalent
WTNT = E over 4.68 MJ per kg] C --> D[Calculate Scaled Distance
Z = R over WTNT cube root] D --> E{Scaled Distance Z} E -- Z less than 1 --> F[Use Baker Strong Shock Equation] E -- Z greater than 1 --> G[Use Baker Weak Shock Equation] F --> H[Overpressure Pa] G --> H H --> I[Cross-Reference Damage Table
Threshold for Injury] I --> J[Output Safe Separation Distance] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define a **Mechanical Explosion**. How is it fundamentally different from a chemical explosion? **Model Answer:** A mechanical explosion is the sudden, catastrophic failure of a pressurized containment system, releasing stored pressure-volume energy in the form of a blast wave and kinetic fragments. **Difference Table:** | **Parameter** | **Mechanical Explosion** | **Chemical Explosion** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Energy Source | Compressed gas/liquid | Rapid oxidation reaction | | Initiation | Vessel rupture | Ignition of fuel-oxidant mix | | Energy Density | Lower (kJ/m³) | Higher (MJ/kg fuel) | | Example | Compressed air tank | TNT detonation | --- > [!NOTE] > **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`** What is a **BLEVE**? State **three** essential conditions for a BLEVE to occur. **Model Answer:** **BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion):** A catastrophic event in which a liquid stored above its atmospheric boiling point experiences sudden depressurization, causing instantaneous flash vaporization and the formation of a destructive fireball. **Three Essential Conditions:** 1. The liquid must be **superheated** (i.e., its temperature exceeds its normal boiling point at atmospheric pressure). 2. There must be a **catastrophic vessel failure** (caused by corrosion, overheating, or mechanical damage). 3. The vessel contents must be **combustible** (LPG, propane, butane) for the fireball component. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Instructions:** Answer **ANY ONE** from the following two choices. Each carries 14 marks split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`** **A.(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the **sequence of events** leading to a **BLEVE** in a LPG storage vessel exposed to external fire. State the **TNO correlation** for maximum fireball radius. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Introduction to BLEVE Context:** A BLEVE is most commonly observed in LPG (liquefied petroleum gas) storage tanks subjected to external fire impingement, such as from a neighbouring pool fire or jet fire during an unrelated process upset. **Step 2 — Six-Phase Sequence of Events:** 1. **Heat Exposure:** External fire heats the unshielded (or inadequately water-cooled) portion of the vessel shell.
