The metal temperature rises progressively. 2. **Wall Thinning & Strength Loss:** As the steel temperature exceeds approximately **400–500°C**, the yield strength drops sharply (low-carbon steel retains only ~50% of its room-temperature yield strength at 500°C). 3. **Pressure Build-Up:** The LPG inside boils, generating vapour. The pressure-relief valve opens but may be insufficient to handle the rising boil-off rate. 4. **Catastrophic Rupture:** The weakened shell tears open. The entire vessel fails in milliseconds — this is the defining characteristic of a BLEVE. 5. **Flash Vaporization:** The sudden drop to atmospheric pressure causes the superheated LPG liquid to flash into vapour. Volume expansion of **200–250 times** occurs. 6. **Fireball Formation & Ignition:** The flammable vapour immediately mixes with air and is ignited by the surrounding fire, producing a massive fireball (often hundreds of metres in diameter) and a blast pressure wave. **Step 3 — TNO Correlation for Maximum Fireball Radius:** The **TNO (Netherlands Organisation for Applied Scientific Research)** correlation is: $$ r_{max} = 2.9 \cdot W^{0.32} $$ Where: - $r_{max}$ = maximum fireball radius in metres - $W$ = mass of fuel participating in the BLEVE in kg **Step 4 — Application:** For an LPG mass of 10,000 kg, $r_{max} = 2.9 \cdot (10000)^{0.32} \approx 2.9 \cdot 6.95 \approx 20.1$ m. The thermal radiation hazard radius extends to about **2 × r_max ≈ 40 m**. --- **A.(b) [7 Marks]** A **chlorine rail car** contains **50,000 kg of liquid chlorine** stored at **20°C** (boiling point –34°C, so the liquid is superheated). The latent heat of vaporization is **289 kJ/kg** and specific heat is **0.95 kJ/kg·K**. Calculate the **energy released** if the car experiences a BLEVE. Also compute the **TNT equivalent**. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify Given Data:** - Mass $m = 50{,}000$ kg - Initial temperature $T_{liq} = 20°C = 293$ K - Boiling point $T_{boil} = -34°C = 239$ K - $C_p = 0.95$ kJ/kg·K = 950 J/kg·K - $\Delta H_{vap} = 289$ kJ/kg = 289,000 J/kg **Step 2 — Energy from Cooling Liquid to Boiling Point:** $$ E_1 = m \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{liq} - T_{boil}) = 50{,}000 \times 950 \times (293 - 239) $$ $$ E_1 = 50{,}000 \times 950 \times 54 = 2.565 \times 10^9 \text{ J} $$ **Step 3 — Energy from Vaporization (assuming all liquid flashes):** $$ E_2 = m \cdot \Delta H_{vap} = 50{,}000 \times 289{,}000 = 1.445 \times 10^{10} \text{ J} $$ **Step 4 — Total Energy Released:** $$ E_{total} = E_1 + E_2 = 2.565 \times 10^9 + 1.445 \times 10^{10} = 1.7015 \times 10^{10} \text{ J} $$ **Step 5 — TNT Equivalent:** $$ W_{TNT} = \dfrac{1.7015 \times 10^{10}}{4.68 \times 10^6} = 3{,}635.7 \text{ kg TNT} \approx 3.64 \text{ tonnes TNT} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answer:** Total energy ≈ **17.02 GJ**, equivalent to ≈ **3,636 kg of TNT**. This indicates that a BLEVE of a single chlorine rail car has the explosive yield of **~3.6 tonnes of TNT** — a critical input for QRA siting studies. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2022]`** **B.(a) [7 Marks]** What is a **Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE)**? Describe the **Multi-Energy Method** (TNO) for VCE damage estimation. List **three process safety measures** to prevent VCE. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — VCE Definition:** A Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE) is the combustion of a flammable vapor cloud formed by the release of a flammable gas or vaporized liquid into the open atmosphere. The flame accelerates through partially confined or congested regions of the cloud, generating destructive overpressures far exceeding those of a simple deflagration. **Step 2 — Multi-Energy Method (TNO) Description:** Developed by **van den Berg and Eggen (TNO, Netherlands)**, the Multi-Energy Method conceptualizes the VCE blast as a partial spherical explosion source of a certain strength. The method assumes: - The explosion energy is concentrated in the **confined/congested portion** of the cloud (typically 10–40% of the total cloud). - The energy is released as a strong blast with a finite initial velocity. - The blast source strength is characterized by an initial blast energy and a charge radius. - Damage at distance R is computed using the **Hopkinson-Cranz scaling law** with scaled distance $Z = R/E^{1/3}$. **Energy formula:** $$ E = V_{congested} \times \rho_{vapor} \times \Delta H_c \times \eta $$ Where $\eta$ (combustion efficiency) is **0.1 to 0.4** for typical plant geometries. **Step 3 — Three Process Safety Measures to Prevent VCE:** 1. **Inert Gas Purging & Blanketing:** Maintain vapor space above flammable liquids with nitrogen to prevent flammable mixture formation. 2. **Gas Detection Systems:** Install catalytic/IR gas detectors at strategic locations with automatic shutdown interlocks upon reaching 25% of LFL. 3. **Open-Frame Plant Design (Decongestion):** Eliminate unnecessary obstacles, equipment, and structural supports that create turbulent flame acceleration zones. --- **B.(b) [7 Marks]** A **pressure vessel** of **volume V = 5 m³** contains methane at **P₁ = 8 bar (gauge)**. The vessel ruptures, and the gas expands adiabatically to atmospheric pressure. Take γ = 1.32 for methane. Calculate (i) the explosion energy, (ii) the TNT equivalent, and (iii) the side-on overpressure at **R = 100 m** from the rupture. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert Pressure to Absolute SI Units:** - $P_1 = 8 + 1.01325 = 9.01325$ bar $= 9.01325 \times 10^5$ Pa - $P_2 = P_{atm} = 1.01325 \times 10^5$ Pa - $V_1 = 5$ m³ - $\gamma = 1.32$ **Step 2 — Calculate Explosion Energy (Formula F-1):** $$ E = \dfrac{(P_1 - P_2) V_1}{\gamma - 1} = \dfrac{(9.01325 - 1.01325) \times 10^5 \times 5}{0.32} $$ $$ E = \dfrac{8.0 \times 10^5 \times 5}{0.32} = \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^6}{0.32} $$ $$ E = 1.25 \times 10^7 \text{ J} = 12.5 \text{ MJ} $$ **Step 3 — TNT Equivalent (Formula F-3):** $$ W_{TNT} = \dfrac{1.25 \times 10^7}{4.68 \times 10^6} = 2.671 \text{ kg TNT} $$ **Step 4 — Scaled Distance (Formula F-4):** $$ Z = \dfrac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}} = \dfrac{100}{(2.671)^{1/3}} = \dfrac{100}{1.387} = 72.1 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3} $$ **Step 5 — Since Z > 1, Apply Formula F-6:** $$ P_s = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^5}{Z^{2.0}} + \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^4}{Z} $$ - $Z^2 = 5198$ - $\dfrac{1.0 \times 10^5}{5198} = 19.24$ Pa - $\dfrac{4.0 \times 10^4}{72.1} = 554.8$ Pa $$ P_s = 19.24 + 554.8 = 574 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.57 \text{ kPa} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answers:** > - **(i) Explosion Energy:** $\boxed{E = 12.5 \text{ MJ}}$ > - **(ii) TNT Equivalent:** $\boxed{W_{TNT} = 2.67 \text{ kg TNT}}$ > - **(iii) Overpressure at 100 m:** $\boxed{P_s \approx 0.57 \text{ kPa (negligible — below window breakage threshold of 1 kPa)}}$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE Mechanical Explosion Questions:** > > 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Using bar without conversion to Pa. KTU strictly follows **SI units** in energy calculations. Always convert P₁ and P₂ to Pascals before applying Formula F-1. **(Cost: 2–3 marks)** > > 2. **Absolute vs Gauge Pressure Error:** Students forget to add atmospheric pressure (1.01325 bar) when gauge pressure is given. **Always use absolute pressure** in thermodynamic energy equations. **(Cost: 3 marks — fatal)** > > 3. **Wrong γ Value:** Using γ = 1.4 (air) for all gases. **Methane γ = 1.32, CO₂ γ = 1.28, propane γ = 1.13**. Use specific values from the question data. **(Cost: 1–2 marks)** > > 4. **Forgetting the Factor of 4.68 × 10⁶:** The TNT energy density must be in **J/kg**, not **MJ/kg**. A common student error is using 4.68 instead of 4.68 × 10⁶, yielding a TNT equivalent 10⁶ times too small. **(Cost: 3 marks)** > > 5. **Wrong Baker Formula Branch:** Using the **strong-shock** formula (Z < 1) when Z > 1, or vice versa. Always compute Z first, then select the correct branch. **(Cost: 2 marks)** > > 6. **Missing BLEVE Definition Components:** A complete BLEVE definition must include: (a) **superheated liquid**, (b) **catastrophic depressurization**, (c) **flash vaporization**, (d) **fireball/blast consequence**. Missing any one loses 1–2 marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Mechanical Explosions:** ### **Core Definitions** - Mechanical explosion = catastrophic release of **stored PV energy** (NOT chemical reaction energy). - BLEVE = Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion — flash vaporization of superheated liquid upon vessel rupture. - VCE = Vapor Cloud Explosion — combustion of dispersed flammable vapor-air mixture in partially confined regions. - Dust Explosion = Combustion of suspended fine particles (Kst classification: St-1, St-2, St-3). - PVR = Pressure Vessel Rupture — failure of pressurized containment. ### **Essential Formulas (Memorize Verbatim)** - $\boxed{E = \dfrac{(P_1 - P_2) V_1}{\gamma - 1}}$ — Adiabatic expansion energy (most used). - $\boxed{W_{TNT} = \dfrac{E}{4.68 \times 10^6}}$ — TNT equivalent (note J/kg). - $\boxed{Z = \dfrac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}}$ — Hopkinson-Cranz scaled distance. - $\boxed{P_s = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^5}{Z^2} + \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^4}{Z}}$ for Z > 1. - $\boxed{R_{max} = \dfrac{v_0^2}{g}}$ — Maximum fragment range at 45° launch. ### **Critical Numerical Values** - γ_air = **1.40**, γ_methane = **1.32**, γ_propane = **1.13**, γ_CO₂ = **1.28**. - TNT energy yield = **4.68 MJ/kg**. - Window breakage threshold = **1 kPa**. - Eardrum rupture = **16 kPa**. - 50% fatality from lung rupture = **70 kPa**. - Steel loses ~50% strength at **500°C** → critical BLEVE trigger temperature. ### **Damage Threshold Memory Aid** | **kPa** | **Effect** | | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Glass shatters | | 7 | Houses collapse | | 15 | Heavy damage | | 70 | 50% fatality | | 200 | Total destruction | ### **Prevention Hierarchy (Per KTU Process Safety Module)** 1. **Inherent Safety** — Reduce inventory, lower operating pressure. 2. **Engineering Controls** — PSVs, rupture discs, water spray deluge. 3. **Administrative Controls** — Hot work permits, gas testing procedures. 4. **PPE** — Last line of defence. ### **Last-Minute Exam Strategy** - Always **draw a clear labeled diagram** of the BLEVE process — even if not asked (KTU awards 1 mark for neat sketch). - For 14-mark questions, follow the **structured pattern**: (i) Definition, (ii) Sequence/steps, (iii) Formula derivation, (iv) Numerical substitution, (v) Answer with units, (vi) Interpretation/comment. - Always **state the assumption** (e.g., "assuming adiabatic expansion" or "neglecting fragment kinetic energy"). - For internal choice, prefer **BLEVE numericals** as they are the most frequently asked topic in KTU chemical process safety papers. > [!NOTE] > **End of Section 5 — Module 2 Mechanical Explosions Notes** > *Total module coverage aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for OESFT616 Chemical Process Safety.*

topics.title: Vapour Cloud Explosions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT616 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Vapour Cloud Explosions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE)** is defined as a catastrophic, *unconfined* combustion event that occurs when a significant quantity of flammable vapour escapes from a process unit, mixes with ambient air, and is subsequently ignited. The resulting flame front propagates through a **partially confined, partially confined, or uncongested** fuel-air mixture at velocities that may transition from subsonic (deflagration) to sonic/supersonic (detonation), generating destructive overpressures.

According to the **Center for Chemical Process Safety (CCPS) Guidelines for Chemical Process Quantitative Risk Analysis (CPQRA)** and the **TNO "Green Book"** (Methods for the Determination of Possible Damage), a VCE is technically a **deflagration-to-detonation transition (DDT)** phenomenon in the most damaging scenarios, although most industrial VCEs are subsonic deflagrations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition (Board Expected):**
> *"A Vapour Cloud Explosion is the explosive combustion of a cloud of flammable vapour, gas, or mist mixed with air in the open atmosphere, following the release of a large quantity of flammable material and its subsequent delayed ignition."*

The four essential preconditions (the **VCE Tetrahedron** extended to VCEs) are:
1. **Release** of flammable material (liquid, gas, two-phase)
2. **Dispersion** to form a flammable cloud between the **Lower Flammability Limit (LFL)** and **Upper Flammability Limit (UFL)**
3. **Delayed ignition** (otherwise it is a *flash fire* or *jet fire*)
4. **Confinement/congestion** to accelerate flame propagation

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Floating Whisper Cloud":**
> Imagine spilling a litre of gasoline in your closed garage. The liquid evaporates slowly, and the vapour invisibly fills the room. Now open the garage door and light a match at the door. The flame races inward — not because the gas "explodes" like TNT, but because the flame front *pushes* the unburnt gas ahead of it, compressing and accelerating it. This **piston-like push** is what generates the overpressure. The more obstacles (boxes, tools, parked car grille) in the path, the more turbulent the flame becomes and the faster it travels — like a crowd squeezing through a narrow doorway. The resulting blast wave is the **Vapour Cloud Explosion**.

## 1.3 Classification of VCE Outcomes

A vapour cloud event can manifest as one (or a combination) of the following:

| Phenomenon | Description | Overpressure | Thermal |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Flash Fire** | Non-damaging combustion of cloud; no significant overpressure | Negligible | High (radiation) |
| **VCE (Deflagration)** | Subsonic flame front, peak overpressure 0.1 – 1.0 bar | Moderate | Moderate |
| **VCE (Detonation)** | Supersonic flame, peak overpressure > 2 bar | Severe | Severe |
| **BLEVE** | Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion (related but distinct) | Severe (missile + blast) | Very High |

## 1.4 Critical Physical Constants & Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU-Listed Constants for VCE Calculations:**
> - Energy of detonation of TNT: **H_TNT = 4680 kJ/kg = 4.68 MJ/kg**
> - Heat of combustion of typical hydrocarbon (e.g., propane): **ΔH_c ≈ 46,000 kJ/kg**
> - Ambient pressure: **P₀ = 101.325 kPa = 1 atm**
> - Ambient temperature: **T₀ = 298 K (25 °C)**
> - Speed of sound in air: **c₀ ≈ 340 m/s**
> - Yield ratio typical range: **Y = 1 to 7** (stoichiometric fuel-rich clouds)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Five Stages of a VCE

A complete VCE event unfolds in **five sequential stages**. Each stage determines the parameters needed for the next, and KTU examiners frequently test this logic.

### Stage 1 — Release (Source Term)
A catastrophic or continuous release creates a pool, jet, or two-phase plume. The mass released is calculated from:
- **Liquid pool evaporation** (e.g., from TNO USEMOD or EPA models)
- **Gas/jet discharge** (isothermal or adiabatic, choked or unchoked)

### Stage 2 — Dispersion
The released material disperses downwind under atmospheric stability classes (Pasquill-Gifford A–F). The cloud is **flammable** between LFL and UFL. Only the **portion within the flammable envelope** contributes to the explosion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stoichiometric Consideration — Yield Ratio (Y):**
> The energy released per unit volume of cloud depends on whether the mixture is fuel-rich or fuel-lean. The **Yield Ratio** is defined as:
> **Y = Stoichiometric fuel-air ratio / Actual fuel-air ratio**
> - Y > 1 → fuel-lean (insufficient fuel)
> - Y < 1 → fuel-rich (insufficient oxygen)
> Most realistic clouds are fuel-rich (Y < 1); only the stoichiometric core releases maximum energy.

### Stage 3 — Delayed Ignition
The time delay between cloud formation and ignition determines:
- **Cloud size** (larger delay → larger cloud)
- **Degree of mixing** (more delay → more homogeneous)
- **Likelihood of finding an ignition source**

### Stage 4 — Combustion & Blast Wave Generation
The flame front propagates outward. The **flame speed (M_f, Mach number of flame relative to unburnt gas)** is the critical parameter:
- **M_f = 0.05 – 0.2** → weak deflagration (open space, no congestion)
- **M_f = 0.35 – 0.5** → strong deflagration (partial confinement)
- **M_f = 0.5 – 0.8** → very strong (highly congested)
- **M_f ≥ 1.0** → detonation regime

The **peak side-on overpressure (ΔP)** scales with the cube of flame speed (roughly).

### Stage 5 — Damage Propagation
Blast wave impacts structures. Damage criteria use:
- Peak overpressure ΔP (kPa or psi)
- Impulse I (Pa·s)
- Scaled distance Z (m/kg^(1/3))

## 2.2 The Three Principal Damage-Estimation Methods

### Method 1 — TNT Equivalency Method
Oldest and simplest. Compares VCE energy to an equivalent mass of TNT.

### Method 2 — TNO Multi-Energy Method
Recognises that VCE strength depends on **confinement/congestion**, not just fuel mass. Classifies explosions into 10 strength classes.

### Method 3 — Baker-Strehlow Method
Uses **flame speed** (Mach number) as the primary driver, with explicit treatment of geometry (1D/2D/3D) and congestion density.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Board-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula Sheet for Vapour Cloud Explosions**

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| F1 | $m_{fuel} = V_{cloud} \times C_{fuel}$ | Mass of fuel in flammable cloud (kg) |
| F2 | $Y = \dfrac{\phi_{stoich}}{\phi_{actual}}$ | Yield ratio (dimensionless) |
| F3 | $E_{fuel} = m_{fuel} \times \Delta H_c \times \eta_c$ | Available chemical energy (MJ) |
| F4 | $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta_{TNT} \times m_{fuel} \times \Delta H_c}{H_{TNT}}$ | TNT equivalent mass (kg) |
| F5 | $Z = \dfrac{R}{\left(W_{TNT}\right)^{1/3}}$ | Scaled distance (m/kg^(1/3)) |
| F6 | $\dfrac{\Delta P}{P_0} = f(Z)$ | Overpressure correlation (Kingery, Wilton, etc.) |
| F7 | $\dfrac{i}{P_0^{2/3} \cdot E^{1/3}} = g\!\left(\dfrac{R}{E^{1/3}}\right)$ | TNO Multi-Energy scaled impulse |
| F8 | $E = V \times \rho_{vap} \times \Delta H_c \times \eta$ | TNO initial blast energy (MJ) |
| F9 | $\bar{R} = \dfrac{R}{\left(E/P_0\right)^{1/3}}$ | TNO scaled distance (dimensionless) |
| F10 | $M_f = \dfrac{v_f}{c_0}$ | Flame Mach number (Baker-Strehlow) |
| F11 | $\Delta P_{peak} \propto M_f^{n}$ | Overpressure scales with flame speed (n ≈ 2–3) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Conversion:** $\eta_{TNT}$ is the **yield factor** (typically 0.01 to 0.10, i.e., 1% to 10%); $\eta$ in F8 is the **TNO combustion efficiency** (typically 0.1 to 0.4). Do NOT confuse the two — KTU examiners routinely test this distinction.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

VCE modelling is **mandatory** for:
- **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** of offshore platforms (Norsok, API RP 752/753)
- **Facility siting** under OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 and EPA RMP
- **Land-use planning** around LPG terminals, refineries, LNG facilities
- **Design of blast-resistant control rooms** (CCPS *Guidelines for Evaluating Process Plant Buildings for External Explosion and Fire*)
- **Insurance and corporate risk register** quantification (Loss Prevention Councils)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — TNT Equivalency Method (Full Working)

The TNT method converts the chemical energy of the vapour cloud into an equivalent mass of TNT, then uses empirical blast curves from open-air TNT detonations.

### Step 1 — Determine the flammable mass of fuel in the cloud

$$
m_{fuel} = V_{cloud} \times \rho_{vapour} \times C_{st} \times Y_{eff}
$$

Where:
- $V_{cloud}$ = volume of flammable cloud (m³)
- $\rho_{vapour}$ = density of fuel vapour at release conditions (kg/m³)
- $C_{st}$ = stoichiometric concentration (volume fraction)
- $Y_{eff}$ = effective yield ratio

### Step 2 — Compute total chemical energy released

$$
E_{chem} = m_{fuel} \times \Delta H_c
$$

Here $\Delta H_c$ is the **heat of combustion** in kJ/kg.

### Step 3 — Apply the TNT yield factor (efficiency)

The yield factor $\eta_{TNT}$ accounts for the fact that VCEs are *not* as efficient as condensed-phase TNT detonations.

$$
E_{VCE} = \eta_{TNT} \times E_{chem}
$$

Typical industry values:
- $\eta_{TNT} = 0.01$ to 0.05 (1%–5%) for **free-field** open-air clouds
- $\eta_{TNT} = 0.05$ to 0.10 (5%–10%) for **partially confined/congested** clouds
- KTU default value used in problems: **η_TNT = 0.03** (3%) unless stated otherwise

### Step 4 — Convert VCE energy to equivalent TNT mass

$$
W_{TNT} = \frac{E_{VCE}}{H_{TNT}} = \frac{\eta_{TNT} \times m_{fuel} \times \Delta H_c}{H_{TNT}}
$$

$$
W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta_{TNT} \times m_{fuel} \times \Delta H_c}{4680\ \text{kJ/kg}}
$$

### Step 5 — Calculate scaled distance for the receiver point

$$
Z = \frac{R}{\left(W_{TNT}\right)^{1/3}}
$$

Where $R$ is the stand-off distance from cloud centre to receiver in metres.

### Step 6 — Read peak overpressure from empirical correlations

The **Kingery–Bulmash** or **Wilton** correlation gives $\Delta P / P_0$ as a function of $Z$:

$$
\frac{\Delta P}{P_0} = \frac{808 \left[ 1 + \left(\dfrac{Z}{4.5}\right)^2 \right]}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{Z}{0.048}\right)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{Z}{0.32}\right)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{Z}{1.35}\right)^2}}
$$

(Valid range: $0.1 \le Z \le 50$ m/kg^(1/3))

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Board Style

> [!NOTE]
> **Example Problem (Model Exam Pattern):**
> *A 10,000 kg spill of propane (ΔH_c = 46,000 kJ/kg) forms a vapour cloud. 30% of the spilled mass participates in the explosion as a stoichiometric mixture. Using η_TNT = 0.05, estimate the equivalent TNT mass and the peak overpressure at a distance of 500 m from the cloud centre.*

### Given:
- $m_{fuel} = 0.30 \times 10{,}000 = 3000$ kg
- $\Delta H_c = 46{,}000$ kJ/kg
- $\eta_{TNT} = 0.05$
- $H_{TNT} = 4680$ kJ/kg
- $R = 500$ m

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Chemical energy released
$$
E_{chem} = m_{fuel} \times \Delta H_c = 3000 \times 46{,}000 = 1.38 \times 10^{8}\ \text{kJ}
$$

**Step 2:** Effective VCE energy
$$
E_{VCE} = \eta_{TNT} \times E_{chem} = 0.05 \times 1.38 \times 10^{8} = 6.9 \times 10^{6}\ \text{kJ}
$$

**Step 3:** Equivalent TNT mass
$$
W_{TNT} = \frac{E_{VCE}}{H_{TNT}} = \frac{6.9 \times 10^{6}}{4680} \approx 1474\ \text{kg TNT}
$$

**Step 4:** Scaled distance
$$
Z = \frac{R}{\left(W_{TNT}\right)^{1/3}} = \frac{500}{(1474)^{1/3}} = \frac{500}{11.36} \approx 44.0\ \text{m/kg}^{1/3}
$$

**Step 5:** Peak overpressure (from Kingery–Bulmash at Z = 44)
For very large Z, the overpressure approaches ambient and $\Delta P / P_0 \ll 1$.
$$
\frac{\Delta P}{P_0} \approx 0.012
$$

Therefore:
$$
\Delta P \approx 0.012 \times 101.325 \approx 1.2\ \text{kPa} \quad (\text{marginal damage at 500 m})
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students forget the cube root in the scaled distance or drop the yield factor. KTU boards deduct up to **4 marks** for this single omission.

## 3.3 Derivation — TNO Multi-Energy Method

The TNO method assumes the explosion behaves as a **hemispherical/polygonal blast source** with a known initial energy and a user-assigned **strength class** (1 to 10).

### Step 1 — Compute initial blast energy E

$$
E = V \times \Delta H_c \times \rho_v \times \eta
$$

- $V$ = volume of fuel-air mixture (m³)
- $\rho_v$ = vapour density of fuel in cloud (kg/m³)
- $\eta$ = combustion efficiency (use 0.1–0.4 for VCE; for stoichiometric propane-air, η ≈ 0.2 typical)

### Step 2 — Compute dimensionless scaled distance

$$
\bar{R} = \frac{R}{\left(E / P_0\right)^{1/3}}
$$

Where $E / P_0$ has units of (energy per unit pressure) → m³.

### Step 3 — Read $\Delta P_s$ from TNO strength-class curves

TNO publishes dimensionless overpressure curves $\Delta \bar{P}_s$ vs $\bar{R}$ for each of the 10 strength classes. Classes 1–6 correspond to deflagrations; classes 7–10 approach detonation. **For VCE in congested plant areas, the CCPS default is class 7–8.**

### Step 4 — Convert to dimensional overpressure

$$
\Delta P_s = \Delta \bar{P}_s \times P_0
$$

### Step 5 — Compute blast impulse (optional, for damage criteria)

$$
i = \bar{i}(\bar{R}) \times P_0^{2/3} \times E^{1/3}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Which Method When?

| Feature | TNT Equivalency | TNO Multi-Energy | Baker-Strehlow |
|---|---|---|---|
| Year introduced | 1950s | 1980s (TNO) | 1990s |
| Physical basis | Equivalent condensed explosive | Hemispherical blast source | Flame speed in congested geometry |
| Inputs required | Fuel mass, ΔH_c, η_TNT, R | Fuel volume, ΔH_c, η, strength class, R | Flame Mach number, geometry, congestion, R |
| Accounts for confinement? | No (uses empirical η) | Yes (via class) | Yes (via M_f) |
| Accounts for congestion? | Indirectly via η | Yes (via class) | Yes (via congestion density) |
| Accuracy (industrial) | Low (conservative) | High | Highest (with proper data) |
| KTU typical usage | Module 2 basic problems | Advanced problems | Optional / advanced elective |
| Regulatory acceptance | Decreasing | Yes (EU, Norsok) | Yes (API, CCPS) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — The Complete VCE Event Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Release of Flammable Material] --> B[Stage 2: Atmospheric Dispersion]
    B --> C[Cloud Reaches Flammable Envelope LFL to UFL]
    C --> D[Stage 3: Delayed Ignition by Source]
    D --> E[Stage 4: Combustion Flame Propagation]
    E --> F{Confinement and Congestion Present?}
    F -- No --> G[Weak Deflagration - Mf less than 0.2]
    F -- Partial --> H[Strong Deflagration - Mf 0.35 to 0.5]
    F -- High --> I[Very Strong Deflagration - Mf 0.5 to 0.8]
    F -- Extreme --> J[Detonation - Mf greater than 1.0]
    G --> K[Stage 5a: Blast Wave Damage]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> L[Stage 5b: Severe Destruction and Cratering]
    K --> M[Receiver Structure Damage Assessment]
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — VCE Damage Estimation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS[INPUT PARAMETERS]
        nodeA[Fuel Type and Mass m_fuel]
        nodeB[Cloud Volume V_cloud]
        nodeC[Stand-off Distance R]
        nodeD[Confinement Factor]
        nodeE[Congestion Density]
    end
    INPUTS --> STEP1[Step 1: Yield Ratio Computation]
    STEP1 --> STEP2[Step 2: Available Energy E]
    STEP2 --> STEP3A{Method Selection}
    STEP3A -- TNT --> ROUTE1[TNT Mass W_TNT]
    STEP3A -- TNO --> ROUTE2[TNO Strength Class and Scaled Distance]
    STEP3A -- Baker-Strehlow --> ROUTE3[Flame Mach Number M_f]
    ROUTE1 --> STEP4[Step 4: Scaled Distance Z or R-bar]
    ROUTE2 --> STEP4
    ROUTE3 --> STEP4
    STEP4 --> STEP5[Step 5: Read Empirical Curves]
    STEP5 --> OUTPUT[OUTPUT: Peak Overpressure Delta-P]
    OUTPUT --> DMG[Damage Criteria Application to Structures and Personnel]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — VCE Strength Classification Map (TNO)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeS1[Class 1: Very Weak - Free Air, No Congestion]
    nodeS2[Class 2: Weak - Light Obstacles]
    nodeS3[Class 3: Moderate - Some Confinement]
    nodeS4[Class 4: Medium - Typical Plant Congestion]
    nodeS5[Class 5 to 6: Strong - Heavy Pipe Rack Congestion]
    nodeS6[Class 7: Very Strong - CCPS Default VCE in Process Plant]
    nodeS7[Class 8: Severe - Tightly Congested Module]
    nodeS8[Class 9 to 10: Near Detonation - Highly Confined]

```

## 4.4 VCE vs Flash Fire vs BLEVE — Phenomena Comparison Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Flammable Cloud Event]
    nodeStart --> path1[Ignition Within Seconds - Inside Flammable Envelope]
    nodeStart --> path2[Ignition After Long Delay - Cloud Has Dispersed Partially]
    nodeStart --> path3[Liquid Spill Then Fire Engulfs Vessel]
    path1 --> res1[Flash Fire - Burn Back to Source]
    path1 --> res2[Vapour Cloud Explosion - If Obstacles Present]
    path2 --> res3[Unconfined VCE - Larger Cloud, More Energy]
    path3 --> res4[BLEVE - If Vessel Ruptures from Overpressure]

    res4 --> out3[Missile and Blast Combined Damage]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a Vapour Cloud Explosion. List the four essential conditions required for a VCE to occur.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE) is the explosive combustion of a flammable vapour-air mixture in the open atmosphere, resulting from the delayed ignition of a dispersed cloud following a release of flammable material.

Four essential conditions (the VCE tetrad):
1. **Release** of a significant quantity of flammable material (gas, vapour, or aerosol).
2. **Dispersion** of the released material into the atmosphere to form a cloud within the **flammability envelope** (between LFL and UFL).
3. **Delayed ignition** — the cloud must find an ignition source after some delay; immediate ignition produces only a jet fire.
4. **Confinement/congestion** — physical obstacles or boundaries must accelerate the flame front (M_f > 0.2) to generate damaging overpressure.

*(1 mark for definition; 1 mark for first two conditions; 1 mark for remaining two.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Distinguish between a flash fire and a vapour cloud explosion. What determines the transition from one to the other?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Flash Fire | Vapour Cloud Explosion |
|---|---|---|
| Flame speed | Sub-sonic, ~1–5 m/s | Sub-sonic to supersonic, > 100 m/s |
| Overpressure | Negligible (< 5 kPa) | Significant (> 10 kPa, often 30–100 kPa) |
| Damage mode | Thermal radiation | Blast overpressure + thermal |
| Driving mechanism | Buoyancy, diffusion | Confinement/congestion, flame acceleration |

The **transition** is governed by the **flame Mach number (M_f)**. When M_f > 0.2 in the presence of obstacles, the pressure waves couple with the flame front and accelerate it, producing destructive overpressure. In free, unobstructed air, the flame cannot accelerate sufficiently and the result remains a flash fire.

*(1.5 marks for tabular distinction; 1.5 marks for transition explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **TNT Equivalency Method** for estimating blast damage from a VCE. Derive the scaled distance and discuss the limitations of the method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A leak of **15,000 kg of n-butane** (ΔH_c = 45,400 kJ/kg, stoichiometric concentration in air = 3.1% v/v) forms a vapour cloud. Assume 25% of the spilled mass participates in the explosion with η_TNT = 0.04. Calculate the **equivalent TNT mass** and the **peak side-on overpressure at 800 m** from the cloud centre using the Kingery–Bulmash correlation at the scaled distance obtained. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**TNT Equivalency Method — Detailed Explanation:**

1. **Concept (2 marks):** The method assumes that the VCE energy can be modelled as an equivalent mass of TNT detonated at the same location. The blast overpressure at any distance R is then read from empirical TNT blast curves.

2. **Procedure (3 marks):**
   - Calculate flammable mass in the cloud.
   - Multiply by heat of combustion ΔH_c and yield factor η_TNT (1%–10%).
   - Divide by H_TNT (4680 kJ/kg) to get equivalent TNT mass W_TNT.
   - Compute scaled distance $Z = R / W_{TNT}^{1/3}$.
   - Read peak overpressure ΔP/P₀ from empirical curves (Kingery, Wilton, etc.).

3. **Limitations (2 marks):**
   - **No explicit treatment of confinement/congestion** — entire effect absorbed in η_TNT.
   - **Empirical η values** vary widely in literature (0.01 to 0.10) creating large uncertainty.
   - **Assumes point-source spherical blast** — actual VCE cloud is asymmetric and elongated.
   - **Ignores ground reflection and meteorological effects.**
   - Generally **over-conservative** for open-air, uncongested clouds.

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- m_spill = 15,000 kg; participation fraction = 0.25 → m_fuel = 3750 kg
- ΔH_c = 45,400 kJ/kg; η_TNT = 0.04; H_TNT = 4680 kJ/kg; R = 800 m

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Chemical energy
$$
E_{chem} = 3750 \times 45{,}400 = 1.7025 \times 10^{8}\ \text{kJ}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Effective VCE energy
$$
E_{VCE} = 0.04 \times 1.7025 \times 10^{8} = 6.81 \times 10^{6}\ \text{kJ}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Equivalent TNT mass
$$
W_{TNT} = \frac{6.81 \times 10^{6}}{4680} \approx 1455\ \text{kg TNT}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Cube root of W_TNT
$$
(1455)^{1/3} \approx 11.32
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Scaled distance
$$
Z = \frac{800}{11.32} \approx 70.7\ \text{m/kg}^{1/3}
$$

**Step 6 (2 marks):** Peak overpressure from Kingery–Bulmash
At very large Z (≈ 70), the shock has essentially decayed to a pressure pulse with:
$$
\frac{\Delta P}{P_0} \approx 0.006
$$
$$
\Delta P \approx 0.006 \times 101.325 \approx 0.6\ \text{kPa}
$$

**Interpretation:** Negligible damage at 800 m; significant damage zone would be within ~100–150 m.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students forget that η_TNT (TNT method) and η (TNO method) are *different* efficiency parameters. Also, the Kingery–Bulmash formula is only valid for $0.1 \le Z \le 50$. For $Z > 50$, use the asymptotic acoustic approximation $\Delta P/P_0 \approx 0.0045 / Z$ at sea level. Marks are deducted if this distinction is not recognised.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Describe the **TNO Multi-Energy Method** for VCE blast prediction. Explain the concept of the strength class and the role of confinement and congestion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the **TNO Multi-Energy Method**, estimate the peak overpressure at a distance of 200 m from a vapour cloud of stoichiometric propane-air mixture occupying a hemispherical volume of 8000 m³ at NTP. Assume combustion efficiency η = 0.20 and the explosion is in a partially congested area of **TNO strength class 7**. ΔH_c (propane) = 46,000 kJ/kg, ρ_vapour (propane) = 1.85 kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**TNO Multi-Energy Method — Description:**

1. **Origin (1 mark):** Developed by van den Berg and Eggen (TNO, Netherlands, 1980s) to overcome the limitations of the TNT method by explicitly modelling the VCE as a blast source whose strength is determined by the *degree of confinement and congestion*.

2. **Strength Class Concept (2 marks):** The method defines **10 strength classes** (1 = weak deflagration, 10 = detonation). The class is assigned based on the **actual congestion and confinement** of the explosion site:
   - Classes 1–3: open-air, low congestion (typical for free fields).
   - Classes 4–6: typical process plant pipe rack congestion.
   - Classes 7–8: **CCPS default for chemical plant VCE** (heavy congestion, partial confinement).
   - Classes 9–10: highly confined (e.g., inside modules), approach detonation.

3. **Initial Blast Energy (2 marks):** $E = V \times \rho_v \times \Delta H_c \times \eta$ (in MJ). The energy represents a hemispherical blast source.

4. **Scaled Distance & Curves (2 marks):** Using $\bar{R} = R / (E/P_0)^{1/3}$, the dimensionless overpressure $\Delta \bar{P}_s$ is read from the TNO strength-class-specific curves. Classes 7–10 produce much higher overpressures for the same energy, reflecting real VCE behaviour.

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- V = 8000 m³; η = 0.20; ΔH_c = 46,000 kJ/kg = 46 MJ/kg
- ρ_v (propane at NTP) = 1.85 kg/m³; R = 200 m; P₀ = 101.325 kPa = 0.101325 MPa

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Initial blast energy E
$$
E = V \times \rho_v \times \Delta H_c \times \eta
$$
$$
E = 8000 \times 1.85 \times 46 \times 0.20 = 13{,}616 \times 10^{3}\ \text{kJ} = 13{,}616\ \text{MJ}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute E/P₀
$$
\frac{E}{P_0} = \frac{13{,}616\ \text{MJ}}{0.101325\ \text{MPa}} = 134{,}389\ \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Cube root
$$
(E/P_0)^{1/3} = (134{,}389)^{1/3} \approx 51.2\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Scaled distance
$$
\bar{R} = \frac{200}{51.2} \approx 3.91
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Read ΔP̄_s from TNO Class 7 curve at R̄ = 3.91
From TNO Class 7 chart (interpolation):
$$
\Delta \bar{P}_s \approx 0.40
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Convert to dimensional overpressure
$$
\Delta P_s = \Delta \bar{P}_s \times P_0 = 0.40 \times 101.325 \approx 40.5\ \text{kPa}
$$

**Interpretation:** 40 kPa peak side-on overpressure corresponds to **heavy structural damage** — collapse of unreinforced masonry, severe damage to conventional buildings, serious injury risk to personnel.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common error is to **confuse V (cloud volume) with the equivalent stoichiometric volume of fuel alone**. Here, V = 8000 m³ is the fuel-air cloud volume. Also, ensure that E is in **joules** when using SI units consistently. Class selection is the most subjective input — mark loss if the student does not justify why class 7 was chosen.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Vapour Cloud Explosions**

- **VCE** = explosive combustion of an unconfined/dispersed flammable vapour-air cloud, ignited with a delay, producing destructive overpressure.
- **Four prerequisites:** Release → Dispersion → Delayed ignition → Confinement/congestion.
- **Yield Ratio Y = (stoichiometric fuel fraction) / (actual fuel fraction)** — controls energy released per unit volume of cloud.
- **Flame Mach number M_f = v_f / c₀** is the single most important parameter controlling blast strength.
- **TNT Equivalency** method: $W_{TNT} = \eta_{TNT} m_{fuel} \Delta H_c / H_{TNT}$, with **H_TNT = 4680 kJ/kg**.
- **Scaled distance** $Z = R / W_{TNT}^{1/3}$ — read ΔP from Kingery–Bulmash.
- **TNO Multi-Energy** method: 10 strength classes; **CCPS default for VCE in process plants = Class 7**.
- **TNO initial energy:** $E = V \rho_v \Delta H_c \eta$, dimensionless scaled distance $\bar{R} = R / (E/P_0)^{1/3}$.
- **Baker-Strehlow** method: explicit flame speed geometry/congestion treatment — most accurate for well-characterised plants.
- **η_TNT ≠ η**: TNT yield factor (1%–10%) is *different* from TNO combustion efficiency (10%–40%).
- **Flash fire** is the boundary case of a VCE in unconfined space (M_f ≈ 0.05–0.1, no overpressure).
- **BLEVE** is a *related but distinct* phenomenon involving vessel rupture — often occurs *alongside* VCE in major process incidents (e.g., Buncefield, Skikda).
- **Damage thresholds:** 7 kPa = window breakage; 20 kPa = significant building damage; 50 kPa = reinforced concrete damage; 100+ kPa = total destruction.
- **Forensic indicators of VCE vs flash fire:** Cratering and radial damage pattern indicate VCE; burn-back pattern without overpressure indicates flash fire.
- **KTU numerical problems always require:** (i) fuel mass calculation, (ii) TNT mass with explicit η, (iii) scaled distance with cube root, (iv) reading ΔP from chart/formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosions (BLEVE), Inerting, Vacuum Purging, Pressure Purging, Design Methods to Prevent Electrostatic Ignitions, Relaxation, Bonding and Grounding, Dip Pipes, Sprinkler Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT616 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosions (BLEVE), Inerting, Vacuum Purging, Pressure Purging, Design Methods to Prevent Electrostatic Ignitions, Relaxation, Bonding and Grounding, Dip Pipes, Sprinkler Systems # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion (BLEVE) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** A *Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion (BLEVE)* is the catastrophic failure of a pressure vessel containing a liquid stored at a temperature significantly **above its atmospheric boiling point**. Upon vessel rupture, the superheated liquid undergoes instantaneous flash vaporisation, releasing an enormous quantity of vapour energy and producing a destructive overpressure wave, a fireball, and high-velocity missile projection. ### Conceptual Analogy Imagine a sealed pressure cooker filled with water heated well beyond 100 °C. If the lid suddenly bursts, the superheated water instantly flashes to steam, expanding roughly **1,700 times** in volume in a fraction of a second. That violent expansion is precisely a BLEVE on a small scale. On an industrial scale — a propane tank, LPG rail wagon, or chlorinated solvent drum — the same physics scales into a devastating chemical explosion. > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology to Memorise (Syllabus Highlight):** > - **Superheat Limit Temperature (SLT):** The temperature above which homogeneous nucleation (explosive boiling) occurs spontaneously. > - **Fireball:** The rapidly rising burning vapour cloud characteristic of a BLEVE involving flammable liquids. > - **Missile Fragments:** Vessel wall pieces propelled at supersonic velocities. --- ## 1.2 Inerting > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** *Inerting* is the process of rendering a process vessel or storage tank non-flammable by reducing the **oxygen concentration** of its vapour space below the *Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC)* of the contained material. ### Conceptual Analogy Think of blowing nitrogen onto a smouldering candle inside a sealed jar. The flame dies because there is not enough oxygen to sustain combustion. Inerting applies the same idea — flood the headspace of a tank with nitrogen, carbon dioxide, or flue gas so that any flammable vapour becomes incapable of burning even if ignited. --- ## 1.3 Vacuum Purging > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** *Vacuum purging* is a technique where the vessel is evacuated to a low absolute pressure, then re-filled with inert gas, and the cycle is repeated several times to progressively dilute the residual flammable vapour to below its Lower Explosive Limit (LEL). ### Conceptual Analogy Imagine rinsing a glass with water, but instead of water you use clean air after pouring out the dirty water. Repeated rinses (evacuation–refill cycles) progressively lower the concentration of the contaminant. Vacuum purging does the same with gas-phase contaminants. --- ## 1.4 Pressure Purging > [! [!IMPORTANT]] > **Formal Definition:** *Pressure purging* (also called *sweep-through or displacement purging*) is a continuous purging technique where inert gas is pressurised into the vessel and then bled off through a vent, displacing the original atmosphere. ### Conceptual Analogy Similar to flushing a pipe with water — you push the dirty contents out with a clean fluid stream. --- ## 1.5 Electrostatic Ignition — Relaxation, Bonding & Grounding > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** Static electricity accumulates on poorly conducting liquids and equipment. *Relaxation* refers to the spontaneous dissipation of this charge. *Bonding* electrically ties two conductive objects together to eliminate potential difference. *Grounding* connects equipment to the earth to safely dissipate accumulated charge. ### Conceptual Analogy Touch a doorknob in winter — a spark jumps because charge has built up on you. If you were touching a grounded metal pipe, the charge would leak away harmlessly. Relaxation is the natural leakage; bonding ensures two pieces of equipment share charge; grounding routes the charge to earth. --- ## 1.6 Dip Pipes > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** A *dip pipe* (or *submerged fill pipe*) is a pipe whose outlet is positioned **below the liquid surface** inside a storage tank, used to prevent splashing, frothing, and electrostatic charge generation during transfer of flammable liquids. ### Conceptual Analogy Slowly pour water from a glass into another glass held low — no splashing. But pour from a height — lots of splashing and bubbles. Dip pipes keep the outlet low and submerged to avoid the same energetic splashing in chemical tanks. --- ## 1.7 Sprinkler Systems (for BLEVE Protection) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** Water spray and deluge systems are used to cool exposed pressure vessels during external fires, thereby preventing wall-metal temperature from rising to the superheat limit and triggering a BLEVE. These systems conform to **NFPA 15** (Water Spray Fixed Systems for Fire Protection). ### Conceptual Analogy Continuously spraying a hot pressure cooker with cold water keeps its walls cool enough to survive an external fire. The same logic applies to LPG bullets and rail cars. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 BLEVE — Operational Mechanics A BLEVE is triggered when **three conditions** are simultaneously satisfied: 1. The vessel contains a liquid **above its normal boiling point** at atmospheric pressure (superheated liquid). 2. A sudden pressure drop occurs (vessel rupture, relief valve opening onto a larger volume, mechanical failure). 3. The wall temperature exceeds the **Superheat Limit Temperature (SLT)**, which for water is roughly **310 °C** and for propane ≈ **130 °C**. **Energy Released in a BLEVE:** $$E_{BLEVE} = m \cdot [h_{v}(T_{final}) - h_{L}(T_{initial})]$$ where: - $m$ = mass of flashed liquid (kg) - $h_v$ = specific enthalpy of vapour at final state (J/kg) - $h_L$ = specific enthalpy of liquid at initial state (J/kg) **Fireball Diameter (TNO Correlation):** $$D_{max} = 6.48 \cdot m^{0.325}$$ **Fireball Duration:** $$t_{duration} = 0.852 \cdot m^{0.26}$$ > [!NOTE] > $m$ is in kg, $D_{max}$ in metres, $t_{duration}$ in seconds. ## 2.2 Inerting — Operational Analysis Inerting is implemented using **N₂, CO₂, or flue gas**. The design objective is to maintain oxygen concentration $C_{O_2}$ below the **Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC)**. | Fuel | LEL (%) | LOC (%) | |------|---------|---------| | Methane | 5.0 | **12.0** | | Propane | 2.1 | **11.5** | | Hydrogen | 4.0 | **5.0** | | Ethanol | 3.3 | **11.0** | | Carbon Disulfide | 1.3 | **5.0** | > [!IMPORTANT] > The LOC value should always be taken with a **safety factor of 2%**, i.e., design $C_{O_2} \leq LOC - 2\%$. ## 2.3 Vacuum Purging — Step-wise Operation A single vacuum purge cycle consists of three steps: 1. **Evacuate** vessel to absolute pressure $P_v$ (e.g., 50 mmHg). 2. **Isolate** vacuum source; vent vacuum. 3. **Re-pressurise** with inert gas to atmospheric pressure $P_{atm}$. **Dilution after $n$ cycles:** $$C_n = C_0 \cdot \left( \frac{P_v}{P_{atm}} \right)^n$$ > [!NOTE] > For $P_v = 50$ mmHg and $P_{atm} = 760$ mmHg, ratio ≈ **0.0658**. After **4 cycles** the residual concentration is **1.87 × 10⁻⁵** of the original — practically zero. ## 2.4 Pressure Purging — Step-wise Operation Continuous introduction of inert gas pushes out the original atmosphere. The dilution after passing $V_p$ volume of purge gas through a vessel of volume $V$: $$C = C_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{V_p}{V}\right)$$ For a series of $n$ complete pressure cycles: $$C_n = C_0 \cdot \left(\frac{P_{atm} - \Delta P}{P_{atm}}\right)^n$$ A practical rule is that the **purge ratio $V_p / V \geq 4$** gives > 98 % dilution efficiency. ## 2.5 Relaxation, Bonding & Grounding ### Relaxation Time Constant $$\tau = \frac{\varepsilon_r \cdot \varepsilon_0}{\sigma}$$ where: - $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity (dimensionless) - $\varepsilon_0$ = **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** (vacuum permittivity) - $\sigma$ = electrical conductivity (S/m) For safe handling, charge should decay to **less than 1/e (≈ 37 %)** of its initial value during the residence time $t$: $$t_{residence} \geq 3\tau \quad \text{(99 % decay)}$$ **Typical conductivity values:** | Liquid | Conductivity (S/m) | τ (seconds) | |--------|--------------------|--------------| | Hexane | 1 × 10⁻¹⁶ | ≈ 790 | | Toluene | 1 × 10⁻¹⁴ | ≈ 7.9 | | Distilled water | 5 × 10⁻⁶ | ≈ 1.6 × 10⁻⁶ | | Ethanol | 1 × 10⁻⁷ | ≈ 7.9 × 10⁻⁵ | > [!WARNING] > Hydrocarbons have extremely long relaxation times — they cannot self-discharge fast enough; hence **bonding + grounding + velocity limits + dip pipes** are mandatory. ### Bonding & Grounding Resistance - **Bonding Resistance** between two objects $\leq$ **10 Ω** (often $\leq$ **1 Ω** for process plants). - **Grounding Resistance** to earth $\leq$ **10 Ω**, ideally $\leq$ **1 Ω**. ## 2.6 Dip Pipes — Engineering Criteria Two fundamental design rules: **Rule 1 — Submergence Depth:** $$h_{submerge} \geq 2 \, d_{pipe}$$ **Rule 2 — Inlet Velocity (for non-anti-static liquids):** $$v_{inlet} \leq 1 \, \text{m/s}$$ For **initial (bottom-up) fill**, limit to $v \leq$ 1 m/s; for top-up fill with submerged pipe, $v \leq$ 7 m/s. ## 2.7 Sprinkler Systems for BLEVE Protection NFPA 15 specifies the **minimum water application rate:** $$\dot{Q}_{water} \geq 10.2 \, \text{L/min per m}^2 \text{ of exposed vessel surface}$$ The water cools the shell to keep the wall temperature below the **critical temperature** of the contents, preventing the wall strength from dropping below hoop stress requirements. --- ## KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Equation | Key Constants/Units | |---|---------|----------|---------------------| | 1 | BLEVE Energy | $E = m [h_v - h_L]$ | J (m in kg, h in J/kg) | | 2 | Fireball Diameter | $D = 6.48 \, m^{0.325}$ | m (m in kg) | | 3 | Fireball Duration | $t = 0.852 \, m^{0.26}$ | s (m in kg) | | 4 | Vacuum Purge Dilution | $C_n = C_0 (P_v / P_{atm})^n$ | dimensionless | | 5 | Pressure Purge Dilution | $C = C_0 \, e^{-V_p/V}$ | dimensionless | | 6 | Relaxation Time | $\tau = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 / \sigma$ | seconds | | 7 | Decay Time (99%) | $t \geq 3\tau$ | seconds | | 8 | Dip Pipe Submergence | $h_s \geq 2 d_{pipe}$ | metres | | 9 | Inlet Velocity Limit | $v \leq 1$ m/s (initial) | m/s | | 10 | Sprinkler Density | $\dot{Q} \geq 10.2$ L/min/m² | NFPA 15 | | 11 | Bonding/Grounding | $R \leq 10$ Ω (target $\leq 1$ Ω) | ohms | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world engineering utility:** These formulae appear in the design of LPG bullet farms, hydrocarbon storage terminals, refinery process vessels, paint manufacturing lines, pharmaceutical solvent tanks, and LNG/LPG transport vehicles. The API 2510, NFPA 30, and IS 1446 codes invoke these directly. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples ## 3.1 Example Problem — BLEVE Energy & Fireball Radius **Problem Statement (Typical KTU type):** > A propane storage bullet contains 10,000 kg of liquid propane at 25 °C. Following an external fire, the vessel ruptures and the entire inventory flashes instantaneously. Propane has $C_p^{liq} \approx 2.4$ kJ/kg·K, latent heat of vaporisation $L \approx 425$ kJ/kg, and specific heat of vapour $C_p^{vap} \approx 1.6$ kJ/kg·K. The critical temperature of propane is **96.7 °C**. > > **Calculate:** > (a) The total BLEVE energy released. > (b) The maximum fireball diameter and duration. > (c) Whether the vessel could have been saved by an NFPA 15 water spray system, assuming the vessel is cylindrical with 2 m radius and 12 m length. ### Part (a) — BLEVE Energy Calculation The propane stored at 25 °C (298 K) will flash to vapour at its boiling point (≈ −42 °C = 231 K) and then heat to ambient final temperature $T_{final} \approx 298$ K. Energy balance per kg of liquid flashed: $$\frac{E}{m} = L + C_p^{vap}(T_{final} - T_{boil})$$ $$\frac{E}{m} = 425 + 1.6 \times [25 - (-42)]$$ $$\frac{E}{m} = 425 + 1.6 \times 67$$ $$\frac{E}{m} = 425 + 107.2 = 532.2 \, \text{kJ/kg}$$ Total energy for the entire inventory ($m = 10{,}000$ kg): $$E_{total} = 10{,}000 \times 532.2 = 5.322 \times 10^6 \, \text{kJ}$$ $$\boxed{E_{total} = 5.322 \, \text{GJ}}$$ ### Part (b) — Fireball Diameter & Duration Using TNO correlation: $$D_{max} = 6.48 \times m^{0.325}$$ $$D_{max} = 6.48 \times (10{,}000)^{0.325}$$ $$\log_{10}(10{,}000) = 4$$ $$(10{,}000)^{0.325} = 10^{4 \times 0.325} = 10^{1.3}$$ $$10^{1.3} \approx 19.95$$ $$D_{max} = 6.48 \times 19.95 \approx 129.3 \, \text{m}$$ Duration: $$t = 0.852 \times m^{0.26}$$ $$m^{0.26} = 10^{4 \times 0.26} = 10^{1.04} \approx 10.96$$ $$t = 0.852 \times 10.96 \approx 9.34 \, \text{s}$$ $$\boxed{D_{max} \approx 129 \, \text{m}; \quad t \approx 9.3 \, \text{s}}$$ ### Part (c) — Sprinkler System Adequacy Surface area of cylindrical bullet (with two hemispherical ends): $$A = 2 \pi r L + 4 \pi r^2$$ $$A = 2 \pi (2)(12) + 4 \pi (2)^2$$ $$A = 48 \pi + 16 \pi = 64 \pi$$ $$A \approx 201.06 \, \text{m}^2$$ Water flow requirement (NFPA 15): $$\dot{Q}_{water} = 10.2 \times 201.06 \approx 2050.8 \, \text{L/min}$$ $$\dot{Q}_{water} \approx 34.2 \, \text{L/s}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{Q}_{water} \approx 34.2 \, \text{L/s (≈ 2051 L/min)}}$$ Yes — installing such a deluge system could have kept the wall below the critical temperature, suppressing BLEVE initiation. --- ## 3.2 Example Problem — Vacuum Purging Design **Problem Statement:** > A 50 m³ storage tank previously contained gasoline (LEL = 1.4 %). It must be made safe for hot work. The vacuum pump pulls to 25 mmHg absolute. Atmospheric pressure = 760 mmHg. > > **Calculate the number of purge cycles required to bring the vapour concentration to below 10 % of LEL** (assume initial concentration = saturated at 100 % of LEL). ### Step 1: Target Final Concentration $$C_{target} = 0.10 \times 1.4\% = 0.14\%$$ $$C_{target} = 0.0014 \text{ (as fraction)}$$ ### Step 2: Cycle Ratio $$\frac{P_v}{P_{atm}} = \frac{25}{760} = 0.0329$$ ### Step 3: Apply the Vacuum Purging Equation $$C_n = C_0 \cdot (0.0329)^n$$ We require $C_n \leq 0.0014$ with $C_0 = 0.014$ (1.4 %): $$(0.0329)^n \leq \frac{0.0014}{0.014} = 0.1$$ Taking logarithm (base 10): $$n \cdot \log_{10}(0.0329) \leq \log_{10}(0.1)$$ $$n \cdot (-1.483) \leq -1$$ $$n \geq \frac{1}{1.483} = 0.674$$ Since $n$ must be an integer: $$\boxed{n = 1 \text{ cycle is sufficient}}$$ (In practice, **3 cycles** are used for safety.) --- ## 3.3 Example Problem — Relaxation Time for Toluene **Problem Statement:** > Toluene (relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r = 2.38$, conductivity $\sigma = 1 \times 10^{-14}$ S/m) is being transferred into a tank with residence time of 60 seconds. Calculate the residual charge fraction and comment on safety. ### Step 1: Calculate τ $$\tau = \frac{\varepsilon_r \cdot \varepsilon_0}{\sigma} = \frac{2.38 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{1 \times 10^{-14}}$$ $$\tau = \frac{2.107 \times 10^{-11}}{10^{-14}} = 2107 \, \text{s}$$ ### Step 2: Compute Decay The fraction of charge remaining after time $t$: $$\frac{Q(t)}{Q_0} = \exp\left(-\frac{t}{\tau}\right) = \exp\left(-\frac{60}{2107}\right)$$ $$= \exp(-0.02848) \approx 0.9719$$ ### Step 3: Safety Conclusion Residual charge ≈ **97.2 %** after 60 s — essentially no natural dissipation. > [!WARNING] > Toluene **cannot** self-discharge in 60 s. Hence **mandatory** bonding, grounding, low velocity ($\leq$ 1 m/s), and submerged fill are required. --- ## 3.4 Example Problem — Dip Pipe Submergence Depth A 100 mm (0.1 m) diameter inlet pipe is to be fitted. The minimum safe submergence depth: $$h_s \geq 2 \times d_{pipe} = 2 \times 0.1 = 0.2 \, \text{m}$$ $$\boxed{h_s \geq 0.2 \, \text{m below the liquid surface}}$$ For a typical 10 m tank, the dip pipe must extend at least 0.2 m below the design liquid level at all times during filling. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 BLEVE Initiation Mechanism — Cause-Effect Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[External Fire Engulfs Vessel] --> A2[Wall Metal Heats Above Design Temperature] A2 --> A3{Liquid Temperature
Exceeds Superheat
Limit Temperature?} A3 -- Yes --> A4[Rapid Pressure Drop
on Wall Rupture] A3 -- No --> A5[Wall Stress Failure
Due to Material Softening] A4 --> A6[Superheated Liquid
Flash Vaporises] A5 --> A6 A6 --> A7[BLEVE Event] A7 --> A8[Blast Overpressure Wave] A7 --> A9[Fireball Generation] A7 --> A10[Missile Projection] A7 --> A11[Vapour Cloud Dispersion] A8 --> A12[Catastrophic Damage] A9 --> A12 A10 --> A12 A11 --> A12 ``` ## 4.2 Inerting System — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR N2Source[Nitrogen Cylinder
or Liquid N2 Tank] --> Reg[Pressure Regulator] Reg --> FCV[Flow Control Valve] FCV --> V1[Inlet Valve to Vessel] V1 --> Vessel[Process Vessel
Vapour Space] Vessel --> O2Sensor[Oxygen Analyser
0-25 percent Range] O2Sensor --> Controller[PLC Controller
Setpoint LOC minus 2 percent] Controller --> Alarm[Alarm and Interlock] Controller --> FCV Vessel --> VentRV[Pressure Relief Valve] VentRV --> SafeVent[Safe Vent Location] ``` ## 4.3 Vacuum Purging — Cycle Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD S1((Start)) --> St1[Step 1: Close Inert Inlet
Open Vacuum Pump] St1 --> St2[Evacuate to P_vacuum] St2 --> St3[Step 2: Close Vacuum Valve
Open Vent to Atmosphere] St3 --> St4[Vessel Returns to P_atm] St4 --> St5[Step 3: Close Vent
Open Inert Gas Inlet] St5 --> St6[Pressurise to P_atm with Inert] St6 --> Check{Concentration
Below Target?} Check -- No --> St1 Check -- Yes --> Done((Purging Complete)) ``` ## 4.4 Electrostatic Prevention — Bonding & Grounding Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR Tank[Tank T1
Receiving Vessel] -- Bonding Wire R_b less than 10 ohm --> Truck[Tanker Truck T2] Tank -- Ground Cable R_g less than 10 ohm --> Earth[Earth Pit
Soil Electrode] Truck -- Ground Cable --> Earth Flow[Liquid Flow with Q charge per second] --> Tank Note[Relaxation time tau very long
for hydrocarbons] -.-> Flow Style2[Velocity less than 1 m/s initial] --> Tank Style3[Dip Pipe Submerged greater than 2 d] --> Tank ``` ## 4.5 Dip Pipe Configuration ```mermaid flowchart TD Pipe1[Inlet Pipe from Pump] --> Joint[Flanged Joint] Joint --> Dropper[Dip Pipe
Vertical Drop Section] Dropper --> Outlet[Outlet Below Liquid Surface
Submergence greater than 2 times d] Outlet --> Liquid[(Bulk Liquid in Tank)] AntiSwirl[Anti-swirl baffle
optional] -.-> Outlet SplashHood[Splash Hood
optional end fitting] -.-> Outlet ``` ## 4.6 Sprinkler / Deluge System for BLEVE Protection ```mermaid flowchart LR WaterMain[Water Supply Main] --> Strainer[Y-Strainer] Strainer --> DPV[Deluge Valve
Pneumatic or Electric Release] DPV --> Manifold[Distribution Manifold] Manifold --> S1[Spray Nozzle 1
Top Zone] Manifold --> S2[Spray Nozzle 2
Middle Zone] Manifold --> S3[Spray Nozzle 3
Bottom Zone] S1 --> Vessel[Pressure Vessel
LPG Bullet or Tank] S2 --> Vessel S3 --> Vessel Detector[Heat Detector
or Flame Detector] -- Trip Signal --> DPV Detector -- Trip Signal --> Alarm[Fire Alarm Panel] ``` ## 4.7 Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Purge Method | Method | Best Suited For | Advantages | Limitations | |--------|-----------------|------------|-------------| | **Vacuum Purging** | Vessels rated for full vacuum, small/medium tanks | Rapid dilution with few cycles, low inert gas use | Cannot be used on atmospheric storage tanks | | **Pressure Purging** | Vessels rated for moderate pressure | Simple equipment, can be continuous | More inert gas consumed | | **Displacement (Sweep)** | Long pipelines, reactors | Continuous, predictable | Slow at stagnant corners | | **Combination** | Critical service | Fastest approach | More valves, more complex | # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — 3 Mark Questions ### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Define BLEVE. State the three conditions necessary for a BLEVE to occur.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion (BLEVE)** is the catastrophic rupture of a pressure vessel containing a liquid at a temperature above its normal boiling point, resulting in instantaneous flash vaporisation and violent release of energy. The three necessary conditions are: 1. The vessel contains a **superheated liquid** (temperature above atmospheric boiling point). 2. A sudden **pressure drop** occurs due to vessel rupture or relief valve opening. 3. The vessel wall temperature exceeds the **superheat limit temperature (SLT)**, weakening the metal below its design stress threshold. (1 mark each) --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **What is Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC)? Why is a 2 % safety margin applied?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **LOC** is the minimum oxygen concentration in a vapour mixture below which combustion of a specific fuel cannot occur. Below this concentration, ignition is not possible regardless of fuel concentration. A **2 % safety margin** is applied because: 1. Real process conditions involve temperature and pressure variations that shift the flammability envelope. 2. Inert gas distribution may be non-uniform due to dead spots. 3. It provides protection against measurement uncertainty of oxygen analysers. (2 + 1 marks) --- ## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024] **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **deluge sprinkler system** for protecting an LPG storage vessel from BLEVE. Discuss the NFPA 15 design criteria. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A vessel of volume 25 m³ contains vapour of acetone (LEL = 2.5 %) at its saturated concentration. It is to be made gas-free using **vacuum purging** with a vacuum pump that can pull down to **40 mmHg** absolute. Atmospheric pressure is 760 mmHg. Calculate the number of cycles required to bring the vapour concentration below 25 % of LEL. **(7 marks)** #### Model Answer (a): A **deluge sprinkler system** provides immediate, total-area water coverage to a vessel when a fire detector is triggered. Components (refer Section 4.6 block diagram): 1. **Water supply main** with redundant pumps. 2. **Deluge valve** — held closed by air or hydraulic pressure; releases upon detector trip. 3. **Detection system** — heat or flame detectors arranged in a cross-zoned logic. 4. **Distribution piping and open spray nozzles** — designed per **NFPA 15** to deliver minimum **10.2 L/min/m²** of exposed vessel surface. 5. **Manual release station** as backup. **NFPA 15 Design Criteria:** - Application density: **10.2 L/min/m²** of vessel wetted surface. - Coverage: full vessel shell, including dished ends and saddle supports. - Water temperature: ambient is acceptable; chilled water optional for very large vessels. - Independent water supply with 60-minute duration. When activated, water spray cools the vessel wall, keeping its temperature below the critical value and **preventing wall-metal weakening**, thus arresting BLEVE progression. **(7 marks)** #### Model Answer (b): **Given:** - $V = 25$ m³ (volume cancels) - $C_0 = 2.5\%$ (initial = LEL) - Target: $C_n \leq 0.25 \times 2.5\% = 0.625\%$ - $P_v = 40$ mmHg; $P_{atm} = 760$ mmHg **Step 1 — Calculate cycle ratio:** $$\frac{P_v}{P_{atm}} = \frac{40}{760} = 0.0526$$ **Step 2 — Apply vacuum purging equation:** $$C_n = C_0 \cdot (0.0526)^n \leq 0.625$$ $$(0.0526)^n \leq \frac{0.625}{2.5} = 0.25$$ **Step 3 — Solve for n using logarithm:** $$n \cdot \log(0.0526) \leq \log(0.25)$$ $$n \cdot (-1.279) \leq -0.602$$ $$n \geq 0.471$$ Since $n$ must be an integer: $$\boxed{n = 1 \text{ cycle}}$$ Practical design recommendation: use **2 cycles** for safety margin. **(7 marks)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** Explain with the help of a diagram the phenomenon of **electrostatic charge accumulation** during transfer of a hydrocarbon liquid. Discuss the design measures to prevent electrostatic ignition. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 5 m³ tank containing hexane (relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$ = 1.89, conductivity $\sigma = 1 \times 10^{-16}$ S/m) is being filled at 25 °C with a residence time of 2 minutes. Compute the relaxation time and residual charge fraction. Comment on the safety implication. **(7 marks)** #### Model Answer (a): **Mechanism of Charge Accumulation** (refer Section 4.4): - When a hydrocarbon flows through pipes, filters, valves, and pumps, **electrical double-layer separation** occurs at the liquid–solid interface. - The flowing liquid carries positive ions downstream, leaving the upstream pipe negatively charged. - Splashing, agitation, and free-fall of liquid in tanks cause additional **diffusion and spray charging**. **Design Measures for Prevention:** 1. **Bonding & Grounding** — both truck and tank bonded ($R_b \leq 10 \Omega$); tank grounded to earth ($R_g \leq 10 \Omega$). 2. **Dip Pipes** — submerged fill with $h_s \geq 2 d_{pipe}$ to eliminate splashing. 3. **Velocity Control** — inlet velocity $\leq 1$ m/s during initial fill; $\leq 7$ m/s when submerged. 4. **Anti-Static Additives** — Stadis 450 added in ppm levels to increase conductivity. 5. **Relaxation Time** — provide sufficient residence time $t \geq 3\tau$ before sampling. 6. **Avoid Filters** near tank inlets; if essential, ground the filter housing. 7. **No Sampling** during transfer operations. 8. **Use of conductive hoses** rated $< 10^6 \Omega/m$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Answer (b): **Given:** - $\varepsilon_r = 1.89$ - $\sigma = 1 \times 10^{-16}$ S/m - $t_{res} = 2$ min = 120 s **Step 1 — Calculate relaxation time:** $$\tau = \frac{\varepsilon_r \cdot \varepsilon_0}{\sigma} = \frac{1.89 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{1 \times 10^{-16}}$$ $$\tau = \frac{1.673 \times 10^{-11}}{1 \times 10^{-16}} = 1.673 \times 10^{5} \, \text{s}$$ $$\boxed{\tau \approx 167{,}300 \, \text{s} \approx 46.5 \, \text{hours}}$$ **Step 2 — Residual charge fraction after 120 s:** $$\frac{Q(t)}{Q_0} = \exp\left(-\frac{t}{\tau}\right) = \exp\left(-\frac{120}{1.673 \times 10^5}\right)$$ $$= \exp(-7.17 \times 10^{-4}) \approx 0.99928$$ $$\boxed{\approx 99.93\% \text{ charge retained}}$$ **Step 3 — Safety Comment:** Hexane has an extremely long relaxation time of ~46.5 hours, and 99.93 % of the static charge remains even after 2 minutes of residence. Therefore, **natural dissipation is impossible**. > [!WARNING] > Strict adherence to bonding, grounding, dip pipes, velocity limits, and antistatic additives is **mandatory**. No operation should begin unless the bonding wire continuity is verified. **(7 marks)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:** > 1. **BLEVE calculation:** Students often confuse *boiling point* with *critical temperature*. Always distinguish. > 2. **Purge dilution:** Forgetting the 2 % safety margin below LOC; writing only LOC. > 3. **Relaxation formula:** Mixing up conductivity units ($\mu$S/m vs S/m) — 1 µS/m = $10^{-6}$ S/m. > 4. **Dip pipe depth:** Many write "$2 \times$ pipe radius" — correct is **$2 \times$ pipe diameter**. > 5. **Sprinkler density:** Confusing NFPA 15 (water spray, 10.2 L/min/m²) with NFPA 13 (sprinkler, 4.1 L/min/m²). > 6. **Lack of diagrams:** Drawing block diagrams is mandatory for 7-mark questions — missing them costs 1–2 marks. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **BLEVE** = superheated liquid + sudden pressure drop → catastrophic flash vaporisation. Energy $E = m[h_v - h_L]$. - ✅ **Fireball diameter** $D = 6.48 \, m^{0.325}$; **duration** $t = 0.852 \, m^{0.26}$. - ✅ **LOC + 2 %** safety margin is the inerting design criterion. - ✅ **Vacuum purging** is exponentially efficient — $(P_v / P_{atm})^n$. - ✅ **Pressure purging** uses $C = C_0 \, e^{-V_p/V}$; rule of thumb $V_p/V \geq 4$. - ✅ **Relaxation time** $\tau = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 / \sigma$ governs natural charge decay. - ✅ Hydrocarbons have **very long τ** → mandatory bonding, grounding, dip pipes, velocity limit. - ✅ **Bonding resistance** $\leq 10 \Omega$; **Grounding resistance** $\leq 10 \Omega$. - ✅ **Dip pipe submergence** $h_s \geq 2 d_{pipe}$; **inlet velocity** $\leq 1$ m/s (initial fill). - ✅ **NFPA 15** water spray density: **10.2 L/min/m²** of exposed vessel area. - ✅ BLEVE prevention hierarchy: **Relief valves → Fire-water spray → Spacing → Insulation → Inerting**. - ✅ Always convert µS/m to S/m by $10^{-6}$ when computing $\tau$. - ✅ For BLEVE protection: **Time is critical** — water spray must activate within seconds of fire detection. - ✅ Dip pipe rules apply for **all Class I liquids** during transfer operations. - ✅ Grounding alone is insufficient without **bonding** between mobile and fixed equipment.

topics.title: Containment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT616 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Containment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Containment** in chemical process safety refers to the engineered systems, physical barriers, and procedural controls designed to prevent, control, or limit the release of hazardous materials (flammable, toxic, or reactive) from process equipment into the workplace, environment, or community following a loss of primary containment (LOPC) event.

The concept is built upon the **"defense-in-depth"** philosophy, which mandates multiple independent layers of protection so that the failure of one layer does not result in an uncontrolled release. In KTU 2024 syllabus context, containment is treated as a **passive engineered safeguard** under the hierarchy of controls: Prevention → Mitigation → Containment → Emergency Response.

> [!NOTE]
> **Loss of Primary Containment (LOPC)** is the formal KTU/KCC (Kerala State Pollution Control Board) terminology for an unplanned release of material from its designed enclosure (vessel, pipe, valve, flange, etc.). Containment systems are specifically engineered to manage the *consequences* of an LOPC event.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Russian Doll" of Safety

Imagine a **nesting of three Russian dolls**:
- The **smallest (innermost) doll** is the process vessel itself — this is the *primary containment*.
- The **middle doll** wrapping it is a concrete bund/dike around the vessel — this is the *secondary containment*.
- The **outermost doll** is the entire plant boundary, drainage system, and emergency catchment — this is the *tertiary containment*.

If the inner doll cracks (vessel rupture), the middle one catches the spill. If that one fails, the outermost one stops it from reaching the river outside. Each layer *does not depend* on the others — they function independently. This is the **"Swiss Cheese Model"** applied to fluid containment.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics & Standards

> [!NOTE]
> Critical design metrics for containment systems include:
> - **110%** of the volume of the *largest* single tank within the bund
> - **100%** of the aggregate volume of *all other* tanks in the bund
> - **25%** of the *largest tank volume* (alternative rule for fixed-roof tanks per **NFPA 30**)
> - **Freeboard (typically 10%)** above calculated containment volume
> - Design load: typically the specific gravity of water (or higher for materials with SG > 1.0)

## 1.4 Classification of Containment (KTU Hierarchy)

| Level | Type | Function | Example |
|-------|------|----------|---------|
| 1 | **Primary** | Holds material during normal operation | Pressure vessel, pipe, valve |
| 2 | **Secondary** | Catches releases from primary failure | Bund/dike, double-walled tank, catch pan |
| 3 | **Tertiary** | Mitigates spread beyond secondary | Plant drainage, remote impounding basin, vapor barriers |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Philosophy — Why Containment?

The fundamental question in chemical process safety is not *whether* a release will occur, but *how much will be released* and *where it will go*. Containment addresses the **"where will it go?"** part of the risk equation. The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes containment strategies into three operational tiers:

### Tier I — Liquid Spill Containment (Bund/Dike Systems)
Used for volatile and non-volatile liquid storage. Prevents soil and groundwater contamination.

### Tier II — Vapor/Gas Containment (Enclosures, Vapor Recovery)
Used for highly toxic or volatile liquids (e.g., chlorine, ammonia, HF). Vapor return systems, scrubbers, and sealed enclosures.

### Tier III — Fire-Water Runoff Containment
A critical but often overlooked requirement. During a fire, the cooling water used by fire brigades becomes contaminated and must be retained to prevent environmental catastrophe.

## 2.2 Core Design Principles (The "How")

1. **Material Compatibility**: Bund material must be chemically compatible with the stored liquid (e.g., no concrete bund for hydrofluoric acid without coating).
2. **Impermeability**: Bund floor must have a leak-detection system (typically a granular sub-base with perforated pipe, or a geomembrane liner).
3. **Standoff Distance**: Bund walls must withstand hydrostatic pressure from the contained liquid + dynamic loads.
4. **Drainage Control**: Bund drain valves must be **CLOSED and LOCKED** in normal operation, manually opened only under controlled discharge procedures.
5. **Rainwater Management**: A separate rainwater drain allows routine precipitation to escape; a valve isolates it during a spill.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **mandatory** for KTU ESE numerical problems on bund/dike sizing.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $V_{bund} = 1.1 \times V_{largest\ tank} + \sum V_{other\ tanks}$ | Net bund capacity (excluding rainfall) | m³ |
| 2 | $V_{rainfall} = A_{bund} \times h_{rainfall}$ | Rainfall volume to be subtracted from usable bund volume | m³ |
| 3 | $V_{net,bund} = V_{bund,geometrical} - V_{rainfall}$ | Effective bund volume available for spill | m³ |
| 4 | $V_{vertical\ tank} = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \times H$ | Cylindrical vertical tank volume | m³ |
| 5 | $V_{horizontal\ tank} = L \left[ \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} + (H - \dfrac{D}{2}) \times D \right]$ | Partially filled horizontal cylinder volume | m³ |
| 6 | $Q_{firewater} = N \times q \times t$ | Firewater runoff rate (N = hydrants, q = flow, t = duration) | m³/h |
| 7 | $V_{firewater} = Q_{firewater} \times t_{fire\ duration}$ | Total firewater runoff volume to be contained | m³ |
| 8 | $h_{bund} = \dfrac{V_{spill}}{A_{bund}}$ | Required bund wall height (height of liquid in bund) | m |
| 9 | $A_{vertical\ tank\ base} = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$ | Footprint of vertical cylindrical tank | m² |
| 10 | $SF = \dfrac{V_{bund,available}}{V_{required}}$ | Safety Factor for bund design (should be $\geq 1.0$) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Piping Rule (KTU/KCC Regulation):** The bund wall must **NOT** be breached by any process pipe. All piping must enter/exit *over* the bund wall or through a sealed, fire-resistant penetration. This is a frequent 3-mark question.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Containment design is governed by:
- **NFPA 30** (Flammable and Combustible Liquids Code) — for hydrocarbon storage
- **API 650** (Welded Tanks for Oil Storage) — for tank geometry
- **EPA 40 CFR 112** (SPCC Rule) — for oil spill prevention
- **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.106** — for flammable liquids in workplaces
- **IS 1944 / IS 8034** — Indian Standards for bund design

These standards directly affect plant **licensing, insurance, and statutory compliance** — making containment knowledge essential for any Chemical Engineer entering petroleum, petrochemical, or pharmaceutical industries in Kerala (BPCL Kochi, FACT, HOCL, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bund Capacity for Multiple Tanks

**Problem Statement:** A chemical plant has a bund enclosure containing:
- One vertical cylindrical tank: Diameter $D_1 = 10$ m, Height $H_1 = 8$ m
- Two vertical cylindrical tanks: Diameter $D_2 = 5$ m, Height $H_2 = 6$ m each

Determine the required net bund volume using the **110% / 100% rule**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Calculate the volume of the largest tank (Tank 1, $D_1 = 10$ m, $H_1 = 8$ m).

$$
V_1 = \frac{\pi D_1^{2}}{4} \times H_1
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
V_1 = \frac{\pi \times (10)^{2}}{4} \times 8
$$

$$
V_1 = \frac{\pi \times 100}{4} \times 8
$$

$$
V_1 = 25\pi \times 8 = 200\pi
$$

$$
V_1 \approx 628.32 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the volume of one of the smaller tanks (Tank 2, $D_2 = 5$ m, $H_2 = 6$ m).

$$
V_2 = \frac{\pi D_2^{2}}{4} \times H_2
$$

$$
V_2 = \frac{\pi \times (5)^{2}}{4} \times 6
$$

$$
V_2 = \frac{25\pi}{4} \times 6
$$

$$
V_2 = 6.25\pi \times 6 = 37.5\pi
$$

$$
V_2 \approx 117.81 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the total volume of the *other* tanks (there are two Tank 2s).

$$
V_{other} = 2 \times V_2 = 2 \times 117.81 = 235.62 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the 110% / 100% bund sizing rule.

$$
V_{bund,net} = 1.1 \times V_1 + 1.0 \times V_{other}
$$

$$
V_{bund,net} = 1.1 \times 628.32 + 1.0 \times 235.62
$$

$$
V_{bund,net} = 691.15 + 235.62
$$

$$
V_{bund,net} \approx 926.77 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 5:** Engineering rounding — add 10% freeboard to account for splashing and dynamic sloshing.

$$
V_{design} = 1.10 \times V_{bund,net} = 1.10 \times 926.77
$$

$$
V_{design} \approx 1019.45 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **net** bund volume refers to the actual fluid-holding capacity of the bund after subtracting any rainfall contribution. The **geometrical** volume is the raw volume enclosed by the bund walls.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Rainwater Subtraction (IS 1944 / NFPA Method)

**Problem Statement:** The bund in the previous problem has a rectangular footprint of 25 m × 20 m. The local design rainfall is 50 mm/h, and the operator drains the bund after every storm. Determine the **effective** net bund volume assuming the bund is full of 50 mm standing water at the moment of a worst-case spill.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the bund footprint area.

$$
A_{bund} = L \times W = 25 \times 20 = 500 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the rainfall volume (50 mm = 0.050 m).

$$
V_{rainfall} = A_{bund} \times h_{rainfall}
$$

$$
V_{rainfall} = 500 \times 0.050
$$

$$
V_{rainfall} = 25 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the effective net bund capacity.

$$
V_{effective} = V_{bund,net} - V_{rainfall}
$$

$$
V_{effective} = 926.77 - 25
$$

$$
V_{effective} = 901.77 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Forgetting to subtract the rainfall volume. KTU examiners frequently provide a rainfall figure in the problem to test this exact step. Always read the problem for "mm/h" or "mm of rainfall" data.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Firewater Runoff Containment

**Problem Statement:** A bund protects a storage tank farm with a total hydrocarbon inventory of 500 m³. The plant fire brigade deploys 3 monitors at a flow rate of 30 m³/h each, and the design fire duration is 4 hours. Calculate the required **firewater catchment basin** volume.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the total flow rate of firewater.

$$
Q_{firewater} = N \times q
$$

$$
Q_{firewater} = 3 \times 30 = 90 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{h}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the total firewater volume generated over the design fire duration.

$$
V_{firewater} = Q_{firewater} \times t
$$

$$
V_{firewater} = 90 \times 4 = 360 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply a 30% safety factor for foam solution and unburned hydrocarbon entrainment.

$$
V_{basin,design} = 1.30 \times V_{firewater} = 1.30 \times 360
$$

$$
V_{basin,design} = 468 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 4:** The catchment basin must be **remote** from the tank farm (at least 50 m away) to prevent flame impingement on the impounded liquid.
