## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Containment Levels

| Feature | Primary | Secondary | Tertiary |
|---------|---------|-----------|----------|
| Trigger | Normal operation | LOPC event | Bund overflow / Fire scenario |
| Capital Cost | Low–High (built into equipment) | Moderate (10–20% of project) | High (remote basin + drainage network) |
| Failure Mode | Vessel rupture, gasket leak | Bund wall collapse, drain valve open | Basin overflow, soil seepage |
| Detection | Pressure / level instrumentation | Visual + leak-detection in sub-base | Environmental monitoring wells |
| Regulatory Driver | ASME Sec VIII, API 650 | NFPA 30, EPA SPCC, IS 1944 | EPA, KSPCB Consent Orders |
| Test Frequency | 5–10 years (hydrotest) | Annual inspection | Continuous groundwater monitoring |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Multi-Level Containment Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    LOPC["Loss of Primary Containment Event"]
    
    subgraph Primary["PRIMARY CONTAINMENT - Level 1"]
        V1["Storage Vessel"]
        V2["Process Piping"]
        V3["Valves and Flanges"]
        V4["Pumps and Compressors"]
    end
    
    subgraph Secondary["SECONDARY CONTAINMENT - Level 2"]
        B1["Concrete Bund / Dike"]
        B2["Double-Walled Tank"]
        B3["Spill Pallets and Trays"]
        B4["Vapor Return System"]
    end
    
    subgraph Tertiary["TERTIARY CONTAINMENT - Level 3"]
        T1["Plant Drainage Network"]
        T2["Remote Impounding Basin"]
        T3["Vapor Scrubber"]
        T4["Firewater Retention Pond"]
    end
    
    LOPC --> Primary
    Primary --> Secondary
    Secondary --> Tertiary
    
    T2 --> ENV["Environment - Public and Waterways"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Bund Capacity Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Identify Largest Tank Volume V1"] --> B["Compute 1.10 multiplied by V1"]
    C["Identify All Other Tanks Volumes V_other"] --> D["Sum V_other"]
    B --> E["Required Net Bund Volume"]
    D --> E
    F["Rainfall Data mm/h"] --> G["Compute V_rainfall = A_bund multiplied by h_rainfall"]
    G --> H["Effective Bund Capacity = Net Volume minus V_rainfall"]
    E --> H
    H --> I["Add 10 percent Freeboard"]
    I --> J["Final Design Volume V_design"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flow — Bund Drainage and Isolation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Normal["Normal Operation Mode"]
    Spill["Spill Detection Event"]
    
    Normal --> V1["Rainwater Drain Valve OPEN"]
    V1 --> V2["Routine Stormwater Discharge to Oil-Water Separator"]
    
    Spill --> S1["Manual or Automatic Valve Closure"]
    S1 --> S2["Lock and Tag the Drain Valve"]
    S2 --> S3["Bund Now Isolated - Holds Full Spill Volume"]
    S3 --> S4["Visual Inspection by Safety Officer"]
    S4 --> S5{"Liquid Confirmed Hydrocarbon?"}
    S5 -->|"Yes"| S6["Pump Out via Slop Tank to Hazardous Waste"]
    S5 -->|"No - Rainwater Only"| S7["Slowly Drain to OWS with Monitoring"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The drainage isolation logic above is **mandatory knowledge** for KTU 14-mark questions. Students are expected to know the **lock-and-tag** protocol, the **manual override** of automatic systems, and the **segregation** of clean stormwater from contaminated firewater/runoff.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the term "Loss of Primary Containment" (LOPC). Give two examples of LOPC events in a chemical plant.**

**Model Answer:**
LOPC is the unplanned or uncontrolled release of hazardous material from its designed primary enclosure (vessel, piping, gasket, seal) into the surrounding environment. It is the initiating event for which all containment and mitigation systems are designed.

**Examples:**
1. A flange gasket failure on a 6-inch benzene transfer line, releasing liquid into the process area.
2. A storage tank shell rupture due to internal corrosion, releasing 200 m³ of hydrocarbon into the bund.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State the "110% / 100% rule" for secondary containment sizing. Why is this rule used instead of a single 100% rule?**

**Model Answer:**
The 110% / 100% rule states that the bund must hold **110% of the volume of the single largest tank** plus **100% of the aggregate volume of all other tanks** within the same bund.

**Reason:** The 110% factor on the largest tank accounts for: (i) dynamic sloshing of liquid during the release, (ii) foam expansion during firefighting, and (iii) freeboard for splash. The 100% on other tanks is sufficient because the *simultaneous* catastrophic failure of all tanks is considered an independent-event assumption; the dominant release is the single largest tank.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - May 2024] (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain in detail the **three levels of containment** with suitable examples. Discuss the regulatory standards applicable to each level in India. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The three levels of containment form the **defense-in-depth** strategy:

**Level 1 — Primary Containment:**
This is the *first* and *innermost* barrier holding the hazardous material under normal operating conditions. It includes storage vessels, piping, pumps, valves, flanges, gaskets, and instrument connections. In India, primary containment is governed by:
- **ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code, Section VIII** (also adopted under Indian Rules via the Central Boiler Board)
- **IS 2825** (Unfired Pressure Vessels)
- **API 650** for atmospheric storage tanks

**Level 2 — Secondary Containment:**
This is the engineered passive barrier that catches releases if primary containment fails. Examples include:
- **Concrete bund walls** around tank farms
- **Double-walled underground storage tanks** (USTs) with interstitial leak detection
- **Spill pallets** under small drum storage
- **Drip trays** beneath pumps and valves

Indian standards include:
- **NFPA 30** (used in India via OISD standards for hydrocarbon)
- **IS 1944** (Code for bunding design for petroleum installations)
- **OISD-118** (Oil Industry Safety Directorate)

**Level 3 — Tertiary Containment:**
This is the *final* barrier limiting environmental damage if both primary and secondary fail. Examples:
- **Plant-wide stormwater drainage system** with isolation valves
- **Remote impounding basins** for firewater runoff (typically sized for the **worst-case firewater volume**)
- **Vapor scrubbers and thermal oxidizers** for toxic vapor capture
- **Groundwater monitoring wells** around the plant boundary

Regulations: **EPA (Central Pollution Control Board)** and **KSPCB (Kerala State Pollution Control Board)** Consent Orders, plus **IS 2494 / IS 9668**.

---

**(b)** A bulk storage area has the following inventory inside one bund:
- One vertical cylindrical tank: Diameter = 12 m, Height = 10 m
- Three vertical cylindrical tanks: Diameter = 4 m, Height = 6 m each
- Bund footprint: 30 m × 25 m
- Local design rainfall: 75 mm/h

Calculate: **(i)** The required net bund volume using the 110% / 100% rule. **(ii)** The effective bund capacity after rainfall subtraction. **(iii)** The required bund wall height. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Volume of largest tank ($D_1 = 12$ m, $H_1 = 10$ m)**

$$
V_1 = \frac{\pi \times (12)^{2}}{4} \times 10 = \frac{144\pi}{4} \times 10 = 360\pi \approx 1130.97 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 2: Volume of one small tank ($D_2 = 4$ m, $H_2 = 6$ m)**

$$
V_2 = \frac{\pi \times (4)^{2}}{4} \times 6 = \frac{16\pi}{4} \times 6 = 24\pi \approx 75.40 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 3: Total volume of other tanks (three tanks)**

$$
V_{other} = 3 \times 75.40 = 226.19 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 4: Net bund volume (110% / 100% rule)**

$$
V_{bund,net} = 1.1 \times 1130.97 + 1.0 \times 226.19 = 1244.07 + 226.19 = 1470.26 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 5: Rainfall volume ($A_{bund} = 30 \times 25 = 750$ m², $h = 0.075$ m)**

$$
V_{rainfall} = 750 \times 0.075 = 56.25 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 6: Effective bund capacity**

$$
V_{effective} = 1470.26 - 56.25 = 1414.01 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 7: Required bund wall height**

$$
h_{bund} = \frac{V_{effective}}{A_{bund}} = \frac{1414.01}{750} \approx 1.89 \text{ m}
$$

**Conclusion:** A bund wall height of approximately **1.90 m** is required. Engineering practice adds 10% freeboard, so design for **2.10 m**.

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] (14 Marks)

**(a)** Discuss the design philosophy of **firewater containment** systems. Why is firewater runoff considered a serious environmental hazard, and what is the role of the **remote impounding basin**? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Why firewater is hazardous:**
During a hydrocarbon pool fire or vessel fire, fire brigades apply thousands of liters of water (via monitors, sprinklers, and hose streams) to suppress the fire. This water becomes contaminated with:
- Unburned hydrocarbon (50–80% of the released material)
- Combustion products (PAHs, soot)
- Foam concentrate and its degradation products
- Dissolved toxic gases (SO₂, HCN, HCl)

If this contaminated firewater enters the storm drain and flows to a river, it causes **catastrophic downstream pollution** — historically causing multi-kilometer fish kills (e.g., the 2005 Jaipur petroleum depot fire).

**Design Philosophy:**
The remote impounding basin is sized for the **worst credible firewater volume**:
- Flow from all firewater monitors in the area
- Foam solution if applied (typically 3–6% of water flow)
- Duration: 4–8 hours (per NFPA 11 / OISD-116)

The basin is **remote** (at least 50 m, ideally 100 m, from the tank) to avoid flame impingement and to provide hydraulic retention time for oil-water separation. The basin must be **lined with an impervious membrane** (HDPE or similar) and equipped with a **skimming weir** to recover floating hydrocarbon for re-processing.

**Indian Regulations:** OISD-118, CPCB Hazardous Waste Rules, KSPCB Consent to Operate.

---

**(b)** A petroleum tank farm has a 1500 m³ fixed-roof crude oil storage tank within a rectangular bund of 35 m × 20 m. The local rainfall is 60 mm/h. A neighboring pump house uses 4 hydrants at 25 m³/h each, and the design fire duration is 6 hours.

Calculate: **(i)** The required bund volume per the **25% rule** (NFPA 30 fixed-roof tank provision). **(ii)** The firewater volume to be contained in a **remote basin**. **(iii)** Comment on the design adequacy if both release scenarios are considered. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Bund volume using the 25% rule (NFPA 30)**

For fixed-roof atmospheric tanks, NFPA 30 permits a remote impounding basin sized for at least 25% of the largest tank's volume, provided a controlled drainage system is in place.

$$
V_{bund,NFPA30} = 0.25 \times 1500 = 375 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 2: Firewater flow rate**

$$
Q_{firewater} = 4 \times 25 = 100 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{h}
$$

**Step 3: Total firewater volume over 6 hours**

$$
V_{firewater} = 100 \times 6 = 600 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 4: Add 30% safety factor for foam and entrained hydrocarbon**

$$
V_{basin} = 1.30 \times 600 = 780 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 5: Check bund dimensions for 375 m³ capacity**

$$
h_{bund} = \frac{V_{bund}}{A_{bund}} = \frac{375}{35 \times 20} = \frac{375}{700} = 0.536 \text{ m}
$$

**Comment on Adequacy:**
- The local bund (375 m³) handles the *spill* scenario.
- The **remote impounding basin** must be designed for the larger 780 m³ firewater volume.
- The two systems are **independent** (defense-in-depth): bund catches the spill, basin catches contaminated firewater from a *separate* fire-fighting scenario.
- **Critical check:** If the bund is the only catchment, it is grossly undersized for the fire scenario — the plant would have failed environmental compliance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.1 factor** on the largest tank (deducted 1 mark per occurrence in past papers).
> 2. **Not subtracting rainfall** — read the question for "mm/h" data carefully.
> 3. **Confusing primary vs secondary containment** — primary is the equipment itself, secondary is the engineered passive barrier.
> 4. **Ignoring the "no pipe penetration" rule** for bund walls — this is a 3-mark question trap.
> 5. **Failing to mention the lock-and-tag protocol** for bund drain valves.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Containment**

- ✅ Containment = *passive* engineered safeguard, the **third layer** in the hierarchy of controls (after prevention and mitigation).
- ✅ **Primary containment** = the equipment itself (vessel, pipe, valve). Failure = LOPC.
- ✅ **Secondary containment** = bund, double wall, spill pallet, drip tray.
- ✅ **Tertiary containment** = plant drainage, remote impounding basin, vapor scrubber.
- ✅ **110% / 100% rule** is the *primary* bund sizing rule (largest tank × 1.1 + sum of all others).
- ✅ **25% rule** is the *NFPA 30 alternative* for fixed-roof tanks with engineered drainage.
- ✅ **Freeboard** = typically 10% of bund wall height, added for splashing.
- ✅ **Bund walls** must NOT be breached by process pipes — pipes must enter *over* the wall.
- ✅ **Drain valve** must be CLOSED, LOCKED, and TAGGED in normal operation.
- ✅ **Rainfall subtraction** = $A_{bund} \times h_{rainfall}$.
- ✅ **Firewater runoff** must be captured in a *remote* impounding basin (typically $\geq 50$ m away).
- ✅ **Freeboard (h)** = $V_{spill}/A_{bund}$ for rectangular bunds.
- ✅ **Compatibility** — bund concrete must be coated for acids (HF requires special coatings).
- ✅ **Detection** — leak detection in the sub-base (granular layer + perforated pipe) is mandatory for USTs.
- ✅ **Indian standards** — IS 1944, IS 8034, OISD-118, EPA SPCC, KSPCB Consent Orders.
- ✅ **Typical 14-mark question pattern:** Theory (7 marks) + Numerical bund sizing (7 marks).
- ✅ **Common pitfall:** Confusing containment with *mitigation* — containment is **passive**, mitigation often involves *active* systems (sprinklers, foam).
- ✅ **Key figures to memorize:** 110%, 100%, 25%, 10% freeboard, 50 m remote basin distance, 4–8 hours fire duration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT616 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Chemical Process Safety (CPS)**, **Detection** refers to the systematic identification of a hazardous condition — such as a flammable atmosphere, toxic gas release, fire, over-pressure, or runaway reaction — using dedicated sensor technologies, alarm architectures, and placement strategies, *before* the hazard escalates into a loss-of-containment event, fire, or explosion. Detection is the **first pillar of the "Detection–Mitigation–Response" hierarchy** of process safety management and forms the foundation of every layer-of-protection analysis (LOPA).

The official KTU 2024 syllabus (OESFT616, Module 3) defines detection as the engineered set of **sensing elements, signal transmitters, logic solvers, and alarm presentation devices** used to identify the onset of an unsafe process deviation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – Detection):** Students must be able to (a) classify detectors by sensing principle, (b) compute detector coverage and placement spacing, (c) explain detector response time and the trade-off between sensitivity and false-alarm rate, and (d) interpret Threshold Limit Values (TLV), Permissible Exposure Limits (PEL), and Lower/Upper Explosive Limits (LEL/UEL) in the context of detector set-points.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a smoke detector mounted on the ceiling of your hostel room. It cannot *put out* a fire, and it cannot *stop* you from leaving your stove on — but the instant it senses even a wisp of smoke, it screams loudly enough to wake you. **That is exactly what a chemical-plant detector does, but for gases, vapours, flames, and process upsets.**

> Think of a chemical plant as a sleeping giant. Sensors are its **nervous system**; alarms are its **voice**. Without detection, mitigation systems never know *when* to act.

### 1.3 Key Metrics and Constants

- **LEL (Lower Explosive Limit):** the minimum vapour concentration in air that can ignite. Standard benchmark: **LEL ≈ 1 % v/v for most hydrocarbons**.
- **UEL (Upper Explosive Limit):** the maximum concentration above which the mixture is "too rich" to burn.
- **TLV–TWA (Threshold Limit Value – Time Weighted Average):** typically **8-hour exposure** average.
- **IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health):** concentration above which an unprotected worker could suffer irreversible effects within **30 minutes**.
- **Response Time (t₉₀):** time for the detector to reach **90 %** of the final reading. Industry benchmark: **t₉₀ ≤ 30 s** for toxic gas detectors in refinery service.
- **STEL (Short Term Exposure Limit):** **15-minute** time-weighted average.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference:** All industrial detector set-points in the US are referenced to **NFPA 72** (National Fire Alarm and Signaling Code) and **API RP 1160** for pipeline detection. Indian PSM rules follow the **Factories Act 1948, Schedule I** and **OISD–STD–144** for petroleum installations.

---

### 1.4 Classification of Detectors (At a Glance)

| Hazard Class | Typical Sensor | Physical Principle |
|---|---|---|
| Flammable gas/vapour | Catalytic bead, IR, PID | Heat of combustion / IR absorption |
| Toxic gas | Electrochemical, PID | Redox reaction / UV ionisation |
| Oxygen deficiency/enrichment | Galvanic, paramagnetic | Electrochemical / magnetic susceptibility |
| Fire/flame | UV, IR, dual-spectrum | Photon emission at 4.4 µm or 0.2–0.3 µm |
| Smoke | Photoelectric, ionisation | Light scatter / ion current change |
| Heat | Rate-of-rise, fixed temp | Thermistor expansion |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Detection Decision Chain

A complete detection loop in a process plant consists of **four sequential nodes**, and the overall safety performance is limited by the *weakest* node. This is sometimes called the **"Swiss-cheese" reliability chain.**

1. **Sensor element** – converts a physical/chemical change into an electrical signal.
2. **Transmitter** – amplifies and conditions the signal (4–20 mA, HART, or fieldbus).
3. **Logic solver (PLC / DCS / SIS)** – compares the signal to a set-point and makes a trip decision.
4. **Final Element / Annunciator** – horn, beacon, block valve, deluge, or ESD actuator.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education framework classifies detection as a **CO₃ / CO₄ level competency** (Apply and Analyse). Expect 14-mark questions asking you to *design* a detector layout, not merely list sensor types.

### 2.2 Detector Operating Principles — Deep Dive

#### (a) Catalytic Bead (Pellistor) Sensor
A platinum coil is embedded in a catalytic pellet. Flammable gas oxidises catalytically → heat → resistance change. Wheatstone bridge converts ΔR into mV signal.

$$\Delta R = R_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

where **α** is the temperature coefficient of resistance and **ΔT** is the temperature rise from combustion. Typical bead operating temperature: **500 °C – 550 °C**.

#### (b) Infrared (NDIR) Detector
Uses **Beer–Lambert absorption** at a wavelength characteristic of the C–H stretch (~3.4 µm). Immune to poisoning, longer life, but **cannot detect H₂** (no IR absorption).

#### (c) Photoionisation Detector (PID)
UV lamp (10.0 eV or 11.7 eV) ionises molecules with ionisation potential < lamp energy. The resulting ion current is proportional to concentration.

$$I = k \cdot C \cdot \phi \cdot P$$

where **C** = gas concentration, **φ** = ionisation efficiency, **P** = lamp photon flux, **k** = cell constant.

#### (d) Electrochemical Sensor
A three-electrode cell (working, counter, reference). Target gas diffuses through a membrane and undergoes a redox reaction, generating a current:

$$I_{cell} = n \cdot F \cdot \frac{D \cdot A}{\delta} \cdot C$$

where **n** = electrons per mole, **F** = Faraday constant (**96,485 C mol⁻¹**), **D** = diffusivity, **A** = electrode area, **δ** = membrane thickness, **C** = gas concentration.

#### (e) Flame Detector Logic
Uses **AND-gate voting** across two spectral bands (e.g., UV + IR) to suppress false alarms from sunlight, arc-welding, or lightning. Industry standard: **triple-IR voting** (3-IR) for offshore platforms.

### 2.3 Detector Set-Point Selection

The correct alarm set-point is **NOT** the LEL or TLV itself. Industry practice (CCPS / API) is:

- **Low alarm:** **10 % of LEL** (or ½ TLV, whichever is lower).
- **High alarm:** **25 % of LEL** (or full TLV).
- **Trip / ESD:** **60 % of LEL** (per API RP 1160, Class I).

For toxic gases:
- **First alarm:** **½ TLV–TWA**.
- **Evacuation alarm:** **TLV–STEL** or **10 × TLV**, whichever is smaller.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Symbol Meaning | Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta R = R_0 \alpha \Delta T$ | Resistance change of pellistor | Ω | Catalytic bead output |
| 2 | $I = \ell^{-k(\lambda)\, c\, L}$ | Beer–Lambert absorption (decadic) | – | NDIR gas concentration |
| 3 | $I_{cell} = \dfrac{nFAD}{\delta}\,C$ | Electrochemical cell current | A | Toxic gas sensor |
| 4 | $t_{90} = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{Q}$ | Detector time to 90 % response | s | Response time |
| 5 | $S/N = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_s}{V_n}\right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | dB | Detector sensitivity |
| 6 | $\rho_{cov} = \dfrac{A_{sensor}}{A_{floor}} \times 100\,\%$ | Coverage ratio | % | Layout design |
| 7 | $\lambda_d = -\dfrac{\ln(1-PFD)}{T_{proof}}$ | Failure rate from PFD | h⁻¹ | SIL calculation |
| 8 | $C_{alarm} = k_{safety}\cdot LEL$ | Alarm threshold | ppm or % | Set-point design |
| 9 | $D_{coverage} = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{A_{floor}}{N_{s}}}$ | Square-root rule spacing | m | Detector placement |
| 10 | $PFD_{total} = \sum PFD_i$ | Sum of probability of failure on demand | – | Reliability of loop |

> **Memory trick:** *"Loud horn ⇒ 10 % LEL first whisper, 60 % LEL last shout."*

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

Detection is the cornerstone of:
- **Refinery and petrochemical safety** (LPG, LNG, H₂S, benzene monitoring).
- **Pharmaceutical batch reactors** (solvent vapour, O₂ exclusion).
- **Semiconductor fabrication** (HF, H₂, silane leak detection).
- **Offshore platforms** (triple-IR flame and gas detection under IEC 61508 SIL 2).
- **Confined-space entry** (portable 4-gas monitors: LEL, O₂, CO, H₂S).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Detector Coverage Radius and the "Square-Root Rule"

The placement of point gas detectors in an open process area follows an empirical rule derived from turbulent diffusion theory. We will derive it rigorously.

**Given:**
- A rectangular process floor of dimensions **L × W** (m²).
- We wish to install **N_s** detectors, each providing circular coverage of radius **r** (m).
- The detectors are arranged on a square grid (most common layout).

**Step 1 – Total sensor coverage area:**

The geometric coverage of **N_s** detectors, each with area $\pi r^2$, is:

$$A_{sensor} = N_s \cdot \pi r^2$$

**Step 2 – Coverage ratio requirement:**

For the layout to be considered "adequately covered" per **API RP 1160 / IS 5571** guidelines:

$$\rho_{cov} = \frac{A_{sensor}}{A_{floor}} \ge \rho_{min}$$

Typical **ρ_min ≈ 0.80** (i.e. 80 % coverage). This 20 % gap is intentional to allow redundancy overlap and avoid dead zones.

**Step 3 – Substitute the floor area:**

$$A_{floor} = L \cdot W$$

Therefore:

$$0.80 \le \frac{N_s \cdot \pi r^2}{L \cdot W}$$

**Step 4 – Solve for the number of detectors required:**

$$N_s \ge \frac{0.80 \cdot L \cdot W}{\pi r^2}$$

**Step 5 – Spacing on a square grid (the "square-root rule"):**

The detectors are placed at equal spacing **d** in both x and y directions. The number of detectors then becomes:

$$N_s = \left\lceil \frac{L}{d} \right\rceil \cdot \left\lceil \frac{W}{d} \right\rceil$$

For a square floor (L = W = √A), and assuming **d ≈ 2r** (each detector's coverage just touches its neighbour's):

$$N_s = \left(\frac{L}{2r}\right)^2 = \frac{L^2}{4 r^2}$$

Equating the two expressions for N_s:

$$\frac{L^2}{4 r^2} = \frac{0.80 \cdot L^2}{\pi r^2}$$

**Step 6 – Solve for the effective spacing constant:**

$$4 = \frac{\pi}{0.80} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \pi = 3.20 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; d = \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{0.80}}\cdot r \approx 1.98\,r$$

> **Final result (square-root rule):** Place detectors at a spacing of **d ≈ 2r** in a square grid, where **r** is the manufacturer's certified gas-cloud detection radius. This guarantees ≥ 80 % geometric coverage.

### 3.2 Worked Example (KTU Numerical Style)

**Problem:** A compressor shed measures **40 m × 20 m**. The chosen infrared (NDIR) methane detector has a manufacturer-certified detection radius of **r = 8 m**. Using the square-root rule and 80 % coverage criterion, determine the required number of detectors and their grid spacing.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute the floor area.

$$A_{floor} = 40 \times 20 = 800 \text{ m}^2$$

Step 2: Apply the 80 % coverage formula.

$$N_s \ge \frac{0.80 \times 800}{\pi \times 8^2} = \frac{640}{201.06} \approx 3.18$$

Round up → **N_s = 4 detectors**.

Step 3: Determine the square grid spacing for 4 detectors.

$$N_s = \left(\frac{L}{d}\right) \times \left(\frac{W}{d}\right) = 4 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{L}{d} = 2,\;\; \frac{W}{d} = 2$$

$$d = \frac{L}{2} = \frac{40}{2} = 20 \text{ m} \quad \text{(along length)}$$

$$d = \frac{W}{2} = \frac{20}{2} = 10 \text{ m} \quad \text{(along width)}$$

> **Note:** The two spacings are unequal because 4 detectors form a 2 × 2 grid in a 40 × 20 hall. To maintain equal spacing we would need **3 × 2 = 6 detectors** at d = **13.3 m × 10 m**.

Step 4: Verify the actual coverage with 6 detectors.

$$\rho_{cov} = \frac{6 \times \pi \times 8^2}{800} = \frac{6 \times 201.06}{800} = \frac{1206.4}{800} = 1.508$$

That is **150.8 %** — meaning the design is over-specified (redundancy), which is acceptable for SIL-2 service.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Detector Selection by Hazard

| Hazard | Recommended Sensor | Set-point Logic | Typical Set-point | Common Mistake |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Methane (CH₄) leak | NDIR or catalytic bead | % LEL | 10 %, 25 %, 60 % LEL | Using a PID for methane (no ionisation) |
| H₂S release | Electrochemical | TLV-based | 10 ppm (TLV) | Setting alarm at LEL (irrelevant — toxic, not flammable) |
| Benzene vapour | PID (10.0 eV) | TLV–TWA | 0.5 ppm (½ TLV) | Confusing TLV with TWA basis |
| Hydrogen fire | UV or triple-IR | Flame signature | Spectral AND-gate | Single-band UV (false alarms from sun) |
| O₂ deficiency | Galvanic or paramagnetic | % O₂ | 19.5 % low, 23.5 % high | Reversing high/low logic |
| HF vapour | Electrochemical (acidic) | TLV | 0.5 ppm ceiling | Using alkaline-electrolyte sensor (corrodes) |
| Ammonia (NH₃) | Electrochemical | TLV | 25 ppm | Confusing with refrigerant codes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Detection Loop Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **functional architecture of a complete detection-to-mitigation loop** as typically implemented in a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) per IEC 61511.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process Plant] --> B[Sensor Element]
    B --> C[Transmitter 4-20mA]
    C --> D[SIS Logic Solver PLC]
    D --> E1[Horn and Beacon]
    D --> E2[Block Valve ESD]
    D --> E3[DCS Alarm HMI]
    D --> E4[Deluge System]
    A -. feedback .- B
    E2 -. mitigation .- A
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **process plant** generates a hazard (e.g., gas cloud).
- The **sensor** detects it, producing an electrical signal.
- The **transmitter** converts this to a standardised **4–20 mA** loop current.
- The **SIS logic solver** (a separate, certified PLC) compares the signal with the trip set-point.
- On a confirmed trip, the SIS energises **four parallel final elements**: horn, ESD block valve, HMI alarm in DCS, and the **deluge / fire-suppression** system.
- The **dashed lines** show that the final element then acts back on the process to *mitigate* the hazard (closing the loop).

### 4.2 Detector Placement Strategy (Top View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Plant_Area[40 m x 20 m Compressor Shed]
        D1((Detector 1)) --- D2((Detector 2))
        D2 --- D3((Detector 3))
        D3 --- D4((Detector 4))
        D4 --- D1
        D1 --- D5((Detector 5)):::alt
        D5 --- D6((Detector 6)):::alt
    end
    classDef alt fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The 6 detectors form a 3 × 2 grid with 13.3 m × 10 m spacing. Yellow-coloured units (D5, D6) are *redundant* for SIL-2 compliance.

### 4.3 Sensor Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[What hazard to detect?] --> B{Flammable gas?}
    A --> C{Toxic gas?}
    A --> D{Fire or flame?}
    A --> E{Oxygen level?}
    B -->|Yes| F{H2 present?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Catalytic bead or electrochemical]
    F -->|No| H[NDIR or PID]
    C -->|Yes| I[Electrochemical or PID]
    D -->|Yes| J[UV or Triple-IR]
    E -->|Yes| K[Galvanic or paramagnetic]
```

### 4.4 Alarm Hierarchy State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Normal
    Normal --> LowAlarm: C between 10% and 25% LEL
    LowAlarm --> HighAlarm: C between 25% and 60% LEL
    HighAlarm --> Trip: C greater than 60% LEL
    Trip --> Isolation: ESD closes block valve
    Isolation --> Normal: Manual reset after purge
    LowAlarm --> Normal: Concentration falls below 10% LEL
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is a **finite-state machine** describing how the alarm system escalates. Note that only the **Trip → Isolation** transition is automatic; the **Isolation → Normal** transition requires a *manual* reset by the control-room operator after verifying the area is safe.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define the term "detector response time (t₉₀)". Why is it critical in a flammable-gas detection system?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *Definition (1 mark):* t₉₀ is the time elapsed between the instant the target gas reaches the sensor face and the instant the instrument reads 90 % of the final (steady-state) value.
- *Criticality (1 mark):* In flammable-gas service the LEL trip is set at 60 % LEL. If t₉₀ is too long, the gas cloud may reach flammable concentration (100 % LEL) *before* the trip fires, defeating the safety function.
- *KTU standard value (1 mark):* Per API RP 1160, **t₉₀ must be ≤ 30 s** for refinery fixed gas detectors.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Why is a Photoionisation Detector (PID) unsuitable for detecting methane (CH₄) in a process plant?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *Principle of PID (1 mark):* A PID ionises molecules using a UV lamp of fixed photon energy (commonly **10.0 eV or 11.7 eV**).
- *Methane property (1 mark):* The ionisation potential of methane is **12.61 eV**, which is *higher* than the lamp energy.
- *Conclusion (1 mark):* Therefore CH₄ cannot be ionised by a standard PID, and an alternative sensor (catalytic bead or NDIR) must be used. This is a frequently-asked KTU trap question.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### Question A — 14 Marks *(Descriptive + Numerical)*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

**(a)** *Explain, with a neat block diagram, the four functional elements of a process-industry detection loop. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** *A 60 m × 30 m pump house handles gasoline (LEL = 1.4 % v/v). The chosen NDIR detector has a manufacturer-certified detection radius of 10 m. Using the 80 % coverage criterion and the square-root rule, determine (i) the number of detectors required, (ii) their grid spacing, and (iii) the low, high, and trip alarm set-points in % LEL. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) The four functional elements** (1 mark each, plus 3 marks for explanation):
1. **Sensor element** – converts the physical/chemical input (gas concentration, IR radiation, ion current) into a measurable electrical signal.
2. **Transmitter** – amplifies, linearises, and converts the signal into a standardised industrial current (4–20 mA) or digital fieldbus (HART, Foundation Fieldbus).
3. **Logic solver (PLC / SIS)** – implements the trip logic. Typically a separate, certified safety PLC per IEC 61508 with a target SIL of 1, 2, or 3.
4. **Final element / Annunciator** – the actuator that delivers the mitigation: horn, beacon, ESD block valve, deluge valve, or foam pourer.

> A clean block diagram with these four boxes, clearly labelled, is expected for full 7 marks.

**(b) Numerical solution:**

(i) Number of detectors:

$$A_{floor} = 60 \times 30 = 1800 \text{ m}^2$$

$$N_s \ge \frac{0.80 \times 1800}{\pi \times 10^2} = \frac{1440}{314.16} \approx 4.58 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N_s = 5 \text{ (round up)}$$

For a clean 3 × 2 grid in a 60 × 30 hall, we choose **N_s = 6**.

(ii) Grid spacing for 6 detectors in a 3 × 2 layout:

$$d_x = \frac{60}{3} = 20 \text{ m}, \quad d_y = \frac{30}{2} = 15 \text{ m}$$

Spacing matrix: **20 m × 15 m**.

(iii) Alarm set-points per API RP 1160:
- **Low alarm:** 10 % of LEL = **0.14 % v/v** (1400 ppm).
- **High alarm:** 25 % of LEL = **0.35 % v/v** (3500 ppm).
- **Trip / ESD:** 60 % of LEL = **0.84 % v/v** (8400 ppm).

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks *(Alternative Choice – Conceptual + Design)*

**(a)** *Compare the working principles, advantages, and limitations of (i) catalytic bead, (ii) NDIR, and (iii) PID sensors. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** *A storage tank farm has the following hazards: methane vapour (LEL = 5 % v/v), H₂S (TLV–TWA = 10 ppm), and oxygen deficiency risk. Design a detection philosophy — specify the sensor type, set-point logic, and the placement rule (minimum 2 sentences) for each hazard. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison table** (≈ 2 marks per sensor + 1 mark for synthesis):

| Property | Catalytic Bead | NDIR | PID |
|---|---|---|---|
| Principle | Catalytic combustion on hot Pt coil | Beer–Lambert IR absorption | UV photo-ionisation |
| Detects | Most combustible gases | Any gas with IR-active bond (C–H, C=O) | VOC with IP < 10 eV |
| Detects H₂? | Yes (but flammable only) | **No** | **No** |
| Detects CH₄? | Yes | Yes (3.4 µm band) | **No** (IP = 12.6 eV) |
| Poison resistance | Poor (silicone/sulphur) | Excellent | Moderate (lamp cleaning) |
| Calibration drift | 3–6 months | 12 months | 1–3 months |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High |

**(b) Detection philosophy:**

- **Methane (LEL = 5 % v/v):** Use **NDIR** sensor (immune to poisoning, suits outdoor tank farm). Set-points: low 0.5 % v/v, high 1.25 % v/v, ESD at 3.0 % v/v. Place on perimeter of bund at 15 m spacing, plus one above each tank roof vent.
- **H₂S (TLV = 10 ppm):** Use **electrochemical** sensor (specific, ppm-level). Set-points: low 5 ppm, high 10 ppm, evacuation 50 ppm. Place at breathing height (1.5 m) near pump seals and sampling points, with a maximum spacing of 10 m.
- **Oxygen (deficiency):** Use **galvanic** sensor. Set-points: low 19.5 % v/v (alarm), high 23.5 % v/v (enrichment alarm). Place one unit at every confined-space entry point and at operator breathing height inside the bund.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** use a single-band UV detector for outdoor fire detection — sunlight triggers false trips. Always use **dual- or triple-spectral IR voting**.
> 2. **Do not** set the toxic-gas trip at the LEL. Many students confuse "explosive" with "toxic" — these are *different* properties and use *different* set-point bases (LEL vs. TLV).
> 3. **Do not** forget to specify the **detector height**. Methane is lighter than air (place near ceiling); H₂S is heavier than air (place near floor); O₂ monitors are placed at breathing height (1.5 m). This single mistake can cost 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Do not** quote detector count without justifying the **coverage ratio (ρ ≥ 80 %)**. Marks are awarded for the *method*, not the final number.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Detection is the *first* layer of the safety hierarchy — *identify* the hazard before *mitigating* it.
- **Four elements of any detection loop:** Sensor → Transmitter → Logic Solver (SIS/PLC) → Final Element (alarm/valve/deluge).
- **Detector types (memorise!):** Catalytic bead (flammable), NDIR (hydrocarbons, no H₂), PID (VOC, no CH₄), Electrochemical (toxic gases), UV/IR (flame), Galvanic/Paramagnetic (O₂).
- **Set-point memory line:** *"10 % LEL whisper, 25 % LEL shout, 60 % LEL trip out."* (API RP 1160)
- **Toxic-gas set-point:** Low = ½ TLV, High = TLV, Trip = 10 × TLV or IDLH (whichever is lower).
- **Square-root rule:** Place detectors at spacing **d ≈ 2r** for ≥ 80 % coverage. Use $N_s \ge 0.80\,A_{floor}/(\pi r^2)$.
- **Detector height rule:** Lighter-than-air gas (CH₄, H₂) → ceiling; heavier-than-air (H₂S, LPG, Cl₂) → floor; O₂ → breathing zone (1.5 m).
- **Response time:** Industry benchmark **t₉₀ ≤ 30 s** for fixed gas detectors in refinery service.
- **Reliability metric:** PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand). For SIL 2, **PFD ∈ [10⁻², 10⁻¹]**.
- **Common KTU trap:** Methane cannot be detected by a PID; H₂ cannot be detected by NDIR.
- **Standards to quote in answers:** NFPA 72, API RP 1160, IEC 61508/61511, IS 5571, OISD–STD–144.
- **Design sequence for an exam answer:** (1) Identify hazard, (2) Pick sensor principle, (3) Decide set-points, (4) Calculate N_s using coverage rule, (5) Specify placement height and spacing, (6) Justify with a standard.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip:** In KTU 14-mark answers, always *close* with a sentence tying your design back to a recognised standard (API, NFPA, IEC). Examiners reward standards-aware answers with full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT616 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Control # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is "Control" in Chemical Process Safety? In the context of **Chemical Process Safety (CPS)** and the KTU 2024 syllabus, the term **"Control"** refers to a structured, engineered layer of protection that uses instrumentation, feedback mechanisms, and automated systems to keep process variables (such as temperature, pressure, flow, and level) within their safe operating limits. It is the **first engineered safeguard** in the hierarchy of protection layers and forms the backbone of every modern chemical plant's safety strategy. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** > *Process Control in safety engineering is the continuous, automatic monitoring and manipulation of process variables using sensors, controllers, and final control elements to maintain the process in a safe, stable, and desired operating state, thereby preventing the deviation of parameters that could lead to hazardous incidents.* In simple terms, **Control = Automation that prevents accidents before they occur**. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Cruise Control of a Car To make this concept crystal clear, imagine you are driving a car on a highway: - **The Car's Speed** = The Process Variable (e.g., reactor temperature) - **The Speedometer** = The Sensor (e.g., RTD, thermocouple) - **Your Brain** = The Controller (e.g., PID controller) - **The Accelerator/Brake** = The Final Control Element (e.g., control valve) You set a desired speed (the **setpoint**). If the car starts climbing a hill, the speed drops. The brain sees this difference (the **error**) and presses the accelerator harder. Once the speed is restored, the brain relaxes. This continuous, automatic correction is exactly what a **process control loop** does in a chemical plant — but instead of speed, it controls temperature, pressure, flow, or composition. > [!NOTE] > **Key Standard Metrics You Must Know:** > - **Setpoint (SP):** Desired value of the process variable. > - **Process Variable (PV):** The actual measured value. > - **Error (e):** e = SP − PV. > - **Manipulated Variable (MV):** The variable adjusted by the controller. > - **Control Loop:** Sensor → Controller → Final Control Element → Process. ## 1.3 Why is "Control" a Safety Topic? Without proper control: - A reactor could **overheat** → runaway reaction → explosion. - A pressure vessel could **over-pressurize** → mechanical failure. - A toxic gas line could **leak excessively** → exposure to personnel. Control systems are the **silent guardians** of chemical plants. They act faster, more accurately, and more reliably than any human operator could. ## 1.4 Position of Control in the Layers of Protection Process Control sits at the **Inherent Process Design → Control → Safety Instrumented System → Alarms → Interlocks → Relief Devices → Dikes → Emergency Response** chain. It is the first **active engineered layer** of protection. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** > The module emphasizes how a properly designed control system acts as a **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** of typically **10 to 100**, meaning it reduces the probability of a hazardous event by 1 to 2 orders of magnitude. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Building Blocks of a Control Loop Every industrial control loop — whether for a heat exchanger, a distillation column, or a chemical reactor — has exactly four building blocks: 1. **Sensor (Measuring Element / Transmitter):** - Detects the process variable (e.g., thermocouple measures temperature). - Converts the physical quantity into a standardized signal (typically **4–20 mA** in industry). 2. **Controller:** - The "brain" of the loop. - Compares PV with SP, calculates the error, and decides what corrective action to take. - Common types: P, PI, PD, PID. 3. **Final Control Element (Actuator + Control Valve):** - The "muscle" that physically adjusts the process. - Example: A pneumatic diaphragm control valve modulating steam flow to a heater. 4. **Process (Plant):** - The chemical/physical system being controlled. - Has its own **process dynamics** (time constant, dead time, gain). > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Note:** > In modern DCS (Distributed Control Systems) like Honeywell Experion, Emerson DeltaV, or Siemens PCS 7, the controller and HMI live inside redundant processors. The signal path is: > **Field Sensor → I/O Card → Controller → I/O Card → Field Valve → Process**. ## 2.2 Types of Control Strategies | Strategy | Description | Industrial Example | |---|---|---| | **Feedback Control** | Measures PV *after* the disturbance has affected the process; corrects it. | Jacket coolant flow on a CSTR to control temperature. | | **Feedforward Control** | Measures the disturbance *before* it affects the process; takes pre-emptive action. | Measuring feed flow rate and pre-adjusting steam. | | **Cascade Control** | Two loops: *primary* (outer) sets the SP of a *secondary* (inner) loop. | Reactor temp controller sets SP of jacket temp controller. | | **Ratio Control** | Maintains a fixed ratio between two flows. | Air-to-fuel ratio in a fired heater. | | **Split-Range Control** | One controller output drives two or more valves in different ranges. | Heating steam valve (0–50%) + cooling water valve (50–100%). | | **Override Control** | A second controller takes over when a constraint is approached. | High-temperature override closes a fuel valve. | ## 2.3 PID Controller — The Workhorse of Process Control Most industrial controllers are of the **PID (Proportional–Integral–Derivative)** family. The controller output $u(t)$ is given by the master equation: $$ u(t) = K_c \, e(t) + \frac{K_c}{\tau_I}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_c \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt} $$ Where: - $K_c$ = Proportional gain (dimensionless) - $\tau_I$ = Integral time (seconds or minutes) — also called *reset time* - $\tau_D$ = Derivative time (seconds or minutes) — also called *rate time* - $e(t)$ = Error = Setpoint − Process Variable ### 2.3.1 Role of Each Term - **P (Proportional):** Reacts to the *current* error. Larger $K_c$ = faster response but more oscillation. **Always used.** - **I (Integral):** Eliminates *steady-state offset* (the persistent gap between SP and PV). It "remembers" past errors. - **D (Derivative):** Reacts to the *rate of change* of error. It "anticipates" future error and damps oscillations. Used in slow, noisy processes (rare in safety-critical loops). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Tuning Rule of Thumb (Ziegler–Nichols Ultimate Gain Method):** > Set $K_c$ to the value at which sustained oscillations just begin ($K_u$), measure the oscillation period ($P_u$). Then: > - **P-only:** $K_c = 0.5 K_u$ > - **PI:** $K_c = 0.45 K_u$, $\tau_I = P_u / 1.2$ > - **PID:** $K_c = 0.6 K_u$, $\tau_I = P_u / 2$, $\tau_D = P_u / 8$ ## 2.4 Safety Instrumented System (SIS) A **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** is a *separate*, *independent* control system dedicated solely to safety functions. It is the *last line of automatic defense* before a relief device or operator intervention. Per **IEC 61511 / ISA 84.00.01** (the global standard for process industry safety): > [!IMPORTANT] > **SIS Definition:** > An SIS is a system composed of sensors, logic solvers, and final elements designed to take a process to a safe state when predetermined conditions are violated. ### 2.4.1 Safety Integrity Level (SIL) A **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** is a discrete level (1, 2, or 3) for specifying the safety performance of a safety function. It is defined by the **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)**: $$ \text{PFD} = \frac{1}{\text{Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)}} $$ | SIL | PFD (per IEC 61511) | Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) | |---|---|---| | **SIL 1** | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ | 10 to 100 | | **SIL 2** | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ | 100 to 1,000 | | **SIL 3** | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | 1,000 to 10,000 | | **SIL 4** | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ | 10,000 to 100,000 | > Note: SIL 4 is generally not used in the process industry (more common in nuclear/aviation). ### 2.4.2 The Three Components of an SIS 1. **Sensor(s):** Detect the dangerous condition (e.g., high-pressure transmitter). 2. **Logic Solver:** A dedicated safety PLC (e.g., Honeywell Safety Manager, Siemens S7 F-systems, Triconex) that decides what action to take. 3. **Final Element(s):** Take the process to safe state (e.g., shutdown valve, fire pump motor). ## 2.5 Alarms and Interlocks - **Alarm:** An audible/visual signal to alert the operator of a deviation. The operator decides the action. (Operator action: ~1 in 10 chance of failure per demand, RRF ≈ 10). - **Interlock:** A *mandatory* automatic action — the system *forces* a safe state without operator intervention. (RRF ≈ 10 to 100). > [!NOTE] > **Practical Engineering Insight:** > Alarms should be *layered*: First-level alarm for operator attention, second-level for supervisor, and third-level for emergency action. This is the **Alarm Hierarchy** in modern DCS design (per **ISA 18.2 / IEC 62682**). ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---| | Error | $e(t) = SP - PV$ | Same as PV | | PID Output | $u(t) = K_c e + \frac{K_c}{\tau_I}\int e\,dt + K_c \tau_D \frac{de}{dt}$ | Controller output (%) | | Process Gain | $K_p = \frac{\Delta PV}{\Delta MV}$ | At steady state | | Time Constant ($\tau$) | Time for PV to reach 63.2% of final value | seconds | | Dead Time ($\theta$) | Delay between MV change and PV response | seconds | | PFD | $\text{PFD} = 1/\text{RRF}$ | Dimensionless | | RRF | $\text{RRF} = 1/\text{PFD}$ | Dimensionless | | SIL vs PFD Range | $\text{SIL}_i$ if $10^{-(i+1)} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-i}$ | Per IEC 61511 | | Z-N P-only | $K_c = 0.5\,K_u$ | Ultimate gain method | | Z-N PI | $K_c = 0.45 K_u$, $\tau_I = P_u / 1.2$ | — | | Z-N PID | $K_c = 0.6 K_u$, $\tau_I = P_u / 2$, $\tau_D = P_u / 8$ | — | | 4–20 mA Scaling | $PV = \frac{(I - 4)}{16} \times (PV_{max} - PV_{min}) + PV_{min}$ | Linear scaling | | Failure Rate | $\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | per hour | | Availability | $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Dimensionless, 0–1 | ## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Chemical Engineering - **Refineries:** Control of distillation column reflux ratio, furnace excess O₂. - **Polymer Plants:** Tight temperature control in CSTR for molecular weight distribution. - **Pharma:** Batch reactor temperature control for FDA-compliant reproducibility. - **LNG Plants:** Cascade control of cryogenic heat exchangers. - **Ammonia Plants:** Steam-to-carbon ratio control to prevent catalyst damage. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Basic P-Controller to Full PID We will derive the controller equation in three logical stages. ### 3.1.1 Stage 1: Proportional (P) Action **Conceptual basis:** The corrective action is *proportional* to the current error. Let the controller output be $u(t)$ and the error be $e(t) = SP - PV$. Define proportional gain $K_c$. **Step 1:** Assume a linear relationship. $$ u(t) \propto e(t) $$ **Step 2:** Introduce the proportional constant $K_c$. $$ u(t) = K_c \cdot e(t) $$ **Step 3:** This is the **P-controller equation**. It is instantaneous and fast but leaves a **steady-state offset** because once $PV = SP$ momentarily, $e = 0$, so $u = 0$, and the disturbance re-creates error. > Real-world interpretation: A room heater controlled only by a P-controller will leave the room slightly colder than the thermostat setpoint on a cold day. ### 3.1.2 Stage 2: Adding Integral (I) Action **Conceptual basis:** The I-term accumulates past errors and eliminates offset. **Step 1:** The I-term is proportional to the time-integral of the error. $$ u_I(t) = \frac{K_c}{\tau_I} \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau $$ **Step 2:** Combining with the P-term: $$ u(t) = K_c \, e(t) + \frac{K_c}{\tau_I} \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau $$ **Step 3:** The factor $\frac{K_c}{\tau_I}$ is called the **integral gain $K_I$**. If a small persistent error $e_{ss}$ remains, the integral grows with time until $u$ is large enough to eliminate it. > Real-world interpretation: The I-term is like the brain of a car driver on a long hill — even if the speed momentarily equals setpoint, the driver keeps pressing the accelerator a tiny bit more, knowing the slope will require sustained effort. ### 3.1.3 Stage 3: Adding Derivative (D) Action **Conceptual basis:** The D-term predicts future error by sensing its rate of change. **Step 1:** The D-term is proportional to the time-derivative of the error. $$ u_D(t) = K_c \, \tau_D \, \frac{de(t)}{dt} $$ **Step 2:** Combining all three terms yields the **full PID equation**: $$ u(t) = K_c \, e(t) + \frac{K_c}{\tau_I}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_c \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt} $$ **Step 3:** The factor $K_c \tau_D$ is called the **derivative gain $K_D$**. > This is the final form. Each term addresses one aspect: P → now, I → past, D → future. ## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: RRF and PFD for a Redundant System Consider a **1oo2 (1-out-of-2)** voting sensor system (two sensors, either can trip the shutdown). **Step 1:** Define the failure rate of a single sensor as $\lambda$ (per hour). For a single sensor, the average probability of failure on demand over a proof test interval $T$ is: $$ \text{PFD}_{\text{single}} = \frac{\lambda T}{2} $$ **Step 2:** For a 1oo2 system, the system fails *only if both* sensors fail (assuming common cause factor $\beta$ is negligible): $$ \text{PFD}_{1oo2} \approx \frac{(\lambda T)^2}{3} $$ **Step 3:** Derive the **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)**: $$ \text{RRF} = \frac{1}{\text{PFD}_{1oo2}} = \frac{3}{(\lambda T)^2} $$ **Step 4:** Numerical example. Let $\lambda = 10^{-6}$ per hour and $T = 8760$ hours (1 year): $$ \lambda T = 10^{-6} \times 8760 = 8.76 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ (\lambda T)^2 = 7.67 \times 10^{-5} $$ $$ \text{PFD}_{1oo2} = \frac{7.67 \times 10^{-5}}{3} = 2.56 \times 10^{-5} $$ $$ \text{RRF} = \frac{1}{2.56 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 3.9 \times 10^{4} $$ > This is **SIL 3** territory. Redundancy dramatically increases the safety integrity. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Controller Selection for Common Process Variables | Process Variable | Sensor Type | Final Element | Recommended Control Action | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | Temperature | RTD, Thermocouple | Steam valve | PI | Slow, integrating; I-term needed | | Pressure | Strain gauge | Block valve | PI | Fast dynamics; tune carefully | | Flow | Orifice + dPT | Pump speed / valve | PI | Very fast; high $K_c$ | | Level | DP cell, Float | Inlet valve | P or PI | Integrating process | | Composition | IR, GC | Heater / feed | PI + Cascade | Slow, expensive analyzer | | pH | Glass electrode | Reagent pump | PI (nonlinear) | Strong nonlinearity; use gain scheduling | ## 3.4 Time-Response Analysis of a First-Order Process Consider a first-order process: $\tau \frac{dPV}{dt} + PV = K_p \cdot MV$. **Step 1:** With a step change $\Delta MV$, the solution is: $$ PV(t) = PV(0) + K_p \Delta MV \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) $$ **Step 2:** At $t = \tau$: $PV(\tau) = PV(0) + 0.632 \cdot K_p \Delta MV$. This defines the **time constant** — the time to reach 63.2% of the final value. **Step 3:** The **time to reach 95%** is $3\tau$, and **99%** is $5\tau$. This is the engineering rule used to size loops and tune controllers. > [!NOTE] > This is why safety engineers quote a "**3-time-constant rule**" for sizing emergency isolation: a valve must close within ~3$\tau$ of the disturbance for the loop to recover. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: A Classic Feedback Control Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR SP[Setpoint SP] -->|Reference| SUM((Sum)) PV[Process Variable PV] -->|Feedback| SUM SUM -->|Error e| CTRL[Controller
PID Algorithm] CTRL -->|Output u| VALVE[Final Control Element
Control Valve] VALVE -->|Manipulated Variable MV| PROC[Process
Reactor Column] PROC -->|Disturbance| DIST[External Load Disturbance] PROC -->|PV Measurement| SENS[Sensor
Transmitter] SENS -->|Signal 4-20 mA| PV classDef ctrl fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000 classDef field fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72,color:#000 classDef proc fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#78281f,color:#000 class CTRL ctrl class VALVE,SENS,SUM field class PROC,DIST proc ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Safety Instrumented System (SIS) Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD PROC2[Chemical Process
Reactor] -->|Pressure rises| SEN1[Pressure Sensor 1] PROC2 -->|Pressure rises| SEN2[Pressure Sensor 2] SEN1 --> VOT[1oo2 Voting Logic] SEN2 --> VOT VOT -->|Trip Signal| LOGIC[SIS Logic Solver
Safety PLC] LOGIC -->|De-energize| SOL[Shutdown Solenoid] SOL -->|Close| SDV[Shutdown Valve SDV] SDV -->|Block Feed| PROC2 HMI[Operator HMI
Alarm Display] -.->|Status| LOGIC LOGIC -.->|Alarm Ack| HMI classDef sis fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000 classDef plc fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000 classDef proc fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#78281f,color:#000 class SOL,SDV sis class VOT,LOGIC plc class PROC2 proc ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: Layers of Protection with Control ```mermaid flowchart TB L0[Inherent Safe Design
Attenuation Substitution] --> L1[Process Control
Basic Control Loop PFD 0.1 to 1] L1 --> L2[Alarms Operator Action
RRF 10] L2 --> L3[SIS Interlocks
RRF 10 to 1000] L3 --> L4[Pressure Relief Devices
PSV Rupture Disc] L4 --> L5[Physical Protection
Dikes Bunds Blast Walls] L5 --> L6[Emergency Response
Fire Gas Detection] L1 --> NOTE1[Continuous Automatic] L3 --> NOTE2[On Demand Automatic] L2 --> NOTE3[Human in Loop] classDef early fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#196f3d classDef mid fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#7d6608 classDef late fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#78281f class L0,L1 early class L2,L3 mid class L4,L5,L6 late ``` ## 4.4 Process Flow: Cascade Control on a Jacketed Reactor ```mermaid flowchart LR TC1[Primary TC
Reactor Temperature] -->|Setpoint| TC2[Secondary TC
Jacket Temperature] TT1[Reactor T Sensor] --> TC1 TT2[Jacket T Sensor] --> TC2 TC2 -->|Steam Valve Cmd| V1[Steam Control Valve] V1 -->|Steam Flow| JKT[Jacket] JKT -->|Heat Transfer| RX[Reactor] TT1 -->|Actual T| TC1 RX --> TT1 classDef pri fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608 classDef sec fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72 classDef proc fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#78281f class TC1 pri class TC2,V1 sec class RX,JKT proc ``` ## 4.5 Block Diagram: PID Action in a Controller ```mermaid flowchart LR E[Error e] --> P[P Action Kc e] E --> I[I Action Kc tauI integral of e] E --> D[D Action Kc tauD de dt] P --> SUM2((Add)) I --> SUM2 D --> SUM2 SUM2 --> U[Controller Output u] U --> VALVE2[Control Valve] classDef p fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d classDef i fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#7d6608 classDef d fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#78281f class P p class I i class D d ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between a basic process control system (BPCS) and a Safety Instrumented System (SIS).** **Model Answer:** | Feature | BPCS | SIS | |---|---|---| | **Purpose** | Maintain process at desired operating point | Take process to safe state on demand | | **Independence** | Part of process operation | Independent and separate from BPCS | | **Standards** | IEC 61131 (general control) | IEC 61511 / ISA 84 (safety) | | **Action** | Continuous modulation | Discrete trip action | | **Failure** | Fail-safe, can degrade gracefully | Must fail to safe state (de-energize to trip) | | **Hardware** | General-purpose DCS | Safety-certified (SIL-rated) | | **Risk Reduction Factor** | ~1 (not credited as IPL in LOPA) | 10 to 10,000 depending on SIL | --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define Safety Integrity Level (SIL). State the PFD ranges for SIL 1, 2, and 3.** **Model Answer:** A **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** is a discrete level (1, 2, or 3) used in IEC 61511 to specify the safety performance of a safety function. It is expressed in terms of the **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)**. | SIL | PFD Range | RRF Range | |---|---|---| | SIL 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ | 10 to 100 | | SIL 2 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ | 100 to 1,000 | | SIL 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | 1,000 to 10,000 | Lower PFD = higher safety integrity. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]` **(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram, the working of a closed-loop **feedback control system** in a chemical process. Identify each block and explain its function. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive the **PID controller equation** from first principles. Show the contribution of each term (P, I, D) and explain how it improves the control response over a simple on-off controller. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution (a) A **closed-loop feedback control system** continuously measures the process variable (PV) and compares it to a desired setpoint (SP). The difference (error) drives a controller, which adjusts a final control element that influences the process. **Block Diagram (to be drawn in the answer):** ``` Setpoint SP → (+) → Controller → Control Valve → Process → PV (−) ↑ │ └──────── Sensor ←────────────────── ┘ ``` **Functions of each block:** 1. **Setpoint (SP):** The desired value of the process variable entered by the operator (e.g., 80 °C reactor temperature). 2. **Comparator (Summing Junction):** Computes the error $e(t) = SP - PV$. 3. **Controller:** Implements the control law (P, PI, or PID). Decides the corrective action. 4. **Final Control Element:** Typically a pneumatically or electrically actuated control valve that adjusts steam, coolant, or feed flow. 5. **Process:** The actual chemical/physical system (e.g., a CSTR, heat exchanger, or distillation column). 6. **Sensor / Transmitter:** Measures the PV and converts it to a 4–20 mA signal for the controller. **Industrial Example:** A jacketed exothermic reactor where the temperature sensor sends a 4–20 mA signal to a PID controller in the DCS, which modulates a steam valve on the jacket to maintain temperature. **Advantages:** Self-correcting, accommodates disturbances, automatic operation, no need for constant operator intervention. #### Model Solution (b) The **Proportional–Integral–Derivative (PID) controller** is built by combining three corrective actions, each addressing a different aspect of the error history. **Step 1 — Proportional Term (P):** The output is proportional to the instantaneous error: $$ u_P(t) = K_c \cdot e(t) $$ Larger $K_c$ means faster but more oscillatory response. **Step 2 — Integral Term (I):** The output is proportional to the integral (accumulation) of past errors: $$ u_I(t) = \frac{K_c}{\tau_I} \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau $$ This eliminates the **steady-state offset** that a P-controller cannot remove. **Step 3 — Derivative Term (D):** The output is proportional to the rate of change of error: $$ u_D(t) = K_c \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt} $$ This **anticipates** future error and **damps** oscillations in slow processes. **Step 4 — Combined PID Equation:** $$ u(t) = K_c e(t) + \frac{K_c}{\tau_I}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_c \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt} $$ **Step 5 — Comparison with On-Off Controller:** | Feature | On-Off | PID | |---|---|---| | Output | 0% or 100% | Continuous 0–100% | | Oscillation | Continuous around SP | Can be damped | | Offset | Inherent | Eliminated by I-term | | Actuator wear | High | Low | Hence, PID provides **smoother, more accurate, and stable** control. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]` **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** architecture as per IEC 61511. List the Safety Integrity Levels (SIL 1 to SIL 3) with their PFD ranges. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A pressure transmitter on a chemical reactor has a failure rate of $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour. The plant performs a proof test every year ($T = 8760$ hours). Calculate the PFD and RRF for a 1oo2 redundant sensor system. Identify the corresponding SIL. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution (a) An **SIS** is an independent, safety-rated system designed to bring a process to a safe state when process conditions exceed defined safe limits. It is governed by **IEC 61511** (process industries) and **ISA 84.00.01**. **Block Diagram (to be drawn in the answer):** ``` Process → Sensor(s) → Logic Solver → Final Element → Safe Shutdown Action ↓ ↓ ↓ Safety-Rated Safety PLC SOV + Shutdown Valve ``` **Three core components:** 1. **Sensor(s):** Detect the hazardous deviation (e.g., high-pressure transmitter on a reactor). 2. **Logic Solver:** A safety PLC (e.g., Triconex, Honeywell Safety Manager) that executes the safety logic and decides the trip action. It must be **SIL-certified**. 3. **Final Element:** The actuator that physically performs the safe action (e.g., a solenoid-operated shutdown valve that closes feed on trip). **Key Features:** - Physically and logically **separate** from the BPCS. - **Fail-safe** design (typically de-energize to trip). - Subject to **proof testing** at regular intervals. - Has a defined **SIL** based on risk assessment. **SIL Table:** | SIL | PFD Range | RRF Range | |---|---|---| | SIL 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ | 10 to 100 | | SIL 2 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ | 100 to 1,000 | | SIL 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | 1,000 to 10,000 | #### Model Solution (b) **Given:** $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour, $T = 8760$ hours, 1oo2 voting. **Step 1:** Calculate $\lambda T$: $$ \lambda T = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 = 0.0438 $$ **Step 2:** For a 1oo2 system, the PFD is given by: $$ \text{PFD}_{1oo2} \approx \frac{(\lambda T)^2}{3} $$ **Step 3:** Compute the numerator: $$ (\lambda T)^2 = (0.0438)^2 = 1.918 \times 10^{-3} $$ **Step 4:** Compute PFD: $$ \text{PFD}_{1oo2} = \frac{1.918 \times 10^{-3}}{3} = 6.39 \times 10^{-4} $$ **Step 5:** Compute RRF: $$ \text{RRF} = \frac{1}{6.39 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1564 $$ **Step 6:** Identify the SIL: - PFD = $6.39 \times 10^{-4}$ falls in the range $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$. - **Therefore, the system meets SIL 3.** - RRF of ~1564 confirms SIL 3 (RRF range 1,000–10,000). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to square $\lambda T$ for 1oo2.** Many students write $\text{PFD} = \lambda T/2$ (which is for a 1oo1 single sensor) and lose 3 marks. > 2. **Confusing the voting logic.** 1oo2 means *either* sensor can trip; the system fails only if *both* fail. 2oo2 means *both* must trip; system fails if *either* fails — completely different formula. > 3. **Not showing units** in intermediate steps. > 4. **Wrong SIL identification** by misreading the PFD range. > 5. **Not stating the standard** (IEC 61511) explicitly in part (a). > 6. **Forgetting to draw the block diagram** in part (a) — minimum 2 marks lost. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Control** is the first **active engineered layer of protection** in chemical process safety. - The four blocks of any loop: **Sensor → Controller → Final Control Element → Process**. - The four standard signals: **SP, PV, e, MV**. - The **PID equation**: $u(t) = K_c e + \frac{K_c}{\tau_I}\int e\,dt + K_c \tau_D \frac{de}{dt}$. - **P** corrects now, **I** corrects the past (eliminates offset), **D** anticipates the future (damps oscillation). - **Ziegler–Nichols tuning** formulas: $K_c = 0.45 K_u$ (PI), $K_c = 0.6 K_u$ (PID). - A **BPCS** is for *operation*; an **SIS** is for *safety* — keep them **independent**. - **SIL** ranges (IEC 61511): SIL 1 → PFD $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$; SIL 2 → $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$; SIL 3 → $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$. - **RRF = 1/PFD**. - **1oo2 PFD** $\approx (\lambda T)^2 / 3$; **1oo1 PFD** $\approx \lambda T / 2$. - **Alarms** rely on human action (RRF ~10); **Interlocks** are automatic (RRF 10–1000); **SIS** is the highest automatic integrity (RRF up to 10,000). - **Proof testing** is essential — a dormant sensor is a dead sensor. - **Fail-safe principle:** De-energize to trip (DTT) is the standard for SIS valves. - **Standards to memorize:** IEC 61511 (SIS), ISA 84 (US equivalent), ISA 18.2 (Alarm Management), IEC 61131 (DCS programming). - **Time-constant rule:** $3\tau$ to reach 95%, $5\tau$ to reach 99% of a step change.

topics.title: Use of Fail Safe Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT616 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Use of Fail Safe Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fail-Safe Device (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A *fail-safe device* is an engineered component, instrument, or control system element that, upon the occurrence of any specific failure mode (loss of power, loss of signal, loss of air supply, component break-down, or operator error), is designed to automatically revert to a predetermined **safe condition** without requiring external human intervention. The device must transition to a state that minimizes risk to personnel, equipment, and the environment.

In the context of **Chemical Process Safety (CPS)** and **Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS)** as governed by **IEC 61511**, a fail-safe device is a fundamental building block of the *Risk Reduction* layer of protection. It is the physical manifestation of *inherent safety design* at the component level.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Principle (Crowl & Louvar):** "A truly fail-safe system is one in which all conceivable failure modes result in a condition that does not create additional hazard." This is the cornerstone philosophy taught in OESFT616.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Household Stove" Model

Imagine a domestic LPG gas stove connected to a cylinder:
- The burner requires **manual ignition** to release gas.
- If you **accidentally leave the knob open** and the flame goes out, gas keeps flowing — this is a **fail-dangerous** design.
- Now, replace the knob with a **spring-loaded valve** that automatically snaps shut the instant a thermocouple cools down (because the flame went out). The valve has *failed* in the sense that it has activated, but it has *failed* into the **safe condition** (gas off). This is a **fail-safe** device.

In a chemical plant, the philosophy is the same: when something goes wrong, the plant should "snap to safety" rather than "snap to danger."

## 1.3 Classification of Failure Modes

For any protective device, failures are classified into four categories as per **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511**:

| Failure Mode | Description | Example |
|--------------|-------------|---------|
| **Safe Failure (S)** | Device fails and initiates the protective action (e.g., valve closes on air failure) | Spring-return valve slams shut |
| **Dangerous Failure (D)** | Device fails but the protective action does NOT occur | Solenoid valve stuck open |
| **No-Effect Failure (N)** | Failure has no impact on protective function | Cosmetic damage to housing |
| **Annunciation Failure (A)** | Failure prevents detection of a dangerous condition | Burned-out indicator lamp |

The **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)** is a critical metric:

$$SFF = \frac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{total}}$$

where:
- $\lambda_S$ = Safe failure rate
- $\lambda_{DU}$ = Dangerous Undetected failure rate
- $\lambda_{total}$ = Total failure rate (typically per hour)

A higher SFF means a more reliable fail-safe device.

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bolded)

- **SIL — Safety Integrity Level:** Discrete levels (1, 2, 3, 4) indicating the reliability of a safety function.
- **PFD — Probability of Failure on Demand:** Ranges from $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-5}$ depending on SIL.
- **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT):** Ability of a system to continue functioning with one or more faults present.
- **MTTF — Mean Time To Failure:** Typically expressed in **years**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fail-Safe Design Philosophy (Layered Logic)

The fail-safe design principle follows a strict, sequential logic in chemical process plants:

1. **Identify Hazard** → Recognize the process deviation (e.g., over-pressure, over-temperature, toxic release).
2. **Define Safe State** → Specify the safe end condition (e.g., shut off feed, vent reactor, isolate storage tank).
3. **Identify Failure Modes** → Enumerate every way the device can fail (loss of power, air, signal, mechanical jam).
4. **Design Inherent Safety** → Choose the failure direction that *naturally* leads to the safe state.
5. **Select Energization Mode** → Decide between *De-energize-to-Trip (DTT)* or *Energize-to-Trip (ETT)*.
6. **Validate via SIL Analysis** → Quantify reliability using PFD calculations.
7. **Document & Test** → Establish proof-test intervals per IEC 61511.

## 2.2 DTT vs ETT — The Core Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **De-energize-to-Trip (DTT) — "Fail-Safe" Default:**
> In the normal operating condition, the safety device is **energized** (receives power/signal). Upon loss of energy, it **trips** (moves to safe state). This is the **preferred** configuration because loss of any utility (power, air, signal) automatically results in safety.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energize-to-Trip (ETT) — "Fail-Dangerous" by Nature:**
> In the normal operating condition, the safety device is **de-energized**. A specific power/signal pulse is required to trip it. Loss of utility results in the system remaining in its *last state*, which may be dangerous. ETT is used only when the consequences of a spurious trip are economically catastrophic (e.g., furnace trip in a refinery causing weeks of downtime).

### Why DTT Dominates
- The probability of all four failure events (loss of power AND loss of air AND loss of signal AND valve failure) simultaneously is **vanishingly small**.
- Industry data shows **80%+ of ESD systems** worldwide use DTT logic.

## 2.3 Categories of Fail-Safe Devices

| Category | Function | Typical Application |
|----------|----------|---------------------|
| **Spring-Return Pneumatic Valves** | Air-to-Open / Spring-to-Close (or vice versa) | Emergency isolation, feed shutoff |
| **Solenoid-Operated Valves** | Energize to operate, de-energize to safe state | Instrument air lines, ESD valves |
| **Rupture Discs** | Burst at predetermined pressure | Over-pressure protection of vessels |
| **Safety Relief Valves (SRV)** | Open at set pressure, reclose | Boilers, pressure vessels, reactors |
| **Flame Arrestors** | Quench flame propagation | Storage tanks, vent lines |
| **Explosion Vents / Flameless Vents** | Rupture to release pressure | Dust collectors, dryers, mills |
| **Emergency Shutdown (ESD) Systems** | Plant-wide or unit-wide trip logic | Refineries, gas plants |
| **Trip Amplifiers & Relays** | Compare PV vs SP, output trip signal | Burner Management Systems (BMS) |
| **Non-Return (Check) Valves** | Prevent reverse flow | Compressor discharge, pump discharge |
| **Purging Systems** | Maintain inert blanket in vapor space | Flammable liquid storage |

## 2.4 The 2oo3 / 1oo2 / 2oo4 Voting Logic

For high-SIL applications, multiple sensors/voted logic is used:

- **1oo1** — One-out-of-One: Single channel. Simplest but lowest reliability.
- **1oo2** — One-out-of-Two: Either of two signals trips the system. *High safety, low availability.*
- **2oo2** — Two-out-of-Two: Both must trip. *High availability, low safety.*
- **2oo3** — Two-out-of-Three: Two of three must agree. **Best compromise** — used in most BMS and ESD systems.

The **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** for a 2oo3 system:

$$PFD_{2oo3} = 3 \cdot (1 - e^{-\lambda t}) \cdot e^{-2\lambda t} \approx 6\lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot t_{CE}$$

where:
- $\lambda$ = failure rate of each channel (per hour)
- $t$ = proof test interval
- $t_{CE}$ = channel equivalent mean downtime

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Expression | KTU Application |
|------------------|------------|-----------------|
| **Safe Failure Fraction** | $SFF = (\lambda_S + \lambda_{DU}) / \lambda_{total}$ | Reliability classification of devices |
| **Average PFD for 1oo1** | $PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{2}\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}$ | Single sensor SIL analysis |
| **Average PFD for 1oo2** | $PFD_{avg} = \frac{2}{3}\lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot T_{proof}^2$ | Redundant sensor SIL analysis |
| **Average PFD for 2oo3** | $PFD_{avg} = 6\lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot T_{proof}$ | Voted sensor SIL analysis |
| **SIL → PFD Range** | SIL 1: $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$; SIL 2: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$; SIL 3: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | Target PFD per safety function |
| **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** | $RRF = 1 / PFD_{avg}$ | Quantification of safety layer |
| **Set Pressure Rule (ASME)** | $P_{set} \le 1.10 \times MAWP$ | Relief device sizing philosophy |
| **Accumulation Pressure Rule** | $P_{accumulation} \le 1.21 \times MAWP$ for single exposure | Fire-case relief sizing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** These equations are used daily in **HAZOP/LOPA studies**, **SIL classification workshops**, and **SRS (Safety Requirements Specification)** documents. Every B.Tech chemical engineer entering a process plant, EPC firm, or PSM consultancy must be able to apply these formulas.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average PFD for a 1oo1 System

The simplest fail-safe configuration is a single sensor (1oo1) feeding a single final element. We need to derive the average probability that this channel will *fail dangerously* during a complete proof-test cycle of duration $T$.

**Step 1 — Define the dangerous failure rate.**
Let $\lambda_{DU}$ be the constant dangerous undetected failure rate (per hour) of the channel. This is supplied by manufacturer data (e.g., from **exida**, **exIDAcert**, or **Oreda handbook**).

**Step 2 — Express the unavailability function.**
For a single channel, the probability that a dangerous failure has *not yet* been detected and repaired at time $t$ (where $0 \le t \le T$) is given by the reliability function:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda_{DU} t}$$

**Step 3 — The unavailability (failure on demand) function:**
The probability of being in a dangerous failed state at time $t$ is:

$$PFD(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} t}$$

**Step 4 — Average over the entire proof test interval:**
To obtain the *average* unavailability over one complete interval $T$, we integrate $PFD(t)$ from $0$ to $T$ and divide by $T$:

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} (1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} t}) \, dt$$

**Step 5 — Perform the integration term by term:**
Break the integrand into two parts:

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \left[ \int_{0}^{T} 1 \, dt - \int_{0}^{T} e^{-\lambda_{DU} t} \, dt \right]$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the first integral:**
The first integral is simply the duration of the interval:

$$\int_{0}^{T} 1 \, dt = T$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the second integral using the standard exponential form:**
Recall the antiderivative identity: $\int e^{-at} dt = -\frac{1}{a} e^{-at} + C$. Applying with $a = \lambda_{DU}$:

$$\int_{0}^{T} e^{-\lambda_{DU} t} \, dt = \left[ -\frac{1}{\lambda_{DU}} e^{-\lambda_{DU} t} \right]_{0}^{T} = -\frac{1}{\lambda_{DU}} e^{-\lambda_{DU} T} + \frac{1}{\lambda_{DU}}$$

**Step 8 — Combine and simplify:**
Substituting the two results into the average equation:

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \left[ T - \left( -\frac{1}{\lambda_{DU}} e^{-\lambda_{DU} T} + \frac{1}{\lambda_{DU}} \right) \right]$$

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \left[ T - \frac{1}{\lambda_{DU}} + \frac{1}{\lambda_{DU}} e^{-\lambda_{DU} T} \right]$$

$$PFD_{avg} = 1 - \frac{1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} T}}{\lambda_{DU} T}$$

**Step 9 — Apply the small-$\lambda T$ approximation:**
For most chemical plant applications, $\lambda_{DU} \cdot T \ll 1$ (typical values: $\lambda_{DU} = 10^{-6}$/hr, $T = 8760$ hr/yr, so product $\approx 0.0088$). Using the Taylor expansion $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x + \frac{x^2}{2}$:

$$1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} T} \approx \lambda_{DU} T - \frac{(\lambda_{DU} T)^2}{2}$$

Substituting this back:

$$PFD_{avg} \approx 1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU} T - \frac{(\lambda_{DU} T)^2}{2}}{\lambda_{DU} T} = 1 - \left( 1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU} T}{2} \right)$$

**Step 10 — Final simplified result for 1oo1:**

$$\boxed{PFD_{avg, 1oo1} \approx \frac{1}{2} \lambda_{DU} \cdot T}$$

This is the canonical result used in **LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis)** and is a required calculation in every KTU SIL determination problem.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A single pressure transmitter is used as the sensor in a 1oo1 overpressure protection loop on a propane storage bullet. The transmitter has a dangerous undetected failure rate of $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour. The proof-test interval is one year ($T = 8760$ hr). Determine (a) the average PFD and (b) the achieved SIL level.

**Solution (a):**
